topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Prestressing - Types of Pre-stressing - Advantages - Limitations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — CONCEPT OF PRESTRESSING

## 1.1 The Fundamental Definition of Prestressing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 1):** *Concept of Prestressing — Types of Pre-stressing — Advantages — Limitations*

**Prestressing of concrete** is a deliberate, engineered method of introducing **pre-determined internal stresses (pre-compression)** into a structural concrete member **before** it is subjected to the **service (working) loads**. The objective is to counter-balance the tensile stresses that will later be induced by the external loads, so that the concrete — which is **weak in tension but strong in compression** — remains essentially in a state of compression under all working load conditions.

The pre-compression is induced by tensioning high-strength **tendons** (wires, strands, or bars) that are anchored to the concrete member, either by direct bond (bonded tendons) or by external end anchorages (unbonded tendons).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU-Standard Definition:**
> *"Prestressing is the imposition of an internal force distribution upon a structural concrete member such that the member is pre-disposed to resist the stresses produced by the externally applied loads to a degree that the resulting stresses in the concrete are wholly or partly compressive."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Why Do We Prestress?

**Imagine a stack of books** (the concrete, which is brittle and weak in tension). If you try to lift the stack by grabbing the bottom book, the bottom book fails in tension — it tears apart because the upper books pull it apart (flexural tensile stress).

**Solution:** Before lifting, **squeeze the stack tightly together** by binding it with a strong belt (the prestressing tendon). Now when you lift the stack, the belt tries to hold the books together while the load tries to pull them apart — the net effect is that the bottom book is in **compression**, not tension. The stack can now be lifted safely.

In a beam:
- **Concrete =** the stack of books (weak in tension)
- **Tendon =** the binding belt (high-strength steel)
- **External Load =** the lifting action (creates bending tension)
- **Prestress =** the squeezing force from the belt (creates pre-compression)

The two opposing stress systems **algebraically cancel** the tensile portion, leaving the concrete predominantly in compression — a state where it is **~10× stronger** than in tension.

### 1.3 The Core Engineering Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Guiding Axioms of Prestressed Concrete Design:**
> 1. **Axiom of Stress Neutralisation:** Prestress must neutralise (or significantly reduce) the tensile stresses caused by service loads.
> 2. **Axiom of High-Strength Pairing:** Only high-strength concrete (M40 and above) and high-tensile steel (f<sub>y</sub> ≥ 1400 N/mm²) should be used, since ordinary mild steel yields under sustained prestress.
> 3. **Axiom of Load Balancing:** The prestress force should be designed to balance a known portion of the external load, simplifying the structural analysis.

### 1.4 Historical Snapshot (for 2-mark context)

| Year | Milestone | Engineer |
|---|---|---|
| 1886 | First patent for prestressed concrete | P.H. Jackson (USA) |
| 1908 | Earliest meaningful attempt | C.R. Steiner (USA) |
| 1928 | Loss of prestress identified; high-tensile steel proposed | **Eugène Freyssinet (France)** |
| 1940s | Modern PSC using Freyssinet, Magnel, Hoyer, Lee-McCall systems | Freyssinet, Magnel, etc. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PSC stayed dormant for ~40 years (1888–1928):** Early attempts used mild steel, which lost almost all its prestress due to **shrinkage** and **creep** of concrete. Freyssinet's 1928 breakthrough was recognizing that **only high-tensile steel with very low relaxation** can sustain permanent prestress.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Underlying Mechanics — The Stress Superposition Principle

The fundamental principle of prestressing is **linear superposition of stress states** (valid for elastic, uncracked concrete under service loads).

Consider a simply-supported rectangular beam of span $L$ subjected to a uniformly distributed load $w$ (excluding self-weight, which is balanced by prestress in the analysis).

**Stress in concrete at any fibre, due to prestress alone (eccentrically applied force $P$ at eccentricity $e$):**

$$
\sigma_{c,PS} = -\frac{P}{A} \mp \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

Where:
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of concrete
- $I$ = second moment of area
- $y$ = distance of the fibre from the **centroidal axis (CNA)**
- Upper sign convention: **tension positive, compression negative**

**Stress in concrete at the same fibre, due to external load $w$ alone (at the same section):**

$$
\sigma_{c,LL} = +\frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Net stress (Superposition — valid for uncracked elastic section):**

$$
\sigma_{c,\text{net}} = -\frac{P}{A} \mp \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I} + \frac{M \cdot y}{I}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer’s Goal:** Choose $P$ and $e$ such that $\sigma_{c,\text{net}} \le f_{ct,\text{allowable}}$ (often zero) at the bottom fibre under service load. This makes the entire section remain in compression.

## 2.2 The Three Classical Concepts of Prestressing

There are **three distinct physical interpretations** of how prestressing works. All three yield the same governing equations but differ in the *mental model* a designer uses.

### (a) First Concept — **Internal Stress Concept (Force System)**

> Prestressing is the imposition of an **equivalent force system** consisting of:
> - An **axial compression** $P$ acting at the centroid of the tendon
> - **Moments** $M = P \cdot e$ that produce a **linear stress distribution** in the section.

**Use:** Foundation-level design and force-balance diagrams.

### (b) Second Concept — **Strengthening the Steel**

> Treat the high-tensile steel tendon as a **load-carrying partner** with concrete. The tendon is **pre-stretched** so that, under the same external elongation, the concrete and steel deform compatibly. This allows the section to carry **higher tensile loads** before the steel yields.

**Use:** When a designer thinks of the tendon as a tensile reinforcement acting at depth.

### (c) Third Concept — **Load Balancing (Most Useful for Designers)**

> Choose the prestressing tendon profile such that the **transverse load imposed by the tendon on the concrete** exactly balances a chosen portion of the applied transverse load. The net effect is a beam that is **free of bending** — it behaves like an axially loaded member.

**The upward (or transverse) force per unit length** $w_{ps}$ from a parabolic tendon with sag $h$ carrying prestress $P$ is:

$$
w_{ps} = \frac{8 \cdot P \cdot h}{L^2}
$$

Setting $w_{ps} = w$ (the applied UDL) gives a **balanced load** design.

## 2.3 TYPES OF PRESTRESSING

### 2.3.1 Classification Based on **Source of Prestress Force**

| Type | Mechanism | Prestress Achieved By | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Jacking** | Mechanical stretching by hydraulic rams | Tensioning high-tensile tendons against the hardened concrete | **Pre-tensioning & Post-tensioning** |
| **Chemical Prestressing** | Expansive cement / autogenous growth | Using expanding cements that swell during hydration | Experimental, not common in practice |
| **Electrical Prestressing** | Heating tendons electrically | Heating the steel to elongate, anchoring, then cooling | Niche applications |
| **Mechanical Prestressing** | Screw jacks, weights | Sustained external dead-weight loads | Temporary works |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU board examinations, **only hydraulic jacking** is the practically relevant source. The other three are typically asked as **short-note 3-mark questions**.

### 2.3.2 Classification Based on **Stage of Construction** (★ Most Important for KTU)

#### **A. Pre-tensioning**
- Tendons are **stressed first**, **before** the concrete is cast.
- Concrete is cast around the **already-tensioned** tendons in a mould (called a *pretensioning bed*).
- Once the concrete attains the **release strength** (typically $f_{ci} \ge 0.7 \, f_{ck}$), the tendons are **cut** (or slowly released) at the ends.
- Prestress is transferred to the concrete **purely by bond** between the tendon and surrounding concrete (no separate anchorages).

> **Applications:** Railway sleepers, electrical poles, bridge girders in casting yards, hollow-core slabs.

#### **B. Post-tensioning**
- Concrete is cast **first**, with ducts/sheaths placed inside.
- After concrete attains the required strength, **tendons are threaded** through the ducts and stressed using hydraulic jacks against the **end anchorages** cast into the concrete.
- Prestress is transferred **via the end anchorages**, not by bond.
- After stressing, ducts are **grouted** (bonded post-tensioning) to protect tendons from corrosion and provide additional bond.

> **Applications:** Bridge decks, long-span beams, segmental construction, water tanks, slabs-on-grade.

### 2.3.3 Side-by-Side Comparison Table (★ KTU Favourite)

| Parameter | Pre-tensioning | Post-tensioning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sequence** | Stress → Cast → Release | Cast → Cure → Stress |
| **Bond mechanism** | Bond (no anchorages) | Anchorage (+ bond if grouted) |
| **Member size** | Small, mass-producible | Large, custom-built |
| **Prestress loss** | Higher (immediate elastic + bond) | Lower (no bond during release) |
| **End anchorage** | Not required (relying on bond) | Heavy, critical component |
| **Cables/strands** | Straight, single/double strands | Curved, parabolic profiles possible |
| **Field operation** | Limited | Yes (site stressing) |
| **Cost** | Lower per unit (mass production) | Higher per unit |
| **Typical uses** | Sleepers, poles, hollow-core slabs | Bridges, large-span beams |
| **IS Code clause** | IS 1343, IS 6003 | IS 1343, IS 6006 |

### 2.3.4 Classification Based on **Tendon Profile (Geometry)**

| Profile | Bending Moment Diagram Counteracted | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Straight** | Constant (rectangular) | Centric pre-tensioned members |
| **Trapezoidal** | Partially varying | Beams with variable depth |
| **Parabolic** | Uniformly distributed load | Continuous beams, simply-supported slabs |
| **Curved/Harped** | Point loads / shear dominant | Cantilevers, deep beams |

### 2.3.5 Classification Based on **Bonding at Ultimate State**

| Type | Definition | Behaviour at ULS |
|---|---|---|
| **Bonded** | Ducts are grouted after stressing | Strain compatibility between concrete and steel |
| **Unbonded** | Ducts are not grouted; tendons greased and sheathed | Tendon elongates as a whole; larger crack widths |
| **External** | Tendons placed outside the section (external to web) | Inspectable and replaceable |

### 2.3.6 Classification Based on **Degree of Prestress**

Per **IS 1343 : 2012**, three classes are defined:

| Class | Description | Tensile Stress Limit at Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Class 1** | No tensile stress permitted (fully prestressed) | $\sigma_{ct} \le 0$ |
| **Class 2** | Limited tensile stress permitted (no cracking) | $\sigma_{ct} \le 0.25 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ |
| **Class 3** | Cracking permitted (partially prestressed) | $\sigma_{ct} \le 0.5 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ |

## 2.4 ADVANTAGES OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield List — Memorise for 7-Mark Questions:**

### Structural Advantages
1. **Crack-free service behaviour** — Under working loads, the section remains uncracked → better durability, watertightness (essential for **water tanks, pipes, nuclear containments**).
2. **High strength-to-weight ratio** — Smaller, slimmer sections carry the same load as much larger RCC sections → **longer spans, less self-weight**.
3. **Full section utilization** — The entire cross-section is effective (in RCC, cracked section ignores the tension zone in design, only ~50% of the section is effective for stiffness).
4. **Improved shear capacity** — Principal tensile stresses are reduced by the pre-compression → less web reinforcement needed.
5. **Better fatigue resistance** — Smaller stress range under cyclic loading → railway bridges, dynamic loads.
6. **Suitability for high-stress environments** — Bridges, silos, offshore structures, pressure vessels.

### Constructional / Economic Advantages
7. **Rapid construction in precast pre-tensioning** — Factory-controlled quality, mass production.
8. **Longer spans possible** — PSC bridges up to 250 m span are routine; RCC is uneconomical beyond ~25 m.
9. **Reduced material consumption** — Smaller concrete volume and steel quantity per unit load-carrying capacity.
10. **Better deflection control** — Pre-camber offsets downward deflection → very stiff, near-rigid behaviour at service.
11. **No reinforcement congestion** — Tendons are placed at specific locations; the section remains compact.

### Durability & Serviceability
12. **High corrosion resistance of tendons** — Tendons are protected by concrete cover or grouted ducts.
13. **Lower maintenance cost** over the design life.
14. **Improved impact and seismic resilience** due to monolithic action.

## 2.5 LIMITATIONS OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiners penalise "vague" answers here. Use specific technical points:**

1. **High initial cost** — Requires high-tensile steel, anchorages, jacks, ducts, grouting equipment. Economical only for **long spans, mass production, or critical applications**.
2. **Need for skilled labour and strict quality control** — Prestressing is unforgiving; errors during stressing are difficult to correct.
3. **Complicated formwork and staging** — Especially for draped tendons, harped profiles, and post-tensioning ducts.
4. **Specialised equipment required** — Hydraulic jacks, stressing chairs, grouting pumps — not always available on remote sites.
5. **Loss of prestress** — Over time, prestress force reduces due to **elastic shortening, shrinkage, creep of concrete, and relaxation of steel**. This must be carefully estimated and compensated.
6. **Brittle failure mode** — PSC exhibits little warning before failure; the stress–strain curve is closer to a bilinear elastic–sudden-failure profile (unlike the ductile behaviour of RCC).
7. **Difficult to alter / demolish** — Tendons under high tension are hazardous; cutting them releases stored energy suddenly.
8. **Heavy end-anchorages** — Add to member weight and require careful detailing of the bursting-reinforcement zone.
9. **Fire resistance** — Loss of prestress is accelerated at high temperatures (steel relaxes, concrete spalls).
10. **Specialised consultancy and design expertise required** — Cannot be designed by conventional RCC engineers without training.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of the Stress Distribution in a Pre-tensioned Centric Member

### Problem Setup
A rectangular concrete member of cross-section $b \times d$ is **centrically pre-tensioned** with a force $P_0$ (initial prestress force, immediately after release). The area of tendon is $A_p$, and the area of concrete is $A_c$.

### Step 1: Equilibrium Just Before Release

The tendon is stretched to a stress $f_{pi}$ and pulled against the end abutments. The concrete is **stress-free** (just cast, no bond developed yet).

$$
P_0 = f_{pi} \cdot A_p
$$

### Step 2: Release — Tendon Tries to Shorten, Concrete Resists

When the tendons are cut at the ends, they try to regain their original length. But the surrounding concrete (now hardened and bonded) **resists this shortening** by sharing the load.

At the section of release, equilibrium requires:

$$
P_0 = P_c + P_p
$$

Where:
- $P_c$ = compression force taken by concrete
- $P_p$ = residual tension force in tendon (after elastic loss)

### Step 3: Compatibility of Strains

The tendon and the concrete must shorten by the **same amount** at the section of release (strain compatibility due to bond):

$$
\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_p
$$

### Step 4: Force-Displacement in Elastic Range

The compression in concrete: $P_c = \sigma_c \cdot A_c$ where $\sigma_c = \varepsilon_c \cdot E_c$

The residual tension in steel: $P_p = f_p \cdot A_p$ where $f_p = f_{pi} - \varepsilon_p \cdot E_p$

### Step 5: Substitute and Solve

$$
\varepsilon_c \cdot E_c \cdot A_c = \varepsilon_c \cdot E_p \cdot A_p
$$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon_c$:

$$
E_c \cdot A_c = E_p \cdot A_p \quad \text{(NOT equal in general)}
$$

Re-derive using the correct equilibrium:

$$
f_{pi} \cdot A_p = \sigma_c \cdot A_c + f_p \cdot A_p
$$

$$
\sigma_c \cdot A_c = (f_{pi} - f_p) \cdot A_p
$$

From compatibility: $\sigma_c / E_c = (f_{pi} - f_p) / E_p$

$$
f_{pi} - f_p = \sigma_c \cdot \frac{E_p}{E_c}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_c \cdot \frac{E_p}{E_c} \cdot A_p
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_c = \frac{P_0}{A_c + \alpha_p \cdot A_p}}
$$

Where $\alpha_p = E_p / E_c$ is the **modular ratio for prestressing steel** (typically 5 to 7).

Equivalently, the **compressive stress in concrete immediately after release** is:

$$
\sigma_c = \frac{P_0}{A_c + (E_p/E_c) \cdot A_p}
$$

And the **residual steel stress** is:

$$
f_p = f_{pi} - \alpha_p \cdot \sigma_c
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designers usually neglect the steel area $A_p$ in computing $A_c$** (since $A_p \ll A_c$, the difference is < 5%). The simplified formula used in design is: $\sigma_c \approx P_0 / A_c$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Stress at a Section in an Eccentrically Prestressed Beam

For a beam with centroidal neutral axis (CNA) and tendon lying at eccentricity $e$ below CNA:

**Step 1: Identify the two effects of the prestress force $P$ at the section:**

1. **Axial compression** $= P/A$ — uniform stress across the section
2. **Bending moment** $= P \cdot e$ — produces linear stress variation

**Step 2: Express total stress at any fibre at distance $y$ from CNA:**

$$
\sigma = -\frac{P}{A} \mp \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

(Sign convention: tension positive, compression negative; the upper sign corresponds to the fibre on the same side as the tendon when $y$ is positive downward.)

**Step 3: Add the stress due to external load (at the same fibre, due to bending moment $M$):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{net}} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} - \frac{P}{A} \mp \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Step 4: For the bottom fibre (tension side under load), let $y = y_b$ (bottom):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{bottom}} = \frac{M \cdot y_b}{I} - \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_b}{I}
$$

**Step 5: For a "balanced" design where bottom fibre is just at zero stress:**

$$
\frac{M \cdot y_b}{I} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_b}{I}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
P \cdot \left( \frac{1}{A} + \frac{e \cdot y_b}{I} \right) = \frac{M \cdot y_b}{I}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = \frac{M \cdot y_b / I}{\dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{e \cdot y_b}{I}} = \frac{M \cdot y_b \cdot A}{I + A \cdot e \cdot y_b}}
$$

> This is the **required prestress force** to fully neutralise the tensile stress at the bottom fibre.

**Step 6: Check the top fibre at $y = y_t$ (above CNA):**

$$
\sigma_{\text{top}} = -\frac{M \cdot y_t}{I} - \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y_t}{I}
$$

This will be **doubly compressive** — typically the most critical fibre for compressive failure in PSC.

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Classroom Standard)

**Given:**
- Rectangular beam: $b = 200$ mm, $d = 400$ mm
- Span $L = 6$ m, simply supported
- Live load $w = 20$ kN/m (excluding self-weight)
- Initial prestress $P_0 = 400$ kN
- Eccentricity at midspan $e = 100$ mm (below CNA)
- $f_{ck} = 40$ N/mm², $f_{ci} = 30$ N/mm²

**Step 1: Section Properties**

$$
A = b \cdot d = 200 \times 400 = 80{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 400^3}{12} = 1.067 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
y_b = y_t = 200 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{centroid at mid-depth})
$$

$$
Z = I / y = 1.067 \times 10^9 / 200 = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2: Service load bending moment (at midspan)**

$$
M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 6^2}{8} = 90 \text{ kN·m} = 90 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 3: Stresses at midspan (bottom fibre)**

Prestress contribution (compression): 

$$
\sigma_{c,PS} = \frac{P_0}{A} + \frac{P_0 \cdot e}{Z}
$$

$$
\sigma_{c,PS} = \frac{400 \times 10^3}{80{,}000} + \frac{400 \times 10^3 \times 100}{5.333 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\sigma_{c,PS} = 5.0 + 7.5 = 12.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

Bending stress (tension at bottom):

$$
\sigma_{t,LL} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
$$

Net stress at bottom fibre:

$$
\sigma_{\text{net,bottom}} = 16.875 - 12.5 = +4.375 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension — residual)}
$$

> ⚠️ The bottom fibre is in tension — partial prestress only. The allowable tension for Class 2 per IS 1343 is $0.25 \sqrt{40} = 1.58$ N/mm². **The design is unsafe; $P_0$ or $e$ must be increased.**

**Step 4: Top fibre check**

$$
\sigma_{c,PS,top} = \frac{P_0}{A} - \frac{P_0 \cdot e}{Z} = 5.0 - 7.5 = -2.5 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension!)}
$$

> ⚠️ **The top fibre is in tension (2.5 N/mm²) — this is a critical KTU mistake.** When eccentricity is downward and axial compression alone, the **top fibre pre-stress is actually tensile** (because the moment $P \cdot e$ lifts the top fibre).

$$
\sigma_{\text{top,LL}} = -\frac{M}{Z} = -16.875 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{net,top}} = -2.5 - 16.875 = -19.375 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

> ✓ Top fibre is safe in compression (allowable $\approx 0.45 f_{ck} = 18$ N/mm² — slightly over, but acceptable for short-term check).

**Step 5: Required prestress to fully neutralise bottom tension**

$$
P_{\text{req}} = \frac{M}{Z \left( \frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z} \right)} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6 \left( \frac{1}{80{,}000} + \frac{100}{5.333 \times 10^6} \right)}
$$

Denominator: $5.333 \times 10^6 \times (1.25 \times 10^{-5} + 1.875 \times 10^{-5}) = 5.333 \times 10^6 \times 3.125 \times 10^{-5} = 166.66$

$$
P_{\text{req}} = \frac{90 \times 10^6}{166.66} = 540{,}000 \text{ N} = 540 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Required increase in prestress = 35%.** This is the iterative design loop students must understand.

## 3.4 The KTU Analytical Decision Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix as a flow-chart in your 7-mark answers:**

| Design Decision | Choose When | KTU Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Pre-tensioning** | Factory production, small/medium members, short spans | Lower cost, but straight tendons only |
| **Post-tensioning** | On-site, large members, curved tendons, long spans | Higher cost, more flexibility |
| **Bonded PT** | Bridges, structures needing crack control at ULS | Better crack control, less tendon corrosion |
| **Unbonded PT** | Slabs, simple span structures, easy replacement | Tendon replaceable, lower fire resistance |
| **Class 1 (fully prestressed)** | Water tanks, nuclear containments, pipes | No cracks, highest durability, highest $P$ |
| **Class 2 (limited tension)** | Bridges, beams in corrosive environments | Moderate $P$, no visible cracks |
| **Class 3 (partial prestressing)** | Building frames, where some cracking is acceptable | Lowest $P$, allows cracks, hybrid behaviour |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Concept Schematic — Pre-tensioning vs. Post-tensioning (Block Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Design Decision Required] --> B{Stage of Stressing}
    B -- BEFORE Casting --> C[Pre-tensioning Process]
    B -- AFTER Casting --> D[Post-tensioning Process]
    
    C --> C1[Step 1: Stress Tendons Against End Abutments]
    C1 --> C2[Step 2: Cast Concrete Around Tensioned Tendons]
    C2 --> C3[Step 3: Cure Concrete to Release Strength]
    C3 --> C4[Step 4: Cut Tendons at Ends]
    C4 --> C5[Step 5: Bond Transfers Prestress to Concrete]
    C5 --> C6[End: Pretensioned Member with Bonded Tendons]
    
    D --> D1[Step 1: Cast Concrete with Ducts Sheaths]
    D1 --> D2[Step 2: Cure Concrete to Required Strength]
    D2 --> D3[Step 3: Thread Tendons Through Ducts]
    D3 --> D4[Step 4: Stress Tendons Using Hydraulic Jacks]
    D4 --> D5[Step 5: Anchor Tendons at End Blocks]
    D5 --> D6{Grout Ducts?}
    D6 -- Yes --> D7[Bonded Post-tensioning]
    D6 -- No --> D8[Unbonded Post-tensioning]
    D7 --> D9[End: Bonded PT Member]
    D8 --> D10[End: Unbonded PT Member]
```

## 4.2 Stress Distribution in an Eccentrically Prestressed Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Section_RC [Rectangular Concrete Section]
        R1[Top Fibre] -- y_t 200 mm --> CNA[Centroidal Neutral Axis CNA]
        CNA -- y_b 200 mm --> R2[Bottom Fibre]
    end
    
    subgraph Stress_Diagram [Stress Block at Midspan]
        S1[Top Fibre: Net Compression 19.375 N per mm squared]
        S2[Centroid: Net Compression 5 N per mm squared]
        S3[Bottom Fibre: Net Tension 4.375 N per mm squared]
    end
    
    P[Prestress Force P 540 kN] -- Eccentricity e 100 mm --> Section_RC
    M[Service Load Moment M 90 kN m] -- Bending --> Section_RC
```

## 4.3 Load Balancing Concept (Parabolic Tendon)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Parabolic Tendon Profile] -- Sag h --> B[Concrete Beam Simply Supported]
    C[External UDL w] -- Applied Transverse Load --> B
    A -- Upward Transverse Force 8Ph per L squared --> B
    B -- If 8Ph over L squared equals w --> D[Net Bending Moment is ZERO]
    B -- If 8Ph over L squared is less than w --> E[Residual Uniform Load w minus 8Ph per L squared]
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Stress Superposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input1[External Service Load w] --> SS1[Stress State 1: Bending Stress M*y over I]
    Input2[Prestress Force P at Eccentricity e] --> SS2[Stress State 2: Axial P over A plus Moment P*e*y over I]
    SS1 --> SUM[Linear Superposition Valid for Uncracked Elastic Section]
    SS2 --> SUM
    SUM --> Output1[Net Stress: Tension reduced or eliminated]
    SUM --> Output2[Residual Compression in Concrete]
    SUM --> Output3[Residual Tension in Steel Tendon]
```

## 4.5 Master Flow Chart — KTU Exam-Oriented Decision Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Given: Type of Member and Span] --> Q1{Span more than 25 m?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> PT[Prefer Post-tensioning]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Repetitive mass production?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Pret[Prefer Pre-tensioning]
    Q2 -- No --> PT
    PT --> Q3{Curved tendon required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PTyes[Parabolic or Harped Profile]
    Q3 -- No --> PTstraight[Straight Tendon]
    Pret --> Q4[Straight Tendon in Casting Bed]
    PTyes --> Q5[Class 1 or 2 Per IS 1343]
    PTstraight --> Q5
    Q5 --> Q6{Durability Critical?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> C1[Class 1: Zero Tension]
    Q6 -- No --> C2[Class 2: Limited Tension]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define prestressed concrete. How is it different from reinforced cement concrete (RCC)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Prestressed concrete is a structural material in which internal compressive stresses are deliberately induced by tensioned high-tensile steel tendons **before** the application of service loads, so that the tensile stresses from external loads are counter-balanced and the concrete remains predominantly in compression.

**Key Differences (2 marks):**

| Parameter | RCC | PSC |
|---|---|---|
| Pre-existing stress | None | Pre-compression exists |
| Concrete section under load | Tension side cracks | Compression-dominated, no cracks |
| Steel function | Passive (resists tension only when concrete cracks) | Active (pre-stretched, pre-tensioned) |
| Steel strength used | Mild steel (f<sub>y</sub> ≈ 250 N/mm²) | High-tensile steel (f<sub>y</sub> ≥ 1400 N/mm²) |
| Section at service | Cracked, half-section effective | Uncracked, full section effective |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages and three limitations of prestressed concrete.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. Crack-free service behaviour → superior durability and watertightness.
2. High strength-to-weight ratio → longer spans, smaller self-weight, reduced material consumption.
3. Full cross-section is effective in resisting loads (no cracking reduces effective section).

**Three Limitations (1.5 marks):**
1. High initial cost due to special materials, equipment, and skilled labour.
2. Complicated formwork and specialised jacking equipment required.
3. Loss of prestress over time due to shrinkage, creep, and steel relaxation requires careful estimation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1 Internal Choice]**

**A rectangular concrete beam of size 200 mm × 400 mm is simply supported over a span of 8 m and carries an imposed load (excluding self-weight) of 12 kN/m. The beam is prestressed by a straight tendon carrying an effective prestress force of 350 kN, located at an eccentricity of 80 mm below the centroidal axis.**

**Calculate:**
**(a) The stresses at the top and bottom fibres of the beam at midspan under the given loading.** **(7 marks)**
**(b) If the imposed load is increased to 18 kN/m, determine the magnitude of the additional prestress force required (with the same eccentricity) to keep the bottom fibre just free from tensile stress.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1: Section Properties (1 mark)**

$$
A = 200 \times 400 = 80{,}000 \text{ mm}^2
$$

$$
I = \frac{200 \times 400^3}{12} = 1.067 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
$$

$$
Z = I / y = 1.067 \times 10^9 / 200 = 5.333 \times 10^6 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2: Bending Moment at Midspan (1 mark)**

$$
M = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{12 \times 8^2}{8} = 96 \text{ kN·m} = 96 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 3: Stress due to Prestress at Top and Bottom Fibres (2 marks)**

Bottom fibre (compression from prestress):

$$
\sigma_{c,PS,\text{bottom}} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{350 \times 10^3}{80{,}000} + \frac{350 \times 10^3 \times 80}{5.333 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\sigma_{c,PS,\text{bottom}} = 4.375 + 5.250 = 9.625 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

Top fibre (tension from eccentric prestress — common student error zone):

$$
\sigma_{c,PS,\text{top}} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = 4.375 - 5.250 = -0.875 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
$$

**Step 4: Bending Stress at Top and Bottom Fibres (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_{LL,\text{bottom}} = +\frac{M}{Z} = \frac{96 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 18.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{LL,\text{top}} = -18.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

**Step 5: Net Stresses (2 marks)**

Bottom fibre:

$$
\sigma_{\text{net,bottom}} = -9.625 + 18.0 = +8.375 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
$$

Top fibre:

$$
\sigma_{\text{net,top}} = -(-0.875) + (-18.0) = +0.875 - 18.0 = -17.125 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

> **Result:** Bottom fibre tension = **8.375 N/mm²** (unsafe, exceeds Class 1 and Class 2 limits); Top fibre compression = **17.125 N/mm²** (within limit, $\le 0.45 f_{ck} = 18$ N/mm² for M40).

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution [7 marks]**

**Step 1: New Bending Moment (1 mark)**

$$
M_{\text{new}} = \frac{18 \times 8^2}{8} = 144 \text{ kN·m} = 144 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2: New Bending Stress (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_{LL,\text{new}} = \frac{144 \times 10^6}{5.333 \times 10^6} = 27.0 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension at bottom)}
$$

**Step 3: Condition for Zero Tension at Bottom (1 mark)**

$$
\sigma_{\text{net,bottom}} = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{M_{\text{new}}}{Z} = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{A} + \frac{P_{\text{total}} \cdot e}{Z}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for Total Required Prestress (2 marks)**

$$
27.0 = \frac{P_{\text{total}}}{80{,}000} + \frac{P_{\text{total}} \times 80}{5.333 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
27.0 = P_{\text{total}} \left( 1.25 \times 10^{-5} + 1.50 \times 10^{-5} \right) = P_{\text{total}} \times 2.75 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
P_{\text{total}} = \frac{27.0}{2.75 \times 10^{-5}} = 981{,}818 \text{ N} \approx 982 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5: Additional Prestress Required (2 marks)**

$$
\Delta P = P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{original}} = 982 - 350 = 632 \text{ kN}
$$

> **Result:** Additional prestress of **632 kN** must be provided. This represents an 80% increase, indicating that the original design was grossly inadequate for the new loading.

---

### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Choice**

**(a) Explain the three classical concepts of prestressing with neat diagrams, highlighting the design philosophy of each.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Compare pre-tensioning and post-tensioning methods in terms of (i) sequence of operations, (ii) bond mechanism, (iii) loss of prestress, (iv) end anchorages, and (v) typical applications.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Prestressing can be understood through three conceptual lenses — internal force system, steel strengthening, and load balancing. All three are mathematically equivalent; they differ only in the *engineering perspective* a designer adopts.

**Concept 1 — Internal Force System (2 marks):**
The prestressing tendon is replaced by an **equivalent external force system** acting on the concrete:
- An axial compression $P$ applied at the centroid of the tendon
- A moment $M = P \cdot e$ acting about the centroidal axis

This representation is most useful for **force-balance diagrams** and for computing the stress distribution across the section:

$$
\sigma_c = -\frac{P}{A} \mp \frac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I}
$$

**Concept 2 — Strengthening the Steel (2 marks):**
The high-tensile steel is pre-stretched to a stress $f_{pi}$ before the structure is loaded. Both the concrete and steel are forced to deform together under subsequent loads. Since the steel is **already under tension**, it can absorb additional tensile strains before reaching its yield point, allowing the composite section to carry **much higher tensile loads** than a section in which the steel is unstressed (as in RCC).

**Concept 3 — Load Balancing (2 marks):**
The tendon is profiled (parabolic/curved) so that the **transverse force it exerts on the concrete** balances a known portion of the external load. For a parabolic tendon of sag $h$ in a simply-supported beam of span $L$:

$$
w_{ps} = \frac{8 P h}{L^2}
$$

If $w_{ps} = w_{\text{applied}}$, the beam behaves like a member under **pure axial compression** with **zero bending** — an extraordinarily simple design state. This is the most useful concept for **continuous beams and slabs**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]**

| # | Parameter | Pre-tensioning | Post-tensioning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sequence** | Tendons stressed first → concrete cast around them → tendons released after curing | Concrete cast first (with ducts) → cured → tendons threaded and stressed → anchored |
| 2 | **Bond mechanism** | Prestress transferred purely by **bond** between tendon and concrete (no anchorages) | Prestress transferred through **end anchorages**; bond added by grouting ducts |
| 3 | **Loss of prestress** | Higher (immediate elastic shortening + bond slip + relaxation) | Lower (no bond-slip loss; anchorages seat after stressing) |
| 4 | **End anchorages** | **Not required** — relying entirely on bond | **Critical, heavy components** — require bursting-reinforcement zone behind anchorage |
| 5 | **Typical applications** | Railway sleepers, electrical poles, hollow-core slabs, factory-made bridge girders | Bridges, large-span building frames, segmental construction, water tanks, nuclear containments |

**Conclusion (within 7 marks):** Pre-tensioning is economical for **mass production of small-to-medium members** with straight tendons, while post-tensioning is preferred for **large, on-site, or curved-tendon applications** where the additional cost of anchorages is offset by structural flexibility.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–3 to –5 marks):** Students often forget the moment due to eccentricity reverses the stress at the top fibre. The top fibre is in **tension due to prestress alone** when $e$ is below CNA, and this tensile stress must be **subtracted** (not added) to the compression from bending. Always draw a sketch.
> 2. **Confusing the "centroidal axis" with the "kern" (–2 marks):** The eccentricity must lie within the **kern** (a small zone near the centroid) for the entire section to remain in compression. If $e > k$ (kern distance), one fibre goes into tension even at zero external load.
> 3. **Neglecting losses (–3 to –5 marks):** The **effective** prestress $P_e$ (after all losses) is always less than the **initial** prestress $P_0$. For KTU problems, you are given $P_e$ directly, but in design problems, deduct ~15–20% of $P_0$ for losses.
> 4. **Mixing Class 1/2/3 limits (–2 marks):** When asked to keep bottom fibre free of tension, **Class 1** applies, and the answer is *zero tension*. Don't quote the Class 2 limit ($\sigma_{ct} \le 0.25\sqrt{f_{ck}}$) for a Class 1 problem.
> 5. **Skipping unit consistency (–1 mark):** Always keep force in N (or kN), moment in N·mm (or kN·m), section in mm — converting everything to mm-N units before substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (★ Quick Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Cards — Revise before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Prestressing = induced pre-compression in concrete to counter future tensile stresses.
- ✅ **Three Concepts:** Internal force system / Steel strengthening / Load balancing.
- ✅ **Two Main Methods:** Pre-tensioning (bond-based) and Post-tensioning (anchorage-based).
- ✅ **Why PSC needs high-tensile steel:** Mild steel loses prestress to shrinkage and creep of concrete.
- ✅ **Why PSC needs high-strength concrete:** To resist the high compressive stresses at the anchor zones and the bursting-tensile stresses.
- ✅ **Three Classes per IS 1343:** Class 1 (no tension), Class 2 (limited tension), Class 3 (partial prestress).
- ✅ **Key Formula:** $\sigma_{\text{net}} = -P/A \mp P e y/I + M y/I$
- ✅ **Centric vs Eccentric:** Centric → uniform compression; Eccentric → compression + bending.
- ✅ **Top fibre can be in tension due to eccentric prestress** — common KTU trap.
- ✅ **Kern concept:** Eccentricity within kern → no tension anywhere; outside kern → tension develops.
- ✅ **Load-balancing formula:** $w_{ps} = 8 P h / L^2$ (parabolic tendon).
- ✅ **Advantages to remember:** Crack-free, longer spans, full section effective, low maintenance.
- ✅ **Limitations to remember:** High initial cost, skilled labour, prestress loss, brittle failure.
- ✅ **Pre-tensioned:** Used for precast sleepers, hollow-core slabs (mass production).
- ✅ **Post-tensioned:** Used for bridges, large-span beams, on-site casting.
- ✅ **IS Code:** IS 1343 (2012) — Code of Practice for Prestressed Concrete.
- ✅ **Steel grades (typical):** 7-wire strands of 12.7 mm or 15.2 mm diameter, f<sub>pu</sub> = 1860 N/mm².
- ✅ **Concrete grade (typical):** M40 to M80 for PSC.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Prestressing Systems - Anchoring Devices - Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Prestressing Systems - Anchoring Devices - Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Prestressing Systems — The Big Picture

**Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
A *prestressing system* refers to the complete engineered arrangement of high-tensile steel tendons, anchoring devices, jacks, ducts/sheaths, and stressing equipment used to induce and permanently lock-in pre-compressive stresses in a concrete member. The IS 1343 and KTU-recommended classification segregates these into **Pre-tensioning systems** (where tendons are stressed against an external bulkhead *before* concrete casting) and **Post-tensioning systems** (where tendons are stressed *after* concrete hardens, against the member itself using proprietary anchorages).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Exam Highlight:** Module 1 of PECET525 mandates explicit knowledge of *systems* (Hoyer, Freyssinet, Magnel-Blaton, Lee-McCall, Gifford-Udall, BBRV) and their **anchorage hardware**. Marks are frequently lost when students confuse the systems or omit the wire/cable geometry (e.g., 12 × 5 mm, 8 mm wires).

### 1.2 Anchoring Devices — Formal Definition

**Anchoring Devices** are the mechanical hardware assemblies (anchor heads, wedges, bearing plates, cones, grippers, button-heads) that retain the prestressing force in the tendon *after* the jack is released. In **pre-tensioning**, the anchorages are *temporary* (bulkhead grips) because the bond between steel and concrete transfers the prestress. In **post-tensioning**, the anchorages are *permanent and live* — they carry the entire jacking force for the design life of the structure (typically **100 years**).

### 1.3 Materials — Triad of Constituents

The three primary materials are:
1. **High-tensile steel** (ultimate tensile strength **1860 N/mm² to 1960 N/mm²** for strands, **1570 N/mm² to 1860 N/mm²** for wires as per IS 14268 / IS 6006).
2. **High-strength concrete** (minimum M40; commonly **M50–M60** for bridges, **M60+** for long-span PSC).
3. **Non-prestressed (mild steel) reinforcement** for crack control, anchorage zone confinement, and shear.

### 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🥛

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Stack of Books" Model:**
> Imagine you are stacking **10 heavy encyclopedias** on a table. You pre-compress them by squeezing the stack tightly with both hands before letting go. The friction between the books holds them in place — even if you remove your hands, they don't topple.
> 
> - **Your hands** = the **jack + anchorage** (active prestressing force).
> - **The squeezed books** = **concrete in compression** (resists tension cracks).
> - **The friction between books** = the **bond or end-anchorages** that lock the prestress in.
> 
> If you release your hands *after* stacking (pre-tensioning) → books stay via **bond-friction**. If you insert a tight strap *after* stacking and tighten it (post-tensioning) → books stay via the **mechanical anchorage**.

### 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Exams

Module 1 questions test:
- **L1 (Remember):** Naming the system components and wire layouts.
- **L2 (Understand):** Why anchorage zone needs bursting reinforcement.
- **L3 (Apply):** Computing jack pressure, wedge seating loss, anchorage efficiency.
- **L4 (Analyze):** Comparing Hoyer vs. Freyssinet for a given project.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Classification of Prestressing Systems

#### A. Pre-tensioning Systems

| System | Tendon Type | Geometry | Developed By | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hoyer System** (Long-Line / Individual Mould) | Wires / Strands | Straight, deflected, or harped | Hoyer (Germany) | Long stressing bed (≈100 m), wires anchored between two bulkheads, multiple units cast in one line. |
| **Individual Mould System** | Wires / Strands | Straight in each mould | – | Single unit cast; stressing done per mould. |
| **Strand Mould System** | 7-wire strands | Straight / deflected | – | Modern variant; uses larger strands (12.7 mm, 15.2 mm). |
| **Freyssinet Pre-tensioning** | Wires | Deflected at saddles | Freyssinet | Uses saddles/deflectors for parabolic profile. |
| **Magnet-Blaton Pre-tensioning** | Wires | Straight | Magnet (Switzerland) | Sliding rollers for tendon alignment. |
| **Lee-McCall Pre-tensioning** | Bars | Straight | Lee-McCall (UK) | Uses high-tensile bars (typically 25–32 mm). |
| **Baur-Leonhardt Pre-tensioning** | Wires | Deflected at saddles | Germany | Uses stay-blocks to hold deflected tendons. |

#### B. Post-tensioning Systems

| System | Tendon Type | Tendon Layout | Per Cable | Anchorage Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Freyssinet** | 12 No. 5 mm or 8 mm wires | Parallel wires in a circular duct | 12 wires | Concrete cone + Friction wedges |
| **Magnel-Blaton** | Wires (sandwiched plates) | Sandwiched between paired flat plates | 8 wires per plate pair, 2 plates per cable | Sandwich plate with sand grip |
| **Gifford-Udall** | 2/3 wires of 5 mm or 8 mm | Wires looped around a 2-piece anchorage | 2–3 wires per layer | Split cone (U-tendon) |
| **Lee-McCall (Post-tensioning)** | 25–32 mm bar | Single bar | 1 bar | Nut + washer (screw-type) |
| **BBRV (Birkenmaier, Brandestini, Ros, Vogt)** | 36–44 wires of 5/7 mm | Wires with button heads | 36–44 wires | Button-head anchorage + bearing plate |
| **PSC Anchorages (Multi-strand)** | 7-wire strands | Multi-strand in HDPE duct | 4, 7, 12, 19, 27, 37, 55 strands | Wedge plate + bearing trumpet |

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Pre-tensioning Operation

1. **Stage 1 – Stressing the Steel:** High-tensile wires/strands are stretched between two **fixed bulkheads** (abutments) at a force of **70–80%** of the ultimate tensile strength (≈ **0.70 f_pk** to **0.80 f_pk**).
2. **Stage 2 – Casting:** Concrete is placed in the mould around the stressed tendons. Steam curing accelerates strength gain.
3. **Stage 3 – Transfer:** Once concrete reaches **≥ 70% of f_ck** (typically 3–7 days), the tendons are **released (de-tensioned)**. The bond between steel and hardened concrete transfers the prestress to the concrete member.
4. **Stage 4 – Cutting:** Tendons are cut between members in a long-line bed; bond maintains stress in each individual member.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: Post-tensioning Operation

1. **Stage 1 – Duct Placement:** Ducts (HDPE / corrugated metal / grout-duct) are positioned in the formwork with mild-steel reinforcement around anchorage zones.
2. **Stage 2 – Concrete Casting:** Concrete is poured, encasing the ducts (and sometimes the tendons if bonded pre-stressing). Ducts are inflated to prevent collapse.
3. **Stage 3 – Stressing (after hardening):** Tendons are threaded through the ducts, and a hydraulic jack is mounted on the live-end anchorage. The tendon is pulled against the **anchor head** (which bears against the concrete member).
4. **Stage 4 – Lock-off:** Wedges grip the wires/strands; the jack is released. Stress transfers to the concrete via the anchorage.
5. **Stage 5 – Grouting (Bonded PT):** Cementitious grout (w/c ≈ 0.45) is injected to fill the duct, providing bond and corrosion protection.

### 2.4 Materials in Detail

#### 2.4.1 High-Tensile Steel
- **Forms:** Cold-drawn wires, 7-wire strands, high-tensile bars.
- **Stress-relieved wires** have reduced relaxation losses.
- **Low-relaxation (LR) strands** are the modern standard.

| Property | Wire | Strand (7-wire) | Bar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diameter | 5, 6, 7, 8 mm | 9.5, 12.7, 15.2, 17.8 mm | 25, 32 mm |
| f_pk (N/mm²) | 1570–1860 | 1860–1960 | 1030 |
| f_p0.2 (N/mm²) | 0.85 f_pk | 0.88 f_pk | 0.80 f_pk |
| E_p (N/mm²) | 2.0 × 10⁵ | 1.95 × 10⁵ | 2.0 × 10⁵ |
| Relaxation @ 1000h, 0.7 f_pk | ≤ 8% | ≤ 2.5% (LR) | ≤ 4% |

#### 2.4.2 Concrete
- Minimum grade for PSC: **M40** (IS 1343).
- Cube strength at transfer (f_ci): typically **0.70 f_ck** to **0.80 f_ck**.
- E_c (short-term) = **5000√f_ck** N/mm² (IS 456 / IS 1343).

#### 2.4.3 Grout
- Cement grout with **w/c ratio 0.40–0.45**.
- Admixtures: plasticizers, anti-bleed agents, expanding agents.
- 28-day cube strength: **≥ 27 N/mm²**.
- Chloride content: **< 0.05%** of cement weight.

#### 2.4.4 Ducts
- **Corrugated HDPE / metal sheathing** (typical diameter 50–130 mm).
- Coefficient of friction (μ) = **0.20** for strands in HDPE duct; **0.25** for wires.
- Wobble factor (k) = **0.0033 per metre** (rigid metal); **0.0017 per metre** (HDPE).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formula sheet is the examiner's primary checklist for Module 1. Memorize the bolded quantities.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_0 = A_p \cdot f_{p0}$ | Jacking force (initial force in tendon) | N |
| 2 | $P_i = P_0 - \Delta P_{anch} - \Delta P_{fric}$ | Force after anchor slip & friction | N |
| 3 | $P = P_0 \, e^{-(\mu \alpha + kL)}$ | Prestress after friction losses (post-tensioning) | N |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{slip} = \dfrac{E_p \cdot A_p \cdot \delta}{L}$ | Loss due to anchorage slip (δ = 6 mm for Freyssinet cone) | N |
| 5 | $P_j = \eta \cdot P_0$ | Jacking pressure efficiency (η = 0.85–0.95) | – |
| 6 | $f_b = \dfrac{P}{A_p}$ | Stress in tendon after all losses | N/mm² |
| 7 | $f_{cj} = \dfrac{P}{A_c} + \dfrac{P \cdot e \cdot y}{I_c}$ | Stress in concrete at jacking | N/mm² |
| 8 | $A_p$ (Freyssinet, 12 wires) = $12 \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of 12-wire cable | mm² |
| 9 | $A_p$ (7-wire strand) = $\dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot 0.785$ | Strand area (0.785 accounts for 6 outer + 1 inner wire) | mm² |
| 10 | $F_{burst} = 0.3 P \left( 1 - \dfrac{a}{b} \right)$ | Bursting tensile force behind anchorage (Magnel) | N |
| 11 | $\sigma_{spall} = 0.02 P / (a \cdot b)$ | Spalling stress at anchorage (Guyon) | N/mm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pipe-Symbol Rule:** All absolute value bars in the table above use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` operator to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridges & Flyovers:** Freyssinet multi-strand anchorages dominate cable-stayed bridges (e.g., Bandra-Worli Sea Link uses 55-strand anchorages).
- **Nuclear Containments:** BBRV button-head system used in dome construction for its high fatigue resistance.
- **Building Slabs (PT Slabs):** Single-strand unbonded systems (anchorage efficiency ≈ 95%) are used in car parks and office floors.
- **Railway Sleepers:** Hoyer long-line pre-tensioning system produces 500+ sleepers per casting bed in Indian factories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Friction Loss in Curved Post-Tensioned Tendons

**Premise:** A tendon following a curved profile (parabola, circle) experiences friction along the duct wall as it is pulled by the jack.

**Theoretical Starting Point:** Consider an infinitesimal length $\mathrm{d}x$ of the tendon wrapped around a curve. The tendon bends through an angle $\mathrm{d}\alpha$. Two force components act on the elemental contact:
- The differential friction force: $\mathrm{d}F = \mu \cdot N$ where $N$ is the normal reaction.
- The normal reaction equals the curvature-induced centripetal demand: $N = P \cdot \mathrm{d}\alpha$.

**Differential Equation:**

$$\mathrm{d}P = -\mu \cdot P \cdot \mathrm{d}\alpha - k \cdot P \cdot \mathrm{d}x$$

The first term is **curvature friction**; the second is **wobble (intrinsic) friction** because the tendon unintentionally snakes inside the duct.

**Separation of Variables:**

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}P}{P} = -(\mu \, \mathrm{d}\alpha + k \, \mathrm{d}x)$$

**Integration from $P_0$ at the jack to $P_x$ at distance $x$:**

$$\int_{P_0}^{P_x} \frac{\mathrm{d}P}{P} = -(\mu \alpha + k x)$$

**Resulting Exponential Decay Law (the canonical KTU formula):**

$$P_x = P_0 \, e^{-(\mu \alpha + k x)}$$

**Expansion of $e^{-x}$ for small values** (very useful in KTU numericals where $\mu \alpha + kL < 0.3$):

$$P_x \approx P_0 \left[ 1 - (\mu \alpha + k x) \right]$$

**Numerical Verification (KTU-style example):**
Given $\mu = 0.20$, $\alpha = 0.3$ rad, $k = 0.0033$/m, $x = 20$ m, $P_0 = 1500$ kN.

$$\mu \alpha + k x = (0.20)(0.3) + (0.0033)(20) = 0.06 + 0.066 = 0.126$$

$$P_x = 1500 \times e^{-0.126} = 1500 \times 0.8816 = 1322.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Interpretation:** The 1500 kN jacking force drops to **1322.4 kN** at the far end — a **12% friction loss**. This is why long, curved tendons require **two-end stressing**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Anchorage Slip Loss (Wedge-Seating Loss)

**Premise:** When the jack releases the tendon, the wedges retract into the cone by an amount $\delta$ (≈ **6 mm for Freyssinet**, **3 mm for multi-strand**). The released tendon length $\delta$ corresponds to a strain drop in the steel.

**Starting Equation:** Strain change in tendon = slip / free length

$$\varepsilon_{slip} = \frac{\delta}{L}$$

**Stress Drop in Steel:**

$$\Delta f_{p,slip} = E_p \cdot \frac{\delta}{L}$$

**Force Loss in Tendon:**

$$\Delta P_{slip} = E_p \cdot A_p \cdot \frac{\delta}{L}$$

**Numerical Verification:**
Given $E_p = 1.95 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $A_p = 12 \times \pi \times 8^2/4 = 603.2$ mm² (12 wires of 8 mm), $\delta = 6$ mm, $L = 30$ m = 30,000 mm.

$$\Delta P_{slip} = \frac{1.95 \times 10^5 \times 603.2 \times 6}{30{,}000} = 23.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$\Delta f_{p,slip} = \frac{23.5 \times 10^3}{603.2} = 39.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Engineering Insight:** Anchorage slip is a **near-end loss** that affects only a finite length of the tendon (length of influence $\ell_{inf}$). If $\ell_{inf} < L$, the slip loss is **partial**:

$$\ell_{inf} = \sqrt{\frac{E_p \cdot A_p \cdot \delta}{P_0 \cdot k}}$$

(For practical KTU numericals, assume $\ell_{inf} > L$ unless explicitly told otherwise.)

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Jack Pressure and Cable Cross-Sectional Area

**Jack Pressure (Guyon's Formula):**
The hydraulic pressure $p_j$ in the jack cylinder must overcome the tendon force $P_0$ over the piston area $A_{j}$:

$$p_j = \frac{P_0}{A_j} = \frac{P_0}{A_p \cdot f_{p0}}$$

**Freyssinet 12-wire cable (5 mm wires) – standard geometry:**
- Wire area: $A_w = \pi (5)^2/4 = 19.63$ mm²
- Cable area: $A_p = 12 \times 19.63 = 235.6$ mm²
- Initial prestress: $f_{p0} = 0.80 f_{pk} = 0.80 \times 1860 = 1488$ N/mm²
- Jacking force: $P_0 = 235.6 \times 1488 = 350,572$ N ≈ **350.6 kN**
- If jack area $A_j = 50{,}000$ mm² (200 mm diameter piston), required pressure:
$$p_j = 350{,}572 / 50{,}000 = 7.01 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 70 \text{ bar}$$

**Multi-strand 12T13 (12 strands, 12.7 mm) cable — modern bridge design:**
- Strand area: $A_s = 98.7$ mm²
- Cable area: $A_p = 12 \times 98.7 = 1184.4$ mm²
- $P_0 = 1184.4 \times 0.80 \times 1860 = 1{,}762{,}387$ N ≈ **1762 kN**

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Bursting and Spalling Stresses (Magnel & Guyon)

**Bursting Stress behind Anchorage (Magnel's Theory):**
Behind a rectangular bearing plate of dimensions $a \times b$, the concrete is pulled apart in two directions (perpendicular to the tendon axis). The bursting tensile force is:

$$F_{burst} = 0.3 P \left( 1 - \frac{a}{b} \right)$$

The bursting zone extends a distance $\approx 1.5 a$ behind the plate. **Bursting reinforcement** (helical stirrups or U-bars) must resist $F_{burst}$ with stress ≤ **0.87 f_y** (per IS 1343).

**Spalling Stress (Guyon's Formula):**
At the corner of the bearing plate, concrete tends to spall off due to lateral expansion:

$$\sigma_{spall} = 0.02 \cdot \frac{P}{a \cdot b}$$

**Worked Numerical:**
- $P = 1762$ kN, $a = 250$ mm, $b = 250$ mm (square plate).
- $F_{burst} = 0.3 \times 1762 \times (1 - 1) = 0$ kN (square plate, no eccentricity).
- $\sigma_{spall} = 0.02 \times 1{,}762{,}000 / (250 \times 250) = 0.56$ N/mm².
- Required spalling reinforcement: $A_{s,spall} = F_{spall} / (0.87 f_y) = 0.56 \times 62500 / 0.87 \times 500 = ...$ (calculation continues in question bank).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Pre-tensioning vs. Post-tensioning

| Parameter | Pre-tensioning | Post-tensioning |
|---|---|---|
| **When stress is applied** | Before concrete hardens | After concrete hardens (≥ 75% f_ck) |
| **Anchorage type** | Temporary (bulkhead) | Permanent (live) |
| **Bond mechanism** | Direct bond (Hoyer effect) | Mechanical anchorage + (optional) grout bond |
| **Tendon profile** | Mostly straight (limited deflection) | Curved, parabolic, or harped |
| **Concrete grade** | M40+ | M50+ |
| **Maximum span (typical)** | 25 m (bridges, sleepers) | 100+ m (long-span bridges, buildings) |
| **Stressing equipment** | Hydraulic jack against bulkhead | Stressing jack on member |
| **Quality control** | High (factory-controlled) | Moderate (site-controlled) |
| **Cost for small batches** | Economical | Expensive |
| **Losses** | Elastic shortening + relaxation + shrinkage | Friction + anchorage slip + elastic shortening + relaxation + shrinkage + creep |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pre-tensioning Workflow (Hoyer Long-Line System)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Bulkhead Abutment A] -->|Wire Anchored| B[Stress Bed 100m]
    B -->|Stressing Jack at A| C[Steel Tendons stressed to 0.80 fpk]
    C -->|Concrete Cast in Mould| D[Steam Curing fck achieved]
    D -->|De-tension at 70% fck| E[Bond Transfer via Hoyer Effect]
    E -->|Cutting between units| F[Individual PSC Member]
    G[Bulkhead Abutment B] --> B
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Post-tensioning Workflow (Freyssinet System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concrete Casting with HDPE Duct] --> B[Concrete Hardens fck 75 percent]
    B --> C[Thread Tendons 12 wires 5mm]
    C --> D[Mount Jack on Live End Anchor]
    D --> E[Stress to 0.80 fpk]
    E --> F[Lock Wedges Anchor Slip 6mm]
    F --> G[Release Jack]
    G --> H{Grouting Required?}
    H -->|Yes Bonded| I[Inject Cement Grout w/c 0.45]
    H -->|No Unbonded| J[Seal Anchorage with Cap]
    I --> K[Completed PT Member]
    J --> K
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Anchorage Zone Stress Distribution (Magnel)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P0[Jack Force P] --> AP[Bearing Plate a x b]
    AP --> Z1[Bursting Zone 0 to 1.5a]
    AP --> Z2[Local Bearing Zone 0 to a]
    Z1 --> BR[Bursting Reinforcement Helical Stirrups]
    Z2 --> SP[Spalling Reinforcement U-Bars]
    AP --> C[Concrete Member]
    BR --> C
    SP --> C
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Prestressing Material Interaction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Steel
        S1[High Tensile Wires]
        S2[7-Wire Strands]
        S3[High Tensile Bars]
    end
    subgraph Concrete
        C1[M40 M50 M60 PSC Concrete]
    end
    subgraph Anchorage
        A1[Freyssinet Cone]
        A2[BBRV Button Head]
        A3[Multi Strand Wedges]
        A4[Lee McCall Nut]
    end
    subgraph Auxiliaries
        X1[HDPE Duct]
        X2[Cement Grout]
        X3[Bursting Reinforcement]
    end
    S1 --> A1
    S2 --> A3
    S3 --> A4
    S1 --> A2
    A1 --> C1
    A3 --> C1
    A2 --> C1
    A4 --> C1
    X1 --> C1
    X2 --> X1
    X3 --> C1
```

### 4.5 Schematic — Freyssinet Anchorage (Textual)

> [!NOTE]
> **Schematic Interpretation (Top View of Anchorage Cone):**
> 
> ```
>         Anchor Head (Truncated Cone)
>        /                       \
>       / 12 × 5mm wires arranged  \
>      |   in a circular pattern   |
>      |  around a 6mm spacer     |
>       \  (grooves for friction)  /
>        \                         /
>         |--- Bearing Plate 250×250---|
>         |--- Helical Stirrup (6 nos) |
>         |--- Bursting Reinforcement  |
> ```

### 4.6 Schematic — BBRV Button-Head Anchorage

```
        Steel Wires (36-44 nos, 7mm dia)
        |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |  |
        |  Button Heads (cold upset)  |
        v  v  v  v  v  v  v  v  v  v
       _________________________
      |  ANCHOR HEAD (cylindrical)|
      |  bearing on steel shim   |
      |  bearing on concrete     |
       _________________________
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q1a]**

**Q1.** *Differentiate between pre-tensioning and post-tensioning systems of prestressing with **three** distinguishing features.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Timing of Stressing:** In pre-tensioning, the tendons are stressed *before* concrete is cast; in post-tensioning, the tendons are stressed *after* concrete hardens.
- **Anchorage Type:** Pre-tensioning uses *temporary bulkhead anchorages*; post-tensioning uses *permanent mechanical anchorages* (wedges, button heads, nuts).
- **Bond Mechanism:** Pre-tensioning relies on *direct bond* between steel and concrete; post-tensioning relies on *mechanical end-anchorage* (and optionally grout bond in bonded PT).

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Q1b]**

**Q2.** *List the **constituent materials** used in a prestressed concrete member, and state the **minimum grade of concrete** as per IS 1343.*

**Model Answer:**
The three principal materials are:
1. **High-tensile steel** (wires, strands, or bars).
2. **High-strength concrete** (minimum grade **M40** as per IS 1343).
3. **Non-prestressed (mild steel) reinforcement** for anchorage zone, shear, and crack control.
Auxiliary materials: HDPE/metal ducts, cement grout, admixtures.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Hoyer long-line pre-tensioning system** with a neat schematic. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pre-tensioned PSC beam uses **12 wires of 5 mm diameter**, stressed initially to **0.80 f_pk = 1488 N/mm²**. Calculate the **initial prestressing force per cable**, the **stress in concrete at the level of steel** (assuming $A_c = 150 \times 300$ mm², $e = 80$ mm), and the **jacking pressure** for a piston of diameter 200 mm. Take $E_p = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $f_{pk} = 1860$ N/mm², $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ with $f_{ck} = 50$ N/mm². **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Hoyer system** is a long-line pre-tensioning method where high-tensile wires are stretched between two fixed bulkheads (abutments) typically spaced **80–120 m apart**. The wires are anchored at one end and stressed by hydraulic jacks at the other end to **0.70–0.80 f_pk**. Multiple moulds are arranged along the bed; concrete is cast around the stressed wires. After steam-curing to ≥ 70% f_ck, the wires are released, transferring prestress via bond. The individual units are then cut apart.

**Advantages:**
- High productivity (multiple units per casting).
- Superior bond due to Hoyer effect (diameter increase at cut end).
- Factory-quality control.

**Limitations:**
- Heavy initial investment in stressing bed.
- Tendon profile restricted to straight/harped.
- Not suitable for curved members.
- Long bed occupancy time.

**Model Solution (b):**

*Step 1: Wire and Cable Areas*

$$A_w = \frac{\pi (5)^2}{4} = 19.635 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$A_p = 12 \times 19.635 = 235.62 \text{ mm}^2$$

*Step 2: Initial Prestressing Force*

$$P_0 = A_p \cdot f_{p0} = 235.62 \times 1488 = 350{,}602 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{350.6 \text{ kN}}$$

*Step 3: Concrete Section Properties*

$$A_c = 150 \times 300 = 45{,}000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I_c = \frac{150 \times 300^3}{12} = 3.375 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$E_c = 5000 \sqrt{50} = 35{,}355 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

*Step 4: Concrete Stress at Steel Level (e = 80 mm)*

$$f_{c,steel} = \frac{P_0}{A_c} + \frac{P_0 \cdot e^2}{I_c}$$

$$f_{c,steel} = \frac{350{,}602}{45{,}000} + \frac{350{,}602 \times 80^2}{3.375 \times 10^8}$$

$$f_{c,steel} = 7.79 + 6.64 = \mathbf{14.43 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}}$$

*Step 5: Jack Pressure*

$$A_j = \frac{\pi (200)^2}{4} = 31{,}416 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$p_j = \frac{350{,}602}{31{,}416} = 11.16 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx \mathbf{111.6 \text{ bar}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **Freyssinet post-tensioning system** with its anchorage details. Explain the function of the **cone and wedges** and the role of **grouting**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A post-tensioned PSC girder has a tendon of **12 wires of 7 mm diameter**, $f_{pk} = 1860$ N/mm², with profile curvature such that total angle $\alpha = 0.25$ rad over $L = 25$ m. Calculate the **friction loss** and **force at the far end** if the jacking force $P_0 = 1200$ kN. Take $\mu = 0.20$, $k = 0.0033$/m. If anchorage slip = **6 mm**, calculate the **slip loss** in stress and force. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Freyssinet system** uses **12 high-tensile wires of 5 mm or 8 mm diameter** bundled in a circular duct of 40–50 mm internal diameter. The live-end anchorage consists of a **reinforced concrete (or steel) truncated cone** into which **12 friction wedges** grip the wires. The cone is recessed into a bearing plate, distributing the jack force onto the concrete. The dead-end anchorage uses either a similar cone or wires embedded in the concrete.

**Function of the cone and wedges:**
- The cone houses the 12 individual wedges.
- When the jack releases, each wedge retracts ~6 mm and bites the wire, locking the prestress.
- The cone assembly bears on the bearing plate, transmitting force to concrete.

**Role of grouting:**
- Provides bond between tendon and surrounding concrete (bonded PT).
- Corrosion protection for steel.
- Crack control and improved fire resistance.

**Model Solution (b):**

*Step 1: Cable Area*

$$A_p = 12 \times \frac{\pi (7)^2}{4} = 12 \times 38.485 = 461.82 \text{ mm}^2$$

*Step 2: Friction Loss Exponent*

$$\mu \alpha + kL = 0.20 \times 0.25 + 0.0033 \times 25 = 0.05 + 0.0825 = 0.1325$$

*Step 3: Force at Far End*

$$P_x = P_0 \, e^{-0.1325} = 1200 \times 0.8760 = 1051.2 \text{ kN}$$

*Step 4: Friction Loss*

$$\Delta P_{fric} = 1200 - 1051.2 = \mathbf{148.8 \text{ kN}}$$

*Step 5: Slip Loss in Stress*

$$\Delta f_{p,slip} = \frac{E_p \cdot \delta}{L} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5 \times 6}{25{,}000} = 48 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

*Step 6: Slip Loss in Force*

$$\Delta P_{slip} = A_p \cdot \Delta f_{p,slip} = 461.82 \times 48 = 22{,}167 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{22.17 \text{ kN}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deducting Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract 0.5d for bond reduction** in pre-tensioning transfer length numericals.
> 2. **Mixing up** $f_{p0}$ (initial) and $f_{pe}$ (effective) — examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Using the wrong formula** for multi-strand area — for a 7-wire strand, $A_s = 0.785 d^2$ is the correct formula (NOT $\pi d^2/4$).
> 4. **Omitting bursting reinforcement calculation** when designing an anchorage zone — IS 1343 mandates it; loss of 3 marks typical.
> 5. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert metres to mm when using E in N/mm² and $\delta$ in mm.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- **Pre-tensioning** = stress *first*, cast *second*; bond transfers prestress. **Post-tensioning** = cast *first*, stress *second*; mechanical anchorage transfers prestress.
- **Hoyer system** is the *dominant* pre-tensioning method in India (used in railway sleepers, small bridge girders).
- **Freyssinet** (12 wires, 5/8 mm) and **Multi-Strand** (12T13, 19T13, 27T13) are the *dominant* post-tensioning systems in India.
- **BBRV** uses *button heads* (cold-upset ends) — high fatigue resistance, used in nuclear containments and railway bridges.
- **Lee-McCall** uses *high-tensile bars* (25/32 mm) with nuts — suitable for short tendons in building slabs.
- **Magnel-Blaton** uses *sandwich plates* with sand grip — older European system, now rare.
- **Gifford-Udall** uses *loop wires* around a split cone — typical for slab systems and short U-tendons.
- **Freyssinet cone slip = 6 mm;** multi-strand wedge slip = 3–6 mm.
- **Friction coefficient $\mu$:** 0.20 (strands in HDPE), 0.25 (wires in metal duct), 0.30 (lubricated).
- **Wobble factor k:** 0.0033/m (rigid duct), 0.0017/m (HDPE), 0.0050/m (poorly formed duct).
- **Minimum concrete grade for PSC: M40**; at transfer, target **f_ci ≥ 0.70 f_ck**.
- **Relaxation limit @ 1000 h, 0.70 f_pk:** ≤ 2.5% for low-relaxation strands (IS 14268).
- **Anchorage zone reinforcements** (bursting + spalling) are *mandatory* and must be designed per IS 1343 Clause 19.6.
- **Grout water-cement ratio: 0.40–0.45**; use anti-bleed admixtures for vertical tendons.
- **PT slab anchorages** are typically single-strand, recessed, with grease-filled caps (unbonded system).
- For **multi-strand cables** (> 7 strands), always specify a **bulb trumpet** at the live end for gradual stress distribution.
- The **Hoyer effect** (increase in wire diameter at the de-tensioning end) provides the bond for pre-tensioned prestress transfer.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Hack:** When asked to *compare* systems, use a 5-row table: **(1) Tendon type, (2) Anchorage, (3) Typical force per cable, (4) Application, (5) Limitation.** Examiners award full marks only for this structured presentation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties of High Strength Concrete - High Strength Steel - Stress-Strain Curve for High Strength Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties of High Strength Concrete - High Strength Steel - Stress-Strain Curve for High Strength Concrete ## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 High Strength Concrete (HSC) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (IS 1343 & ACI Terminology):** > **High Strength Concrete (HSC)** is a concrete whose characteristic compressive strength ($f_{ck}$) at 28 days is **greater than 50 MPa (M50 grade)**. In modern prestressed concrete practice, HSC ranging from **M60 to M100** is routinely employed because prestressed members demand concrete capable of resisting high compressive stresses at the anchorage zones and in the bottom fibre of simply supported beams. **Composition of HSC (Mandatory ingredients):** - **Low Water-Cement Ratio:** typically **0.25 to 0.35** (vs. 0.45–0.55 for NSC) - **Supplementary Cementitious Materials (SCMs):** Silica Fume (5–10%), Fly Ash (15–30%), Ground Granulated Blast-furnace Slag (GGBS, 30–50%) - **High-Range Water-Reducing Admixtures (HRWR / Superplasticizers):** 0.8–2.0 % by weight of cement - **High-quality crushed aggregates** with well-graded particle distribution - Often **autoclaved or steam-cured** to achieve early strength ### 1.2 High Strength Steel (HSS) — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (IS 1343 / IS 6003 / IS 6006 / ASTM A416):** > **High Strength Steel (HSS)** for prestressed concrete is a heat-treated, cold-drawn alloy steel with **ultimate tensile strength ($f_{pu}$) in the range 1400–2000 MPa** and **yield strength ($f_{py}$) in the range 1100–1700 MPa**, supplied in the form of **wires, strands, or high-tensile bars**. Unlike mild steel, HSS exhibits **no clearly defined yield point** and its stress-strain behaviour is best described by a **continuous curvilinear (parabolic) relationship** up to failure. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "The Gym Body vs. The Average Body"** > - **High Strength Concrete** is to ordinary concrete what an **Olympic athlete's muscle fibre** is to a regular person — denser, stronger, capable of carrying heavier loads but **less forgiving and more brittle** (snap rather than bend). > - **High Strength Steel tendons** are like **extremely stiff, super-elastic rubber bands** that have been cold-stretched in the factory. They want to "snap back" to their original length, and we use this eagerness-to-return to **pre-compress** the concrete before any external load is applied. > - The **stress-strain curve of HSC** is steeper and shorter than that of NSC, similar to how a **stiff spring** (HSC) deflects less under the same load compared to a **softer spring** (NSC). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why HSC + HSS in Prestressed Concrete?** > Prestressed concrete induces **high contact (bearing) stresses at anchorages** and **high sustained compressive stresses** in the concrete. Ordinary NSC would **crush** under such stress concentrations. HSC provides the **compressive reserve** needed, while HSS provides the **tensile reserve** needed to apply the prestressing force economically. ## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Mechanical Properties of High Strength Concrete #### 2.1.1 Compressive Strength - **Cube compressive strength** ($f_{cu}$): typically **60–120 MPa** - **Cylinder compressive strength** ($f_c^{\prime}$): **0.80 × $f_{cu}$** (cylinder/cube ratio) - For HSC above M60, the **cylinder strength is approximately 80–85%** of the cube strength. #### 2.1.2 Modulus of Elasticity ($E_c$) The modulus of elasticity of HSC is **higher** than NSC, but does **not** increase proportionally with $\sqrt{f_{ck}}$. The following code-based formulas are used: | Code | Formula for $E_c$ | Unit Convention | |------|---------------------|-----------------| | **IS 1343 (Draft)** | $E_c = 5500 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | $E_c$ in MPa, $f_{ck}$ in MPa | | **ACI 318-19** | $E_c = 4700 \sqrt{f_{c}^{\prime}}$ | Both in MPa | | **BS 8110 (Part 1)** | $E_c = 20 + 0.2 \cdot f_{cu}$ | $E_c$ in GPa, $f_{cu}$ in MPa | | **Eurocode 2 (EC2)** | $E_{cm} = 22 \cdot \left( \dfrac{f_{cm}}{10} \right)^{0.3}$ | $E_{cm}$ in GPa, $f_{cm}$ in MPa | | **CEB-FIP 1990** | $E_{ci} = 21.5 \cdot 10^3 \left( \dfrac{f_{cm}}{10}\right)^{1/3}$ | $E_{ci}$ in MPa, $f_{cm}$ in MPa | #### 2.1.3 Tensile Strength ($f_{ct}$) - Modulus of rupture $f_{cr} \approx 0.6 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (HSC) - Direct tensile strength $f_{ct} \approx 0.33 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (HSC) - IS 1343: $f_{ct} = 0.24 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (NSC); for HSC **higher value** is adopted. #### 2.1.4 Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) - NSC: $\nu = 0.15$ to 0.20 - HSC: $\nu = 0.18$ to 0.22 (slightly higher) - KTU/IS design assumption: $\nu = 0.20$ #### 2.1.5 Creep and Shrinkage - **Creep coefficient ($\phi$):** NSC: 2.0–3.0; HSC: 1.0–1.6 (significantly lower) - **Shrinkage strain ($\epsilon_{sh}$):** NSC: 300–600 $\mu$; HSC: 150–300 $\mu$ (lower due to dense matrix) - This is a **major advantage** of HSC: reduced long-term prestress losses. #### 2.1.6 Brittleness - **Strain at peak stress** for HSC: $\epsilon_0 \approx 0.0022$ to $0.0028$ - **Strain at peak stress** for NSC: $\epsilon_0 \approx 0.0020$ (similar! But HSC has steeper post-peak branch) - The **descending branch** of HSC is **steeper**, indicating a **more brittle failure mode**. ### 2.2 Mechanical Properties of High Strength Steel | Property | Mild Steel (Fe 415 / 500) | High Strength Steel (HSS) | |----------|---------------------------|----------------------------| | Yield Strength, $f_y$ | **415 / 500 MPa** | **1100–1700 MPa** (0.1% proof stress) | | Ultimate Strength, $f_{pu}$ | **485 / 545 MPa** | **1400–2000 MPa** | | Yield Plateau | **Clearly defined** | **Absent (no yield point)** | | Strain at fracture | **0.20–0.25** | **0.04–0.06** | | Form | Bars | Wires, Strands, Bars | | Behaviour | Ductile | Quasi-brittle | | Density | 7850 kg/m³ | 7850 kg/m³ | #### 2.2.1 Types of HSS Used in Prestressing > [!NOTE] > - **Plain Cold-Drawn Wires** (IS 6003): Diameter 2.5–8 mm; strengths 1400–1800 MPa > - **Indented Wires** (IS 6003): Improved bond characteristics > - **7-Wire Strands** (IS 6006 / ASTM A416): "Standard" grade $f_{pu}$ = 1860 MPa, "Drawn" grade = 1860–1960 MPa. Common diameters: 9.5 mm, 12.7 mm, 15.2 mm, 17.8 mm > - **High Tensile Bars** (IS 2090): Diameter 20–40 mm; used in post-tensioning (Freyssinet, Gifford-Udall systems) #### 2.2.2 Stress-Strain Relationship for HSS (Idealised) For HSS with **no yield point**, IS 1343 / ACI 318 adopt a **0.1% proof stress** convention: $$f_{py} = \text{stress corresponding to a total strain of } \epsilon_{py} = \epsilon_e + 0.001 = \dfrac{f_{py}}{E_s} + 0.001$$ The **elastic modulus of HSS** is taken as: $E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa (= 200 GPa). ### 2.3 Stress-Strain Curve for High Strength Concrete (HSC) The complete stress-strain curve for HSC differs from NSC in three critical aspects: 1. **Initial Slope:** Steeper (higher $E_c$) 2. **Peak Point:** Higher stress at **lower or equal strain** 3. **Descending Branch:** Steeper (more brittle) #### 2.3.1 Popovics-Saenz Model for HSC (Analytical Form) A commonly accepted **analytical expression** for the ascending and descending branches: $$f_c = \dfrac{f_{ck} \cdot k \cdot \eta}{1 + (k-1)\eta^{k/(k-1)}}$$ where: - $\eta = \epsilon_c / \epsilon_0$ — **normalised strain** - $\epsilon_0$ — strain at peak stress (≈ 0.0022 for HSC) - $k$ — curve-fitting parameter related to $E_c / E_0$ ratio > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Takeaway:** For HSC, IS 1343 allows designers to use the **modified Kent-Park model** (Park & Paulay, 1975) which includes a **confinement factor** to account for the brittle post-peak behaviour. The 0.0025 strain at peak is the KTU-board-recommended default for M60 and above. #### 2.3.2 Design Implications of HSC's Stress-Strain Curve | Behavioural Aspect | NSC (≤ M40) | HSC (≥ M60) | Design Consequence | |--------------------|-------------|--------------|---------------------| | Strain at peak ($\epsilon_0$) | 0.0020 | 0.0022–0.0025 | Affects moment-curvature | | Ultimate strain ($\epsilon_{cu}$) | 0.0035 | 0.0030–0.0035 | More ductility demanded | | Modulus $E_c$ | Lower | Higher | Lower initial losses | | Creep coefficient | 2.0–3.0 | 1.0–1.6 | Lower long-term losses | | Failure mode | Ductile | Brittle | Need confinement reinforcement | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Long-span bridges** (e.g., Bandra-Worli Sea Link) use M60–M80 HSC with 15.2 mm seven-wire strands > - **Nuclear containment structures** require M70+ HSC for radiation shielding + strength > - **High-rise floor systems** use HSC for slender columns > - **Offshore prestressed platforms** demand HSC for durability + strength ## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation of the 0.1% Proof Stress Concept for HSS > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why this derivation matters for KTU:** IS 1343 defines the "yield" of HSS as the **0.1% total strain proof stress** because HSS has no flat yield plateau. The examiner frequently tests this concept. **Given:** - HSS stress-strain curve is linear in elastic region: $\sigma = E_s \cdot \epsilon$ - Beyond elastic limit, curve becomes nonlinear - Convention: **0.1% total strain offset** gives "yield stress" **Step 1:** Identify the **total strain** at the proof point: $$\epsilon_{total} = \epsilon_{elastic} + \epsilon_{plastic}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the **0.1% proof-strain offset** convention: $$\epsilon_{offset} = 0.001 \quad (\text{i.e., } 0.1\% \text{ of strain})$$ **Step 3:** Therefore the elastic strain component at the proof stress is: $$\epsilon_{elastic} = \epsilon_{offset} = 0.001$$ **Step 4:** Using Hooke's law for the elastic portion: $$f_{py} = E_s \cdot \epsilon_{elastic} = E_s \cdot 0.001$$ **Step 5:** Substituting $E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa: $$f_{py} = 2.0 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.001 = 200 \text{ MPa (elastic contribution)}$$ **Step 6:** The **total proof stress** is found graphically by drawing a line parallel to the initial elastic slope, starting from $\epsilon = 0.001$ on the strain axis, and finding its intersection with the actual stress-strain curve.
