For typical 7-wire strands: $$f_{py} \approx 0.85 f_{pu} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{py} \approx 0.85 \times 1860 = 1581 \text{ MPa}$$ This is the **characteristic yield stress** used in all KTU calculations. ### 3.2 Derivation of Modulus of Elasticity for HSC > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard:** Use IS 1343 formula: $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (for NSC) and $E_c = 5500\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (for HSC, M60 and above). **Step-by-step calculation for M60 HSC:** **Step 1:** Substitute $f_{ck} = 60$ MPa in the IS 1343 formula for HSC: $$E_c = 5500 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the square root: $$\sqrt{60} = \sqrt{4 \times 15} = 2\sqrt{15} = 2 \times 3.873 = 7.746$$ **Step 3:** Multiply: $$E_c = 5500 \times 7.746 = 42{,}603 \text{ MPa} \approx 42.6 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4:** Compare with ACI 318: $$E_c = 4700\sqrt{60} = 4700 \times 7.746 = 36{,}406 \text{ MPa} \approx 36.4 \text{ GPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > The **IS 1343 estimate is ~17% higher** than ACI 318. KTU expects students to **state the code used** to avoid ambiguity. **Comparative Calculation Matrix (E_c in GPa):** | Grade | $f_{ck}$ (MPa) | IS 1343 ($5500\sqrt{f_{ck}}$) | ACI 318 ($4700\sqrt{f_{ck}}$) | EC2 ($22 \cdot (f_{cm}/10)^{0.3}$) | BS 8110 ($20 + 0.2 f_{cu}$) | |-------|------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------|-----------------------------| | M50 | 50 | **38.89** | **33.23** | **36.3** | **30.0** | | M60 | 60 | **42.60** | **36.41** | **37.4** | **32.0** | | M70 | 70 | **46.01** | **39.33** | **38.4** | **34.0** | | M80 | 80 | **49.19** | **42.05** | **39.3** | **36.0** | | M100 | 100 | **55.00** | **47.00** | **40.9** | **40.0** | ### 3.3 Derivation of Stress in HSS at Service Load **Given:** A 7-wire strand of grade 1860 MPa is stressed to **70% of $f_{pu}$** at transfer (typical for IS 1343): **Step 1:** Calculate the **initial prestress**: $$f_{p,i} = 0.70 \times f_{pu} = 0.70 \times 1860 = 1302 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2:** Apply **immediate losses** — elastic shortening of concrete (5%), draw-in (3%), friction (3%): $$f_{p,after\ immediate} = 1302 \times (1 - 0.05 - 0.03 - 0.03) = 1302 \times 0.89 = 1158.8 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 3:** Apply **time-dependent losses** — relaxation (4%), creep (3%), shrinkage (2%): $$f_{p,final} = 1158.8 \times (1 - 0.04 - 0.03 - 0.02) = 1158.8 \times 0.91 = 1054.5 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4:** **Final effective prestress** as percentage of $f_{pu}$: $$\dfrac{f_{p,final}}{f_{pu}} = \dfrac{1054.5}{1860} \times 100\% = 56.7\%$$ > [!NOTE] > KTU expects an **effective prestress of 50–60% of $f_{pu}$** at service, accounting for all losses. This is a common 14-mark problem. ### 3.4 Strain Energy Comparison: HSC vs NSC vs HSS **Step 1:** Modulus of resilience for HSC (M60, $f_{ck} = 60$ MPa): $$U_{r,HSC} = \dfrac{f_{ck}^2}{2E_c} = \dfrac{60^2}{2 \times 42603} = \dfrac{3600}{85206} = 0.0422 \text{ MPa} = 42.2 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ **Step 2:** Modulus of resilience for NSC (M30, $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa, $E_c = 5000\sqrt{30} = 27386$ MPa): $$U_{r,NSC} = \dfrac{30^2}{2 \times 27386} = \dfrac{900}{54772} = 0.0164 \text{ MPa} = 16.4 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ **Step 3:** Modulus of resilience for HSS (in elastic region, $f_{py} = 1581$ MPa): $$U_{r,HSS} = \dfrac{1581^2}{2 \times 200000} = \dfrac{2{,}499{,}561}{400000} = 6.249 \text{ MPa} = 6249 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > HSS can store **~150× more elastic energy per unit volume** than NSC, and **~148× more** than HSC. This is the **fundamental physics** that makes prestressed concrete possible — the HSS tendons act as **energy reservoirs** that the designer "taps" to compress the concrete. ## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: HSC Behaviour Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD A[HSC Mix Design Inputs] --> B[Low W/C Ratio: 0.25-0.35] A --> C[SCM: Silica Fume + Fly Ash] A --> D[HRWR Superplasticizer] A --> E[Quality Aggregates] B --> F[Fresh Concrete: Workable] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[Hardened HSC Matrix] G --> H[Dense Microstructure] H --> I[High Compressive Strength ≥ 60 MPa] H --> J[High Modulus of Elasticity] H --> K[Low Creep & Shrinkage] H --> L[Brittle Failure Mode] I --> M[Design Application: PSC Members] J --> M K --> M L --> N[Confinement Detailing Required] M --> O[Anchorage Zones, Long-Span Beams] ``` ### 4.2 Flow Diagram: From Raw Materials to Prestressing Force ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[High Strength Steel Billets] --> P2[Hot Rolling] P2 --> P3[Cold Drawing] P3 --> P4[Stabilising Heat Treatment] P4 --> P5[Stress Relieving] P5 --> P6[Final Wires / Strands / Bars] P6 --> P7[Storage at Site] P7 --> P8[Stressing Jack] P8 --> P9[Tendon Elongation] P9 --> P10[Anchorage Lock-off] P10 --> P11[Effective Prestress Force in Member] P11 --> P12[Concrete Pre-Compressed] P12 --> P13[Member Carries External Load] ``` ### 4.3 Comparative Stress-Strain Curves: HSC vs NSC vs HSS ```mermaid graph TD subgraph GRAPH[Stress-Strain Comparison] S1[HSC: M60
Peak 60 MPa at eps 0.0022
Steep post-peak] S2[NSC: M30
Peak 30 MPa at eps 0.0020
Gradual post-peak] S3[HSS: 1860 MPa Strand
Yield 1581 MPa at eps 0.009
No plateau, gradual curve] S4[Mild Steel: Fe 415
Yield 415 MPa at eps 0.002
Long yield plateau] end S1 --> CMP[KTU Comparison] S2 --> CMP S3 --> CMP S4 --> CMP CMP --> R1[1. HSC is steeper than NSC] CMP --> R2[2. HSS has higher strength but no yield point] CMP --> R3[3. HSS strain capacity much lower than mild steel] ``` ### 4.4 Cross-Sectional View: 7-Wire Strand Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph STRAND[Standard 7-Wire Prestressing Strand] direction TB CTR[Central Wire: 1 piece, full diameter d1] OUT[Outer Wires: 6 pieces, diameter d2 around central wire] HEL[Helical Lay: outer wires wrap at 12-16 degree pitch] PITCH[Pitch: typically 12-18 times strand diameter] CTR --- OUT OUT --- HEL HEL --- PITCH end STRAND --> TYP[Common Sizes: 9.5mm, 12.7mm, 15.2mm, 17.8mm] TYP --> APP[Application: PSC beams, slabs, bridges] ``` ### 4.5 Modular Logical Tree: KTU Topic Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD TOP[Module 1: Materials for PSC] --> A1[High Strength Concrete] TOP --> A2[High Strength Steel] A1 --> B1[Mechanical Properties] A1 --> B2[Stress-Strain Curve] A1 --> B3[Code Formulas: IS, ACI, EC2, BS] A2 --> C1[Types: Wires, Strands, Bars] A2 --> C2[Mechanical Properties] A2 --> C3[0.1% Proof Stress Concept] A2 --> C4[Relaxation Behaviour] B1 --> D1[Compressive Strength, Ec, Creep, Shrinkage] B2 --> D2[Popovics, Kent-Park, IS 1343 Curves] B3 --> D3[Comparison Matrix] C2 --> E1[Fu, Fy, Es, Density] C3 --> E2[Derivation] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *Define High Strength Concrete. List the factors influencing its strength development.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** High Strength Concrete is a concrete with characteristic compressive strength $f_{ck} \geq 50$ MPa at 28 days, produced using specialised mix design. - **Factors (2 marks — list 4):** 1. Low water-cement ratio (0.25–0.35) 2. Use of Supplementary Cementitious Materials (Silica Fume, Fly Ash, GGBS) 3. High-Range Water-Reducing Admixtures (Superplasticizers) 4. Quality, well-graded aggregates and proper curing regime 5. Mineralogy and quality of cement --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Explain the "0.1% proof stress" concept used in the design of high strength steel tendons.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - High Strength Steel does not exhibit a well-defined yield point (1 mark). - The 0.1% proof stress is the stress corresponding to a **total strain of 0.1% (0.001)** beyond the elastic limit (1 mark). - Determined by drawing a line parallel to the initial elastic portion of the stress-strain curve, starting from $\epsilon = 0.001$ on the strain axis, and reading the intersection stress (1 mark). --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Common mistake 1:** Using $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for HSC. Always use the **HSC-specific coefficient (5500 per IS 1343 draft) for M60 and above**. > - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to **state the code reference** (IS 1343 / ACI 318 / EC2) when computing $E_c$. > - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing **cube strength $f_{cu}$** and **cylinder strength $f_{c}^{\prime}$**. $f_{c}^{\prime} \approx 0.8 f_{cu}$. > - **Common mistake 4:** In strain-energy / comparison questions, **forgetting to multiply by volume** when asked for total energy. --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice A* **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model Paper]** *(a)* Compare the mechanical properties of High Strength Concrete and Normal Strength Concrete. Discuss the key differences in their stress-strain behaviour with appropriate sketches. **(7 marks)** *(b)* A 7-wire prestressing strand of grade 1860 MPa (cross-sectional area = 140 mm²) is initially stressed to 70% of $f_{pu}$ at transfer. After accounting for all immediate losses (8%) and time-dependent losses (15%), calculate: (i) the initial prestressing force, (ii) the effective prestressing force at service, and (iii) the percentage loss of prestress. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** | Property | NSC (M30) | HSC (M60) | |----------|-----------|-----------| | Compressive strength | 30 MPa | 60 MPa | | Modulus $E_c$ | 27.4 GPa | 42.6 GPa | | Creep coefficient | 2.0–3.0 | 1.0–1.6 | | Shrinkage strain | 300–600 μ | 150–300 μ | | Strain at peak | 0.0020 | 0.0022–0.0025 | | Failure mode | Ductile | Brittle | | Tensile strength | 0.35√fck | 0.33√fck | | Water permeability | Higher | Lower | **Differences in Stress-Strain Behaviour (4 marks for description + 2 marks for sketch justification):** - **Initial slope:** HSC steeper due to higher $E_c$ - **Peak point:** HSC reaches higher stress at similar/lower strain - **Post-peak:** HSC descends more steeply, indicating brittleness - **Linear-elastic range:** HSC maintains linearity up to **80% of peak** (vs. 50% for NSC) **Sketch (sketch description in words — 1 mark):** A two-curve plot with strain (0–0.004) on x-axis and stress (0–70 MPa) on y-axis, showing: - NSC (M30) curve peaking at 30 MPa near strain 0.002, with gentle descent - HSC (M60) curve peaking at 60 MPa near strain 0.0025, with steep descent **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Initial prestress (1 mark): $$f_{p,i} = 0.70 \times 1860 = 1302 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 2:** Initial prestressing force (1 mark): $$P_i = f_{p,i} \times A = 1302 \times 140 = 182{,}280 \text{ N} = 182.28 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3:** Effective prestress after total losses of 8% + 15% = 23% (2 marks): $$f_{p,eff} = 1302 \times (1 - 0.23) = 1302 \times 0.77 = 1002.54 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 4:** Effective prestressing force (1 mark): $$P_{eff} = 1002.54 \times 140 = 140{,}355.6 \text{ N} = 140.36 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 5:** Percentage loss (2 marks): $$\text{Total Loss} = 23\% \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{182.28 - 140.36}{182.28} \times 100 = 23\%$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice B* **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model Paper]** *(a)* With neat sketches, describe the stress-strain curve of high strength concrete. Explain the salient features and the role of confinement in enhancing ductility. **(7 marks)** *(b)* A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam uses M70 concrete and 12 numbers of 12.7 mm diameter, 7-wire strands ($f_{pu}$ = 1860 MPa). Each strand area = 100 mm². If the design is based on IS 1343 recommendations and the strands are stressed to 0.75 $f_{pu}$ at transfer, determine: (i) the modulus of elasticity of concrete, (ii) the initial prestress in the strands, and (iii) the elastic shortening loss using $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa and modular ratio $m = E_s / E_c$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** **Stress-strain curve description (4 marks):** - The HSC stress-strain curve consists of three regions: **ascending (linear up to ~80% peak)**, **peak plateau (brief, 0.0022–0.0025 strain)**, and **descending branch (steep, brittle)**. - The curve is plotted with strain $\epsilon_c$ on x-axis (range 0 to 0.004) and stress $f_c$ on y-axis (range 0 to 1.1 $f_{ck}$). - Key points to label: - **Point A** — Limit of proportionality (~0.6 $f_{ck}$) - **Point B** — Peak stress ($f_{ck}$ at strain $\epsilon_0 \approx 0.0022$) - **Point C** — Failure strain ($\epsilon_{cu} \approx 0.0035$) - The post-peak branch is **steeper** for HSC than NSC. **Confinement and Ductility (3 marks):** - HSC is **brittle** in unconfined state. - **Confinement** (by spirals, hoops, ties) provides lateral pressure, increasing **peak strain $\epsilon_0$** to 0.004–0.005+ and **ultimate strain $\epsilon_{cu}$** to 0.01+. - Confined HSC exhibits **energy absorption capacity** 3–5× higher than unconfined. - Design codes (IS 1343, ACI 318) require **confinement in anchorage zones** and at member ends. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1:** Modulus of elasticity for M70 HSC (1 mark): $$E_c = 5500 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 5500 \times \sqrt{70}$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate $\sqrt{70}$ (1 mark): $$\sqrt{70} = \sqrt{49 \times \tfrac{10}{7}} \approx 7 \times 1.195 = 8.367$$ **Step 3:** Compute $E_c$ (1 mark): $$E_c = 5500 \times 8.367 = 46{,}018.5 \text{ MPa} \approx 46.02 \text{ GPa}$$ **Step 4:** Modular ratio (1 mark): $$m = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^5}{46018.5} = 4.346$$ **Step 5:** Initial prestress (1 mark): $$f_{p,i} = 0.75 \times 1860 = 1395 \text{ MPa}$$ **Step 6:** Initial prestress force in all 12 strands (1 mark): $$P_i = 1395 \times (12 \times 100) = 1395 \times 1200 = 1{,}674{,}000 \text{ N} = 1674 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 7:** Elastic shortening loss (1 mark): $$f_{p,es} = m \cdot f_{c,avg} = m \cdot \dfrac{P_i}{A_c} \text{ (approximate, assuming } A_c \text{ given or assumed)}$$ Assuming $A_c = 200{,}000$ mm² (a typical beam section): $$f_{c,avg} = \dfrac{1{,}674{,}000}{200{,}000} = 8.37 \text{ MPa}$$ $$f_{p,es} = 4.346 \times 8.37 = 36.38 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!NOTE] > If $A_c$ is not given, the answer is left in symbolic form: $f_{p,es} = m \cdot P_i / A_c$ — KTU will provide the value in the question stem. --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1: HSC & HSS** - ✅ **HSC definition:** $f_{ck} \geq 50$ MPa; designed using low W/C (0.25–0.35) + SCMs + HRWR. - ✅ **HSS grades:** Wires (1400–1800 MPa), Strands (1860 MPa std.), Bars (1100–1700 MPa). - ✅ **HSS has NO yield point** — use **0.1% proof stress** convention per IS 1343. - ✅ **$E_s$ for HSS = $2.0 \times 10^5$ MPa** universally (200 GPa). - ✅ **$E_c$ formulas differ by code** — always **state the code**. IS 1343 (HSC): $E_c = 5500\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ MPa. - ✅ **Cylinder strength ≈ 0.80 × Cube strength** for HSC. - ✅ **HSC = more brittle** than NSC — confinement reinforcement is mandatory in PSC anchorage zones. - ✅ **HSC has LOWER creep & shrinkage** → lower time-dependent prestress losses (~30–50% less than NSC). - ✅ **Strain at peak ($\epsilon_0$)** for HSC = **0.0022 to 0.0025** (vs. 0.0020 for NSC). - ✅ **Tensile strength of HSC** ≈ $0.33\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ to $0.6\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (modulus of rupture). - ✅ **Poisson's ratio** for HSC design = **0.20** (IS standard). - ✅ **Energy storage** in HSS tendons is **~150× that of NSC** — the fundamental basis of prestressing. - ✅ **KTU marks killers:** (i) Using NSC formula for HSC; (ii) Confusing $f_{cu}$ and $f_c^{\prime}$; (iii) Forgetting code reference; (iv) Missing the 0.1% proof-stress explanation for HSS. > [!IMPORTANT] > **MNEMONIC — "HSC-LHBBB":** > - **L**ow W/C ratio > - **H**RWR (superplasticizers) > - **B**rittle failure mode > - **B**etter durability + lower creep/shrinkage > - **B**earing capacity at anchorages (high)

topics.title: Losses in Prestress: Loss of Prestress due to Elastic Shortening
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Losses in Prestress: Loss of Prestress due to Elastic Shortening

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Loss of Prestress due to Elastic Shortening** is defined as the reduction in the initial prestressing force caused by the instantaneous elastic deformation (shortening) of the concrete member when the prestress is transferred from the steel tendons to the concrete section.

As per the **IS 1343:2012 (Code of Practice for Prestressed Concrete)** and standard prestressed concrete design textbooks, this is classified as an **immediate (short-term) loss** because it occurs at the moment of prestress transfer, rather than over a long period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Elastic shortening is the loss of prestress that occurs due to the elastic contraction of concrete at the time of application of prestress. It happens because concrete, being a visco-elastic material, deforms instantly under load, and this deformation reduces the strain in the bonded steel tendon.

Mathematically, the loss in steel stress is expressed as:

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c$$

Where:
- $\Delta f_{p,el}$ = Loss of prestress due to elastic shortening (in N/mm²)
- $m = E_s / E_c$ = **Modular Ratio** (dimensionless)
- $f_c$ = Compressive stress in concrete at the level of the centroid of prestressing steel (in N/mm²)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are pulling a **rubber band** stretched between two pillars, and then someone gently pushes one of the pillars a tiny bit closer to the other. The rubber band, which was tightly stretched, would become slightly looser because its anchor point moved.

**How this applies to Prestressed Concrete:**

In a pretensioned beam, the high-tensile steel wires are stretched between external anchors and then concrete is cast around them. Once the concrete hardens, the wires are cut (released) from the external anchors. The wires try to contract (because they were under tension), and in doing so, they **compress the concrete**.

However, the moment the force is transferred, the concrete member itself **shortens slightly** (because it is now being compressed). This shortening of the concrete allows the steel to relax just a tiny bit — and this relaxation of steel is what we call the **Loss due to Elastic Shortening**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Memory Trick for Students:** "Concrete shrinks, steel relaxes." Whenever the concrete gets shorter, the steel tendon gets less stretched, so it loses some prestress. Think of it as a **give-and-take** between two elastic partners connected together.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KTU Examination Standard Values)

The following standard values must be memorized for KTU board examinations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value (KTU/IS 1343) |
|---|---|---|
| Modulus of Elasticity of Steel | $E_s$ | **200 GPa = 200,000 N/mm² = 2 × 10⁵ MPa** |
| Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete | $E_c$ | $5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm² |
| Modular Ratio | $m$ | $E_s / E_c$ |
| Characteristic Strength (typical) | $f_{ck}$ | **40 N/mm²** (commonly used for PSC), 50, 60 N/mm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Numerical Problems:** Whenever a problem does not specify the value of $E_s$, always use **$E_s = 200 \text{ GPa}$**. Also, when asked to calculate $E_c$ using IS 1343, use **$E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$**.

---

### 1.4 Why Elastic Shortening Occurs — The Physical Mechanism

The phenomenon can be broken down into three sequential events:

1. **Pre-transfer Stage:** The tendon is held under tension by external jacks/anchors. The concrete is being cast and cured around the tendon (pretensioning) or ducts are already in place (post-tensioning).

2. **Transfer Stage:** The prestress is transferred from the jacks to the concrete. At this instant, concrete begins to experience compressive force $P$.

3. **Post-transfer Deformation:** Concrete, being elastic, undergoes an instantaneous strain $\varepsilon_c = f_c / E_c$. Because the steel is bonded to (or anchored against) the concrete, the steel must also strain the same amount $\varepsilon_c$. A corresponding stress reduction $f_s = \varepsilon_c \cdot E_s$ occurs in the steel — this is the **elastic shortening loss**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** Elastic shortening is an **irrecoverable immediate loss**. Once it occurs, the tendon will never regain that lost stress under normal service conditions. It is deducted directly from the initial prestressing force.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Classification — Pretensioning vs. Post-Tensioning

The magnitude and calculation of elastic shortening loss **differs fundamentally** between pretensioned and post-tensioned members. KTU examiners **frequently test this distinction**, so it is critical to understand the difference.

#### A. Pretensioned Members (Sequential Release Concept)

In pretensioning, the strands are tensioned first, concrete is cast, and then strands are **released** (cut) one by one or in groups. The moment of release is when elastic shortening occurs.

**Assumption used in KTU problems:** *All strands are released simultaneously* (i.e., the entire prestress force $P_i$ is transferred at once).

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c}$$

Where $f_c$ is the stress in concrete at the centroid of the prestressing steel due to the **full initial prestress** $P_i$.

#### B. Post-Tensioned Members (Sequential Stressing Concept)

In post-tensioning, each tendon is stressed one after another using a jack, against the already-hardened concrete. This creates a unique sequence effect:

- When the **first tendon** is stressed, the full elastic shortening of concrete has not yet occurred. As more tendons are stressed, the concrete progressively shortens. Hence, the first tendon experiences **maximum loss**.
- When the **last tendon** is stressed, no further elastic shortening will occur (because no more tendons remain to be stressed). Hence, the last tendon experiences **zero loss**.
- All tendons in between experience a **proportionate loss** depending on their position in the sequence.

**Standard KTU Approximation (Average Loss Method):**

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{p,el} = \frac{m \cdot f_c}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot f_c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 1343 Recommendation for KTU:** The average loss of prestress due to elastic shortening in post-tensioned work, where successive tendons are stressed, may be assumed as **half the value calculated for pretensioned work**. This is the most-tested formula in KTU Module 1.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c$ | Full elastic shortening loss | Pretensioned members (all strands released together) |
| 2 | $\Delta f_{p,el} = \dfrac{m \cdot f_c}{2}$ | Half (average) loss | Post-tensioned members (successive tendons) |
| 3 | $m = \dfrac{E_s}{E_c}$ | Modular ratio | All elastic shortening problems |
| 4 | $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm² | Concrete modulus (IS 1343) | When $E_c$ is not given directly |
| 5 | $f_c = \dfrac{P_i}{A} + \dfrac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I} - \dfrac{M \cdot e}{I}$ | Concrete stress at steel level | Combined stress at centroid of steel |
| 6 | $\Delta P_{el} = A_{st} \cdot \Delta f_{p,el}$ | Loss of force | Force form of loss |
| 7 | $P_{net} = P_i - \Delta P_{el}$ | Effective prestress | After elastic shortening loss |

Where:
- $P_i$ = Initial prestressing force
- $A$ = Gross cross-sectional area of concrete member
- $e$ = Eccentricity of prestressing tendon at the section
- $I$ = Moment of inertia of gross concrete section
- $M$ = Bending moment due to self-weight and any other loads acting at the time of transfer
- $A_{st}$ = Total cross-sectional area of prestressing steel

---

### 2.3 Decomposing the Concrete Stress $f_c$

The stress in concrete at the level of the prestressing steel is calculated using the **principle of superposition**. The concrete experiences:

1. **Direct compressive stress** due to axial prestress force: $\sigma_1 = P_i / A$
2. **Bending stress** due to eccentricity of prestress force (compressive at bottom fibre when tendon is below the neutral axis): $\sigma_2 = P_i \cdot e^2 / I$
3. **Bending stress** due to self-weight and external loads (often tensile at the level of steel if tendon is near the bottom): $\sigma_3 = M \cdot e / I$

The net concrete stress at the level of steel is:

$$\boxed{f_c = \frac{P_i}{A} + \frac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I} - \frac{M \cdot e}{I}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention for KTU:** Compressive stress is **positive (+)** and tensile stress is **negative (-)**. Since prestressing causes compression and self-weight moment may cause tension at the bottom (where the tendon usually is), the bending stress due to $M$ is typically subtracted.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This concept is critical in the design of:

- **Pretensioned railway sleepers** (where all wires are released together → full loss must be accounted for).
- **Post-tensioned bridge girders** (where tendons are stressed one by one → average half-loss is used).
- **Segmental bridge construction** (where staged stressing demands precise elastic shortening calculations for camber control).
- **Nuclear containment structures** (where accurate loss estimation ensures structural integrity against high pressure loads).

In the construction industry, under-estimating elastic shortening loss leads to **insufficient prestress** at service stage — causing excessive deflection and cracking. Over-estimating it leads to **over-prestressing** at transfer — causing tensile cracking at the top fibre and bursting stresses at anchorage zones.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Complete Derivation of Elastic Shortening Loss

#### Step 1: Define the Compatibility Condition

When prestress is transferred to concrete, the steel tendon and concrete undergo **compatible strain** at the interface (they are bonded together or anchored against each other). This means:

$$\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_c$$

Where:
- $\varepsilon_s$ = Strain in steel due to elastic shortening
- $\varepsilon_c$ = Strain in concrete at the level of steel

#### Step 2: Express Steel Strain in Terms of Stress Loss

The loss of stress in steel ($\Delta f_{p,el}$) and the corresponding strain are related by Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{\Delta f_{p,el}}{E_s}$$

#### Step 3: Express Concrete Strain in Terms of Concrete Stress

By Hooke's Law for concrete at the level of steel:

$$\varepsilon_c = \frac{f_c}{E_c}$$

Where $f_c$ is the compressive stress in concrete at the centroid of prestressing steel.

#### Step 4: Apply the Compatibility Equation

Setting the strains equal:

$$\frac{\Delta f_{p,el}}{E_s} = \frac{f_c}{E_c}$$

#### Step 5: Solve for the Loss

Rearranging:

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = \frac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot f_c = m \cdot f_c$$

This is the **fundamental formula** for elastic shortening loss in pretensioned members.

#### Step 6: Modify for Post-tensioned Members (Sequential Stressing)

In post-tensioning, when the first tendon is jacked, concrete has not yet shortened. As each subsequent tendon is stressed, concrete progressively shortens. The first tendon experiences the **full** loss $m \cdot f_c$, while the last tendon experiences **zero** loss.

**Average Loss across all tendons:**

$$\Delta f_{p,el,avg} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{n - i}{n-1}\right) \cdot m \cdot f_c$$

For continuous averaging as $n \to \infty$ (or standard IS 1343 assumption):

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{p,el,avg} = \frac{m \cdot f_c}{2}}$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Standard)

**Problem Statement:**

A pretensioned concrete beam, 300 mm wide and 500 mm deep, is prestressed by 12 high-tensile wires of 7 mm diameter, initially stressed to 1200 N/mm². The wires are straight and located at an eccentricity of 100 mm. Take $E_s = 210$ kN/mm² and $E_c = 35$ kN/mm². Find the loss of prestress due to elastic shortening. Ignore the effect of self-weight.

---

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

#### Step 1: Calculate the Area of Prestressing Steel

$$A_{st} = 12 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times (7)^2 = 12 \times 38.48 = 461.81 \text{ mm}^2$$

#### Step 2: Calculate the Initial Prestressing Force

$$P_i = A_{st} \times f_{si} = 461.81 \times 1200 = 554,172 \text{ N} \approx 554.17 \text{ kN}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate the Gross Concrete Section Properties

Cross-sectional area:

$$A = 300 \times 500 = 150,000 \text{ mm}^2$$

Moment of inertia (rectangular section):

$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12} = \frac{300 \times 500^3}{12} = 3.125 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

#### Step 4: Calculate Modular Ratio

$$m = \frac{E_s}{E_c} = \frac{210}{35} = 6$$

#### Step 5: Calculate Concrete Stress at the Level of Steel

Since the problem says "ignore the effect of self-weight," $M = 0$. The concrete stress at the level of steel is purely due to prestress:

$$f_c = \frac{P_i}{A} + \frac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I}$$

Substituting values:

$$f_c = \frac{554,172}{150,000} + \frac{554,172 \times (100)^2}{3.125 \times 10^9}$$

$$f_c = 3.694 + \frac{5.5417 \times 10^9}{3.125 \times 10^9}$$

$$f_c = 3.694 + 1.773 = 5.467 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

#### Step 6: Calculate Elastic Shortening Loss

Since this is a **pretensioned** member:

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c = 6 \times 5.467 = 32.80 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

#### Step 7: Calculate Loss in Force

$$\Delta P_{el} = A_{st} \times \Delta f_{p,el} = 461.81 \times 32.80 = 15,148 \text{ N} \approx 15.15 \text{ kN}$$

#### Step 8: Express Results

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Loss in stress | $\Delta f_{p,el} = 32.80 \text{ N/mm}^2$ |
| Loss in force | $\Delta P_{el} = 15.15 \text{ kN}$ |
| Percentage loss | $(32.80/1200) \times 100 = 2.73\%$ |

---

### 3.3 Variation Example — Post-Tensioned Member

**Same Problem, but now the member is POST-TENSIONED with tendons stressed one by one.**

**Solution:**

All values remain the same as above, except the loss formula changes.

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = \frac{m \cdot f_c}{2} = \frac{32.80}{2} = 16.40 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\Delta P_{el} = 461.81 \times 16.40 = 7,574 \text{ N} \approx 7.57 \text{ kN}$$

**Percentage loss:** $(16.40/1200) \times 100 = 1.37\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The loss in a post-tensioned member is exactly **half** that in a pretensioned member (when sequential stressing is performed). This is a 2-mark conceptual question guaranteed to appear in KTU Module 1.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Self-Weight

If self-weight is **included**, the bending moment $M$ at transfer section becomes:

$$M = \frac{w \cdot L^2}{8} \quad \text{(for simply supported beam)}$$

Where $w$ = self-weight per unit length = $\rho_c \cdot A$, with $\rho_c = 25 \text{ kN/m}^3$ (density of reinforced concrete).

The stress at the level of steel becomes:

$$f_c = \frac{P_i}{A} + \frac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I} - \frac{M \cdot e}{I}$$

The minus sign arises because self-weight creates tension at the bottom fibre (where the tendon usually is), which **reduces** the compressive stress at the steel level, thereby **reducing** the elastic shortening loss.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Including self-weight makes the member **less compressed** at the level of steel, which means the concrete shortens less, and hence the loss of prestress is smaller. Designers use this to their advantage — pre-cambering the beam and accounting for self-weight relaxation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Sequence of Events During Elastic Shortening

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Member at Rest: Zero strain in concrete and steel"] --> B["Tendon Tensioned: External jacks apply tensile force to steel"]
    B --> C["Concrete Cast and Cured: Concrete hardens around the tendon"]
    C --> D["Prestress Transfer Event: Steel force is released from jacks"]
    D --> E["Concrete Receives Compressive Force P from Tendon"]
    E --> F["Concrete Undergoes Instantaneous Elastic Strain ec = fc / Ec"]
    F --> G["Strain Compatibility: Steel must also strain by ec"]
    G --> H["Stress Reduction in Steel: Dfs = ec x Es = m x fc"]
    H --> I["Elastic Shortening Loss Registered: Dfp,el = m x fc"]
    I --> J["Effective Prestress at Transfer: Pnet = Pi - A_st x Dfp,el"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Pretensioning vs. Post-Tensioning Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PRE["PRETENSIONING METHOD"]
        P1["Step 1: Tension wires against external bulkhead"]
        P2["Step 2: Cast concrete around wires in mould"]
        P3["Step 3: Cure concrete to achieve transfer strength"]
        P4["Step 4: Release wires simultaneously"]
        P5["Step 5: Elastic shortening = m x fc FULL loss"]
        P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5
    end

    subgraph POST["POST-TENSIONING METHOD"]
        Q1["Step 1: Cast concrete with empty ducts"]
        Q2["Step 2: Cure concrete to required strength"]
        Q3["Step 3: Insert tendon into duct"]
        Q4["Step 4: Stress tendon 1 first, then 2, then 3..."]
        Q5["Step 5: First tendon loses FULL m x fc"]
        Q6["Step 6: Last tendon loses ZERO"]
        Q7["Step 7: Average loss = m x fc / 2"]
        Q1 --> Q2 --> Q3 --> Q4 --> Q5
        Q4 --> Q6 --> Q7
    end
```

### 4.3 Stress Distribution Diagram — Concrete Section at Steel Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Concrete Cross Section"] --> B["Neutral Axis at Centroid"]
    B --> C["Top Fibre: Compression + Pi/A - Pi.e.y_top/I"]
    B --> D["Bottom Fibre: Compression + Pi/A + Pi.e.y_bot/I"]
    C --> E["Tendon Location at Eccentricity e Below NA"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["Stress at Tendon Level fc"]
    F --> G["fc = Pi/A + Pi.e^2/I - M.e/I"]
    G --> H["Loss Dfp,el = m x fc for Pretensioning"]
    G --> I["Loss Dfp,el = m x fc / 2 for Post Tensioning"]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Bar Chart Concept — Loss Magnitudes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pretensioned Member: Full Loss m.fc"] --> B["High Loss: ~3 percent of initial prestress"]
    C["Post Tensioned Member: Half Loss m.fc / 2"] --> D["Moderate Loss: ~1.5 percent of initial prestress"]
    E["Composite Member: Variable Loss"] --> F["Depends on stage of construction"]
```

### 4.5 Block Architecture — Loss Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Begin: Given Pi, A, I, e, Es, Ec, M"] --> CalcMod["Compute Modular Ratio m = Es/Ec"]
    CalcMod --> CalcFc["Compute Concrete Stress fc at Steel Level"]
    CalcFc --> CheckType{"Member Type?"}
    CheckType -- Pretensioned --> Formula1["Use Dfp,el = m x fc"]
    CheckType -- Post Tensioned --> Formula2["Use Dfp,el = m x fc / 2"]
    Formula1 --> LossForce["Convert to Force: DP,el = A_st x Dfp,el"]
    Formula2 --> LossForce
    LossForce --> NetForce["Effective Prestress Pnet = Pi - DP,el"]
    NetForce --> End["Result: Elastic Shortening Loss Computed"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All Mermaid diagrams represent the **sequence of events**, **comparative workflows**, and **analytical steps** involved in elastic shortening loss. Physical strain diagrams (e.g., stress block across depth) are typically rendered using hand sketches in KTU examinations, but the block-level architecture shown captures all key transitions.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Conceptual Short-Answer**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Similar]**

**Q: What is meant by loss of prestress due to elastic shortening of concrete? Explain briefly why it is greater in pretensioned members compared to post-tensioned members.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Loss of prestress due to elastic shortening refers to the reduction in the initial prestressing force caused by the instantaneous elastic deformation (shortening) of concrete at the time of prestress transfer.

**(1 mark for definition)**

In **pretensioned members**, all strands are released simultaneously, so the entire prestress force $P_i$ is transferred at once. This causes the concrete to undergo the full elastic strain $\varepsilon_c = f_c / E_c$, and correspondingly, the steel loses the full stress $m \cdot f_c$. **(1 mark for pretensioning explanation)**

In **post-tensioned members**, tendons are stressed one after another. The first tendon experiences the full loss (as concrete is yet unstressed), but each subsequent tendon experiences progressively less loss because the concrete has already partially shortened. The last tendon experiences no loss. Hence, the average loss is approximately $m \cdot f_c / 2$, which is half that of pretensioning. **(1 mark for post-tensioning explanation)**

---

#### **Question 2: Formula-Based Question**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Similar]**

**Q: State the formula for the loss of prestress due to elastic shortening in (i) pretensioned members and (ii) post-tensioned members where tendons are stressed successively.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(i) Pretensioned Members:**

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c = \frac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot f_c$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**(ii) Post-Tensioned Members (Successive Stressing):**

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot f_c = \frac{E_s}{2 E_c} \cdot f_c$$

**(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Similar Pattern]**

**Q (a): Derive the expression for the loss of prestress due to elastic shortening of concrete in a pretensioned member. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Define the compatibility condition (1 mark)**

At the instant of prestress transfer, the strain in steel must equal the strain in concrete at the level of steel (assuming perfect bond):

$$\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_c$$

**Step 2: Express strains in terms of stresses (2 marks)**

For steel:

$$\varepsilon_s = \frac{\Delta f_{p,el}}{E_s}$$

For concrete at the level of steel:

$$\varepsilon_c = \frac{f_c}{E_c}$$

**Step 3: Equate and solve (2 marks)**

$$\frac{\Delta f_{p,el}}{E_s} = \frac{f_c}{E_c}$$

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = \frac{E_s}{E_c} \cdot f_c = m \cdot f_c$$

**Step 4: Express $f_c$ (1 mark)**

$$f_c = \frac{P_i}{A} + \frac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I} - \frac{M \cdot e}{I}$$

**Step 5: State assumptions (1 mark)**

1. Concrete and steel are perfectly bonded (no slip).
2. All strands are released simultaneously in pretensioning.
3. Plane sections remain plane after deformation.
4. The material is linearly elastic (Hooke's Law applies).

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c}$$

---

**Q (b): A pretensioned concrete beam has a cross-section of 250 mm × 400 mm. It is prestressed by 10 wires of 8 mm diameter, initially stressed to 1100 N/mm². The wires are located at an eccentricity of 80 mm. Take $E_s = 200$ kN/mm² and $E_c = 32$ kN/mm². Calculate the percentage loss of prestress due to elastic shortening. Neglect self-weight. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Area of steel (1 mark)**

$$A_{st} = 10 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 10 \times 50.27 = 502.7 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Initial prestressing force (1 mark)**

$$P_i = 502.7 \times 1100 = 552,970 \text{ N} \approx 553 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3: Section properties (1 mark)**

$$A = 250 \times 400 = 100,000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I = \frac{250 \times 400^3}{12} = 1.333 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4: Modular ratio (0.5 marks)**

$$m = \frac{200}{32} = 6.25$$

**Step 5: Concrete stress at steel level (2 marks)**

$$f_c = \frac{P_i}{A} + \frac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I} = \frac{553,000}{100,000} + \frac{553,000 \times 80^2}{1.333 \times 10^9}$$

$$f_c = 5.53 + \frac{3.539 \times 10^9}{1.333 \times 10^9} = 5.53 + 2.654 = 8.184 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 6: Loss of prestress (1 mark)**

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c = 6.25 \times 8.184 = 51.15 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 7: Percentage loss (0.5 marks)**

$$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{51.15}{1100} \times 100 = 4.65\%$$

**Final Answer:** **Percentage loss of prestress = 4.65%**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Similar Pattern]**

**Q (a): Compare the loss of prestress due to elastic shortening in pretensioned and post-tensioned members. Justify with the IS 1343 recommendation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Pretensioned | Post-Tensioned |
|---|---|---|
| Stressing Sequence | All strands stressed before casting | Tendons stressed after concrete hardens |
| Release/Transfer | All wires released simultaneously | Tendons jacked one by one |
| Loss per Tendon | Every tendon loses the same: $m \cdot f_c$ | First tendon loses $m \cdot f_c$, last loses 0, others in between |
| Average Loss | $m \cdot f_c$ (full) | $m \cdot f_c / 2$ (half) |
| IS 1343 Clause | Direct calculation required | Recommended average = half of pretensioning value |

**Justification (3 marks):**

In pretensioning, since all strands are released simultaneously, the concrete member undergoes the full elastic shortening $\Delta = P_i \cdot L / (A \cdot E_c)$. This shortening is communicated to all strands equally, hence all strands lose the same prestress $m \cdot f_c$.

In post-tensioning, when the first tendon is jacked, the concrete has not yet shortened. Subsequent tendons cause progressive shortening. The first tendon anchored experiences the full shortening caused by all later tendons, while the last tendon anchored experiences no further shortening. Hence, the average loss across all tendons is approximately $m \cdot f_c / 2$.

**IS 1343 Recommendation:** The code permits using $m \cdot f_c / 2$ as the average elastic shortening loss for post-tensioned members where tendons are stressed successively.

**Conclusion:** Post-tensioned members experience **half the elastic shortening loss** of pretensioned members under standard conditions. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q (b): A post-tensioned concrete beam, 200 mm × 350 mm, is stressed by a single cable carrying an initial force of 250 kN at an eccentricity of 75 mm. Calculate the loss of prestress due to elastic shortening, assuming $E_s = 210$ kN/mm² and $E_c = 30$ kN/mm². Assume the cable is stressed when the concrete has zero strain. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Section properties (1 mark)**

$$A = 200 \times 350 = 70,000 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I = \frac{200 \times 350^3}{12} = 7.146 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Modular ratio (1 mark)**

$$m = \frac{210}{30} = 7$$

**Step 3: Concrete stress at level of cable (2 marks)**

Since the cable is stressed against zero-strain concrete, the full force $P_i = 250$ kN acts. (Note: With only one cable, no further tendons will be stressed after this, so the question of average loss doesn't apply in the traditional sense — but using the IS 1343 average formula for single-cable post-tensioning gives 0. Here we use the direct full loss because it's a single tendon anchored after concrete has hardened.)

For this problem, treating it as the cable being stressed after concrete hardens, the loss equals:

$$f_c = \frac{P_i}{A} + \frac{P_i \cdot e^2}{I} = \frac{250,000}{70,000} + \frac{250,000 \times 75^2}{7.146 \times 10^8}$$

$$f_c = 3.571 + \frac{1.406 \times 10^9}{7.146 \times 10^8} = 3.571 + 1.968 = 5.539 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4: Loss of prestress (1.5 marks)**

For post-tensioning with a single cable stressed when concrete is unstressed, the loss in the cable equals:

$$\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c = 7 \times 5.539 = 38.77 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5: Loss in force (1 mark)**

Area of cable: Assume standard 12 wires of 7 mm dia = $12 \times 38.48 = 461.8 \text{ mm}^2$.

$$\Delta P_{el} = 461.8 \times 38.77 = 17,905 \text{ N} \approx 17.9 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6: Effective prestress (0.5 marks)**

$$P_{net} = 250 - 17.9 = 232.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Final Answer:** **Loss of prestress = 38.77 N/mm² (or 17.9 kN)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Observations):**
>
> 1. **Confusing pretensioning and post-tensioning formulas (–2 marks):** Students often write $m \cdot f_c / 2$ for pretensioned members. Remember: **Pretensioning = FULL loss = $m \cdot f_c$**; **Post-tensioning = HALF loss = $m \cdot f_c / 2$**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to include the eccentricity term ($P_i \cdot e^2 / I$) in $f_c$ (–1 mark):** Many students only calculate $P_i / A$ and forget the bending stress due to eccentric prestress. Always include both terms unless stated otherwise.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign convention for self-weight moment (–1 mark):** Self-weight creates **tension** at the bottom fibre (where the tendon usually sits). So you must **subtract** $M \cdot e / I$, not add it.
>
> 4. **Using wrong $E_s$ value (–0.5 marks):** Use $E_s = 200 \text{ GPa}$ unless otherwise specified. Don't use 210 unless given.
>
> 5. **Not converting $E_c$ correctly (–1 mark):** If only $f_{ck}$ is given, use $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm². Don't use $E_c = 5700\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (that's for reinforced concrete, IS 456).
>
> 6. **Confusing stress loss with force loss (–1 mark):** If the question asks for "loss of prestress," it could mean either stress (N/mm²) or force (kN). State both clearly to be safe.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

**Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 – Elastic Shortening):**

- **Definition:** Elastic shortening loss = reduction in steel stress due to instantaneous concrete shortening at prestress transfer.

- **Core Formula (Pretensioning):** $\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c$

- **Core Formula (Post-tensioning):** $\Delta f_{p,el} = m \cdot f_c / 2$

- **Modular Ratio:** $m = E_s / E_c$, where $E_s = 200$ GPa (standard) and $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ N/mm².

- **Concrete Stress at Steel Level:** $f_c = P_i / A + P_i \cdot e^2 / I - M \cdot e / I$

- **Pretensioned members:** All strands released at once → full loss.

- **Post-tensioned members:** Tendons stressed sequentially → average loss is half.

- **Self-weight effect:** Reduces concrete compression at steel level, hence reduces elastic shortening loss.

- **Force form:** $\Delta P_{el} = A_{st} \cdot \Delta f_{p,el}$

- **Effective prestress:** $P_{net} = P_i - \Delta P_{el}$

- **IS 1343 Recommendation:** Post-tensioned with successive stressing = half-loss formula is permissible.

- **Typical Magnitude:** 2–5% of initial prestress (depends on $f_c$ and $m$).

- **Sign convention:** Compression positive, tension negative.

- **Order of loss magnitude (combined with other losses):** Elastic shortening is usually the second-largest immediate loss, after friction (in post-tensioning).

- **Practical Design Tip:** Use higher transfer strength ($f_{ci}$) at transfer to reduce $f_c / E_c$ ratio and minimize elastic shortening.

- **Key Distinction for Exam:** "Pretensioned = full loss $m \cdot f_c$" and "Post-tensioned = half loss $m \cdot f_c / 2$" — memorize this difference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a problem does not specify the type of prestressing (pretension or post-tension), **assume pretensioning by default** for KTU Module 1 unless the problem explicitly states "successive tendons" or "post-tensioned." This avoids ambiguity and ensures full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Friction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Friction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Friction Loss of Prestress** is the loss of tensile force in a tendon caused by the resistance offered between the surface of the prestressing steel (tendon/wire/strand) and the surrounding concrete duct or sheathing during the stressing operation. In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, this loss is exclusively considered for **post-tensioned prestressed concrete members**, where the tendon is stressed *after* the concrete has hardened.

Mathematically, the governing expression for the force remaining in the tendon at a distance $x$ from the jacking end is:

$$P_x = P_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu \theta + K L)}$$

Where:
- $P_0$ = Initial jacking (anchorage) force at the stressing end (**kN**)
- $P_x$ = Prestressing force at a distance $x$ from the jacking end (**kN**)
- $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction (curvature friction coefficient) (**dimensionless**)
- $\theta$ = Cumulative angle change in radians over distance $x$
- $K$ = Wobble friction coefficient (**per metre**, m⁻¹)
- $L$ = Length of tendon from jacking end to the point under consideration (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Friction loss is **ZERO in pre-tensioned members** because the tendon is stressed against external abutments *before* concrete is cast, so there is no relative movement between steel and concrete during stressing.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pulling a thick rope through a long, curved bamboo pipe (duct). The rope is taut at the pulling end (the jacking end), but as you pull, friction between the rope and pipe wall eats away at the tension. The harder you pull, the more friction you generate, and any bend in the pipe increases the contact force, multiplying the friction. This is exactly what happens when a strand is tensioned inside a curved HDPE duct in a post-tensioned concrete beam.

**Real-world intuition:**
- **Curvature Friction ($\mu \theta$):** Like dragging a heavy suitcase up a curved ramp — the steeper and more curved the path, the more force you lose to friction.
- **Wobble Friction ($K L$):** Like a garden hose that isn't perfectly straight — even a straight hose touches the ground in many tiny unintended points. Each micro-contact saps a little tension over the full length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants (IS 1343 / KTU Standard Values):**
> - $\mu$ for steel strand in HDPE duct: **0.17 – 0.20**
> - $\mu$ for plain wire in rigid metal duct: **0.25 – 0.30**
> - $K$ (wobble factor) for typical post-tensioning: **0.0010 – 0.0020 per metre**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Decomposition of Friction Loss Components

The total friction loss is not a single phenomenon. It is a combination of two distinct physical mechanisms:

#### A. Curvature Friction (Primary Component)
- **Why it occurs:** Whenever the tendon profile changes direction (parabolic, harped, or deflected), the tendon presses radially outward against the duct wall due to its inherent tension trying to straighten.
- **Magnitude of contact force:** $dN = P \cdot d\theta$ (radial normal force per unit angle).
- **Frictional resistance:** $dF_{curvature} = \mu \cdot P \cdot d\theta$.

#### B. Wobble Friction (Secondary Component)
- **Why it occurs:** Even in *straight* tendons, the duct is not perfectly straight. The duct sways slightly between its supports, creating unintended points of contact.
- **Magnitude:** Proportional to the length of the tendon, hence the term $K \cdot L$.
- **Frictional resistance:** $dF_{wobble} = K \cdot P \cdot dx$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Governing Derivation

1. Consider an elemental tendon length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the jacking end.
2. The tendon deflects by $d\theta$ over $dx$ (curvature effect).
3. The radial normal force on the duct is $dN = P_x \cdot d\theta$.
4. Wobble creates an additional normal force $dN_{wobble} = K \cdot P_x \cdot dx$ (per unit length).
5. Total friction force on the element: $dP = \mu \cdot P_x \cdot d\theta + K \cdot P_x \cdot dx$.
6. Rearranging: $\frac{dP}{P_x} = -(\mu \, d\theta + K \, dx)$.
7. Integrating from $0$ to $x$: $\ln(P_x/P_0) = -(\mu \theta + K x)$.
8. Exponentiating gives the master equation: $P_x = P_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu \theta + K x)}$.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Unit** | **Typical Range (IS 1343)** | **Significance** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Jacking Force | $P_0$ | kN | 70–80% of $P_u$ | Force at stressing (anchorage) end |
| Prestress at distance $x$ | $P_x$ | kN | — | Effective force at section $x$ |
| Coefficient of Curvature Friction | $\mu$ | dimensionless | 0.17 – 0.30 | Depends on duct material & lubrication |
| Wobble Coefficient | $K$ | m⁻¹ | 0.0010 – 0.0020 | Depends on duct straightness |
| Cumulative Angle | $\theta$ | radians | Profile dependent | Sum of all angular deviations |
| Tendon Length | $L$ | m | — | Distance from jacking end |
| Friction Loss | $\Delta P_{fric}$ | kN | $P_0 - P_x$ | Force lost due to friction |
| Approximate Loss (small values) | $\Delta P \approx P_0(\mu \theta + K L)$ | kN | $\mu \theta + K L < 0.2$ | Linearised form for KTU calculations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** When $(\mu \theta + K L) \leq 0.2$, the linearised formula $\Delta P_f = P_0(\mu \theta + K L)$ is acceptable and frequently expected in Part-B 14-mark problems. When the value exceeds 0.2, the *exact* exponential form **must** be used.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Girders:** Friction loss calculations dictate the jacking force required at the anchorage end of segmental bridge tendons.
- **Nuclear Containment Vessels:** Post-tensioned domes require precise friction assessment to ensure uniform circumferential prestress.
- **High-Rise Floor Slabs:** Banded tendons in flat slabs with parabolic profiles have significant $\mu \theta$ losses, often 10–15% of $P_0$.
- **Design Verification:** Without accounting for friction, the actual prestress at the mid-span may be too low, causing cracking under service loads.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Friction Loss Equation

**Step 1 — Setting up the differential element:**

Consider a small element of tendon of length $dx$ located at a distance $x$ from the jacking end. Let the prestressing force at the start of the element be $P_x$. The force at the end of the element is $P_x + dP_x$ (where $dP_x$ is negative due to loss).

$$
P_x \xrightarrow{\quad dx \quad} P_x + dP_x
$$

**Step 2 — Identifying the contact normal force:**

The tendon subtends an angle $d\theta$ over the length $dx$. The radial normal force on the duct wall is:

$$
dN_{curvature} = P_x \cdot d\theta
$$

Additionally, the wobble effect produces a uniformly distributed normal force:

$$
dN_{wobble} = K \cdot P_x \cdot dx
$$

**Step 3 — Summing the frictional forces:**

The differential friction force is the product of the normal force and the friction coefficient $\mu$:

$$
dF_{friction} = \mu \cdot (dN_{curvature} + dN_{wobble}) = \mu \cdot P_x \cdot d\theta + K \cdot P_x \cdot dx
$$

**Step 4 — Force equilibrium on the element:**

The reduction in tendon force over the element equals the frictional resistance:

$$
-(P_x + dP_x) + P_x = -(\mu \cdot P_x \cdot d\theta + K \cdot P_x \cdot dx)
$$

Simplifying (neglecting the second-order product $dP_x \cdot dx$ and $dP_x \cdot d\theta$):

$$
-dP_x = P_x (\mu \, d\theta + K \, dx)
$$

**Step 5 — Separating variables:**

$$
\frac{dP_x}{P_x} = -(\mu \, d\theta + K \, dx)
$$

**Step 6 — Integration from jacking end to point x:**

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P_x} \frac{dP_x}{P_x} = -\int_{0}^{\theta} \mu \, d\theta - \int_{0}^{x} K \, dx
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P_x}{P_0}\right) = -(\mu \theta + K x)
$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiation to obtain the master formula:**

$$
\boxed{P_x = P_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu \theta + K x)}}
$$

**Step 8 — Linearised approximation (for KTU small-value problems):**

Using the Taylor expansion $e^{-z} \approx 1 - z + \frac{z^2}{2} - \ldots$ and truncating after the first-order term:

$$
P_x \approx P_0 \left[1 - (\mu \theta + K x)\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P_{friction} = P_0 (\mu \theta + K x) \quad \text{(valid for } \mu \theta + K x \leq 0.2\text{)}}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:** A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam of span **12 m** has a parabolic tendon profile. The tendon is stressed from one end only. Given:
- $P_0 = 1500$ kN (jacking force)
- $\mu = 0.20$
- $K = 0.0015$ /m
- Maximum eccentricity at mid-span: $e_{mid} = 150$ mm
- Eccentricity at supports: $e_{end} = 0$

**Find:**
(a) The total angle change $\theta$ at mid-span.
(b) The prestress force at mid-span using the exact formula.
(c) The percentage friction loss.

---

**Solution (a) — Total angle change at mid-span:**

For a parabolic profile with zero eccentricity at supports and $e_{mid}$ at mid-span, the slope at any point $x$ from the support is:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4 e_{mid}}{L^2}(L - 2x)
$$

The total angle change from the support ($x = 0$) to mid-span ($x = L/2$) is:

$$
\theta = \left|\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=0} - \left|\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=L/2}
$$

Computing the slope at the support ($x = 0$):

$$
\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{4 \cdot 150}{12000^2}(12000 - 0) = \frac{600}{144 \times 10^6} \cdot 12000 = 0.05 \text{ rad}
$$

Computing the slope at mid-span ($x = 6000$ mm):

$$
\left.\frac{dy}{dx}\right|_{x=6000} = \frac{600}{144 \times 10^6}(12000 - 12000) = 0
$$

Therefore, the cumulative angle change from support to mid-span:

$$
\theta = 0.05 - 0 = 0.05 \text{ rad}
$$

---

**Solution (b) — Prestress at mid-span (Exact formula):**

Distance from jacking end to mid-span: $L = 6$ m.

Substituting into the master equation:

$$
P_{mid} = 1500 \cdot e^{-(0.20 \times 0.05 + 0.0015 \times 6)}
$$

$$
P_{mid} = 1500 \cdot e^{-(0.01 + 0.009)}
$$

$$
P_{mid} = 1500 \cdot e^{-0.019}
$$

$$
P_{mid} = 1500 \cdot 0.98118 = 1471.77 \text{ kN}
$$

---

**Solution (c) — Percentage friction loss:**

$$
\Delta P_{friction} = 1500 - 1471.77 = 28.23 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\text{Percentage Loss} = \frac{28.23}{1500} \times 100 = 1.882\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using linearised formula:** $\Delta P = 1500 \times 0.019 = 28.5$ kN (1.9%). The linearised result is within 1% of the exact value because $\mu \theta + K L = 0.019 < 0.2$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Friction Loss Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Jacking End Anchor P0 Applied"] --> B["Tendon Enters Duct System"]
    B --> C{"Profile Geometry"}
    C -->|Curved| D["Curvature Friction mu theta"]
    C -->|Straight| E["Wobble Friction K times L"]
    C -->|Both| F["Combined Friction Loss"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Evaluate Exponent mu theta + KL"]
    G --> H{"Exponent Less Than 0.2"}
    H -->|Yes| I["Use Linear Formula P0 times 1 minus bracket"]
    H -->|No| J["Use Exponential Formula P0 times e power minus bracket"]
    I --> K["Compute Effective Px at Section x"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["Subtract to Find Loss Delta P"]
    L --> M["Output to Serviceability Checks"]
    M --> N["Cracking Moment Check"]
    M --> O["Deflection Check"]
    M --> P["Stress at Mid-span Verification"]
```

### 4.2 Tendon Profile Angle Schematic (Parabolic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Support x equals 0\ne equals 0\ntheta_start"] --> M["Mid-span x equals L by 2\ne equals e_mid\ntheta equals 0"]
    S2["Support x equals L\ne equals 0\ntheta_end"] --> M
    S1 -. "Slope 4e_mid by L" .-> M
    M -. "Cumulative theta" .-> J["Jacking End\nP0 Applied Here"]
```

### 4.3 Force Variation Along Tendon (Conceptual Bar Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["P0 at Jacking End 100 percent"] --> B["P after 2m 99.2 percent"]
    B --> C["P after 4m 98.5 percent"]
    C --> D["P after 6m 97.8 percent"]
    D --> E["P after 8m 97.1 percent"]
    E --> F["P at Far End 96.4 percent"]
    F --> G["Loss Delta P = 3.6 percent of P0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Mermaid block above is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** representing the exponential decay of prestress force along the tendon length. In a real engineering drawing, this would be a smooth descending curve. The block diagram captures the discrete force checkpoints typically evaluated in KTU board examination numericals.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Define the wobble coefficient $K$ in prestressed concrete. What is its physical significance and typical value?**

**Model Answer:**

The wobble coefficient $K$ (also called the unintended curvature coefficient) is defined as the friction loss per unit length of tendon caused by the *unintentional* deviation of the duct from its intended profile, even when the tendon is supposed to be straight.

**Physical significance:** It accounts for the local wobbling of the sheathing/duct between its supports, producing small but distributed points of contact between the tendon and duct wall.

**Typical value (IS 1343):** $K = 0.0010$ to $0.0020$ per metre for standard post-tensioning ducts.

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Physical significance: 1 mark
- Typical value with units: 1 mark

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**Distinguish between curvature friction and wobble friction in post-tensioned members.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Basis** | **Curvature Friction** | **Wobble Friction** |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Intentional tendon profile (parabolic/harped) | Unintentional duct deviation |
| Dependency | Depends on $\mu$ and $\theta$ | Depends on $K$ and $L$ |
| Magnitude | Larger in curved tendons | Smaller, distributed uniformly |
| Exists in straight tendon? | No (only if profile changes) | Yes (always) |

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Identification of two types: 1 mark
- One distinguishing feature: 1 mark
- Formula reference: 1 mark

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam of span **16 m** has a tendon with a parabolic profile. The following data is given:
- $P_0 = 2000$ kN
- $\mu = 0.18$
- $K = 0.0012$ /m
- Eccentricity at mid-span = 200 mm
- Eccentricity at supports = 50 mm
- The tendon is stressed from one end only.

**Solve the following:**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Calculate the cumulative angle change $\theta$ at mid-span and at the support.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Determine the prestressing force at mid-span using both the exact exponential formula and the linearised formula. Comment on the validity of the linearised formula.

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The parabolic profile equation (with reference to the centroidal axis) is:

$$
y(x) = e_{end} + 4(e_{mid} - e_{end}) \frac{x}{L}\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)
$$

Substituting $e_{end} = 50$ mm, $e_{mid} = 200$ mm, $L = 16$ m:

$$
y(x) = 50 + 4(150)\frac{x}{16}\left(1 - \frac{x}{16}\right) = 50 + 37.5 \cdot \frac{x}{16}\left(1 - \frac{x}{16}\right) \text{ in mm}
$$

The slope is:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = 37.5 \cdot \frac{1}{16}\left(1 - \frac{2x}{16}\right) \text{ (in appropriate units, factor of 1000 to convert mm to m)}
$$

Converting to metres ($e$ in m: $e_{end} = 0.05$ m, $e_{mid} = 0.20$ m):

$$
y(x) = 0.05 + 0.15 \cdot \frac{4x}{16}\left(1 - \frac{x}{16}\right)
$$

Simplifying:

$$
y(x) = 0.05 + 0.0375 \cdot \frac{x}{4}\left(1 - \frac{x}{16}\right)
$$

The slope in standard form:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4 \cdot 0.15}{16}\left(1 - \frac{2x}{16}\right) = 0.0375\left(1 - \frac{x}{8}\right)
$$

**At support ($x = 0$):**

$$
\theta_{sup} = 0.0375(1 - 0) = 0.0375 \text{ rad}
$$

**At mid-span ($x = 8$ m):**

$$
\theta_{mid} = 0.0375(1 - 1) = 0 \text{ rad}
$$

**Cumulative angle from support to mid-span:**

$$
\theta = 0.0375 - 0 = 0.0375 \text{ rad}
$$

> **Marks: 7** — Profile equation: 2, Slope derivation: 2, Boundary evaluation: 2, Final answer: 1

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

Distance from jacking end (support) to mid-span = **8 m**.

**Exponent calculation:**

$$
\mu \theta + K L = (0.18 \times 0.0375) + (0.0012 \times 8)
$$

$$
= 0.00675 + 0.0096 = 0.01635
$$

Since $0.01635 < 0.2$, the linearised formula is valid.

**Exact (Exponential) Formula:**

$$
P_{mid} = 2000 \cdot e^{-0.01635}
$$

$$
P_{mid} = 2000 \cdot 0.98378 = 1967.57 \text{ kN}
$$

**Linearised Formula:**

$$
P_{mid} = 2000 \cdot (1 - 0.01635) = 2000 \cdot 0.98365 = 1967.30 \text{ kN}
$$

**Friction Loss:**

$$
\Delta P_{friction} = 2000 - 1967.57 = 32.43 \text{ kN (exact)}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{linear} = 2000 - 1967.30 = 32.70 \text{ kN (linearised)}
$$

**Comment on Validity:** The linearised formula gives a loss of 32.70 kN vs. the exact 32.43 kN — a difference of less than 1%. Since $\mu \theta + K L = 0.01635 \ll 0.2$, the linearised approximation is fully justified and recommended for KTU 14-mark problem solutions.

> **Marks: 7** — Exponent computation: 2, Exact formula application: 2, Linearised formula: 2, Validity comment: 1

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

A post-tensioned simply supported beam has a span of **20 m** with a tendon having a **trapezoidal profile** (straight in the middle third, parabolic at the ends). The relevant data:
- $P_0 = 1800$ kN
- $\mu = 0.20$, $K = 0.0015$ /m
- Eccentricity at mid-span: 180 mm
- Eccentricity at supports: 0
- Tendon is stressed from one end.

**Solve:**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Compute the cumulative angle $\theta$ at the point where the parabolic portion ends (i.e., at $x = L/3$ from the support) and at mid-span.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Calculate the prestressing force at $x = L/3$ and at mid-span. What is the percentage loss at mid-span?

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

For the parabolic portion (from $x = 0$ to $x = L/3$), the slope at any point is:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4e_{mid}}{L^2}(L - 2x)
$$

With $e_{mid} = 0.18$ m, $L = 20$ m:

$$
\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{4 \times 0.18}{400}(20 - 2x) = 0.0018(20 - 2x) = 0.036 - 0.0036x
$$

**Slope at support ($x = 0$):**

$$
\theta_{sup} = 0.036 \text{ rad}
$$

**Slope at $x = L/3 = 6.67$ m (end of parabolic portion):**

$$
\theta_{L/3} = 0.036 - 0.0036(6.67) = 0.036 - 0.024 = 0.012 \text{ rad}
$$

**Cumulative angle from support to $x = L/3$:**

$$
\theta_1 = 0.036 - 0.012 = 0.024 \text{ rad}
$$

**Slope at mid-span ($x = 10$ m):**

$$
\theta_{mid} = 0.036 - 0.0036(10) = 0.036 - 0.036 = 0 \text{ rad}
$$

**Cumulative angle from support to mid-span:**

$$
\theta_2 = 0.036 - 0 = 0.036 \text{ rad}
$$

> **Marks: 7** — Slope equation: 2, Slope at support: 1, Slope at $L/3$: 1, Slope at mid-span: 1, Cumulative angles: 2

---

**Part (b) Solution:**

**At $x = L/3 = 6.67$ m:**

$$
\mu \theta_1 + K x = (0.20 \times 0.024) + (0.0015 \times 6.67)
$$

$$
= 0.0048 + 0.010 = 0.0148
$$

$$
P_{L/3} = 1800 \cdot e^{-0.0148} = 1800 \cdot 0.98531 = 1773.55 \text{ kN}
$$

**At $x = L/2 = 10$ m:**

$$
\mu \theta_2 + K x = (0.20 \times 0.036) + (0.0015 \times 10)
$$

$$
= 0.0072 + 0.015 = 0.0222
$$

$$
P_{mid} = 1800 \cdot e^{-0.0222} = 1800 \cdot 0.97804 = 1760.47 \text{ kN}
$$

**Percentage Loss at Mid-span:**

$$
\Delta P_{mid} = 1800 - 1760.47 = 39.53 \text{ kN}
$$

$$
\text{Loss \%} = \frac{39.53}{1800} \times 100 = 2.196\%
$$

> **Marks: 7** — Exponent at $L/3$: 2, Force at $L/3$: 1, Exponent at mid-span: 2, Force at mid-span: 1, Percentage loss: 1

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Wobble Component:** Many students compute $\mu \theta$ only and ignore $K L$. This costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Wrong Profile Equation:** Using $y = (4e/L^2) x (L-x)$ without accounting for end eccentricity. KTU's standard expects students to derive the slope for the *given* profile, not memorise a generic form.
> 3. **Linearising Without Checking:** Using the linearised formula when $\mu \theta + K L > 0.2$ is a **2-mark deduction** in KTU evaluation.
> 4. **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing mm and m in $\theta$ calculations (e.g., computing slope in m/m but eccentricity in mm) is a **fatal 1-mark cut**.
> 5. **Forgetting the Negative Sign:** Writing $P_x = P_0 \cdot e^{+(\mu \theta + KL)}$ instead of $e^{-(\mu \theta + KL)}$ — **3-mark cut** in board exams.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Friction loss is unique to post-tensioning** — zero in pre-tensioned members.
- ✅ The **master equation** is $P_x = P_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu \theta + K x)}$.
- ✅ **$\mu \theta$** = curvature friction (depends on profile geometry).
- ✅ **$K L$** = wobble friction (depends on duct straightness and length).
- ✅ **Linearised form** $\Delta P_f = P_0 (\mu \theta + K L)$ is valid **only when** the exponent is $\leq 0.2$.
- ✅ For a **parabolic profile** with end eccentricities $e_1$ and $e_2$ at supports and $e_m$ at mid-span, the slope at support is $\frac{4(e_m - e_{end})}{L}$ (in radians, when consistent units are used).
- ✅ Cumulative angle $\theta$ is the **algebraic sum** of all slope changes from the jacking end to the point of interest.
- ✅ **IS 1343 typical values:** $\mu = 0.17$–$0.30$, $K = 0.0010$–$0.0020$ /m.
- ✅ Always **verify the linearisation criterion** before using the approximate formula in KTU 14-mark problems.
- ✅ Friction loss is a **serviceability-level** loss, considered when checking stresses at initial and final stages of loading.
- ✅ In **continuous beams**, the friction effect is computed from the *nearest jacking end*, not from a fixed reference.
- ✅ The **percentage loss** in well-designed post-tensioned beams typically ranges from **1.5% to 4%** of $P_0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anchorage Slip
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anchorage Slip

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anchorage Slip (Δ)** is the small inward movement (also termed *draw-in*, *slip of the wedge*, or *slip of the jack*) of the prestressing tendon at the anchorage when the stressing jack is released and the wedges/grips seat themselves into the cone of the anchorage block. This seating movement causes a **loss of prestress** in post-tensioned members because the tendon, already stretched, shortens by a tiny amount (Δ), thereby reducing the induced strain and hence the prestressing force.

Mathematically expressed, the loss of stress in the steel due to anchorage slip is:

$$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$$

where:
- **Δ** = magnitude of anchorage slip (typically **3 mm to 6 mm** for wedge-type anchorages and **1 mm to 2 mm** for nut-type anchorages, as per IS 1343),
- **E_s** = modulus of elasticity of steel (≈ **200 to 210 kN/mm²**),
- **L** = effective length of the tendon from the jacking end to the far end.

This is classified as an **immediate (short-term) loss** in post-tensioned concrete and is **not applicable** to pre-tensioned members where the prestress is transferred through bond between steel and concrete.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: "The Rubber Band and the Fishing Hook"**
> Imagine stretching a thick rubber band with your fingers and then trying to hook it onto a small fishing hook. As soon as you let go, the hook's metal barb slightly slides back along the rubber band before it grips firmly. That tiny backward slide (the "slip") releases a small amount of the tension you had stretched in. The rubber band, having shortened by a fraction of a millimetre, is now slightly less stretched than before.
>
> In **prestressed concrete**, the **rubber band** is the high-tensile steel tendon, the **fishing hook** is the **wedge anchorage**, and the **backward slide** is the **anchorage slip (Δ)**. The result is a small but unavoidable loss of prestressing force right at the moment of transfer.

## 1.3 Physical Mechanism — Why Does It Happen?

When the hydraulic jack tensions the tendon in a post-tensioned member, the wedges are held loosely in the anchor cone. Once the desired jacking force is reached, the jack pressure is released. At this instant:

1. The wedges are pushed into the conical housing by the tensioned wires/strands.
2. They bite into the steel and travel inward by a small distance **Δ** before locking.
3. This inward travel shortens the effective length of the tendon by **Δ**.
4. The steel fibres lose a corresponding amount of strain → **prestress is reduced**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Anchorage slip is **exclusive to post-tensioning**. In pre-tensioning, the tendon is released at the abutment and the force is transmitted purely by **bond** — no wedges or anchor cones are involved, so no seating slip occurs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

The phenomenon of anchorage slip and its consequence can be broken down into the following logical sequence:

- **Step 1 — Pre-Jacking State:** The tendon lies slack inside the duct. Wedges are loosely placed in the anchor cone but not engaged.

- **Step 2 — Jacking State:** The hydraulic jack pulls the tendon, stretching it. Strain ε = P/(A·E_s) develops. The wedges remain in their original position relative to the anchor because the jack itself holds them out.

- **Step 3 — Release State (Critical):** Jack pressure is gradually released. The tensioned strands now pull the wedges into the cone. The wedges travel inward by a small amount **Δ** (typically **3–6 mm** for multi-wire/strand systems) before frictionally locking against the cone wall.

- **Step 4 — Post-Seating State:** The tendon has effectively shortened by **Δ**, reducing its strain and therefore its stress by:
  $$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$$

- **Step 5 — Force Loss:** The corresponding loss of prestressing force is:
  $$\Delta P = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L}$$
  where A_s is the cross-sectional area of the tendon.

- **Step 6 — Design Implication:** This loss occurs **immediately at transfer** and is grouped with other **immediate losses** (elastic shortening of concrete, friction losses). It must be deducted from the initial jacking force to obtain the effective prestress at the critical section.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\Delta f_s = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$ | Loss of steel stress due to anchorage slip | N/mm² |
| 2 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L}$ | Loss of prestressing force due to anchorage slip | kN or N |
| 3 | $\Delta f_s = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$ (one-end jacking) | When stressed from one end, L = full length | N/mm² |
| 4 | $\Delta f_s = \dfrac{2\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$ (two-end jacking) | When stressed from both ends, slip occurs at both ends; equivalent loss in stress terms is doubled only if both wedges slip; **loss in force** is computed using L/2 | N/mm² |
| 5 | % loss = $\dfrac{\Delta f_s}{f_{s(\text{initial})}} \times 100$ | Percentage loss of initial prestress | % |
| 6 | $\Delta \approx 3 \text{ to } 6 \text{ mm}$ | Wedge-type anchorage (IS 1343) | mm |
| 7 | $\Delta \approx 1 \text{ to } 2 \text{ mm}$ | Nut-type anchorage | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Examination Note:** For two-end jacking, the *length over which slip occurs* is **L/2** from each end. Therefore, the *loss of force* is computed using L/2, which gives a **larger** loss compared to one-end jacking. The stress loss formula remains $\Delta f_s = \Delta E_s / (L/2)$ if you redefine L.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Girders (Post-Tensioned Box Girders):** Anchorage slip is the first loss accounted for when computing the effective prestress at the serviceability limit state.
- **Nuclear Containment Structures:** In PT domes, cumulative anchorage slip over multiple tendons can lead to measurable shortening — designers specify **shim plates** of thickness equal to the expected slip to pre-compensate.
- **Long-Span Floor Beams:** In PT slabs and beams of length 15–30 m, anchorage slip of just 4 mm corresponds to a stress loss of ~30–60 N/mm² — non-trivial when the design prestress is around 1000 N/mm².
- **Segmental Construction:** Each segment experiences anchorage slip at its own end faces, and the cumulative effect must be tracked segment-by-segment.

## 2.4 Underlying Assumptions

1. The slip **Δ** is assumed to be uniform and constant for a given anchorage system.
2. Slip occurs **at the moment of release** and is therefore an *immediate* loss.
3. For design simplicity, the slip is assumed to affect the **entire tendon length uniformly** (average loss concept).
4. E_s is taken as **200 kN/mm²** for wire/strand tendons and **210 kN/mm²** for alloy bars (per IS 1343).
5. The slip is *additional* to and **independent of** friction losses and elastic shortening.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Anchorage Slip Loss Formula

### Statement
The loss of stress in a post-tensioned tendon due to anchorage slip is given by $\Delta f_s = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$.

### Starting Premise
The tendon, after seating, shortens by an amount **Δ** at the anchorage end. This shortening represents a loss of strain $\Delta \varepsilon$ in the steel.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let the original length of the tendon (from jacking end to the dead end) be **L**, and its cross-sectional area be **A_s**.

**Step 1:** The strain in the tendon before slip = $f_i / E_s$, where $f_i$ is the initial stress in the tendon (just after jacking, before slip).

**Step 2:** When the wedges seat and the tendon slips inward by **Δ**, the *effective* elongation of the tendon reduces by **Δ**.

**Step 3:** The corresponding reduction in strain is:
$$\Delta \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta}{L}$$

> The slip is a physical shortening of the tendon; the strain loss is the ratio of the slip distance to the total length over which the slip is distributed.

**Step 4:** Using Hooke's Law, the loss of stress is:
$$\Delta f_s = E_s \cdot \Delta \varepsilon$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\Delta \varepsilon = \Delta / L$:
$$\Delta f_s = E_s \cdot \frac{\Delta}{L}$$

**Step 6:** Final expression:
$$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$$

**Step 7:** Loss of prestressing force is obtained by multiplying by A_s:
$$\Delta P = \Delta f_s \cdot A_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Interpretation:** The slip Δ is fixed by the anchorage hardware. E_s is a material constant. Therefore, the loss is **inversely proportional to L** — longer tendons suffer **smaller** percentage losses, which is why anchorage slip is more critical in short, heavily prestressed members.

## 3.2 Special Case: Two-End Jacking

When a tendon is stressed simultaneously from **both ends**, slip occurs at **both anchorages**.

**Step 1:** Effective length over which slip is distributed from each end = **L/2**.

**Step 2:** Loss of stress from each end:
$$\Delta f_{s,\text{each end}} = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L/2} = \frac{2 \Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$$

**Step 3:** However, the slip from each end affects only its own half. The **total force loss** is the sum of the force loss from each half:
