$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L/2} + \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L/2} = \frac{2 \Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L/2} = \frac{4 \Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write $\Delta f_s = (2\Delta E_s)/L$ as the *stress* loss for two-end jacking. The correct interpretation is: the **force loss is doubled**, but it is distributed over the *same* effective tendon. For design purposes, simply compute the loss using L/2 in place of L in the standard formula.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Given:**
- Length of tendon, L = **20 m** = 20,000 mm
- Cross-sectional area of tendon, A_s = **600 mm²**
- Initial prestress, $f_i$ = **1100 N/mm²**
- Modulus of elasticity, E_s = **210 kN/mm²** = 210,000 N/mm²
- Anchorage slip, Δ = **5 mm**
- Jacking from one end

**Find:** Loss of stress, loss of force, and percentage loss.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Loss of stress:**
$$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L} = \frac{5 \times 210{,}000}{20{,}000}$$

$$\Delta f_s = \frac{1{,}050{,}000}{20{,}000} = 52.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Loss of prestressing force:**
$$\Delta P = \Delta f_s \cdot A_s = 52.5 \times 600 = 31{,}500 \text{ N} = 31.5 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage loss:**
$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{52.5}{1100} \times 100 = 4.77\%$$

**Step 4 — Effective prestress at transfer:**
$$f_{pe} = 1100 - 52.5 = 1047.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Verification using force method directly:**
$$\Delta P = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L} = \frac{5 \times 210{,}000 \times 600}{20{,}000} = 31{,}500 \text{ N} = 31.5 \text{ kN} \checkmark$$

> This matches, confirming the formula derivation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Anchorage Slip Mechanism

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the operational sequence of anchorage slip in a post-tensioning system, the interactions between the jack, tendon, anchorage components, and the resulting prestress loss.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Slack Tendon Inside Duct] --> B[Hydraulic Jack Activated]
    B --> C[Tendon Stretched to Jacking Force Pi]
    C --> D[Wedges Loosely Placed in Anchor Cone]
    D --> E[Jack Pressure Released]
    E --> F[Wedges Pulled Inward by Tensioned Tendon]
    F --> G[Tendon Shortens by Slip Distance DELTA]
    G --> H[Loss of Steel Strain: DELTAepsilon equals DELTA over L]
    H --> I[Loss of Steel Stress: DELTAfs equals DELTA times Es over L]
    I --> J[Loss of Prestressing Force: DELTAP equals DELTAfs times As]
    J --> K[Effective Prestress at Transfer: fpe equals fi minus DELTAfs]
    K --> L[Design Uses fpe for SLS and ULS Checks]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style G fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style I fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style J fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style L fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — Influence of Jacking Direction

This Mermaid diagram distinguishes the two scenarios (one-end jacking vs. two-end jacking) and the corresponding effect on anchorage slip loss.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Post-Tensioned Tendon of Length L] --> B1{Number of Jacking Ends}
    B1 -->|One End| C1[Slip DELTA at Jacking End Only]
    B1 -->|Both Ends| C2[Slip DELTA at Both Anchorages]

    C1 --> D1[Effective Slip Length equals L]
    C1 --> E1[Stress Loss: DELTAfs equals DELTA times Es over L]
    C1 --> F1[Force Loss: DELTAP equals DELTA times Es times As over L]

    C2 --> D2[Effective Slip Length equals L over 2 from each end]
    C2 --> E2[Force Loss per end: DELTA Es As over L by 2]
    C2 --> F2[Total Force Loss: 4 DELTA Es As over L]
    C2 --> G2[Equivalent Stress Loss: 2 DELTAfs compared to one-end case]

    F1 --> H1[Add to Other Immediate Losses]
    F2 --> H1
    H1 --> I1[Effective Prestress for Design]

    style A0 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style C1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style C2 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style F2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style I1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
```

## 4.3 Component Schematic — Anchor Cone and Wedge Seating

The diagram below shows the physical layout of a multi-strand wedge anchorage and the direction of the slip Δ when the jack releases.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANCHOR[Anchor Head and Cone Assembly]
        direction TB
        WH[Wedge Housing / Anchor Plate] --> WC[Conical Bore]
        WC --> W1[Wedge for Strand 1]
        WC --> W2[Wedge for Strand 2]
        WC --> W3[Wedge for Strand 3]
    end

    subgraph TENDON[Post-Tensioning Tendon]
        direction TB
        ST1[Strand 1 — High Tensile Steel] --> ST2[Strand 2]
        ST2 --> ST3[Strand 3]
    end

    JACK[Hydraulic Jack] -->|Pulls outward during stressing| TENDON
    TENDON -->|Inserted through| ANCHOR
    JACK -->|Released after stressing| REL[Release Event]
    REL -->|Wedges seat in cone| SLIP[Inward Slip by DELTA mm]
    SLIP -->|Loss of strain| LOSS[Prestress Reduced by DELTAfs]

    style ANCHOR fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style TENDON fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style SLIP fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style LOSS fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define anchorage slip. Why is it considered as a loss of prestress only in post-tensioned members and not in pre-tensioned members?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Anchorage Slip:** It is the small inward movement (Δ) of the tendon at the anchorage when the wedges/grips seat themselves into the anchor cone after the jack is released, causing a shortening of the tendon and hence a loss of prestress.

**Why only in post-tensioning:**
- In **post-tensioned** members, prestress is transferred to concrete **via mechanical anchorage** (wedges, nuts). When the jack releases, the wedges slip inward by Δ, reducing the tendon strain.
- In **pre-tensioned** members, prestress is transferred **purely by bond** between steel and hardened concrete. There are no wedges or anchor cones; hence, no seating slip occurs. The force transfer is governed by **Hoyer effect** and bond stress, not by mechanical anchorage.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Write the expression for the loss of prestress due to anchorage slip and explain each term. What is the typical range of slip for wedge-type anchorages?**

**Model Answer:**

$$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$$

- **Δ** = anchorage slip (in mm) — typically **3 mm to 6 mm** for wedge-type anchorages (per IS 1343), and **1 mm to 2 mm** for nut-type.
- **E_s** = modulus of elasticity of steel (≈ **200–210 kN/mm²**).
- **L** = length of the tendon from the jacking end to the dead end (in mm).

> The loss is **inversely proportional to L**; hence, shorter members suffer larger percentage losses.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module 1 — Internal Choice Format** (Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives)

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`**

A post-tensioned concrete beam of **span 18 m** is prestressed with a **5-wire cable** of cross-sectional area **750 mm²**. The cable is stressed from **one end** only. The initial prestress in the wires is **1200 N/mm²**. Take E_s = **210 kN/mm²** and anchorage slip Δ = **4 mm**.

**(a)** Calculate the **loss of stress** and **loss of prestressing force** due to anchorage slip. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the same beam were stressed from **both ends simultaneously** (with the same Δ), compute the new loss of force and the **percentage loss of initial prestress** in both cases. Compare the results. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- L = 18 m = 18,000 mm
- A_s = 750 mm²
- f_i = 1200 N/mm²
- E_s = 210 kN/mm² = 210,000 N/mm²
- Δ = 4 mm
- Jacking from **one end**

**Step 1 — Loss of stress:**
$$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L} = \frac{4 \times 210{,}000}{18{,}000}$$

$$\Delta f_s = \frac{840{,}000}{18{,}000} = 46.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Loss of prestressing force:**
$$\Delta P = \Delta f_s \cdot A_s = 46.67 \times 750 = 35{,}000 \text{ N} = 35.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage loss of initial prestress:**
$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{46.67}{1200} \times 100 = 3.89\%$$

**Step 4 — Effective prestress at transfer:**
$$f_{pe} = 1200 - 46.67 = 1153.33 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Case 1 — One-End Jacking (already computed):**
- Δf_s = 46.67 N/mm²
- ΔP = 35.0 kN
- % loss = 3.89 %

**Case 2 — Two-End Jacking:**

When stressed from both ends, the slip occurs at **both anchorages**, each distributing the slip over **L/2** = 9000 mm.

**Step 1 — Effective length from each end:**
$$L_{\text{eff}} = \frac{L}{2} = \frac{18{,}000}{2} = 9000 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Force loss from each end:**
$$\Delta P_{\text{each end}} = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{4 \times 210{,}000 \times 750}{9000} = 70{,}000 \text{ N} = 70.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Total force loss:**
$$\Delta P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 70.0 = 140.0 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent average stress loss:**
$$\Delta f_{s,\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{total}}}{A_s} = \frac{140{,}000}{750} = 186.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Percentage loss:**
$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{186.67}{1200} \times 100 = 15.56\%$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

| Parameter | One-End Jacking | Two-End Jacking |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------|
| Force Loss (kN) | 35.0 | 140.0 |
| Avg. Stress Loss (N/mm²) | 46.67 | 186.67 |
| % Loss | 3.89% | 15.56% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Two-end jacking causes **4× the force loss** compared to one-end jacking because slip occurs at both anchorages and the effective length for each slip is halved. Therefore, **one-end jacking is preferred** when anchorage slip is the dominant concern; however, two-end jacking is used to reduce friction losses and improve stress uniformity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Deduction Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $\Delta f_s = 2 \Delta E_s / L$ as the *stress* loss for two-end jacking without clarifying the *length* over which slip is distributed. ❌
> - **Correct Approach:** Compute the **force loss** using L/2 from each end and then sum. ✅
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert kN/mm² to N/mm² (factor of 1000) when plugging E_s into the formula. ❌ Always verify units before substituting.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting only the force loss without the percentage — KTU expects both numerical answer and a comparison statement.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**

A pre-cast post-tensioned concrete pile of **length 12 m** is prestressed using **2 cables**, each of cross-sectional area **400 mm²**. The cables are stressed from **one end** with an initial prestress of **1050 N/mm²**. Take E_s = **200 kN/mm²** and anchorage slip Δ = **6 mm**. The beam is subjected to a working load that produces a maximum bending moment of **180 kN·m** at the mid-span. Section properties: A_c = 96,000 mm², I = 1.92 × 10⁸ mm⁴, e = 120 mm (eccentricity of cables at mid-span).

**(a)** Compute the **total loss of prestress** due to anchorage slip and the **effective prestressing force** available at the critical section. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the **stress in concrete at the soffit and top fibre** at mid-span under the working load, and check whether the section satisfies the **IS 1343 allowable stress limits** (allowable tension = 0, allowable compression at transfer = 12 N/mm², at service = 14 N/mm²). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**
- L = 12 m = 12,000 mm
- A_s (per cable) = 400 mm² → Total A_s = 2 × 400 = **800 mm²**
- f_i = 1050 N/mm²
- E_s = 200 kN/mm² = 200,000 N/mm²
- Δ = 6 mm
- Jacking from one end

**Step 1 — Loss of stress (one-end jacking):**
$$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L} = \frac{6 \times 200{,}000}{12{,}000} = \frac{1{,}200{,}000}{12{,}000} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total loss of prestressing force:**
$$\Delta P = \Delta f_s \cdot A_s = 100 \times 800 = 80{,}000 \text{ N} = 80 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Initial prestressing force (before slip):**
$$P_i = f_i \cdot A_s = 1050 \times 800 = 840{,}000 \text{ N} = 840 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Effective prestressing force at transfer (after slip):**
$$P_{e} = P_i - \Delta P = 840 - 80 = 760 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Effective prestress in steel:**
$$f_{pe} = f_i - \Delta f_s = 1050 - 100 = 950 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Direct stress due to effective prestress at mid-span:**
$$f_{direct} = \frac{P_e}{A_c} = \frac{760{,}000}{96{,}000} = 7.917 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}$$

**Step 2 — Bending stress due to eccentricity:**
$$f_{bending} = \frac{P_e \cdot e}{I} = \frac{760{,}000 \times 120}{1.92 \times 10^{8}}$$

$$f_{bending} = \frac{9.12 \times 10^{7}}{1.92 \times 10^{8}} = 0.475 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Section modulus:**
$$Z = \frac{I}{y} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^{8}}{y}$$

Assuming a rectangular section, we need y. Let the depth be inferred: from I = b·d³/12 and A_c = b·d, taking a typical ratio. **For this problem, take section depth d = 600 mm** (assume b = 160 mm, so A_c = 96,000 mm² ✓, I = 160 × 600³/12 = 2.88 × 10⁹ mm⁴ — but given I = 1.92 × 10⁸, so this is a thinner section, perhaps a voided slab or small pile). For consistency with given I, use the explicit bending stress from Step 2.

**Step 4 — Stress at soffit (bottom fibre, where prestress creates additional compression):**
$$f_{bottom} = f_{direct} + f_{bending} = 7.917 + 0.475 = 8.392 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}$$

**Step 5 — Stress at top fibre:**
$$f_{top} = f_{direct} - f_{bending} = 7.917 - 0.475 = 7.442 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}$$

**Step 6 — Stress due to external moment at mid-span:**

Using the section properties given, the bending stress due to M = 180 kN·m = 180 × 10⁶ N·mm:

$$f_M = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

We need y (distance from NA to extreme fibre). For consistency with the given I and a typical pile section, take y = 300 mm (full depth of 600 mm).

$$f_M = \frac{180 \times 10^{6} \times 300}{1.92 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{5.4 \times 10^{10}}{1.92 \times 10^{8}} = 281.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Note:** The large value of f_M above indicates the assumed depth is too small. For a realistic prestressed pile, the section modulus Z = M/f_allowable would typically be designed as Z = 180 × 10⁶ / 14 ≈ 12.86 × 10⁶ mm³. The given I and the eccentricity are likely from a problem where the depth is much larger; the standard KTU formulation uses Z = I/y given. **For complete credit, students must explicitly use the given I and a stated y from the section geometry.**

**Step 7 — Net stress at soffit (compression side):**
$$f_{soffit,net} = 8.392 - 281.25 = -272.86 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}$$

This is clearly outside allowable range, indicating the section as stated is under-designed or the section modulus in the problem is for a different loading case. **In KTU valuation, state the methodology and the formula; final numerical check depends on the actual depth provided in the section sketch.**

**Proper methodology statement (for full marks):**

$$f_{soffit} = \frac{P_e}{A_c} + \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z} - \frac{M}{Z}$$

$$f_{top} = \frac{P_e}{A_c} - \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z} + \frac{M}{Z}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Methodology check (using formula, not numerical evaluation alone):**
> - Compute f_soffit and f_top using the formulas above.
> - Compare with IS 1343 allowable values: 0 N/mm² tension, 12 N/mm² compression at transfer, 14 N/mm² at service.
> - The **process and formula application** carry the marks; the numerical check is secondary if section data is implicit.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Mark Deduction Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using **initial** P_i instead of **effective** P_e (after slip loss) in the stress calculations. ❌ Always use the **effective** prestress after deducting anchorage slip loss.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to deduct the **anchorage slip loss** as a *separate* line item before combining with elastic shortening, shrinkage, etc. KTU expects anchorage slip to be explicitly computed.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Sign errors in the bending stress term — the eccentricity creates compression at the soffit (where the cable is placed) and tension at the top in a simply supported beam.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Using wrong units for E_s (kN/mm² vs N/mm²) — multiply by 1000 to convert.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Anchorage Slip**

- ⭐ **Definition:** Anchorage slip is the **inward movement of the tendon** when wedges seat in the anchor cone at the moment of jack release. Typical values: **3–6 mm** (wedge-type), **1–2 mm** (nut-type).

- ⭐ **Applies to:** **Post-tensioned** members ONLY. Not applicable to pre-tensioning.

- ⭐ **Key Formula (Stress):**
  $$\Delta f_s = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s}{L}$$

- ⭐ **Key Formula (Force):**
  $$\Delta P = \frac{\Delta \cdot E_s \cdot A_s}{L}$$

- ⭐ **Classification:** **Immediate (short-term) loss** — occurs at the moment of transfer.

- ⭐ **Two-End Jacking:** Use **L/2** in place of L for each end; the **total force loss is 4×** the one-end jacking loss (over the same total length L).

- ⭐ **Inverse Length Relation:** Loss is **inversely proportional to L** — short members suffer the largest percentage losses. This is critical for **short PT beams and pile caps**.

- ⭐ **Units Vigilance:** E_s is given in **kN/mm²** in codes — convert to **N/mm²** (multiply by 1000) before substituting, OR keep all forces in kN and lengths in m to be consistent.

- ⭐ **Design Sequence:** Jacking Force (P_jack) → Anchorage Slip Loss → Elastic Shortening → Relaxation → Shrinkage & Creep → Final Effective Prestress.

- ⭐ **Mitigation Strategies:** (i) Pre-place **shim plates** of thickness equal to expected slip; (ii) Use **oversized jacks** with controlled release; (iii) Specify **low-slip wedge systems** (modern conical anchors with Δ ≈ 2–3 mm); (iv) Use **single-end jacking** where possible.

- ⭐ **IS 1343 Reference Values:** Δ = 6 mm for standard wedge anchorages (commonly used in KTU problems).

- ⭐ **Exam Tip:** When asked to compute anchorage slip loss, **show all three** — stress loss, force loss, and percentage loss — for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creep of Concrete
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Creep of Concrete

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Creep of Concrete (KTU 2024 / IS 1343 Terminology)**
> Creep is defined as the **time-dependent increase in strain of hardened concrete under sustained compressive stress**, after accounting for shrinkage strains and purely elastic instantaneous strain, occurring at a constant temperature and humidity.

In prestressed concrete, creep is one of the **most significant time-dependent effects** and is treated as a major contributor to the **loss of prestress**. The total long-term deformation $\varepsilon_{total}$ of a loaded concrete member can be decomposed as:

$$\varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{e} + \varepsilon_{sh} + \varepsilon_{c}$$

where $\varepsilon_{e}$ is the instantaneous elastic strain, $\varepsilon_{sh}$ is the shrinkage strain (independent of stress), and $\varepsilon_{c}$ is the **creep strain** (dependent on sustained stress). The creep strain is conventionally expressed in terms of the **Creep Coefficient $\varphi$**:

$$\varepsilon_{c} = \varphi \cdot \varepsilon_{e} = \varphi \cdot \frac{\sigma_{c}}{E_{c}}$$

For a concrete member in service, the **ultimate creep coefficient** $\varphi_{u}$ typically lies in the range **1.5 to 3.0**, with **$\varphi = 2.2$** being the standard IS 1343 reference value for concrete loaded at **7 days** under dry atmospheric conditions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Sponge-Cake Analogy"** — Imagine a freshly baked sponge cake placed under a heavy book. The moment you place the book, the cake compresses slightly (this is the *elastic strain*). Now leave the book there for a week. The cake will keep getting flatter and flatter, very slowly, because the moisture inside the cake keeps seeping out and the tiny air pockets collapse over time. This *continued, slow squashing* is exactly what **creep** is in concrete.

Concrete is essentially a **porous, gel-structured material** filled with microscopic water. Under sustained load:

1. The **calcium-silicate-hydrate (C-S-H) gel** — the glue of concrete — slowly rearranges itself.
2. The **adsorbed and capillary water** within the gel is squeezed out progressively, allowing the solid skeleton to densify.
3. Internal **micro-sliding** between aggregate and paste contributes to permanent deformation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Creep Matters in Prestressed Concrete**
> In a *prestressed* member, the high tensile steel is bonded to the concrete and is held in tension by the concrete. If the concrete shortens due to creep, the steel is forced to shorten too — and since it is bonded, it loses some of its tensile pre-strain. This means **less compressive force is applied to the concrete**, reducing the design prestress. Engineers call this the **Loss of Prestress due to Creep**, and it is one of the *biggest single losses* (often 5–15% of initial prestress).

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Creep Coefficient $\varphi$ (dimensionless):** Ratio of creep strain to elastic strain.
  - Loading at 3 days, dry: $\varphi \approx 2.6$
  - Loading at 7 days, dry: $\varphi \approx 2.2$
  - Loading at 28 days, dry: $\varphi \approx 1.6$
  - Loading at 28 days, humid: $\varphi \approx 1.1$
- **Specific Creep $c_{s}$ (units: $\text{mm}^2/\text{N}$ or $\mu\varepsilon/\text{MPa}$):** Creep strain per unit sustained stress. Typical: $c_{s} = 30$ to $100 \times 10^{-6}$ per $\text{N/mm}^2$.
- **Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete $E_{c}$ (N/mm²):** IS 456 gives $E_{c} = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
- **Modulus of Elasticity of Prestressing Steel $E_{p} = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm²** (standard).
- **Ultimate Creep Strain $\varepsilon_{cu}$ (at $\varphi = 2.2$, $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa):** roughly $1000$ to $1200$ microstrain.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Creep — Step-by-Step Physics

The creep of concrete is governed by **three coupled sub-mechanisms**, all of which contribute to the long-term strain:

1. **Viscoelastic Flow of Cement Gel (C-S-H Phase)**
   - The C-S-H gel is a layered, amorphous, nano-porous solid that behaves like a very viscous fluid under load.
   - Under sustained stress, the gel layers slide past each other slowly, producing an irrecoverable viscous strain.
   - This dominates during the **first few months** of loading.

2. **Internal Moisture Migration (Drying Creep)**
   - Water held in the capillary pores and gel pores slowly diffuses out.
   - This produces additional shrinkage-like strain but is *load-induced*, hence is classified as creep.
   - This component is highly sensitive to **ambient Relative Humidity (RH)**.

3. **Delayed Elasticity (Recoverable Creep)**
   - Some portion of creep is **recoverable** upon unloading (about 20–30% of total).
   - This is a delayed elastic response, modeled using a Kelvin–Voigt or Burger's chain analog.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Bottom Line:** Creep is the *single largest time-dependent loss* in pretensioned prestressed concrete, and a *major* loss in post-tensioned concrete. Neglecting it is a common exam trap and leads to under-designed (cracked) structures.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Creep — Structured Analysis

| Factor | Effect on Creep | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Water-Cement Ratio (w/c)** | Higher w/c → Higher creep | Use low w/c (0.35–0.45) for prestressed concrete |
| **Age at Loading** | Younger concrete → Higher creep | Delay stressing as long as practical |
| **Magnitude of Sustained Stress** | Creep ∝ Stress (up to ~0.4 fck) | Limit service stress to ≤ 0.4 $f_{ck}$ |
| **Ambient Humidity** | Lower RH → Higher creep | In Kerala coastal climate (RH 70–85%), creep is moderate |
| **Temperature** | Higher T → Higher creep (within limits) | Hot tropical conditions accelerate creep |
| **Member Size / Volume-Surface Ratio** | Thinner members → Higher creep | Mass concrete creeps less per unit stress |
| **Type of Cement** | Ordinary PC > SRC; Admixtures reduce creep | Use mineral admixtures (GGBS, silica fume) |
| **Aggregate Type & Stiffness** | Stiffer aggregate → Lower creep | Use dense, low-porosity aggregates |
| **Reinforcement / Prestress Steel** | Restraint reduces observed creep | Composite action reduces effective strain |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Creep of Concrete (Board-Exam Ready)**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{c} = \varphi \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{c}}{E_{c}}$ | Creep strain from sustained stress | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\varphi = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{c}}{\varepsilon_{e}}$ | Definition of creep coefficient | dimensionless |
| 3 | $c_{s} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{c}}{\sigma_{c}} = \dfrac{\varphi}{E_{c}}$ | Specific creep | $(\text{N/mm}^2)^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}} = E_{p} \cdot \varepsilon_{c} = \varphi \cdot \alpha_{e} \cdot \sigma_{c}$ | Loss of prestress (pretension) | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 5 | $\alpha_{e} = \dfrac{E_{p}}{E_{c}}$ | Modular ratio (steel/concrete) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\nu_{t} = \nu_{u} \cdot \dfrac{t^{0.6}}{10 + t^{0.6}}$ | ACI 209 creep-time function | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\varphi(t, t_{0}) = \varphi_{0} \cdot \beta_{c}(t - t_{0})$ | CEB-FIP model | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\beta_{c}(t-t_{0}) = \left[\dfrac{t-t_{0}}{\beta_{H} + (t-t_{0})}\right]^{0.3}$ | CEB-FIP time-development | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\beta_{H} = 1.5 h \left(1 + (1.2\,\text{RH}/100)^{18}\right) + 250$ | CEB-FIP humidity-size factor | days |
| 10 | $E_{c} = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | IS 456 modulus formula | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |
| 11 | $\sigma_{c} = \dfrac{P_{i}}{A_{c}} \pm \dfrac{P_{i} \cdot e \cdot y}{I_{c}}$ | Concrete stress under prestress | $\text{N/mm}^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$\sigma_{c}$ is the *sustained* concrete stress at the level of the prestressing steel centroid** — NOT the stress at the extreme fiber. Always evaluate $\sigma_{c}$ at the *c.g. of steel* unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Long-Span PSC Bridges:** Creep losses dictate the *long-term camber* and *deflection profile*. IS 1343 specifies creep loss limits for spans > 25 m.
- **Segmental Construction:** In balanced-cantilever bridges, creep causes *time-dependent moment redistribution* between cantilevers and piers.
- **Nuclear Containment Structures:** Creep strain is a *serviceability limiter* under sustained prestress.
- **Storage Tanks & Silos:** Continuous sustained loading makes creep loss dominant.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Creep Strain from Creep Coefficient

**Statement to Prove:** The creep strain $\varepsilon_{c}$ under a sustained stress $\sigma_{c}$ is given by

$$\varepsilon_{c} = \varphi \cdot \frac{\sigma_{c}}{E_{c}}$$

**Step 1 — Start from the Definition of Creep Coefficient.**
The creep coefficient is defined as the ratio of *creep strain* to *instantaneous elastic strain*:

$$\varphi = \frac{\varepsilon_{c}}{\varepsilon_{e}}$$

**Step 2 — Express the Elastic Strain by Hooke's Law.**
Under the sustained stress $\sigma_{c}$, the instantaneous elastic strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{e} = \frac{\sigma_{c}}{E_{c}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $\varepsilon_{e}$ into the Definition of $\varphi$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi &= \frac{\varepsilon_{c}}{\sigma_{c}/E_{c}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{c} \cdot E_{c}}{\sigma_{c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Creep Strain $\varepsilon_{c}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{c} &= \varphi \cdot \frac{\sigma_{c}}{E_{c}} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This shows that *doubling the sustained stress* doubles the creep strain, and *tripling the modulus* (e.g., using a higher-strength concrete) reduces the creep strain to one-third — a powerful design lever.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Loss of Prestress Due to Creep in a Pretensioned Member

**Statement to Prove:** In a pretensioned (bonded) prestressed concrete member, the loss of prestress in steel due to creep is

$$\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}} = E_{p} \cdot \varphi \cdot \frac{\sigma_{c}}{E_{c}} = \varphi \cdot \alpha_{e} \cdot \sigma_{c}$$

**Step 1 — Compatibility Condition (Steel and Concrete Strain Must Be Equal).**
In a pretensioned bonded member, the steel tendon and the surrounding concrete at the steel level must undergo the **same strain** at all times (no slip assumption). So:

$$\varepsilon_{p,\text{total}} = \varepsilon_{c,\text{total}}$$

**Step 2 — Decompose the Total Strain in Concrete.**
The concrete at the level of steel is subjected to the initial stress $\sigma_{c,0}$ at the moment of prestress transfer. After $t$ days, the total strain is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{c,\text{total}} &= \varepsilon_{e,0} + \varepsilon_{c}(t) \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{c,0}}{E_{c}} + \varphi(t) \cdot \frac{\sigma_{c,0}}{E_{c}} \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{c,0}}{E_{c}} \cdot \left[1 + \varphi(t)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Decompose the Total Strain in Steel.**
The steel also shortens by the *same* amount. The shortening releases some of the initial tensile strain. If the initial prestress was $f_{pi}$, the *change* in steel stress is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f_{p} &= E_{p} \cdot \varepsilon_{c,\text{creep}} \\
&= E_{p} \cdot \varphi \cdot \frac{\sigma_{c,0}}{E_{c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the Modular Ratio $\alpha_{e} = E_{p}/E_{c}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}} &= \varphi \cdot \frac{E_{p}}{E_{c}} \cdot \sigma_{c,0} \\
&= \varphi \cdot \alpha_{e} \cdot \sigma_{c,0} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Exam Insight:** The *concrete* stress $\sigma_{c,0}$ here is at the **level of the steel centroid**, not at the extreme fiber. The IS 1343 code explicitly states this. Many students lose marks by using the extreme fiber stress instead.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — ACI 209 Time Function for Creep

**Statement to Prove:** The creep coefficient at time $t$ under a sustained load is

$$\varphi(t) = \varphi_{u} \cdot \frac{t^{0.6}}{10 + t^{0.6}}$$

**Step 1 — Empirical Observation.**
Experimental data on concrete under sustained load shows that creep develops **rapidly in the first 90 days** and **asymptotically** approaches an ultimate value $\varphi_{u}$. A power-law of the form $\nu_{t} = K \cdot t^{m}$ fits the early data, but fails to asymptote.

**Step 2 — Apply the Ross Hyperbolic-Type Function.**
To make the function asymptotic, ACI 209 adopts the form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(t) &= \varphi_{u} \cdot \frac{(t-t_{0})^{0.6}}{10 + (t-t_{0})^{0.6}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $(t - t_{0})$ is the time under load in days.

**Step 3 — Boundary Check.**
- At $t = t_{0}$ (just after loading): $\varphi = 0$. ✓
- As $t \to \infty$: $\varphi \to \varphi_{u}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Numerical Sanity Check.**
At $t - t_{0} = 365$ days (1 year):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(365) &= \varphi_{u} \cdot \frac{365^{0.6}}{10 + 365^{0.6}} \\
&= \varphi_{u} \cdot \frac{25.34}{35.34} \approx 0.72 \, \varphi_{u}
\end{aligned}
$$

So at one year, **~72% of ultimate creep** has occurred — a useful design benchmark.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model Solution)

**Problem (KTU 2024 Style):**
A pretensioned concrete beam of cross-section 200 mm × 300 mm is prestressed by 10 wires of 7 mm diameter, each initially stressed to 1200 N/mm². Given $E_{p} = 2.1 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm², $E_{c} = 3.2 \times 10^{4}$ N/mm², creep coefficient $\varphi = 2.0$, calculate:

**(a)** The loss of prestress due to creep of concrete.
**(b)** The percentage loss with respect to the initial prestress.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the Cross-Sectional Areas.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{p} &= 10 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 7^{2} = 10 \times 38.485 = 384.85 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_{c} &= 200 \times 300 = 60000 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Initial Prestressing Force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{i} &= A_{p} \times f_{pi} = 384.85 \times 1200 = 461820 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the Concrete Stress at the Steel Centroid (assuming concentric).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{c,0} &= \frac{P_{i}}{A_{c}} = \frac{461820}{60000} = 7.697 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Modular Ratio.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{e} &= \frac{E_{p}}{E_{c}} = \frac{2.1 \times 10^{5}}{3.2 \times 10^{4}} = 6.5625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Part (a) — Loss of Prestress Due to Creep.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}} &= \varphi \cdot \alpha_{e} \cdot \sigma_{c,0} \\
&= 2.0 \times 6.5625 \times 7.697 \\
&= 101.04 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (a) Answer:** $\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}} \approx 101$ N/mm²

**Step 6 — Part (b) — Percentage Loss.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Loss} &= \frac{\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}}}{f_{pi}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{101.04}{1200} \times 100 \\
&\approx 8.42\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Part (b) Answer:** Percentage loss ≈ **8.42%**

This is consistent with the IS 1343 typical range of 5–15% for creep loss in pretensioned members.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Creep Loss to Design Parameters

| Variable | Change | Effect on $\Delta f_{p,\text{creep}}$ | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| $f_{ck}$ (concrete strength) ↑ | +10 MPa | ↓ ~25% | Higher $E_{c}$, lower $\sigma_{c,0}$ |
| $A_{p}$ (steel area) ↑ | +20% | ↑ +20% (linear) | Higher prestress force on concrete |
| $\varphi$ ↑ | +0.5 | ↑ ~25% | Older/leaner concrete creeps more |
| $E_{c}$ ↑ | (via $f_{ck}$) | ↓ | Stiffer concrete deforms less |
| Humidity ↑ | 60% → 90% | ↓ ~30% | Less drying creep |
| Age at loading ↑ | 7 → 28 days | ↓ ~25% | Mature gel is more stable |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Mechanism of Creep at the Micro-Scale

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Sustained External Load Applied"]
    nodeB["Instantaneous Elastic Strain"]
    nodeC["Long-Term Creep Strain"]
    nodeD["Viscoelastic Flow of C-S-H Gel"]
    nodeE["Internal Moisture Migration"]
    nodeF["Delayed Elastic Recovery"]
    nodeG["Irrecoverable Viscous Strain"]
    nodeH["Total Time-Dependent Strain"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeC --> nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeB --> nodeH
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Factors Affecting Creep (Mind-Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeC2["Creep of Concrete"]
    nodeA2["Material Factors"]
    nodeB2["Loading Factors"]

    nodeC2 --> nodeB2
    nodeC2 --> nodeC3
```
