# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Explain the three modes of failure at the ultimate limit state for a prestressed concrete beam. Which mode is recommended by IS 1343 and why?**

**Model Answer:**

At the Ultimate Limit State, a PSC beam can fail in any of the following three modes:

1. **Under-reinforced (Tension-Controlled) Failure:** The prestressing steel yields *before* the concrete crushes. This produces a **ductile** failure with visible cracking and large deflections, giving occupants time to evacuate. The neutral axis is shallow (xf / d < 0.5 for Fe415).
2. **Balanced Failure:** Concrete reaches its ultimate strain (εcu = 0.0035) and the steel reaches its yield strain *simultaneously*. The neutral axis ratio is xf / d = 0.5 (for Fe415, using η = 0.5).
3. **Over-reinforced (Compression-Controlled) Failure:** Concrete crushes *before* the steel yields. The neutral axis is deep (xf / d > 0.5). Failure is **sudden, brittle, and catastrophic** with no warning signs.

**IS 1343 recommends the under-reinforced mode** because it ensures ductile failure, providing adequate warning and safety margin. The balanced condition is used only as a *design boundary* — sections must be designed such that xf / d < 0.5.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state that IS 1343 *requires* the balanced section. This is wrong — the code **prohibits** the over-reinforced section but does not require the section to be exactly balanced.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**State the IS 1343 expression for the stress in the prestressing steel at ultimate load, with all limits.**

**Model Answer:**

The stress in the prestressing steel at the Ultimate Limit State, as per IS 1343, is given by the **strain-compatibility equation**:

$$f_{ps} = f_{pe} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{cu} \cdot E_p \cdot (d - x_f)}{x_f}$$

where:
- $f_{pe}$ = effective prestress in steel after all losses
- $\varepsilon_{cu}$ = **0.0035** (ultimate strain in concrete)
- $E_p$ = modulus of elasticity of prestressing steel (≈ 2 × 10⁵ MPa)
- $x_f$ = depth of neutral axis at ULS
- $d$ = effective depth of the section

**Limits (clamping):**
$$f_{pe} \le f_{ps} \le 0.87 \, f_{pu}$$

The lower bound prevents the steel stress from dropping below the effective prestress. The upper bound prevents rupture of the tendon.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 3 questions in the University ESE carry an *internal choice*. The two questions below are **completely independent alternatives** — solve either Question A *or* Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper]`

A post-tensioned PSC beam has a rectangular cross-section **b = 300 mm** wide and **D = 700 mm** overall depth. The beam is prestressed with **Aps = 1200 mm²** of tendons with **fpu = 1860 MPa** and **fpe = 1050 MPa**. Take $E_p = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. The effective depth is **d = 650 mm**. Concrete grade is **M50** (fck = 50 MPa). Take εcu = 0.0035.

**(a) Compute the ultimate moment of resistance of the section using IS 1343 stress block and strain compatibility.** (7 Marks)

**(b) Check whether the section is under-reinforced, balanced, or over-reinforced, and comment on its safety under IS 1343.** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Ultimate Moment of Resistance**

*Step 1: First guess of fps.*
$$f_{ps,1} = 0.87 \times 1860 = 1618.2 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 2: Compute xf from equilibrium.*
$$0.85 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_f = f_{ps,1} \cdot A_{ps}$$
$$0.85 \times 50 \times 300 \times x_f = 1618.2 \times 1200$$
$$12{,}750 \, x_f = 1{,}941{,}840$$
$$x_f = 152.30 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 3: Compute fps from strain compatibility.*
$$f_{ps,2} = f_{pe} + \dfrac{\varepsilon_{cu} \cdot E_p \cdot (d - x_f)}{x_f}$$
$$f_{ps,2} = 1050 + \dfrac{0.0035 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (650 - 152.30)}{152.30}$$
$$f_{ps,2} = 1050 + \dfrac{700 \times 497.70}{152.30}$$
$$f_{ps,2} = 1050 + 2288.15 = 3338.15 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 4: Clamp fps.*
Since $f_{ps,2} = 3338.15 \text{ MPa} > 0.87 \, f_{pu} = 1618.2 \text{ MPa}$:
$$f_{ps} = 1618.2 \text{ MPa} \; (\text{governed by upper limit})$$

*Step 5: Re-iterate.* Since $f_{ps,1} = f_{ps,2}$ (after clamping), no further iteration. $x_f = 152.30$ mm is final.

*Step 6: Compute the lever arm.*
$$z = d - 0.42 \, x_f = 650 - 0.42 \times 152.30 = 650 - 63.97 = 586.03 \text{ mm}$$

*Step 7: Ultimate moment.*
$$M_u = f_{ps} \cdot A_{ps} \cdot z = 1618.2 \times 1200 \times 586.03$$
$$M_u = 1{,}137{,}932{,}892 \text{ N·mm} \approx \boxed{1137.93 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Part (b) — Failure Mode Check**

*Step 1: Compute xf / d.*
$$\dfrac{x_f}{d} = \dfrac{152.30}{650} = 0.2343$$

*Step 2: Compare with balanced ratio for Fe415-equivalent tendons.*
The balanced ratio is **xf,bal / d = 0.5** (using η = 0.5 per IS 1343).

Since **0.2343 < 0.5**, the section is **under-reinforced**.

*Step 3: Comment on safety.*
The section is **safe and ductile** under IS 1343 provisions. The tendon will yield well before concrete crushes, providing adequate warning before failure. However, the strain-compatibility calculation showed that the section is "over-strong" (fps is capped at 1618 MPa) — the designer may consider **reducing Aps** to optimise material cost, while still maintaining under-reinforced behaviour.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative]`

A pretensioned PSC beam has an I-section with **flange width bf = 600 mm**, **flange thickness Df = 150 mm**, **web width bw = 150 mm**, **overall depth D = 900 mm**. The tendon area is **Aps = 1500 mm²** at effective depth **d = 850 mm**, with **fpu = 1860 MPa**, **fpe = 1000 MPa**, $E_p = 2 \times 10^5$ MPa. Concrete grade is **M60** (fck = 60 MPa). εcu = 0.0035.

**(a) Determine the ultimate moment of resistance assuming the neutral axis lies within the flange.** (7 Marks)

**(b) If the design (factored) moment is 2500 kN·m, check the adequacy of the section.** (7 Marks)

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Ultimate Moment of Resistance (NA in Flange)**

*Step 1: Verify that the NA falls within the flange.*

For NA in flange, $x_f \le D_f = 150$ mm. As a first guess:
$$f_{ps,1} = 0.87 \times 1860 = 1618.2 \text{ MPa}$$
$$x_{f,1} = \dfrac{1618.2 \times 1500}{0.85 \times 60 \times 600} = \dfrac{2{,}427{,}300}{30{,}600} = 79.32 \text{ mm}$$

Since $x_{f,1} = 79.32$ mm $< 150$ mm = Df, **NA lies within the flange**. The T-section can be treated as a rectangular section of width bf = 600 mm.

*Step 2: Strain compatibility check.*
$$f_{ps,2} = 1000 + \dfrac{0.0035 \times 2 \times 10^5 \times (850 - 79.32)}{79.32}$$
$$f_{ps,2} = 1000 + \dfrac{700 \times 770.68}{79.32} = 1000 + 6801.97 = 7801.97 \text{ MPa}$$

This far exceeds $0.87 \, f_{pu} = 1618.2$ MPa. Clamp:
$$f_{ps} = 1618.2 \text{ MPa}$$

*Step 3: Re-iterate (already converged).* $x_f = 79.32$ mm final.

*Step 4: Compute lever arm and Mu.*
$$z = d - 0.42 \, x_f = 850 - 0.42 \times 79.32 = 850 - 33.31 = 816.69 \text{ mm}$$
$$M_u = 1618.2 \times 1500 \times 816.69$$
$$M_u = 1{,}982{,}218{,}170 \text{ N·mm} \approx \boxed{1982.22 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Part (b) — Adequacy Check**

*Step 1: Compute design moment (given).*
$$M_{u,\text{des}} = 2500 \text{ kN·m} \;(\text{already factored})$$

*Step 2: Compare.*
$$M_u = 1982.22 \text{ kN·m} < M_{u,\text{des}} = 2500 \text{ kN·m}$$

The section is **inadequate** by:
$$\text{Shortfall} = 2500 - 1982.22 = 517.78 \text{ kN·m} \;(\approx 20.7\% \text{ deficit})$$

*Step 3: Suggest remedies.*
- Increase Aps (e.g., to $1500 \times 2500/1982.22 = 1892$ mm²).
- Increase fck (use M70 instead of M60).
- Increase effective depth d.
- Use additional non-prestressed reinforcement in the tension zone (partially prestressed design).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.87 fpu upper clamp** in fps (lose 1 mark).
> 2. **Using bf in place of bw** for a T-section when the NA is in the web (lose 2 marks). Always re-verify NA position after first iteration.
> 3. **Forgetting to apply the partial safety factor for load** (γf = 1.5) — many students compare characteristic Mu directly to factored moments. IS 1343 mandates γf = 1.5 on loads.
> 4. **Sign error in the lever arm**: write $z = d - 0.42 x_f$ (correct) and *not* $z = d + 0.42 x_f$.
> 5. **Stating the balanced ratio as 0.5 for all steel grades** — the correct balanced ratio depends on the yield stress of the steel. For Fe500, it is 0.53.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE — Module 3: Analysis at Ultimate Strength**

- The **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** corresponds to **εcu = 0.0035** in concrete (top fibre) and **fps ≈ 0.87 fpu** in the tendon.
- The **three failure modes** are: under-reinforced (ductile, preferred), balanced (boundary), over-reinforced (brittle, prohibited).
- The **balanced neutral axis ratio** is **xf / d = 0.5** for Fe415, **0.53** for Fe500, **0.48** for Fe250 (using η = 0.5 per IS 1343).
- **Strain compatibility** is the physics-based method; the IS 1343 empirical formula $f_{ps} = f_{pe} + 700(d - x_f)/x_f$ (for $E_p$ = 2 × 10⁵ MPa and εcu = 0.0035) is its most-used form.
- **Force equilibrium** gives the NA depth: $0.85 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_f = f_{ps} \, A_{ps}$ (rectangular stress block).
- **Ultimate moment** is computed as $M_u = f_{ps} A_{ps} (d - 0.42 x_f)$.
- Always **iterate** between fps (from strain compat.) and xf (from equilibrium) until convergence.
- Always **clamp** fps between $f_{pe}$ and $0.87 \, f_{pu}$.
- The **lever arm 0.42 xf** is an empirical simplification; the rigorous value is 0.25 xf (centroid of 0.5·xf block).
- Apply **γf = 1.5** to service loads to get design (factored) moment.
- For **T-sections**, first assume NA in the flange and treat as rectangular; if xf > Df, redo the calculation with web width bw and account for flange overhang.
- The **failure mode check** ($x_f/d$ vs $x_{f,\text{bal}}/d$) is a **mandatory final step** in every KTU ULS problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preliminary Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Preliminary Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Preliminary Design** in Prestressed Concrete is the *first-pass, semi-empirical engineering phase* executed immediately after conceptual framing and load estimation, in which the structural engineer establishes a **defensible geometric envelope** (cross-section dimensions, web/bottom-flange thickness, span-to-depth ratio) and a **first-cut prestressing force** ($P_i$ initial / $P_e$ effective) *before* detailed serviceability and ultimate limit-state (SLS / ULS) checks are attempted. It is the **bridge** between architectural intent and rigorous analytical design.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic falls under the umbrella of **CO-3 (Apply preliminary sizing and prestress estimation procedures to PSC flexural members)** and is explicitly tested at the *Apply / Analyze* levels of Bloom's Revised Taxonomy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuitive View

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Strongback and the Tendon":**
> Imagine you are an archer (the *prestressing tendon*) pulling a heavy wooden longbow (the *concrete beam*) into a tensioned state and then locking yourself in. The bow is now *fighting* to straighten, but the string keeps it compressed. **Preliminary design is the moment the archer asks:**
> 1. *"How thick must my bow be so it doesn't snap when drawn?"* → Cross-section sizing
> 2. *"How hard must I pull so the arrow flies straight (no sag)?"* → Prestressing force estimation
> 3. *"Where along the bow should I anchor to get the best arc?"* → Cable profile selection
>
> The archer is **not** doing final ballistics — just **calibrating the rig**.

In real engineering terms, the preliminary design step exists because running a full ULS/SLS iterative design on an unknown cross-section is computationally and economically wasteful. We *guess intelligently*, *check quickly*, and *iterate* to convergence.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Preliminary PSC Design

| # | Pillar | Engineering Question Answered |
|---|--------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | **Cross-Section Selection** | What shape (Rectangular / I / T / Box) and what minimum depth ($D$) is needed? |
| 2 | **Prestressing Force Estimation** | How much jacking force ($P_j$) / effective force ($P_e$) is required to counter service loads? |
| 3 | **Cable Profile & Eccentricity** | What is the *trajectory* of tendons (parabolic / harped / straight) and the maximum permissible eccentricity ($e$)? |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industry Metric for Initial Sizing:**
> As per IS 1343 and KTU conventional practice, the **span-to-depth ratio** for simply supported PSC flexural members is bounded as:
> $$\frac{L}{D} \in \left[15,\ 25\right]$$
> where $L$ = effective span, $D$ = overall depth. The **lower bound (≈ 15)** is used for *heavy industrial/bridge girders*; the **upper bound (≈ 25)** is for *light roof/floor beams*.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Material Benchmarks (Bolded per KTU Norms)

- **Characteristic compressive strength of concrete ($f_{ck}$)** for PSC members: **40 N/mm² to 80 N/mm²** (commonly **M40, M50, M60**).
- **Grade of prestressing steel**: **Fe 415 / Fe 500 (strands)**, with ultimate tensile strength ($f_p$) reaching up to **1860 N/mm²** for low-relaxation 7-wire strands.
- **Density of PSC (assuming normal-weight aggregate)**: **24 kN/m³** (or **25 kN/m³** if heavily reinforced).
- **Modular ratio ($m$) for M40–M60**: **$E_s / E_c \approx 6.67$ to $5.88$** (using IS 1343:2008, $E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$).

---

## 1.5 Why Preliminary Design Matters — The Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** In consultancy and field practice, a poor preliminary guess cascades into:
> - **Over-design** → unnecessary dead load → deeper foundation → cost overrun (10–25 % of project value).
> - **Under-design** → serviceability cracks at SLS, deflection violations, costly retrofitting.
> - **Wrong shape** → constructability failure (e.g., narrow web causes concrete-pour congestion around ducts).
>
> Hence, the *preliminary design phase is where **~70 % of the lifecycle economy** of a PSC member is decided.*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logical Flow of Preliminary Design — Step-by-Step Philosophy

The operation can be decomposed into a **six-step cognitive chain** that maps directly to KTU 2024 scheme CO-3:

1. **Step A — Load Enumeration:** Compute the *factored* and *unfactored* design loads (Dead Load $DL$, Live Load $LL$, Superimposed Dead Load $SIDL$). For preliminary sizing, *unfactored service loads* are used.
2. **Step B — Span-to-Depth Calibration:** Pick an initial $L/D$ from IS 1343 Table to back-calculate $D_{assumed}$.
3. **Step C — Web/Flange Sizing:** For I/T sections, fix web width ($b_w$) and bottom flange breadth ($b_f$) using empirical proportions.
4. **Step D — Self-Weight Estimation:** Compute $g_{sw}$ (kN/m) using the *assumed* cross-section and unit weight of PSC.
5. **Step E — Prestress Force Estimation:** Use serviceability stress limits ($f_{ct,lim}$ and $f_{cc,lim}$) to compute the **required eccentricity-aligned prestress** $P_e$.
6. **Step F — Cable Profile Sketching:** Choose a *parabolic / trapezoidal / harped* tendon trajectory, ensuring $e_{max}$ does not exceed the *kern limits* of the cross-section.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Cross-Section Selection Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shape selection is governed by SPAN and LOAD MAGNITUDE:**
> - **Span < 15 m, light load** → *Rectangular* (simple casting, congested reinforcement).
> - **Span 15–30 m, moderate load** → *I-section* (efficient — removes dead weight from the tension side).
> - **Span > 30 m, heavy load (bridges)** → *Box-girder* (high torsional rigidity + large lever arm).
> - **Composite deck-slab** → *T-section* (uses the slab as compression flange).

### 2.2.2 Preliminary Depth from Span-to-Depth Ratio

For simply supported PSC beams, the **simplified preliminary depth** is:

$$D_{assumed} = \frac{L}{\left(L/D\right)_{assumed}}$$

**Typical assumed ratios (IS 1343 + KTU/PCA recommendations):**

| Member Type | $L/D$ Range | Remarks |
|-------------|-------------|---------|
| Roof/Floor PSC beam | 20 – 25 | Light superimposed load |
| Bridge girder (I) | 15 – 18 | Heavy live load, dynamic factor |
| Continuous PSC girder | 22 – 28 | Negative moment relief |
| Box girder (bridge) | 18 – 22 | High torsional demand |

### 2.2.3 I-Section Empirical Proportions

For an **I-section**, the following empirical proportions (based on Lin & Burns / IS 1343) are used:

$$\frac{b_f}{b_w} \in \left[2,\ 4\right]$$
$$\frac{D}{b_w} \in \left[8,\ 15\right]$$
$$\frac{D_f}{D} \in \left[0.18,\ 0.30\right] \quad \text{(flange depth)}$$

### 2.2.4 Prestress Force Estimation — The Serviceability Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Governing philosophy (IS 1343:2008, Cl. 19.3, and KTU 2024 Module-3 lecture notes):**
> At *service load*, the maximum tensile stress at the soffit ($f_{ct,min}$) and maximum compressive stress at the top fibre ($f_{cc,max}$) must lie within the IS code envelope. This gives **two simultaneous inequalities** that fix $P_e$ and $e$.

The governing pair of equations (for a beam with *tendon eccentricity $e$ at midspan*, section modulus $Z_t$ at top and $Z_b$ at bottom):

$$f_{top} = \frac{P_e}{A} + \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_t} - \frac{M_{sw}}{Z_t} - \frac{M_{L}}{Z_t} \leq f_{cc,lim}$$

$$f_{bot} = \frac{P_e}{A} - \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b} + \frac{M_{sw}}{Z_b} + \frac{M_{L}}{Z_b} \geq -f_{ct,lim}$$

These yield (after algebraic manipulation) a **closed-form expression for the required prestress**:

$$P_{e,req} = \frac{\phi \cdot M_{service}}{A \cdot e \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{Z_t} + \dfrac{1}{Z_b}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Z_t}{A}\right)}$$

A more practical form used by the KTU textbook is:

$$P_e = \frac{\phi \cdot M_{serv} \cdot A}{e \cdot A + \dfrac{Z_b \cdot Z_t}{Z_b + Z_t} \cdot \phi_{ratio}}$$

where $\phi$ is a *prestress loss factor* (typically **0.7 to 0.8** for bonded post-tensioned members; **0.8 to 0.85** for pre-tensioned).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-ready** — every entry is fair game for a KTU 14-mark or 3-mark question.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Use / Bounds | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------------|----------------------|-------|
| 1 | Span-to-depth | $D = L / (L/D)$ | $L/D \in [15, 25]$ | m, m |
| 2 | Self-weight (Rect.) | $g_{sw} = \rho_{PSC} \cdot b \cdot D$ | $\rho_{PSC} = 24$ kN/m³ | kN/m |
| 3 | Self-weight (I) | $g_{sw} = \rho_{PSC} \cdot \left(2 b_f D_f + b_w (D - 2 D_f)\right)$ | Empirical | kN/m |
| 4 | Service moment (midspan, SS beam) | $M_s = (g_{sw} + g_{SIDL} + q_{LL}) L^2 / 8$ | Unfactored | kN·m |
| 5 | Effective prestress | $P_e = \eta \cdot P_j$ | $\eta \in [0.7, 0.85]$ | kN |
| 6 | Jacking force (initial) | $P_j = P_e / \eta$ | $\eta$ = efficiency factor | kN |
| 7 | Minimum eccentricity (no tension limit) | $e_{min} = \dfrac{M_s / Z_b - P_e/A}{P_e / Z_b}$ | Kern boundary | m |
| 8 | Max eccentricity (kern) | $e_{max} = Z_b / A$ | Cannot exceed top kern | m |
| 9 | Pressure line eccentricity | $e_{pl} = M / P_e$ | For load-balancing | m |
| 10 | Balanced load (parabolic cable) | $w_{bal} = 8 P_e \cdot e_0 / L^2$ | Load-balancing concept | kN/m |
| 11 | UDL equivalent from cable | $q_{eq} = 8 P_e \cdot \Delta e / L^2$ | For trapezoidal profile | kN/m |
| 12 | Section modulus ratio | $Z_b / Z_t$ | For unsymmetric sections | — |
| 13 | Stress at top fibre | $f_t = P_e/A + P_e e/Z_t - M_t/Z_t$ | Service stage | N/mm² |
| 14 | Stress at bottom fibre | $f_b = P_e/A - P_e e/Z_b + M_b/Z_b$ | Service stage | N/mm² |
| 15 | Allowable tension (no cracking) | $f_{ct} \leq 0$ (Class 1) or $f_{ct,allow}$ (Class 2/3) | IS 1343 Cl. 19.4 | N/mm² |
| 16 | Modulus of elasticity (concrete) | $E_c = 5000 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | IS 1343 | N/mm² |
| 17 | Initial prestress | $f_{pi} = 0.85 f_p$ (strands) or $0.80 f_p$ (wires) | IS 1343 | N/mm² |
| 18 | Loss of prestress (long-term) | $\Delta f = $ creep + shrinkage + relaxation | ≈ 15–25 % of $P_i$ | N/mm² |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** When computing $P_e$ from $P_j$, **always** remember to subtract the *anchorage slip loss*, *elastic shortening*, *creep, shrinkage, and relaxation* — students routinely use $P_j \approx P_e$ and lose 2–3 marks.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **preliminary design step** is the *technical handshake* between the architect's spatial intent and the structural analyst's numerical machinery. Without it:

- **BIM integration** fails (parametric models require seed dimensions).
- **Tender-stage BoQ (Bill of Quantities)** is impossible (no concrete volume, no steel tonnage).
- **Formwork and shoring design** cannot begin.
- **Construction sequencing** loses its anchor.

In **bridge construction**, the preliminary sizing of a box girder dictates the entire *launching truss capacity*; in **high-rise transfer beams**, the preliminary depth determines whether the *post-tensioning subcontractor* can fit the anchorages and trumpet bells within the available column footprint.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Required Prestress Force at Service Stage

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **14-mark favourite** in KTU ESE. We derive the closed-form expression for $P_e$ that simultaneously satisfies the top-fibre compression limit and bottom-fibre tension limit.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Elastic Stress Equations

For a simply supported PSC beam, at the *critical midspan section*, the combined stress at the **top fibre** is:

$$f_{top} = \underbrace{\frac{P_e}{A}}_{\text{axial comp.}} + \underbrace{\frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_t}}_{\text{eccentric comp.}} - \underbrace{\frac{M_{sw}}{Z_t}}_{\text{DL bending}} - \underbrace{\frac{M_{SIDL+LL}}{Z_t}}_{\text{SDL/LL bending}}$$

The combined stress at the **bottom fibre** is:

$$f_{bot} = \underbrace{\frac{P_e}{A}}_{\text{axial comp.}} - \underbrace{\frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b}}_{\text{eccentric uplift}} + \underbrace{\frac{M_{sw}}{Z_b}}_{\text{DL bending}} + \underbrace{\frac{M_{SIDL+LL}}{Z_b}}_{\text{SDL/LL bending}}$$

### 3.1.2 Code Limit Conditions (IS 1343:2008, Cl. 19.4)

For a **Class-1** (no-tension) member:

$$f_{top} \leq f_{cc,lim} = 0.45 f_{ck}$$

$$f_{bot} \geq 0 \quad \text{(no tension)}$$

### 3.1.3 Solving Simultaneously for $P_e$

The **minimum prestress** is dictated by the *bottom fibre no-tension condition*. Setting $f_{bot} = 0$:

$$\frac{P_e}{A} - \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b} + \frac{M_{sw} + M_{SIDL} + M_{LL}}{Z_b} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{P_e}{A} - \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b} = -\frac{M_{serv}}{Z_b}$$

$$P_e \left(\frac{1}{A} - \frac{e}{Z_b}\right) = -\frac{M_{serv}}{Z_b}$$

$$P_e \left(\frac{Z_b - eA}{A \cdot Z_b}\right) = -\frac{M_{serv}}{Z_b}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{e,min} = \frac{M_{serv}}{A \cdot e - Z_b}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the sign convention:** In Indian code practice, *tension is positive* for $f_{ct}$ and $f_{bot}$. The above is the **minimum** $P_e$ that **just eliminates tension** at the soffit.

### 3.1.4 Checking the Top Fibre Limit

Having obtained $P_{e,min}$, substitute back into the *top fibre* expression:

$$f_{top} = \frac{P_{e,min}}{A} + \frac{P_{e,min} \cdot e}{Z_t} - \frac{M_{serv}}{Z_t}$$

If $f_{top} \leq 0.45 f_{ck}$, **the design is acceptable**; if not, *redesign* the section (increase $A$, $Z_t$, or reduce $e$).

### 3.1.5 Maximum Eccentricity from the Top-Kern Constraint

The *maximum allowable eccentricity* is the *top-kern distance*:

$$e_{max} = \frac{Z_t}{A}$$

because at $e = Z_t/A$, the stress at the top fibre becomes zero (uplift dominates). To maintain **compression** at the top under service loads, we typically limit:

$$e \leq 0.85 \cdot \frac{Z_t}{A} \quad \text{(safe upper bound)}$$

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Balanced Load from a Parabolic Cable

This is the **load-balancing** derivation, used in KTU 14-mark numerical problems for beam-type members.

### 3.2.1 Cable Geometry

For a parabolic tendon with **end eccentricities $e_1$ and $e_2$** at supports and **maximum eccentricity $e_0$** at midspan, the cable profile is:

$$y(x) = e_1 + (e_0 - e_1) \cdot \left(1 - \frac{4x^2}{L^2}\right) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq L/2$$

(symmetric about midspan, by similar formula on the other half).

### 3.2.2 Vertical Component of Cable Force

Differentiating twice to obtain the equivalent **upward distributed load** that the parabolic cable imparts to the concrete:

$$\frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\frac{8(e_0 - e_1)}{L^2} \quad \text{(constant, indicating uniform load)}$$

The transverse force per unit length produced by prestress is:

$$w_{bal}(x) = P_e \cdot \frac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\frac{8 P_e (e_0 - e_1)}{L^2}$$

The **magnitude** (acting upward on concrete, opposing gravity):

$$w_{bal} = \frac{8 P_e (e_0 - e_1)}{L^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention note:** The *negative* second derivative gives an *upward* force on the concrete. This upward force **balances** the downward self-weight and imposed loads — hence the term *load balancing*, attributed to T.Y. Lin.

### 3.2.3 Special Case — Symmetric Parabolic Cable ($e_1 = e_2$)

If the cable enters and exits at the *same eccentricity* (commonly $e_1 = 0$ at simple supports, $e_0$ at midspan):

$$w_{bal} = \frac{8 P_e \cdot e_0}{L^2}$$

This is **Equation 3.1** of the *Preliminary Design* sub-module.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solving a Sample KTU 14-Mark Numerical

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked-Example Skeleton (MUST be practiced):**
> A simply supported PSC beam of effective span **$L = 20$ m**, carries a **live load = 15 kN/m** and a **superimposed DL = 10 kN/m**. Concrete **M40**, $f_{ck} = 40$ N/mm². Class-1 (no tension). Assume $L/D = 20$, $Z_b = Z_t = Z$ (symmetric), and $\eta = 0.80$.

#### Step 1 — Pick the depth
$$D = L / 20 = 20 / 20 = 1.0 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 2 — Assume a rectangular section width (using $D/b \approx 2$ for PSC)
$$b = D / 2 = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute self-weight
$$g_{sw} = \rho_{PSC} \cdot b \cdot D = 24 \times 0.5 \times 1.0 = 12 \text{ kN/m}$$

#### Step 4 — Total service load
$$w_{total} = g_{sw} + g_{SIDL} + q_{LL} = 12 + 10 + 15 = 37 \text{ kN/m}$$

#### Step 5 — Service moment at midspan
$$M_{serv} = \frac{w_{total} L^2}{8} = \frac{37 \times 20^2}{8} = \frac{37 \times 400}{8} = 1850 \text{ kN·m} = 1.85 \times 10^9 \text{ N·mm}$$

#### Step 6 — Section properties (rectangle)
$$A = b \cdot D = 500 \times 1000 = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$Z = \frac{b D^2}{6} = \frac{500 \times 1000^2}{6} = 8.33 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3$$

#### Step 7 — Required eccentricity
Adopt the *maximum practical* eccentricity: cover = 75 mm, half duct diameter = 50 mm → centre of duct at 125 mm from bottom.
$$e_{adopted} = D/2 - 125 = 500 - 125 = 375 \text{ mm}$$

#### Step 8 — Compute $P_e$
$$P_{e,min} = \frac{M_{serv}}{A \cdot e - Z_b} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^5 \times 375 - 8.33 \times 10^7}$$

Compute denominator:
$$A \cdot e = 5 \times 10^5 \times 375 = 1.875 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$A \cdot e - Z_b = 1.875 \times 10^8 - 8.33 \times 10^7 = 1.042 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3$$

Therefore:
$$P_{e,min} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^9}{1.042 \times 10^8} = 17.75 \text{ kN} \quad \text{...WAIT, this is too low}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Re-check — sign convention error.** Using the corrected formula:
> $$P_{e,min} = \frac{M_{serv} \cdot A}{Z_b - eA} \quad \text{(re-derived for tension-positive convention)}$$

Re-computing:
$$Z_b - eA = 8.33 \times 10^7 - 1.875 \times 10^8 = -1.042 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3$$

So we need to **re-frame the sign**: in Indian convention, *compression at top is positive*:

$$\frac{P_e}{A} + \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_t} - \frac{M_{serv}}{Z_t} = 0.45 f_{ck} = 18 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \text{(top fibre)}$$

And the *bottom no-tension condition*:
$$\frac{P_e}{A} - \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b} + \frac{M_{serv}}{Z_b} = 0$$

Solving simultaneously (with $Z_b = Z_t = Z$ for symmetric section):

$$P_e \left(\frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z}\right) = \frac{M_{serv}}{Z} \quad \text{(from bot)}$$

$$P_e = \frac{M_{serv}/Z}{\frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z}} = \frac{M_{serv} \cdot A}{Z + e \cdot A}$$

Substituting values:
$$Z + eA = 8.33 \times 10^7 + 1.875 \times 10^8 = 2.708 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$P_e = \frac{1.85 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^5}{2.708 \times 10^8} \times 10^{-3} \text{ kN}$$

Let me redo cleanly:
$$P_e = \frac{M_{serv} \cdot A}{Z + eA} = \frac{1.85 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^5}{2.708 \times 10^8}$$

Compute numerator: $1.85 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^5 = 9.25 \times 10^{14}$ mm⁴

$$P_e = \frac{9.25 \times 10^{14}}{2.708 \times 10^8} = 3.416 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 3416 \text{ kN}$$

#### Step 9 — Required jacking force
$$P_j = P_e / \eta = 3416 / 0.80 = 4270 \text{ kN}$$

#### Step 10 — Number of strands required
For 12.7 mm dia. 7-wire strand with $f_p = 1860$ N/mm² and $A_{strand} = 98.7$ mm²:
$$P_{j,strand} = 0.85 \times 1860 \times 98.7 = 156,015 \text{ N} = 156 \text{ kN/strand}$$
$$N_{strands} = P_j / P_{j,strand} = 4270 / 156 = 27.4 \rightarrow \text{use 28 strands}$$

#### Step 11 — Verify top fibre stress
$$f_{top} = \frac{P_e}{A} + \frac{P_e e}{Z} - \frac{M_{serv}}{Z}$$

$$f_{top} = \frac{3.416 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^5} + \frac{3.416 \times 10^6 \times 375}{8.33 \times 10^7} - \frac{1.85 \times 10^9}{8.33 \times 10^7}$$

$$f_{top} = 6.83 + 15.38 - 22.21 = 0.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Hmm, that is **not the limit**. The first inequality in the bot gave us the *lower bound*; the top limit gives us a *compatibility check*. Let me re-state the design logic:

> [!NOTE]
> **Design-iteration note:** The actual preliminary design procedure is **iterative**: assume $D$, compute $P_e$ from *bottom no-tension* equation, *check* top stress against $0.45 f_{ck}$. If top stress is **excessive** → *increase $D$* and repeat. If $P_e$ is **excessive** → *increase number of cables* or *use a larger cable eccentricity*. This is what makes preliminary design a **convergent, not closed-form**, exercise.

For this worked example, the **27–28 strand result** with a $500 \times 1000$ mm section is the **preliminary design output**. The student is expected to communicate: cross-section ($500 \times 1000$ mm), $P_j \approx 4270$ kN, $n = 28$ strands of 12.7 mm dia, parabolic cable profile.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Preliminary Design Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Project Inputs L, w_LL, f_ck, f_p] --> B0[Step 1: Architectural & Loading Brief]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2: Choose Cross-Section Type Rect, I, T, Box]
    C0 --> D0[Step 3: Pick L/D Ratio 15 to 25]
    D0 --> E0[Step 4: Compute D_assumed = L / L_D]
    E0 --> F0[Step 5: Pick Width b from D_b = 1.5 to 2.5]
    F0 --> G0[Step 6: Compute Self-Weight g_sw]
    G0 --> H0[Step 7: Compute M_serv at midspan]
    H0 --> I0[Step 8: Estimate Cable Eccentricity e from cover & duct]
    I0 --> J0[Step 9: Compute Required P_e from no-tension equation]
    J0 --> K0{Step 10: Check Top Fibre Stress}
    K0 -- Exceeds 0.45 f_ck --> L0[Increase D and Restart]
    L0 --> D0
    K0 -- Within Limit --> M0[Step 11: Compute P_j = P_e / eta]
    M0 --> N0[Step 12: Select Strand Size and Compute n_strands]
    N0 --> O0[Step 13: Choose Cable Profile Parabolic or Harped]
    O0 --> P0[Step 14: Sketch Tendon Trajectory and Anchorages]
    P0 --> Q0[Step 15: Output Preliminary Design Report]
    Q0 --> R0[Step 16: Proceed to Detailed SLS and ULS Design]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The loop **Step 10 → Step 4** is the **iterative kernel** of preliminary design. In KTU ESE, students are expected to **acknowledge** this iteration explicitly, even if only one cycle is shown.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Cable Profile Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Straight Cable] --> A2[Used for axial prestress e equals 0]
    B1[Parabolic Cable] --> B2[Used for simply supported beams balanced load concept]
    C1[Trapezoidal Cable] --> C2[Used for continuous beams draping over supports]
    D1[Harped or Deflected Cable] --> D2[Used for short heavy loads sharp angle at hold-down]
    E1[Curved or Segmented Cable] --> E2[Used for bridge decks variable eccentricity]
    A2 --> F1[Output: Preliminary Cable Profile]
    B2 --> F1
    C2 --> F1
    D2 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Verify Eccentricity lies within Kern Zone]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Service Stress Envelope (Kern Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Top Fibre Stress f_t] --> H1{Is f_t within 0.45 f_ck?}
    H1 -- Yes --> H2[OK - Top fibre OK]
    H1 -- No --> H3[NOT OK - Increase D or Reduce e]
    J0[Bottom Fibre Stress f_b] --> J1{Is f_b greater than or equal to 0 for Class 1?}
    J1 -- Yes --> J2[OK - No tension at soffit]
    J1 -- No --> J3[NOT OK - Increase P_e or Increase e]
    H2 --> K0[Preliminary Design Complete]
    H3 --> L0[Iterate Step 3 to Step 10]
    J2 --> K0
    J3 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[Restart Iteration Loop]
    M0 --> D0
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic — Pressure Line (Thrust Line) Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024, examiners appreciate the **pressure-line / thrust-line** concept for advanced preliminary design. The pressure line is the *trajectory of the resultant force* ($C$ at top, $T$ at bottom) under combined prestress + external loads.

```
              ←————— L —————→
   .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .
   .  P (cable resultant)  .   .
   .     ↑ eccentricity e   .  .
   .  ┌──────────────────┐ .  .
   .  │   PSC BEAM       │ .  .
   .  │   ▓▓▓▓▓ (concrete)│ .  .
   .  │  ▒▒▒▒▒ (tendons) │ .  .
   .  └──────────────────┘ .  .
   .   ↓ resultant C at top   .
   .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .  .
            ↑ applied load
```

**Pressure line at midspan** (centroid of compression force): $C = T = P_e$, located at eccentricity $e$ from the centroid of the section. The pressure line **must** stay inside the **kern zone** at every cross-section for a *Class-1* member.

---

## 4.5 Schematic — Iterative Design Convergence Plot (Conceptual)

```
   P_e required (kN)
        │
   5000 ┤              ●────────────────●  ← upper bound (top stress)
        │            ╱
   4000 ┤          ●        ← solution (P_e, D)
        │        ╱
   3000 ┤      ●
        │    ╱  
   2000 ┤  ●─────●────────────  ← lower bound (no tension at soffit)
        │
   1000 ┤●
        │
      0 └─┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──┬──── D (mm)
        600 700 800 900 1000 1100

       → As D increases, the gap between upper and lower P_e bounds narrows.
         The acceptable design is the overlap zone.
```

> [!NOTE]
> This *overlap-zone* chart is a **conceptual tool** — in KTU exams, students may be asked to **sketch** this logic or **explain** why deeper sections reduce the required prestress.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are KTU Part A questions — direct conceptual recall with a one-paragraph precise model answer.

### **Q1. Define 'Preliminary Design' of a prestressed concrete flexural member. List any three design parameters fixed at this stage.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*Preliminary design is the initial sizing and prestress estimation phase of a PSC member, executed after load estimation and before detailed SLS/ULS verification. It establishes a first-cut geometric envelope and a first-cut effective prestress, which are then refined iteratively.*

*Three parameters fixed at this stage:*
1. *Cross-section type and overall depth $D$ (from $L/D$ ratio).*
2. *Web width $b_w$ and flange dimensions (for I/T).*
3. *Magnitude of effective prestress $P_e$ and number of prestressing strands $N$.*

---

### **Q2. What is the 'span-to-depth ratio' criterion used in PSC preliminary design? State the typical range for simply supported and continuous PSC beams.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

*The span-to-depth ratio ($L/D$) is an empirical preliminary-design parameter that prescribes the overall depth of a flexural member based on its effective span. It is derived from historical practice and code guidance to ensure adequate stiffness against serviceability deflection.*

*Typical ranges (IS 1343:2008 + KTU convention):*
- *Simply supported PSC beam: $L/D \in [15, 20]$*
- *Continuous PSC beam: $L/D \in [22, 28]$*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark ESE Question (Module 3 Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Part B questions on Module 3 carry 14 marks with internal choice. Below are TWO independent alternatives. Each part (a) and (b) is 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q.A.** A simply supported prestressed concrete bridge girder has the following data:
- Effective span: $L = 24$ m
- Live load (unfactored): $w_{LL} = 25$ kN/m (excluding impact)
- Superimposed dead load: $w_{SIDL} = 12$ kN/m
- Concrete grade: **M50** ($f_{ck} = 50$ N/mm²)
- Prestressing steel: 12.7 mm dia. low-relaxation 7-wire strand, $f_p = 1860$ N/mm²
- Prestress efficiency factor: $\eta = 0.80$
- Section assumed: **I-section** with $b_f = 1200$ mm, $D_f = 200$ mm, $b_w = 300$ mm, $D = 1800$ mm
- Cover to duct centre = 175 mm (from soffit)
- Class of member: **Class 1** (no tension at service).

#### **Part (a)** — *(7 marks)*
Compute the **effective prestress $P_e$** required at the *midspan critical section* such that the bottom fibre is just in zero tension. Verify the *top fibre* stress against the IS 1343 limit of $0.45 f_{ck}$.

#### **Part (b)** — *(7 marks)*
Determine the **number of strands** required, the **jacking force $P_j$**, and the **balanced load** assuming a parabolic cable with $e_1 = 0$ at supports and $e_0 = e_{adopted}$ at midspan. Comment on whether the *balanced load* is less than the *total service load*.

---

### **Solution to Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Self-weight of the I-section:**
$$g_{sw} = \rho_{PSC} \times \left[2 b_f D_f + b_w (D - 2 D_f)\right]$$
$$g_{sw} = 24 \times \left[2 \times 1.2 \times 0.2 + 0.3 \times (1.8 - 0.4)\right]$$
$$g_{sw} = 24 \times \left[0.48 + 0.42\right] = 24 \times 0.90 = 21.6 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2 — Total service load and moment:**
$$w_{serv} = 21.6 + 12 + 25 = 58.6 \text{ kN/m}$$
$$M_{serv} = \frac{w_{serv} L^2}{8} = \frac{58.6 \times 24^2}{8} = \frac{58.6 \times 576}{8} = 4219.2 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Section properties of the I-section:**

Area:
$$A = 2 (b_f D_f) + b_w (D - 2 D_f) = 2(1200 \times 200) + 300 \times 1400 = 480{,}000 + 420{,}000 = 900{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 9.0 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Moment of inertia (parallel-axis):
$$I = \frac{b_f D^3}{12} - \frac{(b_f - b_w)(D - 2 D_f)^3}{12}$$
$$I = \frac{1200 \times 1800^3}{12} - \frac{(1200 - 300) \times 1400^3}{12}$$
$$I = \frac{1200 \times 5.832 \times 10^9}{12} - \frac{900 \times 2.744 \times 10^9}{12}$$
$$I = 5.832 \times 10^{11} - 2.058 \times 10^{11} = 3.774 \times 10^{11} \text{ mm}^4$$

Section modulus (symmetric): $Z = I / (D/2) = 3.774 \times 10^{11} / 900 = 4.193 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^3$

**Step 4 — Adopted eccentricity:**
$$e_{adopted} = (D/2) - 175 = 900 - 175 = 725 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Required $P_e$ (Class-1, no tension at soffit):**

Using the formula derived in Section 3.1.3:
$$P_e = \frac{M_{serv} \cdot A}{Z + e \cdot A} = \frac{4.2192 \times 10^9 \times 9.0 \times 10^5}{4.193 \times 10^8 + 725 \times 9.0 \times 10^5}$$

Numerator: $4.2192 \times 10^9 \times 9.0 \times 10^5 = 3.797 \times 10^{15}$ N·mm²

Denominator: $4.193 \times 10^8 + 6.525 \times 10^8 = 1.0718 \times 10^9$ mm³

$$P_e = \frac{3.797 \times 10^{15}}{1.0718 \times 10^9} = 3.543 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 3543 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of top fibre stress:**

$$f_{top} = \frac{P_e}{A} + \frac{P_e \cdot e}{Z} - \frac{M_{serv}}{Z}$$

$$f_{top} = \frac{3.543 \times 10^6}{9.0 \times 10^5} + \frac{3.543 \times 10^6 \times 725}{4.193 \times 10^8} - \frac{4.2192 \times 10^9}{4.193 \times 10^8}$$

$$f_{top} = 3.937 + 6.124 - 10.064 = -0.003 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Allowable limit: $0.45 f_{ck} = 0.45 \times 50 = 22.5$ N/mm²

Since $f_{top} \approx 0 \leq 22.5$, the **design is acceptable** with substantial reserve.

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Jacking force:**
$$P_j = \frac{P_e}{\eta} = \frac{3543}{0.80} = 4428.75 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Capacity of one 12.7 mm strand:**
$$A_{strand} = 98.7 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$P_{j,strand} = 0.85 \times f_p \times A_{strand} = 0.85 \times 1860 \times 98.7 = 156{,}015 \text{ N} = 156.0 \text{ kN/strand}$$

**Step 3 — Number of strands:**
$$n = \frac{P_j}{P_{j,strand}} = \frac{4428.75}{156.0} = 28.4 \rightarrow \text{adopt 28 strands}$$

Adopt a **practical layout** of 4 cables × 7 strands each, in **two ducts per flange row**.

**Step 4 — Balanced load from parabolic cable ($e_1 = 0$):**

$$w_{bal} = \frac{8 P_e \cdot e_0}{L^2} = \frac{8 \times 3543 \times 0.725}{24^2} = \frac{20{,}549}{576} = 35.68 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with total service load:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| $w_{serv}$ (total) | 58.6 kN/m |
| $w_{bal}$ (parabolic) | 35.68 kN/m |
| $w_{net}$ (residual) | 58.6 - 35.68 = 22.92 kN/m |

> **Comment:** The balanced load (35.68 kN/m) is **less than** the total service load (58.6 kN/m). This means the prestressing does **not** fully balance the applied loads; the *residual load* of ≈ 22.92 kN/m will produce additional bending stress that must be checked in the *detailed SLS design phase*. The preliminary design is therefore **acceptable as a first pass**, and the residual effects are accounted for in the next design stage (curvature, deflection, and stress check).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q.B.** A post-tensioned prestressed concrete floor beam has the following data:
- Span $L = 16$ m (simply supported)
- Superimposed dead load = 8 kN/m
- Live load = 14 kN/m
- Concrete grade: **M45** ($f_{ck} = 45$ N/mm²)
- 15.2 mm dia. 7-wire strand, $f_p = 1860$ N/mm²
- Section: rectangular, $b = 350$ mm, $D = 800$ mm
- Duct diameter = 75 mm, cover = 60 mm to duct centre
- Class of member: **Class 2** (tension allowed up to $0.3\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ at soffit).

#### **Part (a)** — *(7 marks)*
Estimate the **span-to-depth ratio** for the assumed depth. State whether the assumed depth is adequate. Compute the **self-weight** and the **service moment**.

#### **Part (b)** — *(7 marks)*
Using the *load-balancing approach*, determine the **effective prestress $P_e$** if the parabolic cable provides a balanced load of **70 %** of the total service load. Compute the required number of strands and the **jacking force $P_j$** (assume $\eta = 0.82$).

---

### **Solution to Question B**

#### **Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Span-to-depth check:**
$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{16{,}000}{800} = 20$$

This is **within** the KTU/IS 1343 recommended range of 15–25. Hence the assumed depth of 800 mm is **adequate**.

**Step 2 — Self-weight of rectangular beam:**
$$g_{sw} = \rho_{PSC} \cdot b \cdot D = 24 \times 0.35 \times 0.8 = 6.72 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 3 — Total service load:**
$$w_{serv} = 6.72 + 8 + 14 = 28.72 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 4 — Service moment at midspan:**
$$M_{serv} = \frac{w_{serv} L^2}{8} = \frac{28.72 \times 16^2}{8} = \frac{28.72 \times 256}{8} = 919.04 \text{ kN·m}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Balanced load magnitude (70 % of $w_{serv}$):**
$$w_{bal} = 0.70 \times 28.72 = 20.10 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2 — Cable geometry (parabolic, $e_1 = 0$):**
$$w_{bal} = \frac{8 P_e \cdot e_0}{L^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_e \cdot e_0 = \frac{w_{bal} \cdot L^2}{8}$$

$$P_e \cdot e_0 = \frac{20.10 \times 16^2}{8} = \frac{20.10 \times 256}{8} = 643.2 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Adopted eccentricity:**
From the cover and duct dimensions:
$$e_{adopted} = D/2 - \text{cover} - \text{duct radius} = 400 - 60 - 37.5 = 302.5 \text{ mm} = 0.3025 \text{ m}$$

> Note: We also need to **check that this eccentricity is within the kern**:
> $e_{max} = Z_t / A = (bD^2/6) / (bD) = D/6 = 800/6 = 133.3$ mm
>
> ❌ $e_{adopted} = 302.5 \text{ mm} > 133.3 \text{ mm}$ — **EXCEEDS KERN LIMIT**

**Step 4 — Design correction (Kern-limited eccentricity):**
Adopt $e_{0,adopted} = 0.85 \times (D/6) = 113.3$ mm = **0.113 m** (safe).

**Step 5 — Recompute $P_e$ with corrected eccentricity:**
$$P_e = \frac{643.2 \text{ kN·m}}{0.113 \text{ m}} = 5692 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Jacking force:**
$$P_j = \frac{P_e}{\eta} = \frac{5692}{0.82} = 6942 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7 — Number of 15.2 mm strands:**
$$A_{strand, 15.2} = 140 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$P_{j,strand} = 0.85 \times 1860 \times 140 = 221{,}340 \text{ N} = 221.34 \text{ kN/strand}$$
$$n = \frac{6942}{221.34} = 31.36 \rightarrow \text{adopt 32 strands}$$

**Step 8 — Practical layout:**
Use **4 ducts × 8 strands** each, or **2 ducts × 16 strands** each — confirmed with the contractor's anchorage capacity and duct spacing constraints.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MARK-LOSS ZONES in Preliminary Design Problems (KTU 2024 scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add SELF-WEIGHT to the imposed load** before computing $M_{serv}$. Students compute $M = w_{LL} L^2/8$ and miss $g_{sw} L^2/8$ — this underestimates $M_{serv}$ by 15–25 %.
> 2. **Using $P_j$ instead of $P_e$** in the service-stress equations. Recall: **service stage uses $P_e$**, *not* $P_j$.
> 3. **Exceeding the kern limit** with adopted eccentricity $e$. The cover + duct radius sum must always be $\leq D/2 - Z_t/A$.
> 4. **Mixing the $L/D$ range** for different member types (e.g., using $L/D = 25$ for a bridge girder — wrong; bridges use 15–18).
> 5. **Forgetting to convert units** between N/mm², kN, kN·m, and mm — a single unit slip loses 1–2 marks silently.
>
> **Pro-tip:** In every step of your solution, *box the final numerical answer* and *write the units*. KTU examiners reward unit-aware answers with full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam:**

- [x] **Preliminary design = first-cut sizing + first-cut prestress**, iterated until convergence.
- [x] **Span-to-depth ratio** $L/D$: 15–25 (PSC flexure); choose lower for bridges, higher for light floors.
- [x] **Section shape** = function of span + load: Rect (<15 m), I (15–30 m), Box (>30 m), T (composite).
- [x] **I-section empirical proportions**: $b_f/b_w \in [2,4]$; $D/b_w \in [8,15]$; $D_f/D \in [0.18, 0.30]$.
- [x] **Self-weight unit weight** of PSC: **24 kN/m³** (normal-weight); **25 kN/m³** (heavily reinforced).
- [x] **Service moment formula** (SS beam): $M_{serv} = (g_{sw} + g_{SIDL} + q_{LL}) L^2 / 8$.
- [x] **Governing service stress equations** (top and bottom fibres) — *memorize the pair*.
- [x] **No-tension (Class-1) design** gives: $P_e = M_{serv} \cdot A / (Z + eA)$ for symmetric sections.
- [x] **Eccentricity** is bounded by the *kern limit* $e_{max} = Z_t / A$. Adopt $e \leq 0.85 \, e_{max}$.
- [x] **Parabolic cable balanced load**: $w_{bal} = 8 P_e \cdot e_0 / L^2$ (when $e_1 = 0$).
- [x] **Load-balancing philosophy** (T.Y. Lin): choose $P_e$ and $e_0$ to *balance* 60–80 % of the service load.
- [x] **Jacking force**: $P_j = P_e / \eta$, with $\eta \in [0.70, 0.85]$.
- [x] **Strand capacity** (12.7 mm): $P_{j,strand} \approx 156$ kN; (15.2 mm): $P_{j,strand} \approx 221$ kN.
- [x] **Modular ratio** $E_s / E_c$: use **$E_c = 5000\sqrt{f_{ck}}$** (IS 1343) and **$E_s = 2.0 \times 10^5$ N/mm²** for steel.
- [x] **Allowable stresses** (Class 1): $f_{top} \leq 0.45 f_{ck}$, $f_{bot} \geq 0$ (no tension).
- [x] **Iteration is mandatory** — a single pass almost never gives a final design. Loop until top and bottom stress limits are *simultaneously* satisfied.
- [x] **Code reference**: IS 1343:2008, Cl. 19 (Serviceability) and Cl. 20 (Ultimate Limit State).

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 — PRELIMINARY DESIGN NOTES**
> *Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol. Aligned to PECET525, 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcome-based pedagogy.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Final Design for Type 1 Members. Design for Shear: Analysis for Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Final Design for Type 1 Members. Design for Shear: Analysis for Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Type 1 Prestressed Concrete Members — Formal Definition

As per **IS 1343:2012** (the Indian Standard Code of Practice for Prestressed Concrete), members are classified into three types based on the permissible level of tensile stress in the concrete at service conditions (i.e., after all prestress losses have occurred, under the working/service load):

| Member Type | Tensile Stress Condition at Service Load |
|:---:|:---|
| **Type 1** | **No tensile stress** is permitted anywhere in the member |
| Type 2 | Tensile stress permitted, but no visible cracking |
| Type 3 | Tensile stress permitted, with limited cracking (crack width ≤ 0.2 mm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 3:** "Final Design for Type 1 Members" explicitly means the design must be carried out ensuring that the **maximum tensile stress in the extreme fibre at any stage is zero (or compressive)**. This is the *most conservative* design approach, used for structures exposed to aggressive environments, water-retaining structures, bridges, and railway sleepers.

## 1.2 Design for Shear — Conceptual Overview

In a **prestressed concrete (PSC) beam**, the application of an eccentric prestressing force creates a longitudinal compressive stress ($\sigma_{cx}$) along the centroidal axis. When the beam is subjected to transverse (shear) loading, two stresses coexist at a point in the web:

1. **Longitudinal stress** ($\sigma_{cx}$) due to prestress + bending
2. **Transverse shear stress** ($\tau$) due to the vertical shear force $V$

The combination of these two produces **principal stresses** (one tensile, one compressive) acting on inclined planes. In reinforced concrete, we are worried about diagonal tension. In **PSC, the pre-compression from prestress directly counteracts the diagonal tension**, which is the *single biggest advantage* of prestressing in shear design.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Squeezing Effect"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (The Book on a Shelf):** Imagine trying to slide a heavy book off a shelf by pushing it horizontally. If the shelf is dry, the book slips easily. Now imagine the same shelf, but someone is pressing the book down firmly from above (like prestress). The horizontal force needed to slide the book increases dramatically. The vertical "squeeze" (prestress) resists the horizontal "slide" (shear-induced diagonal tension). This is *exactly* how prestressing improves shear capacity — by introducing a beneficial longitudinal compression that suppresses the diagonal tensile cracks.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Modulus of Elasticity of Concrete ($E_c$)** = **5000 √f_ck MPa** (as per IS 1343)
- **Design Compressive Strength ($f_{ck}$)** = characteristic cube strength at 28 days (typically **40 MPa, 50 MPa, or 60 MPa** for PSC)
- **Tensile Strength of Concrete ($f_t$)** = **0.7 √f_ck MPa** (IS 1343)
- **Permissible Principal Tensile Stress (Type 1)** = **0 MPa** (no tension at all)
- **Modular Ratio ($m$)** = **280 / (3 σ_cbc)** or **E_s / E_c** as per IS 1343

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Stress in a PSC Beam (Critical for Type 1 Design)

A PSC member passes through three critical stages. **For Type 1 design, no tensile stress is permitted at any stage.**

| Stage | Description | Bending Stress | Prestress Stress | Net Extreme Fibre Stress |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Stage I** | Initial (transfer) | $P_0/A$ (compressive) | $-P_0 e / Z_{top}$ (compression at top fibre for sagging) | $f_{top} = P_0/A + P_0 e / Z_{top}$ |
| **Stage II** | Service (after all losses) | $P/A$ (reduced) | $-P e / Z_{top}$ | Net stress under self-weight + DL + LL |
| **Stage III** | Ultimate (collapse) | — | — | Used for flexural ultimate strength |

## 2.2 Shear Stress Distribution in PSC Beams

For a simply-supported PSC beam, the **transverse shear stress** at any fibre is given by the classical elastic flexure formula:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Transverse shear force at the section
- $Q$ = First moment of area of the region beyond the fibre about the neutral axis
- $I$ = Moment of inertia of the gross section
- $b$ = Width of the section at the fibre level

The shear stress is **maximum at the neutral axis** and **zero at the extreme fibres**. For a rectangular section, the maximum shear stress at the neutral axis is:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bh} = 1.5 \cdot \frac{V}{A_{web}}$$

## 2.3 Principal Stresses in a PSC Beam (The Heart of Shear Analysis)

At any point in the web, the **state of stress** is:
- Longitudinal (axial) stress: $\sigma_x = -\sigma_{cx}$ (compressive due to prestress + bending)
- Transverse stress: $\sigma_y = 0$ (no vertical normal stress in web)
- Shear stress: $\tau_{xy} = \tau$ (from flexural shear)

The **principal stresses** (maximum and minimum normal stresses on mutually perpendicular planes where shear is zero) are obtained from the standard 2D stress transformation:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

With $\sigma_y = 0$, this simplifies to:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_1$ = **Maximum principal (compressive) stress** (taking the $+$ sign when $\sigma_x$ is negative/compressive)
- $\sigma_2$ = **Minimum principal (tensile) stress** (the diagonal tension we are worried about)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Diagonal Tension Formula (most-used equation in KTU exams):**
> $$\boxed{\sigma_{pt} = -\left[\frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}\right]}$$
> The *negative* sign indicates tensile (if we follow the convention where compression is positive in structural analysis). The absolute magnitude of principal tension is the term inside the brackets.

## 2.4 IS 1343 Code Provisions for Shear (Type 1 Members)

| Condition | Requirement |
|:---|:---|
| $\sigma_{pt} \leq 0.67 \cdot f_t$ | **Only minimum shear reinforcement** is required |
| $0.67 f_t < \sigma_{pt} \leq f_t$ | **Designed shear reinforcement** required |
| $\sigma_{pt} > f_t$ | **Section must be redesigned** (depth increased) |
| **Maximum principal compressive stress** $\sigma_{pc} \leq 0.33 f_{ck}$ | To prevent web crushing |

Where $f_t = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ MPa as per IS 1343.

## 2.5 Design Shear Strength of Concrete ($V_c$)

Once the principal tensile stress is computed, the equivalent **shear force resisted by concrete** is derived from the principal stress formula. Setting $\sigma_{pt} = \tau_c$ (limiting principal tension) and rearranging:

$$\tau_c = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} - \frac{\sigma_x}{2}$$

The shear resisted by concrete is then: $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$

The **balance shear force** to be resisted by shear reinforcement:

$$V_{us} = V - V_c$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Save this table — these are the equations that appear in 95% of KTU ESE questions on shear in PSC.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} \pm \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} \pm \frac{M_{sw + LL}}{Z}$ | Longitudinal stress (prestress + bending) | MPa |
| 2 | $\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bh}$ | Max shear stress (rect. section, at NA) | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$ | Principal stresses | MPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{pt} = -\left[\frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}\right]$ | Principal tensile stress (magnitude) | MPa |
| 5 | $\sigma_{pc} = -\left[\frac{\sigma_x}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}\right]$ | Principal compressive stress (magnitude) | MPa |
| 6 | $\tau_c = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} - \frac{\sigma_x}{2}$ | Shear stress resisted by concrete (in terms of $\sigma_{pt}$) | MPa |
| 7 | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ | Shear capacity of concrete section | kN |
| 8 | $V_{us} = V - V_c$ | Balance shear for stirrup design | kN |
| 9 | $\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{V_{us}}{0.87 f_y d}$ | Spacing of vertical stirrups | mm²/mm |
| 10 | $\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} \geq \frac{0.4 b}{0.87 f_y}$ | Minimum shear reinforcement (IS 1343) | mm²/mm |
| 11 | $\sigma_{pc} \leq 0.33 f_{ck}$ | Max principal compression (web crushing check) | MPa |
| 12 | $f_t = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$ | Tensile strength of concrete | MPa |

> **Note on Table Symbols:** The symbols $\vert \cdot \vert$ (absolute value) are written as `\vert` to prevent markdown table syntax break.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Girders:** PSC I-girders for highway bridges (Type 1 design ensures water-tightness and durability).
- **Railway Sleepers:** Pre-tensioned sleepers must be Type 1 to resist dynamic and impact loads without cracking.
- **Nuclear Containment Structures:** Where zero-cracking is a safety requirement.
- **Water Tanks and Pipes:** To prevent leakage and corrosion of steel.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Principal Stress Formula (for Shear Analysis in PSC)

We start from the general 2D stress transformation in an inclined plane at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal:

$$\sigma_n = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

For the principal plane, the condition is $d\sigma_n / d\theta = 0$, which gives:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

The principal stresses are obtained by substituting this angle back:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

In a PSC beam web, $\sigma_y = 0$ and $\tau_{xy} = \tau$ (the transverse shear stress). Therefore:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation of $\tau_c$ in Terms of $\sigma_{pt}$ (Reverse Mapping)

If the **principal tensile stress** $\sigma_{pt}$ is the limiting value, we can solve for the corresponding $\tau$:

Given:
$$\sigma_{pt} = -\left[\frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}\right]$$

Rearranging (let $C = \sigma_x / 2$):
$$\sigma_{pt} = -\left[C + \sqrt{C^2 + \tau^2}\right]$$

$$\sqrt{C^2 + \tau^2} = -\sigma_{pt} - C$$

Squaring both sides:
$$C^2 + \tau^2 = (-\sigma_{pt} - C)^2 = \sigma_{pt}^2 + 2 C \sigma_{pt} + C^2$$

$$\tau^2 = \sigma_{pt}^2 + 2 C \sigma_{pt}$$

Substituting back $C = \sigma_x / 2$:

$$\tau^2 = \sigma_{pt}^2 + \sigma_x \cdot \sigma_{pt}$$

$$\tau = \sqrt{\sigma_{pt}^2 + \sigma_x \cdot \sigma_{pt}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{pt}(\sigma_{pt} + \sigma_x)} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — KTU Standard Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example 1 (KTU ESE Typical Problem Style):**
> A post-tensioned PSC beam (Type 1) has a rectangular section $b = 250$ mm, $h = 600$ mm. The prestressing force is $P = 1500$ kN with eccentricity $e = 100$ mm. At a critical section, the shear force is $V = 200$ kN and the bending moment is $M = 100$ kN·m. Check whether the section is safe in shear as per IS 1343. Take $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa.

### Step 1: Section Properties

$$A = b \cdot h = 250 \times 600 = 150{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.15 \text{ m}^2$$

$$I = \frac{bh^3}{12} = \frac{250 \times 600^3}{12} = 4.5 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{h/2} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^9}{300} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$d = h - 50 = 600 - 50 = 550 \text{ mm (assuming 50 mm cover)}$$

### Step 2: Compute Longitudinal Stress ($\sigma_x$) at Extreme Fibres

Direct compressive stress due to prestress:
$$\sigma_c = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3}{150{,}000} = 10.0 \text{ MPa (compression)}$$

Bending stress due to eccentricity (at top fibre, assuming downward eccentricity = sagging):
$$\sigma_{b,\text{prestress}} = \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} = \frac{1500 \times 10^3 \times 100}{1.5 \times 10^7} = 10.0 \text{ MPa (tension at top)}$$

Bending stress due to applied moment $M = 100$ kN·m (sagging moment → tension at bottom fibre):
$$\sigma_{b,\text{applied}} = \frac{M}{Z} = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{1.5 \times 10^7} = 6.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**At the bottom fibre (most critical for tension under service load):**

$$\sigma_{x,\text{bottom}} = -\frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z} + \frac{M}{Z}$$

Note: The convention used here is **compression positive** (standard structural convention). Prestress gives compression, eccentricity at bottom gives tension at top and compression at bottom, applied moment gives tension at bottom.

$$\sigma_{x,\text{bottom}} = -10.0 + (-10.0) + 6.67 = -13.33 \text{ MPa (compression)}$$

*Wait — let me correct the sign convention.* The standard KTU convention is **tension negative, compression positive** in concrete design. Let me redo:

**Re-doing Step 2 with clear convention (Compression Positive):**

- Prestress: $\sigma_{p} = +P/A = +10.0$ MPa (uniformly compressive)
- Eccentricity effect (e = 100 mm below centroid → bottom fibre gets MORE compression, top fibre gets tension):
  - At **bottom fibre**: $+P e / Z = +10.0$ MPa (additional compression)
  - At **top fibre**: $-P e / Z = -10.0$ MPa (tension from eccentric prestress)
- Applied moment (sagging → bottom fibre tension, top fibre compression):
  - At **bottom fibre**: $-M/Z = -6.67$ MPa
  - At **top fibre**: $+M/Z = +6.67$ MPa

**At bottom fibre (most likely to crack under service):**

$$\sigma_{x,\text{bottom}} = +10.0 + 10.0 - 6.67 = +13.33 \text{ MPa (compression)} \checkmark$$

**At top fibre (most critical during initial transfer — Stage I, with $M = 0$ and full $P_0$):**

If we consider Stage II (after losses, with applied moment), at the top fibre:

$$\sigma_{x,\text{top}} = +10.0 - 10.0 + 6.67 = +6.67 \text{ MPa (compression)} \checkmark$$

> **Both fibres are in compression → Type 1 condition satisfied at mid-span!**

### Step 3: Compute Maximum Shear Stress at Neutral Axis

For a rectangular section, the maximum shear stress (which occurs at the neutral axis):

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bh} = \frac{3 \times 200 \times 10^3}{2 \times 250 \times 600} = 2.0 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 4: Compute Principal Stresses at Neutral Axis

At the neutral axis: $\sigma_x = +P/A = +10.0$ MPa (only the uniform prestress, since bending stress is zero at NA).

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{10.0}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{10.0}{2}\right)^2 + (2.0)^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 5.0 + \sqrt{25.0 + 4.0} = 5.0 + \sqrt{29.0} = 5.0 + 5.385 = +10.385 \text{ MPa (compression)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{10.0}{2} - \sqrt{29.0} = 5.0 - 5.385 = -0.385 \text{ MPa (tension)}$$

**Principal tensile stress at NA:** $\sigma_{pt} = 0.385$ MPa (tension)
**Principal compressive stress at NA:** $\sigma_{pc} = 10.385$ MPa (compression)

### Step 5: Check Code Limits (IS 1343)

$$f_t = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.7 \sqrt{40} = 4.43 \text{ MPa}$$

**Check 1: Principal tensile stress**
$$\sigma_{pt} = 0.385 \text{ MPa} < 0.67 f_t = 0.67 \times 4.43 = 2.97 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

→ **Only minimum shear reinforcement is required.**

**Check 2: Principal compressive stress (web crushing)**
$$\sigma_{pc} = 10.385 \text{ MPa} < 0.33 f_{ck} = 0.33 \times 40 = 13.2 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

→ **Section is safe against web crushing.**

### Step 6: Design Minimum Shear Reinforcement

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{0.4 b}{0.87 f_y} = \frac{0.4 \times 250}{0.87 \times 415} = 0.277 \text{ mm}^2/\text{mm}$$

Using 8 mm diameter 2-legged stirrups ($A_{sv} = 2 \times 50.3 = 100.6$ mm²):

$$s_v = \frac{100.6}{0.277} = 363 \text{ mm}$$

As per IS 1343, the **maximum spacing** is also limited to:
- $s_v \leq 0.75 d = 0.75 \times 550 = 412.5$ mm
- $s_v \leq 300$ mm (minimum of the two — the **300 mm** governs for beams)

$$\boxed{s_v = 300 \text{ mm c/c, 8 mm dia. 2-legged stirrups}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Shear Design Flow

| Step | Action | Output | Decision Criterion |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Compute $\sigma_x$ at critical fibre (NA) | $\sigma_x = P/A$ | Compression is **positive** |
| 2 | Compute $\tau_{max}$ at NA | $\tau_{max} = 1.5 V/(bh)$ | Web shear stress |
| 3 | Compute $\sigma_{pt}$ and $\sigma_{pc}$ | Two principal stresses | Diagonal tension vs crushing |
| 4 | Compare $\sigma_{pt}$ with $0.67 f_t$ | $<, =, >$ | Triggers minimum/design/redesign |
| 5 | Compare $\sigma_{pc}$ with $0.33 f_{ck}$ | $<, =, >$ | Triggers redesign for web crushing |
| 6 | Compute $V_c$ from $\sigma_{pt}$ limit | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ | Concrete shear capacity |
| 7 | If $V > V_c$, design stirrups | $A_{sv}/s_v$ spacing | $V_{us} = V - V_c$ |
| 8 | Apply minimum stirrups clause | $A_{sv}/s_v \geq 0.4 b / 0.87 f_y$ | Final spacing |
| 9 | Check max spacing limits | $s_v \leq \min(0.75 d, 300 \text{ mm})$ | Constructability |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shear Analysis Procedure for Type 1 Members

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: P, e, V, M, b, h, fck] --> B[Compute Section Properties: A, I, Z, d]
    B --> C[Compute Longitudinal Stress sigma_x at NA]
    C --> D[Compute Max Shear Stress tau_max = 3V / 2bh]
    D --> E[Compute Principal Stresses sigma_pt and sigma_pc]
    E --> F{Check: sigma_pc <= 0.33 fck ?}
    F -- No --> G[REDESIGN: Increase depth h]
    F -- Yes --> H{Check: sigma_pt <= 0.67 ft ?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Provide Minimum Shear Reinforcement Only]
    H -- No --> J{0.67 ft < sigma_pt <= ft ?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Design Shear Reinforcement from Vus = V minus Vc]
    J -- No --> L[REDESIGN: Increase depth or prestress]
    K --> M[Compute Asv and sv]
    I --> N[Check min stirrups: Asv/sv >= 0.4b / 0.87fy]
    M --> N
    N --> O{sv <= min of 0.75d, 300 mm?}
    O -- No --> P[Reduce spacing to max limit]
    O -- Yes --> Q[Final Design: Provide stirrups at sv c/c]
    P --> Q
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — State of Stress at a Point in the Web

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Web Element in PSC Beam] --> B1[Longitudinal Stress sigma_x from P and M]
    A1 --> C1[Transverse Stress sigma_y = 0]
    A1 --> D1[Shear Stress tau from V]
    B1 --> E1[Combined 2D Stress State]
    C1 --> E1
    D1 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Apply Principal Stress Formula]
    F1 --> G1[Principal Compression sigma_pc]
    F1 --> H1[Principal Tension sigma_pt]
    G1 --> I1[Check 0.33 fck limit]
    H1 --> J1[Check 0.67 ft limit]
    I1 --> K1[Determine Stirrup Design]
    J1 --> K1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Type 1 Stress Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Stage1[Stage I - Initial at Transfer]
        S1A[Top Fibre: sigma_t1 = P0/A minus P0e/Z_top]
        S1B[Bottom Fibre: sigma_b1 = P0/A + P0e/Z_bottom]
    end
    subgraph Stage2[Stage II - Service After Losses]
        S2A[Top Fibre: sigma_t2 = P/A minus Pe/Z_top + M/Z_top]
        S2B[Bottom Fibre: sigma_b2 = P/A + Pe/Z_bottom minus M/Z_bottom]
    end
    Stage1 --> Check1{All fibres compressive?}
    Stage2 --> Check1
    Check1 -- Yes --> OK[Type 1 Design SATISFIED]
    Check1 -- No --> REVISE[REVISE Section Dimensions or Prestress]
```
