# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**Define Type 1 prestressed concrete members as per IS 1343. State the design philosophy for shear in such members.**

**Model Answer:**

> As per IS 1343:2012, **Type 1 members** are those in which **no tensile stress is permitted** in the concrete at any point, at any stage of loading (initial prestress transfer, service load, or any intermediate stage). This is the most conservative classification, typically adopted for structures exposed to aggressive environments or those requiring zero-crack performance.
>
> The **shear design philosophy** for Type 1 members revolves around ensuring that the **principal tensile stress** ($\sigma_{pt}$) in the web is **zero or compressive** at the service stage. The pre-compression from prestress directly counteracts the diagonal tension caused by transverse shear. If $\sigma_{pt} \leq 0.67 f_t$, only minimum shear reinforcement (stirrups) is required as per IS 1343; otherwise, the section must be redesigned (depth increased or prestress enhanced).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**State the expression for principal tensile stress in the web of a prestressed concrete beam and explain each term.**

**Model Answer:**

> The principal tensile stress in the web of a PSC beam is given by:
>
> $$\sigma_{pt} = -\left[\frac{\sigma_{cx}}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{cx}}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}\right]$$
>
> Where:
> - $\sigma_{cx}$ = **Longitudinal compressive stress** at the fibre level (from prestress and bending). Includes $P/A$ and the bending stress $M/Z$.
> - $\tau$ = **Transverse shear stress** at the fibre level (from the shear force $V$). Maximum value is $\tau_{max} = 1.5 V / (b h)$ at the neutral axis for a rectangular section.
> - $\sigma_{pt}$ = **Principal tensile stress** (negative sign indicates tension in compression-positive convention).
>
> This expression is derived from 2D stress transformation. The first term $\sigma_{cx}/2$ represents the pre-compression benefit, and the term under the square root represents the diagonal tension from combined axial + shear.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model]

A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam (Type 1) of rectangular section, $b = 300$ mm and $h = 700$ mm, is prestressed with a force of $P = 2000$ kN at an eccentricity of $e = 150$ mm. The beam supports a uniformly distributed load producing a maximum shear force of $V = 250$ kN and maximum bending moment of $M = 350$ kN·m at mid-span. Take $f_{ck} = 50$ MPa and $f_y = 415$ MPa.

**(a)** Calculate the principal stresses (tensile and compressive) at the neutral axis of the critical section near supports.

**(b)** Check the safety of the section as per IS 1343 and design the shear reinforcement, if required.

#### Model Solution for (a) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Section Properties**

$$A = 300 \times 700 = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2$$
$$I = \frac{300 \times 700^3}{12} = 8.575 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4$$
$$Z = \frac{I}{h/2} = \frac{8.575 \times 10^9}{350} = 2.45 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3$$
$$d = 700 - 50 = 650 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal Stress at Neutral Axis (At NA, only uniform prestress acts — bending stress is zero at NA)**

$$\sigma_{cx} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{2000 \times 10^3}{2.1 \times 10^5} = 9.524 \text{ MPa (compression)}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress at NA**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2bh} = \frac{3 \times 250 \times 10^3}{2 \times 300 \times 700} = 1.786 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Principal Stresses**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_{cx}}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{cx}}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{9.524}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{9.524}{2}\right)^2 + (1.786)^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 4.762 + \sqrt{22.68 + 3.19} = 4.762 + \sqrt{25.87}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 4.762 + 5.086 = 9.848 \text{ MPa (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 4.762 - 5.086 = -0.324 \text{ MPa (tensile)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{pt} = 0.324 \text{ MPa (tension)}, \quad \sigma_{pc} = 9.848 \text{ MPa (compression)}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 5: Calculate Limiting Stresses (IS 1343)**

$$f_t = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.7 \sqrt{50} = 4.95 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Compare with Code Limits**

- **Check web crushing:** $\sigma_{pc} = 9.848$ MPa $\leq 0.33 f_{ck} = 16.5$ MPa ✓
- **Check diagonal tension:** $\sigma_{pt} = 0.324$ MPa $\leq 0.67 f_t = 3.32$ MPa ✓

**Step 7: Shear Reinforcement**

Since $\sigma_{pt} \leq 0.67 f_t$, **only minimum shear reinforcement** is required:

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} \geq \frac{0.4 b}{0.87 f_y} = \frac{0.4 \times 300}{0.87 \times 415} = 0.332 \text{ mm}^2/\text{mm}$$

**Using 10 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups** ($A_{sv} = 2 \times 78.5 = 157$ mm²):

$$s_v \leq \frac{157}{0.332} = 472.9 \text{ mm}$$

**Check maximum spacing limit:**

$$s_v \leq \min(0.75 d, 300 \text{ mm}) = \min(487.5, 300) = 300 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Provide 10 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups at } s_v = 300 \text{ mm c/c}}$$

The section is **safe** in shear as per IS 1343.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]

A pre-tensioned PSC bridge girder (Type 1) has an I-section with the following properties: flange width $b_f = 500$ mm, flange thickness $t_f = 150$ mm, web width $b_w = 200$ mm, overall depth $h = 1200$ mm. The effective prestress after all losses is $P = 3000$ kN acting at $e = 400$ mm. The maximum shear at the critical section is $V = 600$ kN with $M = 1200$ kN·m. Take $f_{ck} = 50$ MPa, $f_y = 415$ MPa.

**(a)** Compute the principal stresses in the web at the level of the centroid (NA) of the I-section.

**(b)** If the principal tensile stress exceeds $0.67 f_t$, design the shear reinforcement. Comment on whether the section dimensions are adequate.

#### Model Solution for (a) — [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Section Properties of I-Section**

$$A = 2(b_f t_f) + b_w (h - 2 t_f)$$
$$A = 2(500 \times 150) + 200 \times (1200 - 300)$$
$$A = 150{,}000 + 180{,}000 = 3.3 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2$$

For a symmetric I-section, the moment of inertia about the centroidal axis:

$$I = \frac{b_f h^3}{12} - \frac{(b_f - b_w)(h - 2t_f)^3}{12}$$
$$I = \frac{500 \times 1200^3}{12} - \frac{300 \times 900^3}{12}$$
$$I = 7.2 \times 10^{10} - 1.823 \times 10^{10} = 5.377 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2: Longitudinal Stress at NA (Only P/A at NA)**

$$\sigma_{cx} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{3000 \times 10^3}{3.3 \times 10^5} = 9.091 \text{ MPa (compression)}$$

**Step 3: Maximum Shear Stress at NA**

For an I-section, the maximum shear stress in the web is given by:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{NA}}{I \cdot b_w}$$

where $Q_{NA}$ is the first moment of the area above (or below) the NA about the NA:

$$Q_{NA} = b_f t_f \cdot (h/2 - t_f/2) + b_w \cdot (h/2 - t_f) \cdot (h/2 - t_f)/2$$

$$Q_{NA} = 500 \times 150 \times 525 + 200 \times 450 \times 225$$
$$Q_{NA} = 3.9375 \times 10^7 + 2.025 \times 10^7 = 5.9625 \times 10^7 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{600 \times 10^3 \times 5.9625 \times 10^7}{5.377 \times 10^{10} \times 200}$$
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{3.5775 \times 10^{13}}{1.0754 \times 10^{13}} = 3.327 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Principal Stresses at NA**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{9.091}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{9.091}{2}\right)^2 + (3.327)^2}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 4.545 + \sqrt{20.66 + 11.07} = 4.545 + \sqrt{31.73}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 4.545 + 5.633 = 10.178 \text{ MPa (compression)}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 4.545 - 5.633 = -1.088 \text{ MPa (tension)}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{pt} = 1.088 \text{ MPa (tension)}, \quad \sigma_{pc} = 10.178 \text{ MPa (compression)}}$$

#### Model Solution for (b) — [7 Marks]

**Step 5: Code Checks**

$$f_t = 0.7 \sqrt{50} = 4.95 \text{ MPa}$$
$$0.67 f_t = 3.32 \text{ MPa}$$
$$0.33 f_{ck} = 16.5 \text{ MPa}$$

- $\sigma_{pc} = 10.178$ MPa $< 16.5$ MPa ✓ (no web crushing)
- $\sigma_{pt} = 1.088$ MPa $< 3.32$ MPa ✓ (only minimum stirrups needed)

**Step 6: Shear Resisted by Concrete**

Setting $\sigma_{pt} = 0.67 f_t = 3.32$ MPa (limiting principal tension):

$$\tau_c = \sqrt{(\sigma_{pt})^2 + \sigma_{cx} \cdot \sigma_{pt}}$$

$$\tau_c = \sqrt{(3.32)^2 + 9.091 \times 3.32} = \sqrt{11.02 + 30.18} = \sqrt{41.20} = 6.419 \text{ MPa}$$

$$V_c = \tau_c \cdot b_w \cdot d = 6.419 \times 200 \times 1150 / 1000 = 1476.4 \text{ kN}$$

Since $V = 600$ kN $< V_c = 1476.4$ kN, the concrete section itself can resist the entire shear.

**Step 7: Minimum Shear Reinforcement**

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{s_v} = \frac{0.4 \times 200}{0.87 \times 415} = 0.2215 \text{ mm}^2/\text{mm}$$

Using 8 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups ($A_{sv} = 100.6$ mm²):

$$s_v \leq \frac{100.6}{0.2215} = 454.2 \text{ mm}$$

Apply maximum spacing limit: $s_v \leq \min(0.75 \times 1150, 300) = 300$ mm.

$$\boxed{\text{Provide 8 mm Ø 2-legged stirrups at } s_v = 300 \text{ mm c/c}}$$

**Step 8: Comment on Adequacy of Section**

The section is **safe and adequate** in shear. The principal tensile stress (1.088 MPa) is well below the $0.67 f_t$ limit, indicating that the prestress is efficiently resisting diagonal tension. The principal compressive stress (10.178 MPa) is also within safe limits. No increase in section depth is necessary.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Resulting in Mark Deductions):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (Loses 1-2 marks):** Students frequently mix up tension and compression signs in the principal stress formula. **Always declare your sign convention at the start** (compression-positive is recommended per IS 1343). A wrong sign in $\sigma_{cx}$ gives a completely wrong answer.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Apply Bending Stress at NA (Loses 1 mark):** At the **neutral axis**, the bending stress $\sigma_{b} = M y / I$ is **zero** (because $y = 0$). Only the uniform $P/A$ contributes. Many students incorrectly add $M/Z$ at the NA, which is a critical error.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\sigma_{pt}$ for the Bottom Fibre vs NA (Loses 2 marks):** The **maximum principal tensile stress** for shear design occurs at the **neutral axis** (where $\tau$ is maximum), NOT at the bottom fibre. The bottom fibre may have $\sigma_{pt} = 0$ but high tensile axial stress — these are two different failure modes.
>
> 4. **Using $\tau = V / b d$ Instead of $1.5 V / b h$ (Loses 1 mark):** For a rectangular section, the **maximum shear stress is at NA** and equals $1.5 V / bh$ (not $V/bd$). The $V/bd$ formula is for the **average** shear, used only for rectangular sections where $d \approx 0.9 h$.
>
> 5. **Not Checking Web Crushing ($\sigma_{pc} \leq 0.33 f_{ck}$):** Students often only check $\sigma_{pt}$ and forget the **principal compression** check. The web can fail by crushing in deep beams.
>
> 6. **Ignoring Maximum Spacing Limits:** The minimum stirrups formula gives a *theoretical* spacing, but the **maximum of 300 mm** (IS 1343) often **governs** in practice. Always check both.
>
> 7. **Forgetting Loss Factor:** In real design, after all losses, the effective prestress is $P_e = \eta P_0$ where $\eta$ accounts for losses (typically 0.8 to 0.85). Use the *effective* prestress, not the initial jacking force.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **Type 1 PSC members** = **No tension allowed** anywhere, at any stage. Most conservative.
- ☐ **Three critical stages:** Initial (transfer), Service (after losses), Ultimate (collapse).
- ☐ **Principal stress formula** (master equation):
  $$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_{cx}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{cx}}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$
- ☐ **At the Neutral Axis (NA):** $\sigma_x = P/A$ only (no bending contribution at NA).
- ☐ **Maximum shear stress at NA (rectangular):** $\tau_{max} = 1.5 V / (bh)$.
- ☐ **Code limit checks (IS 1343):**
  - $\sigma_{pt} \leq 0.67 f_t$ → only minimum stirrups
  - $0.67 f_t < \sigma_{pt} \leq f_t$ → design stirrups from $V - V_c$
  - $\sigma_{pt} > f_t$ → **redesign section**
  - $\sigma_{pc} \leq 0.33 f_{ck}$ → no web crushing
- ☐ **$f_t = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$** (tensile strength of concrete per IS 1343).
- ☐ **Minimum shear reinforcement:** $A_{sv}/s_v \geq 0.4 b / (0.87 f_y)$.
- ☐ **Maximum stirrup spacing:** $s_v \leq \min(0.75 d, 300 \text{ mm})$.
- ☐ **Conceptual advantage of PSC:** Longitudinal pre-compression **cancels diagonal tension** — this is why PSC can carry higher shear than equivalent RCC.
- ☐ **Conversion:** $1$ MPa $= 1$ N/mm² = $10^3$ kN/m² — keep units consistent.
- ☐ **Sign convention:** Always **state and use** compression-positive (or tension-positive, but stick to one).
- ☐ **The diagonal tension is the diagonal tension of the principal stress** — do not confuse with direct tensile stress from bending alone.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components of Shear Resistance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Components of Shear Resistance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the design of **Prestressed Concrete (PSC)** members, the total ultimate shear resistance (or nominal shear strength) of a section is defined as the algebraic summation of three distinct mechanical contributions acting in parallel to resist the applied factored shear force. As per **IS 1343:2012** and IS 456:2000 design philosophy, the total nominal shear strength $V_u$ of a prestressed concrete member is expressed as:

$$V_{u} \;=\; V_{c} \;+\; V_{p} \;+\; V_{s}$$

where each term represents an independent load-resisting mechanism. The term $V_c$ is the **shear resistance of the concrete** (i.e., the uncracked web of the section), $V_p$ is the **vertical component of the effective prestressing force** at the section, and $V_s$ is the **shear contribution of the transverse steel reinforcement** (stirrups, links, or bent-up bars). This decomposition is central to the **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** design of PSC beams as mandated in Module 3 of the PECET525 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Anchor Concept:** The three components $V_c$, $V_p$, and $V_s$ act **simultaneously** and are **additive**, NOT alternative. A student must not confuse the *Serviceability* check (principal tensile stress) with the *Ultimate* check (codal $V_c, V_p, V_s$).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Three-Person Dam"

Imagine a massive river dam holding back a flood (the applied shear force $V$). Three workers stand on the dam, each pushing against the water:

1. **Worker A — The Concrete Web (V_c):** The dam's own concrete body provides friction and bulk. This is the **inherent shear capacity of the uncracked concrete** in the beam's web — it works even before any steel is placed.
2. **Worker B — The Anchored Cable (V_p):** A diagonal steel cable pre-tensioned from the foundation to the top of the dam pulls it sideways, reducing the net horizontal push the water exerts. This is the **vertical pull of the curved/inclined prestressing tendon**, which actively *unloads* the web from shear.
3. **Worker C — The Steel Crossbars (V_s):** Horizontal steel bars are bolted across the dam, catching the water and transferring it through the stirrups. These are the **transverse shear links** that engage only after diagonal cracking begins.

The total force these three can resist together equals the maximum shear the dam can take before failing. None of them alone is sufficient; the design philosophy is to ensure **all three work in concert**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Connection:** In modern bridge girders (e.g., the **Neyyar River Bridge** in Kerala or segmental metro viaducts), the inclined tendon profile is intentionally designed so that $V_p$ offsets up to **25–40%** of the dead-load shear, dramatically reducing the stirrup requirement. This is the essence of efficient PSC design.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Characteristic compressive strength | $f_{ck}$ | **30, 40, 50** | MPa |
| Yield strength of shear steel | $f_y$ | **500** (Fe 500) | MPa |
| Partial safety factor for steel | $\gamma_m$ | **1.15** | — |
| Partial safety factor for concrete | $\gamma_m$ | **1.50** | — |
| Concrete tensile strength (used in serviceability) | $f_{ct}$ | **0.7$\sqrt{f_{ck}}$** | MPa |
| Inclination of stirrups to longitudinal axis | $\alpha$ | **90°** (vertical) or 45°–60° | degrees |

---

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in KTU Board Exams:** Almost every shear design problem in ESE (End Semester Examination) of PECET525 begins with the phrase *"Determine the ultimate shear resistance of the section and design the shear reinforcement."* If you cannot decompose $V$ into $V_c + V_p + V_s$, you cannot solve the problem. Memorize the three components first.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Components — Operational Breakdown

### 🔹 Component 1: Concrete Shear Resistance ($V_c$)

**WHY it exists:** Even an unreinforced concrete web can resist shear through aggregate interlock, uncracked tension zone contribution, and dowel action at the compression face. IS 1343 quantifies this empirically.

**HOW it is calculated (IS 1343 / IS 456 codal formula):**

$$V_{c} \;=\; \frac{\beta_{1}\,\sqrt{f_{ck}}\;b\,d}{1000} \quad \text{(using simplified expression, alternative form)}$$

The **more rigorous IS 1343 expression** (also appearing in IS 456 Cl. 40.2.1 for beams) is:

$$V_{c} \;=\; \frac{0.67\,b\,d}{1 \,+\, \beta_{1}\,\dfrac{M_{u}}{V_{u}\,d}} \;\; \text{N (with $b,d$ in mm, $f_{ck}$ in MPa)}$$

- $b$ = breadth of the web (mm)
- $d$ = effective depth (mm)
- $M_u / V_u$ = shear span ratio (mm)
- $\beta_1$ = codal factor depending on $f_{ck}$:
  - $f_{ck} = 30$ MPa → $\beta_1 = 0.8$
  - $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa → $\beta_1 = 1.0$
  - $f_{ck} = 50$ MPa → $\beta_1 = 1.2$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundaries on $V_c$:** The ratio $M_u / (V_u \, d)$ is restricted between **1.0 and 3.0**. Outside this range, IS 1343 caps the value (i.e., use 1.0 minimum and 3.0 maximum).

### 🔹 Component 2: Vertical Component of Prestress ($V_p$)

**WHY it exists:** When the prestressing cable is **curved or inclined** at the section under consideration, the tendon exerts a vertical (or transverse) force on the concrete. This force directly **opposes** the applied shear.

**HOW it is calculated:**

$$V_{p} \;=\; P_{e}\,\sin\alpha$$

where:
- $P_e$ = effective prestressing force after all losses at that section (N)
- $\alpha$ = angle of inclination of the tendon with the horizontal at the section under consideration (degrees or radians)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** $V_p$ is taken as **positive** if the vertical component of the prestress is acting in the *opposite direction* to the applied shear. For a simply supported beam with a parabolic tendon, the tendon exerts an upward thrust — this *reduces* the net shear.

For a **parabolic tendon profile** with eccentricity $e$ at supports and $e_0$ at midspan, the vertical force per unit length is:
$$w_{p} \;=\; \frac{8\,P_{e}\,e_0}{L^{2}} \quad \text{(uniformly distributed, upward)}$$

The corresponding $V_p$ at a section $x$ from the support is:
$$V_{p}(x) \;=\; P_{e}\,\sin\alpha(x) \;\approx\; \frac{8\,P_{e}\,e_0}{L^{2}}\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) \quad \text{(for parabolic profile, small angle approx.)}$$

### 🔹 Component 3: Shear Resistance of Transverse Steel ($V_s$)

**WHY it exists:** Once the principal tensile stress in the web exceeds the tensile strength of concrete, **diagonal cracks** form. Stirrups cross these cracks and provide tensile resistance normal to the crack plane, contributing a vertical force component.

**HOW it is calculated (IS 1343 Cl. 11.4):**

For **vertical stirrups** ($\alpha = 90°$):

$$V_{s} \;=\; \frac{0.87\,f_{y}\,A_{sv}\,d}{s}$$

For **inclined stirrups** (at angle $\alpha_s$ to horizontal):

$$V_{s} \;=\; \frac{0.87\,f_{y}\,A_{sv}\,d}{s}\,\bigl(\sin\alpha_{s} + \cos\alpha_{s}\bigr)$$

where:
- $A_{sv}$ = total cross-sectional area of stirrup legs crossing the section (mm²)
- $s$ = spacing of stirrups along the longitudinal axis (mm)
- $d$ = effective depth (mm)
- $f_y$ = yield strength of stirrup steel (MPa)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Codal Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Hall Quick Reference** — This table is the **minimum** you must memorize. Carry it mentally into the exam hall.

| # | Component | Formula | Variable Meaning | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total nominal shear strength | $V_u = V_c + V_p + V_s$ | $V_u$ is total capacity | N or kN |
| 2 | Concrete shear (codal, IS) | $V_c = \dfrac{0.67\,b\,d}{1 + \beta_1\,M_u/(V_u\,d)}$ | $\beta_1$ from $f_{ck}$ | N |
| 3 | Concrete shear (simplified) | $V_c = \dfrac{\beta_1\sqrt{f_{ck}}\,b\,d}{1000}$ | alternative quick form | kN |
| 4 | Vertical prestress component | $V_p = P_e \sin\alpha$ | $\alpha$ = tendon angle | N |
| 5 | Vertical stirrup contribution | $V_s = \dfrac{0.87\,f_y\,A_{sv}\,d}{s}$ | $\alpha_s = 90°$ | N |
| 6 | Inclined stirrup contribution | $V_s = \dfrac{0.87\,f_y\,A_{sv}\,d}{s}(\sin\alpha_s + \cos\alpha_s)$ | general case | N |
| 7 | Spacing limits (IS 1343) | $s \le s_{max} = 0.75\,d \le 300$ mm | minimum of the two | mm |
| 8 | Minimum shear reinforcement | $\dfrac{A_{sv}}{b\,s} \ge \dfrac{0.4}{0.87\,f_y}$ | when $V > V_c + V_p$ | — |
| 9 | Principal tensile stress (service) | $f_t = \dfrac{\sigma_x}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$ | at uncracked web | MPa |
| 10 | Shear stress at NA (elastic) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{(V - V_p)\,Q}{I\,b}$ | classical mechanics | MPa |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL — Table Pipe Rule:** In your answer sheet, never write $\vert x \vert$ or $\vert \beta_1 \vert$ inside a formula table — examiners reject it. Always write $x$, $\beta_1$, or use parentheses. In your rough work, $\mid$ is fine.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The decomposition into $V_c$, $V_p$, $V_s$ is not merely academic — it directly drives **economy and safety** in design:

- **Bridge Girders (Post-Tensioned Box Girders):** Designers exploit $V_p$ by using **harped (inclined) tendons** near supports, where shear is critical. The vertical component of tendon force can offset 30–50% of the dead-load shear, drastically reducing stirrup congestion.
- **Building Floor Beams (Pretensioned):** Tendons are typically straight and horizontal → $V_p \approx 0$. All shear must be resisted by $V_c + V_s$. Designers increase $V_c$ by widening the web (increasing $b$).
- **Nuclear Containment Structures:** $V_c$ is intentionally over-estimated conservatively, and **two independent shear paths** (closed stirrups + inclined tendons) are used to satisfy the redundant load-path requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** In numerical problems, the examiner typically gives a parabolic/eccentric tendon profile, and you must compute the slope $\tan\alpha = dy/dx$ at the required section, then use $\sin\alpha = \tan\alpha / \sqrt{1 + \tan^2\alpha}$. This is where most students lose marks — they forget the small-angle approximation is valid only when $\tan\alpha \le 0.1$.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Principal Tensile Stress Method (Serviceability Check)

This is the **elastic, uncracked-section** approach used in *Module 3* of KTU syllabus to verify that diagonal cracking does not occur at service loads.

**Step 1: Define the stress state at a generic point in the web.**

A point in the web of a PSC beam, located at distance $y$ from the centroidal axis, experiences:
- Longitudinal stress $\sigma_x$ (due to prestress + bending due to external loads)
- Transverse (vertical) stress $\sigma_y \approx 0$ in the web
- Shear stress $\tau$ from the shear force

$$\sigma_{x} \;=\; -\frac{P_{e}}{A} \;-\; \frac{P_{e}\,e\,y}{I} \;+\; \frac{M\,y}{I}$$

where:
- $P_e / A$ = uniform compressive stress from prestress (negative = compression)
- $P_e \, e \, y / I$ = bending stress from tendon eccentricity
- $M \, y / I$ = bending stress from external moment

**Step 2: Apply the 2D stress transformation to find principal stresses.**

For a 2D state of stress with $\sigma_y = 0$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} \;\pm\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} \;+\; \tau^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Identify the principal tensile stress (the smaller, potentially tensile root).**

The principal tensile stress is the **subtractive** root:

$$f_{t} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} \;-\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} \;+\; \tau^{2}}$$

**Step 4: Apply the limit on $f_t$ (IS 1343 Cl. 11.2, depending on class of structure).**

$$f_{t} \;\le\; f_{t,\text{allowable}}$$

- For **Class 1** (no tensile stress): $f_{t,\text{allow}} = 0$
- For **Class 2** (no visible cracking): $f_{t,\text{allow}} = 0.3\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ to $0.5\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ depending on exposure
- For **Class 3** (cracking permitted): $f_{t,\text{allow}} = f_{ct}$ or the design crack-width condition

**Conversion logic:** This is purely a serviceability check. It tells us whether the *uncracked* concrete alone ($V_c$ alone) can resist the applied shear. If $f_t$ exceeds the allowable, we move to the **ultimate** approach with stirrups ($V_s$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Stress at the Neutral Axis (Elastic Web)

This is needed to compute the principal stress at the **most critical** location, which is the neutral axis where $\sigma_x = -P_e/A$ (no bending contribution if $y = 0$).

**Step 1: Start from the classical shear formula for a composite section.**

For an uncracked prestressed beam, the shear force is *partially* resisted by the vertical component of the tendon itself. The net shear that produces web shear stress is:

$$V_{\text{net}} \;=\; V \;-\; P_{e}\sin\alpha \;=\; V \;-\; V_{p}$$

**Step 2: Apply the elastic shear formula.**

The shear stress at a fiber distance $y$ from the NA is:

$$\tau(y) \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{net}}\,\cdot Q(y)}{I\,\cdot\,b(y)}$$

where:
- $Q(y)$ = first moment of area of the section above (or below) the fiber, about the NA
- $I$ = second moment of area of the entire section
- $b(y)$ = width of the section at the fiber

**Step 3: For a rectangular section, evaluate at the NA ($y = 0$).**

For a rectangle of width $b$ and depth $D$:

$$Q_{\text{NA}} \;=\; b \cdot \frac{D}{2} \cdot \frac{D}{4} \;=\; \frac{b\,D^{2}}{8}$$

$$I \;=\; \frac{b\,D^{3}}{12}$$

$$\tau_{\text{NA}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{net}}\,\cdot\,(b D^{2}/8)}{(b D^{3}/12)\,\cdot\,b} \;=\; \frac{1.5\,V_{\text{net}}}{b\,D} \;=\; \frac{1.5\,(V - P_{e}\sin\alpha)}{b\,D}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor **1.5** is the well-known form factor for a rectangular section — it comes from $\frac{3}{2} = \frac{1.5 \times 12}{8} = 1.5$. For an I-section, replace with the actual $Q/I$ ratio for each fiber.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Codal $V_c$ Expression (IS 1343 Background)

The codal expression for $V_c$ is **empirical**, not a first-principles derivation. It comes from a regression of test data on RC/PSC beams with various $M/V$ (shear span) ratios.

**Step 1: Define the shear span ratio.**

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{M_{u}}{V_{u}\,\cdot\,d} \quad \text{(dimensionless, restricted to } 1 \le \lambda \le 3\text{)}$$

**Step 2: Read off the empirical constant from IS 1343 Table 7 (or IS 456 Cl. 40.2.1).**

The expression $\dfrac{0.67\,b\,d}{1 + \beta_1\,\lambda}$ was fitted to ensure:
- At **small $\lambda$ (deep beams):** $V_c \to 0.67\,b\,d$ — a plateau (governed by arch action).
- At **large $\lambda$ (slender beams):** $V_c \to 0.67\,b\,d / (1 + 3\beta_1)$ — controlled by bending-flexural-shear interaction.

**Step 3: Recognize the limit boundaries.**

$$\boxed{\;1.0 \;\le\; \frac{M_{u}}{V_{u}\,d} \;\le\; 3.0\;}$$

If the calculated ratio is below 1.0, the section behaves as a **deep beam** and additional rules apply (strut-and-tie). If above 3.0, the contribution of $V_c$ is small, and shear is dominated by flexural-shear cracking.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Pattern (KTU-Standard)

**Problem Pattern (typical 14-mark KTU question):**
*"A post-tensioned PSC beam of rectangular section $b = 250$ mm, $D = 700$ mm, $d = 650$ mm is prestressed by a parabolic tendon with eccentricity $e_0 = 180$ mm at midspan and $0$ at supports. Effective prestress $P_e = 1200$ kN. The beam carries a factored shear $V_u = 380$ kN at a section $x = 2.0$ m from the support, where the corresponding factored moment is $M_u = 220$ kN·m. $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa. Design the shear reinforcement."*

**Step-by-step solution skeleton:**

**Step 1: Compute $V_p$ at $x = 2.0$ m.**

For a parabolic tendon, slope at $x$:
$$\frac{dy}{dx} \;=\; \frac{4\,e_0}{L}\left(1 - \frac{2x}{L}\right) \;\;\approx\;\; \tan\alpha$$

Assume $L = 10$ m, $x = 2$ m:
$$\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.180}{10}\left(1 - \frac{2\times 2}{10}\right) \;=\; 0.072 \times 0.6 \;=\; 0.0432$$

$$\sin\alpha \;\approx\; \tan\alpha \;=\; 0.0432 \quad (\text{since } \alpha \text{ is small})$$

$$V_{p} \;=\; P_{e}\sin\alpha \;=\; 1200 \times 0.0432 \;=\; 51.84 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2: Compute $M_u / (V_u \, d)$.**

$$\frac{M_{u}}{V_{u}\,d} \;=\; \frac{220 \times 10^{6}}{380 \times 10^{3} \times 650} \;=\; \frac{220 \times 10^{6}}{247 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 0.89$$

Since this is **less than 1.0**, IS 1343 says to **clamp it to 1.0** (i.e., use $\lambda = 1.0$).

**Step 3: Compute $V_c$ using $\beta_1 = 1.0$ (for $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa).**

$$V_{c} \;=\; \frac{0.67 \times 250 \times 650}{1 + 1.0 \times 1.0} \;=\; \frac{108875}{2.0} \;=\; 54437.5 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 54.44 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Compute the balance to be carried by shear steel.**

$$V_{\text{steel,req}} \;=\; V_{u} - V_{c} - V_{p} \;=\; 380 - 54.44 - 51.84 \;=\; 273.72 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 5: Provide vertical stirrups (assume 2-legged 10 mm $\phi$, $A_{sv} = 157$ mm²).**

$$V_{s} \;=\; \frac{0.87\,f_{y}\,A_{sv}\,d}{s} \;\Rightarrow\; s \;=\; \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 157 \times 650}{273.72 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$s \;=\; \frac{44{,}396{,}475}{273{,}720} \;\approx\; 162 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 6: Apply IS 1343 spacing limits.**

$$s_{\max} \;=\; \min(0.75\,d,\; 300 \;\text{mm}) \;=\; \min(487.5,\; 300) \;=\; 300 \;\text{mm}$$

Since $162 \le 300$, provide **2-legged 10 mm $\phi$ stirrups at 160 mm c/c** (round down to standard spacing).

**Step 7: Check minimum shear reinforcement.**

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b\,s} \;\ge\; \frac{0.4}{0.87\,f_{y}} \;=\; \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times 500} \;=\; 9.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b\,s} \;=\; \frac{157}{250 \times 160} \;=\; 3.93 \times 10^{-3} \;\ge\; 9.2 \times 10^{-4} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Note on $V_p$ Sign:** If the parabola sags *downward* (e.g., in a cantilever), $\sin\alpha$ becomes negative at certain sections, and $V_p$ is added (not subtracted). Always draw the FBD of the tendon and resolve forces carefully.

## 3.5 Summary Table — When Each Component Dominates

| Loading Scenario | Dominant Component | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Near midspan, simply supported, low shear | $V_c$ alone sufficient | No stirrups needed (only nominal links) |
| Near supports, simply supported | $V_c + V_p$ (parabolic tendon) | Stirrups provided as minimum |
| Near supports, heavy concentrated load | $V_s$ dominates | Closer stirrup spacing or inclined links |
| Cantilever (negative moment region) | All three required | Check both top and bottom stirrups |
| Precast pretensioned (straight tendon) | $V_p \approx 0$ | Full reliance on $V_c + V_s$ |
| Segmental post-tensioned (inclined ducts) | $V_p$ is large, $V_s$ reduced | Stagger tendon anchors to optimize $V_p$ profile |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Components of Shear Resistance

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Factored Shear V_u] --> B{Decomposition at Section}
    
    B --> C1[Component 1: V_c]
    B --> C2[Component 2: V_p]
    B --> C3[Component 3: V_s]
    
    C1 --> D1[Concrete Web Capacity]
    D1 --> D1a[Depends on f_ck, b, d, M_u/V_u ratio]
    D1 --> D1b[IS 1343 / IS 456 empirical formula]
    D1 --> D1c[Active in uncracked elastic stage]
    
    C2 --> D2[Vertical Component of Tendon]
    D2 --> D2a[From inclined or curved tendons]
    D2 --> D2b[V_p = P_e times sin alpha]
    D2 --> D2c[Reduces net shear on web]
    
    C3 --> D3[Shear Steel Capacity]
    D3 --> D3a[Stirrups, links, bent up bars]
    D3 --> D3b[Engages after diagonal cracking]
    D3 --> D3c[IS 1343 Cl. 11.4 expression]
    
    D1 --> E[Total: V_u_calc = V_c + V_p + V_s]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    
    E --> F{Check V_u_calc >= V_u_applied}
    F -->|Yes| G[Section SAFE in Shear]
    F -->|No| H[Revise Spacing, Increase A_sv, or Add Inclined Tendons]
    
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow of Shear Through PSC Web

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[External Shear V] --> Q[Subtract V_p from inclined tendon]
    Q --> R[Net V enters concrete web]
    R --> S[Concrete web resists V_c via aggregate interlock and uncracked tension zone]
    S --> T{Diagonal Crack Forms?}
    T -->|No| U[Section OK in service]
    T -->|Yes| V[Stirrups V_s activated across crack]
    V --> W[Failure prevented by combined V_c + V_p + V_s]
    
    style P fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333
    style U fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400
    style W fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Decision Matrix for Component Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Start: Identify Tendon Profile] --> Q1{Type of Tendon}
    
    Q1 -->|Straight Horizontal| P1[V_p = 0]
    Q1 -->|Curved Parabolic| P2[V_p = P_e sin alpha at section x]
    Q1 -->|Inclined Harped| P3[V_p = P_e sin alpha constant over inclined length]
    
    P1 --> R1{Compute M_u over V_u d ratio}
    P2 --> R1
    P3 --> R1
    
    R1 --> S1{Compute V_c from IS formula}
    S1 --> T1{V_u_applied > V_c plus V_p}
    
    T1 -->|No| OK[No shear steel needed beyond minimum]
    T1 -->|Yes| ST[Compute V_s required and design stirrups]
    
    ST --> CHK{Spacing s <= s_max and minimum steel check}
    CHK -->|Pass| FIN[Provide stirrups at spacing s]
    CHK -->|Fail| REV[Revise stirrup diameter or use 4 legged]
    
    style Start fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333
    style OK fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style FIN fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B
    style REV fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description — Free-Body of a Cracked PSC Web

For a student visualizing a beam, picture this layout:

```
              ↑ Applied Shear V (downward on right face)
              |
   ═══════════════════════════════════════════════  ← Top fiber (compression)
   ┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄  ← Cracks start here
   |        |              |          |
   |  V_c   |              |   V_s    |   ← Concrete web + stirrups
   |  ↕     |              |   ↑↓     |
   |        |              |          |
   ┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄┄  ← Diagonal crack (45°)
   ╲        ╲              ╲          ╲
    ╲________╲______________╲__________╲_______  ← Neutral axis
   ═══════════════════════════════════════════════  ← Bottom fiber (tension)
                  ↖ Tendon (parabolic, V_p upward)
```

**Key visual cues:**
- The diagonal crack propagates from bottom-center upward toward the load point.
- $V_c$ acts along the uncracked concrete compression zone (top).
- $V_s$ acts where the stirrups cross the diagonal crack.
- $V_p$ acts upward at the tendon anchor (parabolic drape).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Diagram Expectation:** In the 14-mark question, drawing this FBD with the **three components clearly labeled** earns at least 2-3 marks even if the numerical part is incomplete. Always draw the FBD first.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the three components of shear resistance in a PSC beam. State the IS 1343 formula for each. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The total ultimate shear resistance of a prestressed concrete member is the sum of three independent contributions:

1. **$V_c$ — Shear resistance of concrete web:** The inherent capacity of the uncracked concrete in the web to resist shear through aggregate interlock and uncracked tension zone action.
   $$V_{c} \;=\; \frac{0.67\,b\,d}{1 + \beta_{1}\,M_{u}/(V_{u}\,d)}$$

2. **$V_p$ — Vertical component of effective prestressing force:** The upward (or downward) transverse force exerted by the inclined/curved tendon at the section.
   $$V_{p} \;=\; P_{e}\,\sin\alpha$$

3. **$V_s$ — Shear resistance of transverse reinforcement:** The contribution of stirrups, links, or bent-up bars crossing the potential diagonal crack.
   $$V_{s} \;=\; \frac{0.87\,f_{y}\,A_{sv}\,d}{s} \quad \text{(vertical stirrups)}$$

The total nominal shear strength is: $V_u = V_c + V_p + V_s$. *(1 mark for the decomposition statement, 2 marks for the three formulas.)*

---

### Question 2: Why is the vertical component of prestress ($V_p$) treated as a shear-resisting component in IS 1343 but not in IS 456? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

IS 456 governs **Reinforced Concrete (RC)** design, where steel reinforcement does not impose a pre-existing transverse force on the member. In contrast, IS 1343 governs **Prestressed Concrete (PSC)** design, where the tendon is **actively stressed** against the concrete. When the tendon is curved or inclined, it exerts a vertical force on the concrete equal to $P_e \sin\alpha$, which directly **opposes** the applied shear. Therefore, IS 1343 explicitly recognizes this as a load-resisting component $V_p$ and subtracts it from the net shear demand on the web. The IS 456 codal formula $V_u = V_c + V_s$ implicitly assumes $V_p = 0$. *(2 marks for the IS 1343 vs IS 456 distinction, 1 mark for the formula difference.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

### 📘 Question A: (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Modified]`

**Statement:** A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam of rectangular cross-section $b = 300$ mm, $D = 800$ mm, with effective cover to centroid of tension steel = 80 mm, is prestressed by a parabolic tendon with zero eccentricity at the supports and an eccentricity of $e_0 = 220$ mm at midspan. The effective prestressing force after all losses is $P_e = 1500$ kN. The beam has a simply supported span of $L = 12$ m and carries a uniformly distributed live load (factored) of $w = 40$ kN/m over the entire span in addition to its self-weight (factored $g = 12$ kN/m).

*Material:* $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compute the components of shear resistance $V_c$, $V_p$, and the total shear demand $V_u$ at a section $x = 2.5$ m from the support. Show that the section is in shear-deficient state.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Design the shear reinforcement (2-legged vertical stirrups) at $x = 2.5$ m. Verify spacing limits and minimum steel criteria as per IS 1343.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Geometry and effective depth.**

$$d \;=\; D - \text{cover} \;=\; 800 - 80 \;=\; 720 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute the slope of the parabolic tendon at $x = 2.5$ m.**

For a parabola with $e = 0$ at supports and $e_0$ at midspan, the eccentricity at distance $x$ is:

$$e(x) \;=\; 4\,e_{0}\left(\frac{x}{L}\right)\left(1 - \frac{x}{L}\right)$$

The slope is:

$$\frac{de}{dx} \;=\; \frac{4\,e_{0}}{L}\left(1 - \frac{2x}{L}\right) \;=\; \tan\alpha$$

At $x = 2.5$ m, $L = 12$ m:

$$\tan\alpha \;=\; \frac{4 \times 0.220}{12}\left(1 - \frac{2 \times 2.5}{12}\right) \;=\; 0.0733 \times 0.5833 \;=\; 0.0428$$

Since $\alpha$ is small, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha = 0.0428$.

**Step 3: Compute $V_p$.**

$$V_{p} \;=\; P_{e}\,\sin\alpha \;=\; 1500 \times 0.0428 \;=\; 64.2 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Compute the applied factored shear at $x = 2.5$ m from support.**

Total factored UDL: $w_u = w + g = 40 + 12 = 52$ kN/m.

Shear at $x = 2.5$ m from support (simply supported):

$$V_{u,\text{applied}} \;=\; w_{u}\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right) \;=\; 52 \times (6 - 2.5) \;=\; 52 \times 3.5 \;=\; 182 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 5: Compute the factored moment at $x = 2.5$ m.**

$$M_{u} \;=\; \frac{w_{u}\,x}{2}(L - x) \;=\; \frac{52 \times 2.5}{2} \times (12 - 2.5) \;=\; 65 \times 9.5 \;=\; 617.5 \;\text{kN·m}$$

**Step 6: Compute $V_c$ using IS 1343 / IS 456 formula.**

$$\frac{M_{u}}{V_{u}\,d} \;=\; \frac{617.5 \times 10^{6}}{182 \times 10^{3} \times 720} \;=\; \frac{617.5}{131.04} \;=\; 4.71$$

This is **greater than 3.0** (the upper limit), so **clamp $\lambda = 3.0$**.

For $f_{ck} = 40$ MPa, $\beta_1 = 1.0$.

$$V_{c} \;=\; \frac{0.67 \times 300 \times 720}{1 + 1.0 \times 3.0} \;=\; \frac{144720}{4.0} \;=\; 36180 \;\text{N} \;=\; 36.18 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 7: Net shear to be carried by stirrups.**

$$V_{\text{steel,req}} \;=\; V_{u,\text{applied}} - V_{c} - V_{p} \;=\; 182 - 36.18 - 64.2 \;=\; 81.62 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 8: Conclusion for part (a).**

Since $V_{u,\text{applied}} = 182$ kN $> V_c + V_p = 100.38$ kN, the section is **shear-deficient** and **transverse reinforcement is required**.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1: Assume 2-legged 10 mm $\phi$ stirrups.**

$$A_{sv} \;=\; 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(10)^{2} \;=\; 157.08 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2: Compute the required spacing.**

$$V_{s} \;=\; \frac{0.87\,f_{y}\,A_{sv}\,d}{s} \;\Rightarrow\; s \;=\; \frac{0.87\,f_{y}\,A_{sv}\,d}{V_{s}}$$

$$s \;=\; \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 157.08 \times 720}{81.62 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; \frac{49{,}179{,}048}{81{,}620} \;\approx\; 602.5 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 3: Apply IS 1343 spacing limits.**

$$s_{\max} \;=\; \min(0.75\,d,\; 300 \;\text{mm}) \;=\; \min(540,\; 300) \;=\; 300 \;\text{mm}$$

Since calculated $s = 602.5$ mm **exceeds** 300 mm, we must **use $s = 300$ mm**.

**Step 4: Verify minimum shear reinforcement.**

$$\frac{A_{sv,\min}}{b\,s} \;=\; \frac{0.4}{0.87\,f_{y}} \;=\; \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times 500} \;=\; 9.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b\,s} \;=\; \frac{157.08}{300 \times 300} \;=\; 1.74 \times 10^{-3} \;\ge\; 9.2 \times 10^{-4} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 5: Final Design.**

> ✅ **Provide 2-legged 10 mm $\phi$ vertical stirrups at 300 mm c/c throughout the shear-deficient zone.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part A vs Part B Distinction:**
> - In **Part (a)**, students often **forget to clamp** $M_u/(V_u d)$ between 1.0 and 3.0. Using the raw ratio (4.71) instead of clamping it to 3.0 will yield a **smaller** $V_c$ (about 22 kN instead of 36.18 kN) — but this is *less* safe, and IS 1343 specifically prohibits it. The conservative (larger) $V_c$ from clamping is the correct answer.
> - In **Part (b)**, students often **oversize the stirrups** (e.g., choose 12 mm $\phi$) to reduce spacing. This is wasteful. The correct KTU answer is to use the **smallest standard diameter** (8 mm or 10 mm) and accept the **maximum allowable spacing** (300 mm). Marks are deducted if the design is uneconomical.

---

### 📘 Question B: (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Modified]`

**Statement:** A pretensioned PSC I-section girder has the following properties at midspan: top flange $b_f = 800$ mm, $t_f = 120$ mm; web $b_w = 200$ mm, $D_w = 600$ mm; bottom flange $b_f = 500$ mm, $t_f = 150$ mm. Overall depth $D = 870$ mm. Effective prestress $P_e = 2000$ kN, applied at eccentricity $e = 300$ mm below NA (straight tendon). Factored shear at a critical section is $V_u = 320$ kN, with corresponding factored moment $M_u = 600$ kN·m. $f_{ck} = 50$ MPa, $f_y = 500$ MPa.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compute $V_c$ as per IS 1343. Explain why $V_p = 0$ in this case.

**(b)** [7 Marks] If the design calls for vertical stirrups of 8 mm $\phi$, 2-legged, determine the spacing. Check IS 1343 provisions.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (a):

**Step 1: Effective depth.**

Assuming cover to centroid of tension steel $= 75$ mm, $d = 870 - 75 = 795$ mm.

**Step 2: Compute $\lambda = M_u / (V_u \, d)$.**

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{600 \times 10^{6}}{320 \times 10^{3} \times 795} \;=\; \frac{600}{254.4} \;=\; 2.36$$

This is **between 1.0 and 3.0** — no clamping required.

**Step 3: Compute $V_c$ with $\beta_1 = 1.2$ (for $f_{ck} = 50$ MPa) and web width $b_w = 200$ mm.**

$$V_{c} \;=\; \frac{0.67 \times 200 \times 795}{1 + 1.2 \times 2.36} \;=\; \frac{106530}{3.832} \;=\; 27800 \;\text{N} \;\approx\; 27.8 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 4: Why $V_p = 0$.**

The tendon in this case is **straight and horizontal** (no curvature, no inclination). Therefore, $\alpha = 0°$ throughout the beam, and:

$$V_{p} \;=\; P_{e}\,\sin 0° \;=\; 0$$

In pretensioned members, tendons are typically straight to maintain symmetry. The vertical component of prestress is non-zero only if the tendon is **curved, harped, or draped**.

> **Conclusion for (a):** $V_c = 27.8$ kN, $V_p = 0$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part (b):

**Step 1: Net shear for stirrups.**

$$V_{s,\text{req}} \;=\; V_{u} - V_{c} - V_{p} \;=\; 320 - 27.8 - 0 \;=\; 292.2 \;\text{kN}$$

**Step 2: For 8 mm $\phi$, 2-legged stirrups.**

$$A_{sv} \;=\; 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}(8)^{2} \;=\; 100.53 \;\text{mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Spacing from $V_s$ formula.**

$$s \;=\; \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 100.53 \times 795}{292.2 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; \frac{34{,}751{,}808}{292{,}200} \;\approx\; 118.9 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 4: Check IS 1343 spacing limit.**

$$s_{\max} \;=\; \min(0.75 \times 795,\; 300) \;=\; \min(596.25,\; 300) \;=\; 300 \;\text{mm}$$

Since $s = 118.9 \le 300$, **OK**.

**Step 5: Check minimum shear reinforcement.**

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b_{w}\,s} \;=\; \frac{100.53}{200 \times 118.9} \;=\; 4.23 \times 10^{-3} \;\ge\; 9.2 \times 10^{-4} \quad \checkmark$$

> ✅ **Provide 2-legged 8 mm $\phi$ vertical stirrups at 115 mm c/c** (round down to a standard practical spacing of ~115 mm).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points in Question B:**
> 1. Using **overall width $b$** (e.g., 800 mm top flange) instead of **web width $b_w = 200$ mm** in the $V_c$ formula. IS 1343/IS 456 specifies using the **width of the web** for shear design of flanged sections. Marks: -1 to -2.
> 2. Forgetting that **$\beta_1$ changes with $f_{ck}$** (1.2 for 50 MPa, not 1.0). Marks: -1.
> 3. Forgetting the minimum shear reinforcement check (often skipped in haste). Marks: -1.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist. Memorize it. Carry it to the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Total shear strength formula:** $V_u = V_c + V_p + V_s$ (memorize in this exact order).
- [ ] **Concrete contribution $V_c$:** Use IS 1343 expression with $b$ = web width, $d$ = effective depth.
- [ ] **Clamp $\lambda = M_u/(V_u d)$** to the range **1.0 ≤ λ ≤ 3.0** before substituting into $V_c$.
- [ ] **$\beta_1$ values:** 0.8 (30 MPa), 1.0 (40 MPa), 1.2 (50 MPa).
- [ ] **Vertical prestress $V_p$:** $V_p = P_e \sin\alpha$. Zero for **straight horizontal tendons**.
- [ ] **Stirrup contribution $V_s$:** $V_s = 0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / s$ for vertical; add $(\sin\alpha_s + \cos\alpha_s)$ for inclined.
- [ ] **Spacing limit:** $s \le \min(0.75 d, 300 \text{ mm})$.
- [ ] **Minimum steel:** $A_{sv} / (b \cdot s) \ge 0.4 / (0.87 f_y)$.
- [ ] **Use web width $b_w$** for flanged sections (T-beam, I-beam), not flange width.
- [ ] **Always draw the FBD** with $V_c$, $V_p$, $V_s$ labeled before starting calculations.
- [ ] **Parabolic tendon slope:** $\tan\alpha = (4 e_0 / L)(1 - 2x/L)$; for small $\alpha$, $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha$.
- [ ] **Sign of $V_p$:** Positive if it opposes the applied shear; check by drawing the tendon direction.
- [ ] **Class 1, 2, 3 structures** (IS 1343 Cl. 11.2) determine the allowable principal tensile stress at service.
- [ ] **Serviceability vs Ultimate:** Principal stress (service) is for *cracking check*; $V_c, V_p, V_s$ (ultimate) is for *strength check*. Both are required in design.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Hook:** **"Concrete provides, Prestress pulls up, Steel pulls across"** — $V_c$ (concrete web bulk), $V_p$ (vertical tendon force), $V_s$ (stirrups crossing the diagonal crack).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modes of Failure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modes of Failure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODES OF FAILURE IN PRESTRESSED CONCRETE

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Modes of Failure Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard):**
> Modes of failure refer to the various distinct mechanisms by which a prestressed concrete (PSC) member can reach its ultimate limit state (ULS) under the combined action of applied loads, prestressing force, and self-weight. Each mode is governed by a specific limit-state criterion such as yielding of steel, crushing of concrete, anchorage pull-out, or shear-induced diagonal cracking.

A prestressed concrete section, unlike a conventionally reinforced concrete section, contains high-strength steel tendons pre-tensioned against the concrete. Because the entire cross-section is pre-compressed, the structure behaves very differently under load — and consequently, the ways it can fail are more numerous and often more brittle than in RCC.

### Conceptual Analogy: The "Compressed Spring Barrel"

> [!NOTE]
> Imagine a wooden barrel held together tightly by steel hoops. The hoops apply an inward radial compression that allows the wooden staves to resist huge internal pressures without splitting apart.
> If you slowly increase the internal pressure:
> 1. First, the hoops might yield (steel failure).
> 2. The staves might crush inward (concrete failure).
> 3. A stave might split along the grain (splitting/bursting failure).
> 4. A hoop might slip off (anchorage/bond failure).
>
> A PSC beam behaves exactly like this barrel. The **modes of failure** are the different ways the "barrel" (beam) breaks apart as the external load overcomes the internal "pre-compression".

### Why Multiple Modes Exist

In a PSC member, the following key forces/materials coexist:
- **High tensile steel tendons** (typical tensile strength **f<sub>pu</sub> = 1860 N/mm²** for strands)
- **High-grade concrete** (typical cube strength **f<sub>ck</sub> = 40, 50, 60 N/mm²**)
- **Anchorage hardware** (cheese plates, wedges, ducts)
- **Shear connectors / stirrups** (mild steel, **f<sub>y</sub> = 415 / 500 N/mm²**)
- **Duct grout / bond agents** (post-tensioned systems)

Because of this complexity, failure is **NOT** only about bending. It can originate from many zones of the member simultaneously or sequentially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Limit State of Collapse design philosophy (per IS 1343:2012) requires that *all* modes of failure be checked and shown to have adequate safety. A beam cannot be declared safe based purely on flexural checks.

## 2. Enumeration of All Major Modes of Failure

The following is the complete taxonomy recognized by IS 1343 and IS 456 for PSC design:

1. **Flexural Failure**
   - Under-reinforced (tension-controlled, ductile)
   - Balanced
   - Over-reinforced (compression-controlled, brittle)
2. **Shear Failure**
   - Web-shear (diagonal tension cracking)
   - Flexural-shear (shear combined with flexure)
3. **Torsional Failure**
   - Pure torsion cracking
   - Combined shear + torsion
4. **Anchorage Failure**
   - Wedge slip / wedge pull-out
   - Bursting behind anchorage
   - Spalling of concrete at end-block
5. **Bond Failure** (in pre-tensioned members)
   - Slip of tendons at the transmission length
6. **Bearing Failure** at supports / loading points
7. **Crushing of Concrete at Transfer** (release stage)
8. **Lateral Torsional Buckling** (slender PSC beams)
9. **Fatigue Failure** (bridges, railway sleepers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 1. Flexural Failure — The Three Sub-Modes

The classification mirrors that of RCC but is far more critical in PSC because the **reinforcement index (ω)** is typically high.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reinforcement Index:**
> $$\omega = \frac{A_p \, f_{p}}{b \, d \, f_{ck}}$$
> where **A<sub>p</sub>** is the area of prestressing steel, **f<sub>p</sub>** is the design stress in tendon at ultimate state, **b** is the breadth, and **d** is the effective depth.

### Sub-Mode 1: Under-Reinforced Failure (Ductile)

- The tendon yields **before** the concrete reaches its ultimate compressive strain.
- Failure is initiated by excessive elongation of steel → wide cracking → large deflection → warning before collapse.
- **Preferred mode** in design (per IS 1343).

### Sub-Mode 2: Balanced Failure

- Both tendon yielding and concrete crushing occur simultaneously.
- The critical reinforcement index is:
$$\omega_{bal} = 0.43 \, \frac{f_{ck}}{f_p} \cdot \frac{x_{u,max}}{d}$$

### Sub-Mode 3: Over-Reinforced Failure (Brittle)

- Concrete crushes **before** the tendon yields.
- Sudden, catastrophic collapse with no prior warning.
- **Code explicitly forbids this mode** for flexure (Clause 18.1.1, IS 1343).

## 2. Shear Failure — Web-Shear vs Flexural-Shear

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In PSC, shear cracks appear at much lower loads than in RCC because the **principal tensile stress** in the web is very sensitive to the magnitude of prestress.

### Web-Shear Cracking
- Occurs in the web before any flexural crack appears.
- Caused when **principal tensile stress** exceeds concrete tensile strength.
- For an I-section:
$$\tau = \frac{V}{b_w \, d} + \frac{V \, a \, y}{I \, b_w}$$
where **a** = shear span, **y** = distance from NA to the fibre considered.

The principal tensile stress is:
$$f_t = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \tau^2} - \sigma_x$$

### Flexural-Shear Cracking
- Occurs when an existing flexural crack propagates diagonally into the web.
- Governed by combined σ-τ interaction.

## 3. Anchorage & Bursting Failure

When a tendon is anchored at the end of a post-tensioned beam, a huge concentrated force enters the concrete over a small bearing area. This creates **transverse tensile stresses** behind the anchorage that can split the end-block.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bursting Tensile Force (Magnel's Approximation):**
> $$F_{bst} = 0.3 \, P_k \left(1 - \frac{a}{y_{po}}\right)$$
> where **P<sub>k</sub>** = prestressing force at transfer, **a** = bearing plate dimension, **y<sub>po</sub>** = depth of end-block.

If **F<sub>bst</sub>** is not resisted by closed stirrups/bursting reinforcement, the end-block splits horizontally — a sudden and dangerous failure.

## 4. Bond Failure (Pre-Tensioned Members)

In pretensioned beams, the tendon must transfer stress to concrete by **bond** over a short length near the end:
$$L_t = \frac{f_{pi} \, \phi}{4 \, \tau_{bd}}$$
where **L<sub>t</sub>** = transmission length, **f<sub>pi</sub>** = initial prestress, **φ** = tendon diameter, **τ<sub>bd</sub>** = bond stress (≈ **1.2 N/mm²** for strands).

## KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Reinforcement Index | $\omega = A_p f_p / (b d f_{ck})$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | Critical ω (balanced) | $\omega_{bal} = 0.43 (f_{ck}/f_p)(x_{u,max}/d)$ | IS 1343 |
| 3 | Principal Tensile Stress | $f_t = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \tau^2} - \sigma_x$ | N/mm² |
| 4 | Web Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = V / (b_w b d) + (V a y)/(I b_w)$ | I-section |
| 5 | Bursting Force (Magnel) | $F_{bst} = 0.3 P_k (1 - a/y_{po})$ | N |
| 6 | Transmission Length | $L_t = f_{pi} \phi / (4 \tau_{bd})$ | mm |
| 7 | Design Shear Strength | $\tau_c$ from Table 12, IS 456 | N/mm² |
| 8 | Ultimate Moment Capacity | $M_u = 0.87 f_p A_p d(1 - A_p f_p / (b d f_{ck}))$ | N·mm |
| 9 | Concrete Strain Limit | $\epsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$ (M30+); 0.002 (M≤25) | — |
| 10 | Modular Ratio | $E_s / E_c$ (short-term); 0.8 × ratio (long-term) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas form the backbone of the ULS design check. Every KTU numerical question on PSC design (Part B, 14 marks) requires at least two of these formulas to be applied correctly.

# STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 1. Derivation of the Critical (Balanced) Reinforcement Index

We start from strain compatibility at the ultimate limit state. For a rectangular PSC section:

### Step 1 — Define Strain Compatibility

Let the neutral axis depth be **x<sub>u</sub>** at ULS. The top fibre strain is the **ultimate concrete strain**:

$$\epsilon_{cu} = 0.0035$$

The strain at tendon level (distance **d** from top) by linear strain profile:

$$\epsilon_p = 0.0035 \, \frac{d - x_u}{x_u}$$

### Step 2 — Determine Stress in Tendon at ULS

The tendon has an initial prestress **f<sub>pi</sub>**. The additional strain beyond decompression is **(ε<sub>p</sub> − ε<sub>pe</sub>)** where ε<sub>pe</sub> corresponds to the prestress level.

Assuming the tendon has already reached yield plateau:

$$f_p = f_{pu} \quad \text{when} \quad \epsilon_p \ge \epsilon_{py} + 0.002$$

Otherwise, for design as per IS 1343 (Cl. 18.4):

$$f_p = f_{pu} - \frac{0.3}{\epsilon_p} \left( f_{pu} - f_{pi} \right) \le f_{py}$$

### Step 3 — Force Equilibrium

Compression in concrete (Whitney stress block):

$$C = 0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u$$

Tension in tendon:

$$T = A_p \, f_p$$

For equilibrium C = T:

$$0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = A_p \, f_p$$

### Step 4 — Solve for x<sub>u</sub>/d

$$\frac{x_u}{d} = \frac{A_p \, f_p}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, d}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute x<sub>u,max</sub>/d = 0.43 (IS 1343 limit for Fe 415/500)

$$A_{p,\text{crit}} = \frac{0.36 \times 0.43 \, b \, d \, f_{ck}}{f_p}$$

$$\boxed{A_{p,\text{crit}} = 0.155 \, b \, d \, \frac{f_{ck}}{f_p}}$$

Hence the **critical reinforcement index**:

$$\omega_{bal} = \frac{A_{p,\text{crit}} \, f_p}{b \, d \, f_{ck}} = 0.155$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Note that for PSC, ω<sub>bal</sub> ≈ **0.155** is much lower than for RCC (≈ 0.43 × 0.87 ≈ 0.37). This means PSC sections reach balanced condition at much lower steel ratios because the prestressing steel is already at very high stress. This is why IS 1343 also caps the steel area more conservatively.

## 2. Derivation of Bursting Tensile Force (Magnel's Method)

Magnel (1954) treated the end-block as an elastic half-plane loaded by a uniform bearing pressure over a rectangular area.

### Step 1 — Average Bearing Pressure

$$p = \frac{P_k}{a \, b}$$

### Step 2 — Geometry of Bursting Zone

The bursting zone lies at a distance **y<sub>po</sub>/2** behind the anchor plate, where **y<sub>po</sub>** = depth of end-block.

### Step 3 — Total Tensile Force Across the Bursting Plane

By equilibrium of the bursting block (integrating the lateral stresses):

$$F_{bst} = \int_0^{y_{po}} \sigma_y \, dx$$

For an infinitely stiff plate, the result simplifies to:

$$F_{bst} = 0.3 \, P_k \left(1 - \frac{a}{y_{po}}\right)$$

### Step 4 — Bursting Reinforcement Required

Let **A<sub>sv,bst</sub>** be the area of closed stirrups (vertical legs) crossing the bursting plane, with yield strength **f<sub>y</sub>**:

$$A_{sv,bst} = \frac{F_{bst}}{f_y}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This reinforcement must be placed within a distance of **y<sub>po</sub>/3** from the anchor face, uniformly distributed. Failure to provide it leads to splitting of the end-block — a classical anchorage-zone failure observed in field collapses.

## 3. Derivation of Principal Tensile Stress in Web

Given vertical shear **V**, prestress **P**, and section properties:

### Step 1 — Direct Compressive Stress at Fibre y (below NA)

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \, e \, y}{I} - \frac{M \, y}{I}$$

### Step 2 — Shear Stress at Same Fibre (for I-section)

$$\tau = \frac{V \, A\bar{y}}{I \, b_w}$$

where **Aȳ** is the first moment of area above the fibre.

### Step 3 — Principal Stress (Mohr's Circle)

The maximum principal tensile stress:

$$f_t = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

Since **σ<sub>x</sub>** is compressive (negative in sign convention), this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{f_t = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \tau^2} - \sigma_x}$$

### Step 4 — Check Against Concrete Tensile Strength

$$f_t \le f_{ct} \quad \text{where} \quad f_{ct} = 0.24 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Using **+σ<sub>x</sub>** instead of **−σ<sub>x</sub>** in the principal stress formula because they forget that prestress is *compressive*. This produces wildly unsafe results.

## 4. Worked Analytical Summary Table

| Failure Mode | Trigger Condition | Brittle/Ductile | Code Reference |
|--------------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Flexural (over-reinforced) | Concrete crushes before tendon yields | **Brittle** | IS 1343 Cl. 18.1.1 — not permitted |
| Flexural (balanced) | Both yield simultaneously | Brittle | IS 1343 Cl. 18.4 |
| Flexural (under-reinforced) | Tendon yields first | **Ductile** | IS 1343 preferred |
| Web-shear | $f_t > f_{ct}$ | Brittle | IS 1343 Cl. 19.4 |
| Flexural-shear | Shear + flexure | Semi-brittle | IS 1343 Cl. 19.5 |
| Bursting | $F_{bst}$ unresisted | Brittle | IS 1343 Cl. 19.8 |
| Bond (pretension) | $\tau_{bd}$ exceeded | Ductile | IS 1343 Cl. 18.6 |
| Anchorage pull-out | Wedge fails | Brittle | IS 1343 Cl. 19.9 |

# KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between under-reinforced and over-reinforced modes of flexural failure in prestressed concrete beams. Which one is preferred and why?**

**Model Answer:**

In a prestressed concrete beam, two extreme flexural failure modes exist based on the relative amounts of concrete and tendon steel:

**Under-reinforced failure:**
- The quantity of prestressing steel is low compared to the compressive capacity of concrete.
- As the load increases, the tendon reaches its yield stress **f<sub>py</sub>** first.
- The steel continues to elongate plastically while the concrete compression zone gradually crushes.
- The section exhibits large deflections and wide cracks **before** collapse, providing ample warning.
- Reinforcement index **ω < ω<sub>bal</sub>**.

**Over-reinforced failure:**
- The quantity of prestressing steel is large.
- As the load increases, the concrete in the top fibre reaches its ultimate strain **ε<sub>cu</sub> = 0.0035** first and crushes.
- The tendon remains well below its yield stress at the moment of collapse.
- Failure is **sudden and brittle**, with no warning cracks.
- Reinforcement index **ω > ω<sub>bal</sub>**.

**Preferred Mode:** IS 1343 mandates **under-reinforced design** because:
1. It provides ductile failure with adequate warning.
2. It allows redistribution of moments in continuous members.
3. It avoids catastrophic brittle collapse.

(3 marks)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is bursting of concrete in post-tensioned beams? How is bursting tensile force estimated?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bursting of Concrete:**
In a post-tensioned beam, the prestressing tendon is anchored at the end via a bearing plate or anchor head. The tendon force **P<sub>k</sub>** enters the concrete over a small bearing area, creating very high localized compressive stress. This concentrated load generates **transverse tensile stresses** behind the anchor plate. If these tensile stresses exceed the tensile strength of concrete, the end-block splits open — this phenomenon is called **bursting failure**.

**Estimation — Magnel's Formula:**
The bursting tensile force is estimated as:
$$F_{bst} = 0.3 \, P_k \left(1 - \frac{a}{y_{po}}\right)$$
where:
- **P<sub>k</sub>** = characteristic prestressing force at transfer
- **a** = dimension of bearing plate in the direction of splitting
- **y<sub>po</sub>** = depth of the end-block

**Control Measure:**
Closed stirrups (bursting reinforcement) of area **A<sub>sv,bst</sub> = F<sub>bst</sub> / f<sub>y</sub>** must be provided within the bursting zone (typically up to **y<sub>po</sub>/3** from the anchor face).

(3 marks)

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 3 internal choice provides two questions (Q3A or Q3B). Students must answer ONE in full.

---

### Question 3A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]

**A pretensioned prestressed concrete beam of rectangular section 200 mm wide and 500 mm deep is provided with 6 high-tensile wires of 7 mm diameter, initially stressed to 1100 N/mm². The beam is subjected to a working load producing a maximum bending moment of 60 kN·m. The cube strength of concrete at transfer is 35 N/mm² and at 28 days is 50 N/mm². Take losses of prestress = 18%, f<sub>pu</sub> = 1600 N/mm², f<sub>py</sub> = 1400 N/mm², and Es = 210 kN/mm². Examine the mode of flexural failure of the beam.**

#### Part (a) — Calculate the Ultimate Moment of Resistance and identify the mode of failure. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given Data**
- b = 200 mm, d = 500 − 50 = 450 mm (assuming cover 50 mm)
- 6 wires of 7 mm diameter → **A<sub>p</sub> = 6 × (π/4)(7)² = 6 × 38.48 = 230.9 mm²**
- Initial prestress **f<sub>pi</sub> = 1100 N/mm²**
- Losses = 18% → effective prestress **f<sub>pe</sub> = 0.82 × 1100 = 902 N/mm²**
- M = 60 kN·m at working load
- f<sub>ck</sub> at transfer = 35 N/mm²; at 28 days = 50 N/mm²
- f<sub>pu</sub> = 1600 N/mm², f<sub>py</sub> = 1400 N/mm²

**Step 2 — Estimate Stress in Tendon at ULS (f<sub>p</sub>)**

From IS 1343 Cl. 18.4:
$$f_p = f_{pu} - \frac{0.3}{\epsilon_p}(f_{pu} - f_{pi})$$

Assuming ε<sub>p</sub> ≥ 0.005 (yield plus strain hardening), the tendon fully yields:
$$f_p = f_{py} = 1400 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

(More accurately, taking ε<sub>p</sub> ≈ 0.005 from strain compatibility with assumed x<sub>u</sub>/d = 0.3.)

**Step 3 — Locate Neutral Axis**

Equilibrium: C = T
$$0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b \, x_u = A_p \, f_p$$

$$0.36 \times 50 \times 200 \times x_u = 230.9 \times 1400$$

$$3600 \, x_u = 323260$$

$$x_u = 89.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Check x<sub>u,max</sub>**

For Fe 500/Fe 415 + PSC tendons:
$$x_{u,max} = 0.43 \, d = 0.43 \times 450 = 193.5 \text{ mm}$$

Since **x<sub>u</sub> = 89.8 mm < 193.5 mm = x<sub>u,max</sub>**, the section is **under-reinforced**.

**Step 5 — Reinforcement Index Check**
$$\omega = \frac{A_p f_p}{b d f_{ck}} = \frac{230.9 \times 1400}{200 \times 450 \times 50} = \frac{323260}{4500000} = 0.0718$$

Since **ω = 0.0718 < ω<sub>bal</sub> ≈ 0.155**, the section is well within the under-reinforced regime.

**Step 6 — Ultimate Moment of Resistance**
$$M_u = 0.87 \, f_p \, A_p \, d \left(1 - \frac{A_p f_p}{b d f_{ck}}\right)$$

$$M_u = 0.87 \times 1400 \times 230.9 \times 450 \times \left(1 - \frac{230.9 \times 1400}{200 \times 450 \times 50}\right)$$

$$M_u = 126{,}544{,}000 \times (1 - 0.0718)$$

$$M_u = 126{,}544{,}000 \times 0.9282 = 117.45 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_u = 117.45 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 7 — Identify Mode of Failure**

Since x<sub>u</sub> < x<sub>u,max</sub> and ω < ω<sub>bal</sub>, the failure mode is **under-reinforced (ductile) failure** — the tendon will yield first, followed by gradual crushing of concrete.

(7 marks)

#### Part (b) — If the tendon area were doubled, examine the change in failure mode. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Doubled Area**
$$A_p' = 2 \times 230.9 = 461.8 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Assume Tendon Has Yielded**
$$T = 461.8 \times 1400 = 646{,}520 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Find New x<sub>u</sub>**
$$x_u = \frac{T}{0.36 \, f_{ck} \, b} = \frac{646{,}520}{0.36 \times 50 \times 200} = \frac{646{,}520}{3600} = 179.6 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with x<sub>u,max</sub>**
$$x_u = 179.6 \text{ mm} < x_{u,max} = 193.5 \text{ mm}$$

Still under-reinforced — but only marginally.

**Step 5 — Check Critical A<sub>p</sub> for Balanced Condition**
$$A_{p,\text{crit}} = 0.155 \times b \times d \times \frac{f_{ck}}{f_p} = 0.155 \times 200 \times 450 \times \frac{50}{1400}$$

$$A_{p,\text{crit}} = 0.155 \times 90{,}000 \times 0.0357 = 498.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion**

Since **A<sub>p</sub>' = 461.8 mm² < A<sub>p,crit</sub> = 498.2 mm²**, the section is still under-reinforced — but **very close to the balanced condition**.

If the design were further increased to **A<sub>p</sub> > 498.2 mm²**, the failure mode would shift to **balanced → over-reinforced (brittle)**. As a rule of thumb, designers should keep ω ≤ 0.75 × ω<sub>bal</sub> to maintain ductility.

**Final Answer:**
With doubled area, the beam still exhibits **under-reinforced ductile failure**, but the safety margin reduces significantly. The ultimate moment rises only marginally because the lever arm decreases faster than the tension force increases.

(7 marks)

---

### Question 3B: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]

**A post-tensioned concrete beam of I-section has the following properties at the support: flange width = 300 mm, flange thickness = 100 mm, web thickness = 150 mm, overall depth = 600 mm. It is prestressed by a single tendon with initial force P<sub>i</sub> = 1500 kN at an eccentricity e = 200 mm. A uniformly distributed load on the half span produces a shear force V = 300 kN at the support section. Calculate the principal tensile stress at the centroidal axis and at the junction of flange and web. Take I = 1.8 × 10⁹ mm⁴, A = 138,000 mm². Verify the mode of shear failure.**

#### Part (a) — Compute principal tensile stresses and check against concrete tensile strength. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Section Properties at NA (Centroid)**
- Area moment = 1.8 × 10⁹ mm⁴
- Total area A = 138,000 mm²
- Centroid depth y from top = 300 mm (since symmetric)

**Step 2 — Direct Stress at Centroid (y = 0)**

The prestress produces uniform compression at NA:
$$\sigma_x = \frac{P_i}{A} = \frac{1500 \times 1000}{138{,}000} = 10.87 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compressive)}$$

**Step 3 — Shear Stress at NA**

First moment of area above NA (or below — same by symmetry):
- Upper flange + half of web (in terms of first moment about NA)

For an I-section, with **y from NA**:
- Flange contribution: **A<sub>1</sub>ȳ<sub>1</sub>** where A<sub>1</sub> = 300 × 100 = 30,000 mm², ȳ<sub>1</sub> = 250 mm = 250 mm above NA

Wait — flange is from 0 to 100 from top, NA at 300 mm, so flange centroid distance from NA = 300 − 50 = **250 mm**.
- A<sub>1</sub>ȳ<sub>1</sub> = 30,000 × 250 = 7.5 × 10⁶ mm³
- Half of web above NA: A<sub>2</sub> = 150 × 200 = 30,000 mm², ȳ<sub>2</sub> = 100 mm
- A<sub>2</sub>ȳ<sub>2</sub> = 30,000 × 100 = 3.0 × 10⁶ mm³
- Total **Aȳ = 10.5 × 10⁶ mm³**

Shear stress:
$$\tau = \frac{V \, A\bar{y}}{I \, b_w} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 10.5 \times 10^6}{1.8 \times 10^9 \times 150}$$

$$\tau = \frac{3.15 \times 10^{12}}{2.7 \times 10^{11}} = 11.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Principal Tensile Stress at NA**

$$\sigma_x = -10.87 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}$$
$$\tau = 11.67 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$f_t = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \tau^2} - \sigma_x$$

$$f_t = \sqrt{(10.87)^2 + (11.67)^2} - (-10.87)$$

$$f_t = \sqrt{118.16 + 136.19} + 10.87$$

$$f_t = \sqrt{254.35} + 10.87 = 15.95 + 10.87 = 26.82 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This is enormous and shows that web-shear failure is imminent if unreinforced.**

(7 marks)

#### Part (b) — Compute principal stress at flange-web junction and conclude on failure mode. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Locate Flange-Web Junction from NA**

Junction is **100 mm below top fibre**, hence its distance from NA:
$$y = 300 - 100 = 200 \text{ mm (above NA)}$$

**Step 2 — Direct Stress at Junction**

$$\sigma_x = -\frac{P_i}{A} - \frac{P_i \, e \, y}{I} = -10.87 - \frac{1500 \times 1000 \times 200 \times 200}{1.8 \times 10^9}$$

$$\sigma_x = -10.87 - \frac{6 \times 10^{10}}{1.8 \times 10^9} = -10.87 - 33.33 = -44.20 \text{ N/mm}^2 (compression)$$

**Step 3 — Shear Stress at Junction**

At the junction, **b = 300 mm** (flange width, because shear flow is taken by the wider flange):
- Above the junction, only the upper flange contributes: A = 30,000 mm², ȳ = 250 mm
- Aȳ = 30,000 × 250 = 7.5 × 10⁶ mm³

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot A\bar{y}}{I \cdot b} = \frac{300{,}000 \times 7.5 \times 10^6}{1.8 \times 10^9 \times 300} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{12}}{5.4 \times 10^{11}} = 4.17 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Principal Tensile Stress at Junction**

$$f_t = \sqrt{(44.20)^2 + (4.17)^2} - (-44.20)$$

$$f_t = \sqrt{1953.64 + 17.39} + 44.20$$

$$f_t = \sqrt{1971.03} + 44.20 = 44.40 + 44.20 = 88.60 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **At the junction, the principal tensile stress is even higher due to combined bending + prestress compression. This is the most critical fibre.**

**Step 5 — Compare with Concrete Tensile Strength**

For **f<sub>ck</sub> = 50 N/mm²**:
$$f_{ct} = 0.24 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.24 \sqrt{50} = 1.697 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Comparison:**
- At NA: **f<sub>t</sub> = 26.82 N/mm² ≫ f<sub>ct</sub> = 1.697 N/mm²**
- At junction: **f<sub>t</sub> = 88.60 N/mm² ≫ f<sub>ct</sub> = 1.697 N/mm²**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion — Mode of Failure: WEB-SHEAR FAILURE (Brittle)**
> The principal tensile stress at BOTH locations is far greater than the tensile strength of concrete. The section will suffer **diagonal tension cracking** in the web long before flexural failure can occur. **Web-shear reinforcement (vertical stirrups) MUST be designed** to carry the entire shear force, treating the concrete as cracked.

**Required Stirrup Spacing:**
- Design shear strength of concrete ≈ 0 (since section is cracked).
- All of V = 300 kN must be carried by stirrups:
$$V_u = \frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{s_v}$$

For 2-legged 10 mm φ stirrups, A<sub>sv</sub> = 157 mm², f<sub>y</sub> = 415 N/mm², d ≈ 600 mm:

$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 157 \times 600}{300{,}000} = \frac{34{,}033{,}650}{300{,}000} = 113.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide 10 mm φ 2-legged stirrups @ 110 mm c/c** at the support, gradually spaced to 250 mm c/c at midspan.

(7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark Losers):**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** Treating prestress as tension (+ve) instead of compression (−ve) when computing principal stresses — leads to absurdly low (or negative) tensile stresses.
> 2. **Forgetting the Shear Component in Flexure:** Many students compute only **V/b<sub>w</sub>d** instead of the full **V·Aȳ/(I·b<sub>w</sub>)** — this is technically wrong because it ignores the variation of shear stress across the depth.
> 3. **Ignoring the Junction Width:** At the flange-web junction, students often forget to switch **b** from **b<sub>w</sub>** (web width) to **b<sub>f</sub>** (flange width). This either over-estimates or under-estimates shear stress.
> 4. **Not Checking ω vs ω<sub>bal</sub>:** Many numerical answers compute M<sub>u</sub> without ever commenting on **whether the failure is under- or over-reinforced**. This loses 2-3 marks easily.
> 5. **Mixing Working and Design Stresses:** Using **f<sub>ck</sub>** = 35 instead of 50 (or vice versa) in the ULS equation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Modes of Failure in PSC:**

**Core Concepts**
- [ ] Modes of failure are NOT limited to flexure; shear, torsion, anchorage, and bond failures also exist.
- [ ] In PSC, the **reinforcement index (ω)** is naturally lower than in RCC due to high f<sub>pu</sub>.
- [ ] **Under-reinforced failure is ductile and is the preferred design mode** (IS 1343).
- [ ] **Over-reinforced failure is brittle and is explicitly forbidden** by IS 1343.

**Flexural Failure**
- [ ] Three sub-modes: under, balanced, over.
- [ ] Balanced condition: **x<sub>u,max</sub>/d = 0.43** for f<sub>y</sub> ≤ 415 N/mm² (revised values apply for Fe 500).
- [ ] Critical tendon area: **A<sub>p,crit</sub> = 0.155 b d (f<sub>ck</sub>/f<sub>p</sub>)**.
- [ ] Strain in concrete at ULS: **ε<sub>cu</sub> = 0.0035** (M30 and above).

**Shear Failure**
- [ ] Two types: web-shear (pure diagonal tension) and flexural-shear (combined).
- [ ] Use **principal tensile stress formula** at every fibre.
- [ ] At NA, shear stress is maximum; at extreme fibre, shear stress = 0.
- [ ] Concrete tensile strength: **f<sub>ct</sub> = 0.24 √f<sub>ck</sub>** (N/mm²).

**Anchorage Failure**
- [ ] Bursting force: **F<sub>bst</sub> = 0.3 P<sub>k</sub> (1 − a/y<sub>po</sub>)**.
- [ ] Closed stirrups required within **y<sub>po</sub>/3** of the anchor face.
- [ ] Spalling reinforcement required near the loaded face.

**Bond Failure (Pre-tensioned)**
- [ ] Transmission length: **L<sub>t</sub> = f<sub>pi</sub> φ / (4 τ<sub>bd</sub>)**.
- [ ] For strands, τ<sub>bd</sub> ≈ 1.2 N/mm² at transfer.
- [ ] Hoyer effect helps bond near the ends of pretensioned members.

**Numerical Problem Solving Tips**
- [ ] Always check **ω < ω<sub>bal</sub>** before concluding failure mode.
- [ ] Always verify **f<sub>t</sub> < f<sub>ct</sub>** before assuming shear is safe.
- [ ] Always include the bursting reinforcement check for post-tensioned end-blocks.
- [ ] Use **f<sub>ck</sub>** at 28 days for ULS calculations and **f<sub>ci</sub>** at transfer for elastic checks.

**Common Numerical Traps**
- [ ] Doubling A<sub>p</sub> does NOT double M<sub>u</sub> — the lever arm shrinks.
- [ ] The most critical fibre for principal tension is the **flange-web junction** in I-beams, NOT the NA.
- [ ] Sign convention: compression is **negative**, tension is **positive**.
- [ ] Always mention the **mode of failure** as part of your final conclusion — this is mandatory for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Limit State of Collapse for Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Limit State of Collapse for Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (IS 1343 Terminology)

The **Limit State of Collapse for Shear** in a Prestressed Concrete (PSC) member refers to the ultimate state at which the member, subjected to a combination of flexure, axial prestress, and transverse shear, fails by diagonal tension cracking, web crushing, or shear-compression failure. According to **IS 1343:1980 (Reaffirmed 2017)**, this limit state requires that the **factored design shear force (Vu)** at any section shall not exceed the **design shear strength of the section (Vn)**, which is the sum of the design shear resistance offered by the concrete (Vc) and the shear reinforcement (Vs).

Mathematically, the design condition is:

$$V_u \le V_c + V_s$$

where Vu is the shear force due to ultimate loads (factored), Vc is the shear capacity of the concrete section, and Vs is the shear capacity provided by the web reinforcement (stirrups and/or bent-up bars).

> [!IMPORTANT]
