> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PSC beams, the **compressive fiber stress induced by the prestressing force** reduces the magnitude of the **principal tensile stress (σt)** under combined bending and shear. This is the single most important reason why PSC beams exhibit *significantly higher shear resistance* compared to equivalent Reinforced Cement Concrete (RCC) beams of the same cross-section.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you are holding a **thick hardcover book flat on your palm**, with your fingers gripping the long edges.

- If you apply a downward push at the center (**bending moment**), the fibers at the bottom stretch (tension) and top compress.
- If you push one short edge forward and the other backward (**shear force**), the book wants to "slide" — its internal fibers develop diagonal tension.
- Now, imagine pressing the book *strongly down on the table* with your other hand before you do all this — this pre-compression "glues" the pages together, resisting the diagonal sliding. This is exactly what **prestress (P)** does inside a concrete beam: it pre-compresses the section so that diagonal tension cracks find it hard to form.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why shear is critical in PSC:** Unlike RCC, the entire concrete cross-section is *initially stressed in compression*. The high prestress at the centroidal axis can, however, create a different principal stress distribution — sometimes a small **compressive principal stress at the neutral axis** is sufficient, but at the **bottom fiber** where bending tensile stress is added, principal tension may govern.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Concrete grades (IS 1343):** M30, M35, M40, M45, M50, M55, M60 (minimum M30 for PSC)
- **Steel for shear reinforcement:** Mild Steel (Fe 415 / Fe 500 with fy = 415 / 500 N/mm²)
- **Concrete design shear strength (τc):** read from IS 1343 **Table 12** (values in N/mm² or MPa)
- **Maximum shear stress (τc,max):** read from IS 1343 **Table 11**
- **Partial safety factor for concrete (γm):** 1.5
- **Partial safety factor for steel (γm):** 1.15
- **Design shear force factor:** Vu = 1.5 × (shear due to DL + LL) for Limit State method

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Shear Failure in PSC Beams

A PSC beam can fail in shear through one of the following mechanisms:

1. **Diagonal Tension Failure** — most common. Cracks appear at ~45° to the beam axis when principal tensile stress exceeds the tensile strength of concrete.
2. **Shear-Compression Failure** — occurs near supports; concrete crushes above the diagonal crack.
3. **Web Crushing (Web Shear Compression)** — failure due to principal *compressive* stress exceeding concrete compressive strength (rare, but governs for heavily prestressed deep beams).
4. **Flexural-Shear Failure** — combines flexural and shear cracks, initiating as flexural cracks that later propagate diagonally.
5. **Splitting along Prestressing Tendon** — local bond failure near anchorages.

### 2.2 Principal Stress Analysis (Foundation Theory)

For a beam element at any fiber, the stresses acting are:

- **σx** = Flexural stress (from bending) + Pre-compressive stress (from prestress)
- **σy** = 0 (no vertical stress on the beam surface)
- **τ** = Shear stress from transverse loading

The **principal stresses** are calculated as:

$$\sigma_{t}, \sigma_{c} = \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

> **Note:** Here σc (compressive principal) is positive and σt (tensile principal) is negative. Some texts reverse the sign convention; KTU convention follows the formula above.

**Key Insight:** The higher the compressive σx due to prestress, the **smaller the magnitude of σt**, improving shear capacity.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Shear Design Logic (IS 1343 Methodology)

**Step 1 — Compute Factored Shear Force**
$$V_{u} = 1.5 \times V_{\text{(DL+LL at that section)}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Nominal Shear Stress**
$$\tau_{v} = \frac{V_{u}}{b \cdot d}$$

where *b* = breadth of web (in mm) and *d* = effective depth (in mm).

**Step 3 — Check against Maximum Shear Stress Limit**
Compare τv with **τc,max** (IS 1343 Table 11). If τv > τc,max, the section dimensions must be increased.

**Step 4 — Determine Design Shear Strength of Concrete (τc)**
Look up τc from **IS 1343 Table 12** based on:
- Grade of concrete
- Percentage of longitudinal tension steel (pt = 100·Ast/bd)

**Step 5 — Compare τv and τc**

- If **τv ≤ τc** → Minimum shear reinforcement is provided (per IS 1343 Cl. 11.3).
- If **τv > τc** → Shear reinforcement must be designed.

**Step 6 — Compute Shear to be Carried by Reinforcement**
$$V_{s} = V_{u} - \tau_{c} \cdot b \cdot d$$

**Step 7 — Design Web Reinforcement**
- **For vertical stirrups:**
$$V_{s} = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_{y} \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{s_{v}}$$
- **For bent-up bars at angle α to horizontal:**
$$V_{s} = 0.87 \cdot f_{y} \cdot A_{sv} \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot d$$

**Step 8 — Spacing Limits (IS 1343 Cl. 11.4)**
- Minimum shear reinforcement: Asv,bv/(sv·b) ≥ 0.4 / (0.87·fy)
- Maximum spacing: 0.75·d or 300 mm (whichever is less)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula / Quantity** | **Expression** | **Units** | **IS Clause / Source** |
|:-----:|:------------------------|:---------------|:---------:|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Factored shear force | $V_u = 1.5 \cdot V$ | N | IS 1343 Cl. 11.1 |
| 2 | Nominal shear stress | $\tau_v = V_u / (b \cdot d)$ | N/mm² | IS 1343 Cl. 11.2 |
| 3 | Max. shear stress limit | $\tau_v \le \tau_{c,\max}$ | N/mm² | IS 1343 Table 11 |
| 4 | Design shear strength of concrete | $\tau_c$ (from Table 12) | N/mm² | IS 1343 Table 12 |
| 5 | Concrete shear capacity | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ | N | — |
| 6 | Steel shear capacity | $V_s = V_u - V_c$ | N | — |
| 7 | Vertical stirrups | $V_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d / s_v$ | N | IS 1343 Cl. 11.4 |
| 8 | Bent-up bars | $V_s = 0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot \sin\alpha \cdot d$ | N | IS 1343 Cl. 11.4 |
| 9 | Principal stresses | $\sigma_{t,c} = \sigma_x/2 \pm \sqrt{(\sigma_x/2)^2 + \tau^2}$ | N/mm² | Mohr's Circle |
| 10 | Min. shear reinforcement | $A_{sv}/(b \cdot s_v) \ge 0.4 / (0.87 \cdot f_y)$ | — | IS 1343 Cl. 11.3 |
| 11 | Max. stirrup spacing | $\le 0.75 d$ or 300 mm | mm | IS 1343 Cl. 11.4 |
| 12 | Partial safety factor (concrete) | $\gamma_m = 1.5$ | — | IS 1343 Cl. 6.3.1 |
| 13 | Partial safety factor (steel) | $\gamma_m = 1.15$ | — | IS 1343 Cl. 6.3.1 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The shear design methodology is implemented in commercial bridge design software (e.g., MIDAS Civil, CSI Bridge, LUSAS) to size web reinforcement in segmental PSC box girders, post-tensioned I-girders, and railway sleepers. Mastery of the principal-stress-based crack control is essential for **partial prestressing** design and for **Type 2 and Type 3** members (per IS 1343 Cl. 4.2.2) where tension is allowed under service loads.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Principal Stresses in a PSC Beam Element

Consider an infinitesimal element at the **Neutral Axis (NA)** of a PSC beam subjected to:
- Shear force **V** (giving shear stress τ)
- Prestressing force **P** (giving uniform compressive stress σp = P/A)
- Bending moment **M** (which is **zero at the NA**, so flexural stress is zero here)

**Step 1: Identify stresses on the element**

The horizontal stress at NA (where bending stress is zero) is purely due to prestress:
$$\sigma_{x} = -\frac{P}{A}$$
(Negative sign indicates compression; we will treat compression as positive in stress-bock analysis.)

For simplicity, let us adopt the convention where compression is negative and tension is positive (standard sign convention in mechanics):
$$\sigma_{x} = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(compressive)}$$

The vertical stress on a beam surface is zero:
$$\sigma_{y} = 0$$

The shear stress on horizontal and vertical planes is:
$$\tau_{xy} = \tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

**Step 2: Apply the 2D stress transformation equations**

The principal stresses are given by:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_{x} + \sigma_{y}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x} - \sigma_{y}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}}$$

Substituting σy = 0:
$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

**Step 3: Identify principal tension (σt) and principal compression (σc)**

$$\sigma_{c} = \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}} \quad \text{(compressive, positive)}$$

$$\sigma_{t} = \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}} \quad \text{(tensile, negative)}$$

**Step 4: Numerical illustration**

Let P = 1500 kN, A = 200×10³ mm², V = 250 kN, Q = 1.5×10⁶ mm³, I = 8×10⁸ mm⁴, b = 200 mm.

$$\sigma_{x} = \frac{1500 \times 10^{3}}{200 \times 10^{3}} = 7.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

$$\tau = \frac{250 \times 10^{3} \times 1.5 \times 10^{6}}{8 \times 10^{8} \times 200} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{11}}{1.6 \times 10^{11}} = 2.34 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} = 3.75 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{(3.75)^{2} + (2.34)^{2}} = \sqrt{14.06 + 5.48} = \sqrt{19.54} = 4.42 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{c} = 3.75 + 4.42 = 8.17 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (compressive)}$$

$$\sigma_{t} = 3.75 - 4.42 = -0.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (tensile)}$$

**Interpretation:** The tensile principal stress of **0.67 N/mm²** is well within the permissible limit for Type 1 (fully prestressed, no tension) members when concrete grade is ≥ M40. If σt were to exceed the allowable, either the section must be enlarged or the prestress increased.

### 3.2 Worked-Out Design Example (IS 1343 Style)

**Problem Statement:**

A simply supported PSC beam of span 12 m has a rectangular section of **b = 300 mm** and **d = 700 mm**, with overall depth D = 800 mm. The beam is prestressed with a parabolic cable with effective force P = 1800 kN. The uniformly distributed load (including self-weight and superimposed) gives a maximum shear force at support of **V = 280 kN** (service) and bending moment M = 1100 kN·m (service). Grade of concrete: **M40**; Fe 415 steel. Design the shear reinforcement.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Factored Shear Force**
$$V_u = 1.5 \times 280 = 420 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Nominal Shear Stress**
$$\tau_v = \frac{420 \times 10^{3}}{300 \times 700} = 2.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3: Check τc,max**

From IS 1343 Table 11 for M40 concrete: **τc,max = 3.5 N/mm²**

Since **τv (2.0) < τc,max (3.5)** ✓ — section is safe from web crushing.

**Step 4: Determine τc**

Assume longitudinal steel percentage: pt = 100·Ast/(b·d) = 1.5% (typical for PSC)
From IS 1343 Table 12 (M40, pt = 1.5%): **τc ≈ 0.80 N/mm²** (interpolated; exam-acceptable value).

**Step 5: Compare τv and τc**

Since **τv (2.0) > τc (0.80)** — shear reinforcement must be designed.

**Step 6: Shear to be carried by reinforcement**
$$V_s = V_u - \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d = 420 \times 10^{3} - 0.80 \times 300 \times 700$$

$$V_s = 420{,}000 - 168{,}000 = 252{,}000 \text{ N} = 252 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 7: Design vertical stirrups**

Using 2-legged 10 mm dia. stirrups (Fe 415):
$$A_{sv} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^{2} = 157.08 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Spacing required:
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{V_s} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 157.08 \times 700}{252{,}000}$$

$$s_v = \frac{39{,}711{,}510}{252{,}000} = 157.6 \text{ mm}$$

Provide **s_v = 150 mm c/c** (rounded down for safety).

**Step 8: Check maximum spacing**

Maximum spacing = min(0.75·d, 300 mm) = min(525, 300) = **300 mm**.
Provided 150 mm < 300 mm ✓

**Step 9: Check minimum shear reinforcement**
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot s_v} = \frac{157.08}{300 \times 150} = 0.00349$$

$$\frac{0.4}{0.87 \cdot f_y} = \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times 415} = 0.00111$$

Since 0.00349 > 0.00111 ✓ — minimum shear reinforcement is satisfied.

**Final Design:** Provide **2-legged 10 mm dia. vertical stirrups @ 150 mm c/c** throughout the shear-critical region.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Prestress Improves Shear Resistance

| **Scenario** | **σx at NA** | **τ** | **σt (principal tension)** | **Crack Risk** |
|:-------------|:-------------|:-------|:--------------------------|:----------------|
| No prestress (RCC) | 0 | 2.34 | −2.34 N/mm² | High (cracks likely) |
| Mild prestress (P = 800 kN) | 4.0 | 2.34 | −1.21 N/mm² | Moderate |
| Design prestress (P = 1500 kN) | 7.5 | 2.34 | −0.67 N/mm² | Low |
| Heavy prestress (P = 3000 kN) | 15.0 | 2.34 | +0.93 N/mm² | None (σt becomes positive) |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Always calculate the principal tensile stress at the neutral axis of PSC beams at the support and at the quarter-span, then verify it against IS 1343 limits (Class 1, 2, or 3 member classification).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Shear Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: PSC Beam with known geometry and loads] --> B[Step 1: Compute Service Shear V and Bending Moment M]
    B --> C[Step 2: Apply Load Factors to get Vu and Mu]
    C --> D[Step 3: Calculate Nominal Shear Stress tau_v = Vu / bd]
    D --> E{Step 4: Check tau_v vs tau_c_max from IS 1343 Table 11}
    E -- tau_v greater than tau_c_max --> F[FAIL: Redesign Section - Increase b or d]
    E -- tau_v less than or equal to tau_c_max --> G[Step 5: Determine tau_c from Table 12 using pt and concrete grade]
    G --> H{Step 6: Is tau_v greater than tau_c}
    H -- No --> I[Step 7a: Provide MINIMUM Shear Reinforcement per IS 1343 Cl 11.3]
    H -- Yes --> J[Step 7b: Compute Vs = Vu minus tau_c times bd]
    J --> K[Step 8: Choose Stirrup Diameter and Number of Legs]
    K --> L[Step 9: Compute Spacing sv = 0.87 fy Asv d / Vs]
    L --> M{Step 10: Check Spacing Limits}
    M -- sv less than 0.75d and 300 mm --> N[Step 11: Check Minimum Shear Reinforcement Ratio]
    M -- sv exceeds limits --> O[Reduce Spacing - Revise Stirrup Size]
    N --> P[Step 12: Verify Principal Tension sigma_t against IS 1343 Class 1/2/3 limits]
    P --> Q[Step 13: Check Anchorage Length of Bent-up Bars if used]
    Q --> R[Final Design Documentation with Sketches]
    I --> R
    O --> L
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#ffcccc
    style R fill:#d4f4d4
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Stresses Acting on a Beam Element at the Neutral Axis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EL[Beam Element at Neutral Axis] --> SX[Sigma_x: Compressive Stress from Prestress P over A]
    EL --> SY[Sigma_y: Zero - No Vertical Stress on Beam Surface]
    EL --> TAU[tau_xy: Shear Stress from Transverse Load VQ over Ib]
    SX --> PRIN[Principal Stresses sigma_c and sigma_t]
    SY --> PRIN
    TAU --> PRIN
    PRIN --> CRACK[Check sigma_t against IS 1343 Class Limits]
    CRACK -- sigma_t within limit --> SAFE[SECTION SAFE - No Diagonal Crack]
    CRACK -- sigma_t exceeds limit --> UNSAFE[REDESIGN: Increase Prestress or Section]
    style EL fill:#fff4cc
    style PRIN fill:#cce5ff
    style SAFE fill:#d4f4d4
    style UNSAFE fill:#ffcccc
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Shear Reinforcement Types in PSC Beams

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SR[Shear Reinforcement in PSC Beams] --> VS[Vertical Stirrups]
    SR --> BB[Bent-up Bars]
    SR --> COMBO[Combination of Stirrups and Bent-up Bars]
    VS --> V2L[2-Legged Stirrups for Narrow Webs]
    VS --> V4L[4-Legged Stirrups for Deep Girders]
    BB --> B45[Bent at 45 degrees - Most Common]
    BB --> B30[Bent at 30 degrees - For Shallow Members]
    style SR fill:#e1f5ff
    style VS fill:#cce5ff
    style BB fill:#fff4cc
    style COMBO fill:#d4f4d4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid Block Diagrams?** Since shear design in PSC involves complex stress interactions across the depth of the beam (different fibers experience different σx values), a full 3D Mohr's circle rendering is impractical. The **functional block flow** above captures the *logical decision tree* a designer must follow per IS 1343 — which is the heart of KTU 14-mark problems.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define the Limit State of Collapse for Shear in a prestressed concrete member. List the IS 1343 code checks performed for shear design.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Limit State of Collapse for Shear is the ultimate limit state at which a PSC member, subjected to combined bending, axial prestress, and shear, reaches its maximum load-carrying capacity against **diagonal tension cracking, web crushing, or shear-compression failure**.

**IS 1343 design checks:**
1. Check that the nominal shear stress τv does not exceed the **maximum shear stress τc,max** (IS 1343 Table 11) — controls section dimensions.
2. Check that the principal tensile stress **σt** at the neutral axis is within the permissible limit for the chosen **Class (1, 2, or 3) of member** (IS 1343 Cl. 4.2.2).
3. Design the shear reinforcement such that **Vu ≤ Vc + Vs** (IS 1343 Cl. 11.1).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
*Explain how the prestressing force improves the shear capacity of a PSC beam. Provide the principal stress formula used in the analysis.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The prestressing force induces a **uniform compressive stress σx = P/A** across the concrete section (and an additional bending stress due to cable eccentricity). This pre-compression **reduces the magnitude of the principal tensile stress (σt)** when shear and bending act simultaneously. Since diagonal cracks in concrete form when σt exceeds the tensile strength, a higher pre-compression effectively **delays the onset of diagonal cracking**, thereby increasing the shear capacity.

The principal stress formula is:

$$\sigma_{t} = \frac{\sigma_{x}}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau^{2}}$$

The higher the σx (compressive), the closer σt approaches zero, delaying cracking.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

*(a)* A PSC beam of rectangular section **b = 250 mm**, **d = 600 mm** carries an effective prestress of **P = 1500 kN**. The shear force at the support due to a uniformly distributed service load is **V = 200 kN**. The bending moment at a section 3 m from the support is **M = 500 kN·m**. Concrete grade: **M45**. Calculate the **principal stresses** at the neutral axis and at the bottom fiber at the 3 m section, and identify whether the section is safe under **Class 1 (fully prestressed)** conditions. (7 marks)

*(b)* For the same beam, determine the **nominal shear stress** and the **design shear reinforcement** using vertical 2-legged 8 mm dia. Fe 415 stirrups. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principal Stress Calculation**

**Step 1:** Stresses at the Neutral Axis (NA) of the 3 m section.

At NA, bending stress is zero, so the only horizontal stress is from prestress:
$$\sigma_{x,NA} = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{1500 \times 10^{3}}{250 \times 600} = 10.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \text{ (compressive)}$$

Shear stress at NA: First, compute the shear force at 3 m. For a UDL, V varies linearly. If support V = 200 kN and span L = 12 m (assumed), then UDL w = 2·V/L = 33.33 kN/m. At 3 m: V = 200 − 33.33×3 = 100 kN.

$$\tau_{NA} = \frac{1.5 \cdot V}{b \cdot d} = \frac{1.5 \times 100 \times 10^{3}}{250 \times 600} = 1.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Principal stresses at NA:
$$\sigma_{t,NA} = \frac{10}{2} - \sqrt{(5)^2 + (1)^2} = 5 - \sqrt{26} = 5 - 5.099 = -0.099 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

$$\sigma_{c,NA} = 5 + 5.099 = 10.099 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (compressive)}$$

So a very small tensile principal stress of **0.099 N/mm²** exists. For Class 1 (no tension), this must be ≤ 0. **Therefore, the NA violates Class 1 conditions** (slightly). The beam must be reclassified as **Class 2** (tension allowed but no visible cracks).

**Step 3:** Stresses at the bottom fiber (3 m section).

The bottom fiber bending tensile stress:
$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = \frac{500 \times 10^{6} \times 300}{\frac{250 \times 600^{3}}{12}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{6} \times 300}{4.5 \times 10^{9}} = 33.33 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (tensile)}$$

But this is the *flexural* tensile stress. The net horizontal stress at the bottom fiber:
$$\sigma_{x,bottom} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{M \cdot y}{I} = 10 - 33.33 = -23.33 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (tensile)}$$

The shear stress at the bottom fiber = 0 (extreme fiber, where Q = 0).

**Step 4:** Principal stresses at bottom fiber (τ = 0, so σt = σx):
$$\sigma_{t,bottom} = \sigma_{x,bottom} = -23.33 \text{ N/mm}^{2 (tensile — will crack)}$$

This is a **serviceability issue** — the bottom fiber will crack. Conclusion: The beam is **NOT a Class 1 member**; it should be designed as **Class 2 (Type B) or Class 3** with proper crack control reinforcement.

---

**Part (b) — Design of Shear Reinforcement**

**Step 1:** Factored shear at support:
$$V_u = 1.5 \times 200 = 300 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2:** Nominal shear stress:
$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d} = \frac{300 \times 10^{3}}{250 \times 600} = 2.0 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3:** Check τc,max (IS 1343 Table 11, M45): τc,max = **3.7 N/mm²**. Since 2.0 < 3.7 ✓.

**Step 4:** Assume pt = 1.2%. From IS 1343 Table 12, M45 with pt = 1.2% → τc ≈ **0.78 N/mm²**.

**Step 5:** Compute Vs:
$$V_s = V_u - \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d = 300{,}000 - 0.78 \times 250 \times 600 = 300{,}000 - 117{,}000 = 183{,}000 \text{ N} = 183 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6:** Spacing for 2-legged 8 mm stirrups:
$$A_{sv} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 8^2 = 100.53 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.53 \times 600}{183{,}000} = 119.3 \text{ mm}$$

**Provide s_v = 115 mm c/c** (rounded down).

**Step 7:** Check maximum spacing: 0.75·d = 450 mm; 300 mm. Provide 115 mm < 300 mm ✓.

**Final Design:** **2-legged 8 mm dia. Fe 415 vertical stirrups @ 115 mm c/c** at the support region.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

*(a)* A simply supported PSC I-beam of effective span **20 m** has the following properties: top flange width 800 mm, bottom flange width 500 mm, web thickness **b = 250 mm**, overall depth D = 1400 mm, effective depth **d = 1300 mm**. The maximum factored shear force at support is **V_u = 950 kN**. Concrete grade M50. Design the **shear reinforcement** using 12 mm dia. 2-legged vertical stirrups and 25 mm dia. bent-up bars (Fe 500). State clearly the contribution of each. (7 marks)

*(b)* Write a short note on the **classification of PSC members (Type 1, 2, 3)** as per IS 1343 and explain how it affects **shear design philosophy**. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution Outline for Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Nominal shear stress: τv = 950,000 / (250 × 1300) = **2.92 N/mm²**

**Step 2:** τc,max (M50, Table 11) = **4.0 N/mm²**. Check: 2.92 < 4.0 ✓

**Step 3:** For M50, pt assumed = 1.0%, τc ≈ 0.83 N/mm² (Table 12).

**Step 4:** Vc = 0.83 × 250 × 1300 = 269,750 N = 269.75 kN
Vs = 950 − 269.75 = 680.25 kN

**Step 5:** 12 mm 2-legged stirrups: Asv = 2 × π/4 × 12² = 226.19 mm²
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 226.19 \times 1300}{680{,}250} = 150.4 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{provide } 150 \text{ mm c/c}$$

**Step 6:** Bent-up bars (25 mm, Fe 500, 45°): Asv (one bar) = π/4 × 25² = 490.87 mm²
$$V_{s,bar} = 0.87 \times 500 \times 490.87 \times \sin 45^\circ \times 1300$$
$$= 0.87 \times 500 \times 490.87 \times 0.7071 \times 1300 = 196{,}473 \text{ N} \approx 196.5 \text{ kN per bar}$$

If two bent-up bars are provided, Vs contribution = 393 kN. The remaining 680.25 − 393 = 287.25 kN to be carried by stirrups:
$$s_v = \frac{0.87 \times 500 \times 226.19 \times 1300}{287{,}250} = 355.8 \text{ mm} \rightarrow \text{revise to 300 mm c/c (max limit)}$$

**Final Design:** 12 mm 2-legged stirrups @ 300 mm c/c + 2 nos. 25 mm bent-up bars at 45° (combined contribution).

---

**Model Solution Outline for Part (b):**

IS 1343 Cl. 4.2.2 classifies PSC members as:

| **Type** | **Service Tensile Stress in Concrete** | **Cracking Allowed?** | **Reinforcement in Tension Zone** |
|:--------:|:----------------------------------------|:----------------------|:----------------------------------|
| **Type 1** | No tensile stress | No | Nil (only prestress resists) |
| **Type 2** | Tensile stress exists but ≤ modulus of rupture (fcr) | No visible cracks | Minimum (for crack control) |
| **Type 3** | Tensile stress > fcr | Yes (cracks allowed) | Designed for flexural tension |

**Shear design philosophy differs:**
- **Type 1 (Fully prestressed):** Shear reinforcement is sized to take **all** the shear beyond Vc, since the cross-section is assumed un-cracked.
- **Type 2:** Similar to Type 1 but with limits on tensile principal stress to prevent visible cracking.
- **Type 3 (Partially prestressed):** Web shear capacity uses **post-cracking shear equations** (analogous to RCC), since diagonal cracks are anticipated; the stirrups are designed for the full Vs as in RCC.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in Shear Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the load factor of 1.5** when computing Vu from service shear — a 1-mark penalty, very common.
> 2. **Mixing up τc (Table 12) and τc,max (Table 11)** — they are *different values*; τc,max is the upper bound on τv, while τc is the design concrete shear strength.
> 3. **Using wrong units** — τv is in N/mm², not MPa, while V is in N (not kN). Students often forget the 10³ factor.
> 4. **Ignoring the principal stress check at the NA** — IS 1343 mandates that σt at the NA be checked against the class limit; this is a frequent 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Not checking maximum stirrup spacing** (≤ 0.75d or 300 mm) — many students compute the required spacing but fail to apply this *upper limit*.
> 6. **Assuming I-beams have the same τc,max as rectangular sections** — τc,max depends on the *web breadth*, not flange width; the correct b to use is the web width for I/T-beams.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Bulleted Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- [x] **Limit State of Collapse for Shear** requires: **Vu ≤ Vc + Vs** (fundamental inequality)
- [x] **Nominal shear stress:** τv = Vu / (b·d) — where **b = web width** for I/T-beams
- [x] **Three IS 1343 Tables to remember:** Table 11 (τc,max), Table 12 (τc), Table 14 (bond stress)
- [x] **Load factor for shear in PSC:** **1.5** (same as RCC limit state design)
- [x] **Stirrup design formula:** Vs = 0.87 · fy · Asv · d / sv (vertical stirrups)
- [x] **Bent-up bar formula:** Vs = 0.87 · fy · Asv · sinα · d (α = 30°, 45°, 60°)
- [x] **Principal stress formula:** σt,c = σx/2 ± √((σx/2)² + τ²)
- [x] **Class 1 (Type 1):** σt ≤ 0 (no tension) — fully prestressed
- [x] **Class 2 (Type 2):** σt ≤ fcr (modulus of rupture ≈ 0.33√fck) — no visible cracks
- [x] **Class 3 (Type 3):** σt > fcr — cracking allowed, behaves like RCC for shear
- [x] **Minimum shear reinforcement:** Asv/(b·sv) ≥ 0.4/(0.87·fy)
- [x] **Maximum stirrup spacing:** min(0.75d, 300 mm)
- [x] **Effect of prestress on shear:** Pre-compression **reduces σt**, **delays diagonal cracking**, **increases shear capacity** — the fundamental engineering insight.
- [x] **Order of design:** Factored loads → τv → τc,max check → τc lookup → Vs → stirrup/bent-up design → spacing checks → min shear check.
- [x] **Failure modes to remember:** Diagonal tension (most common), web crushing, shear-compression, flexural-shear, and tendon splitting.
- [x] **Mnemonic for shear design steps:** **"V → τv → τc,max → τc → Vs → stirrups"**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Transverse Reinforcement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design of Transverse Reinforcement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transverse Reinforcement in Prestressed Concrete** refers to the secondary reinforcement (stirrups, links, or bent-up bars) provided in a direction perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of a prestressed concrete flexural member, primarily to resist **diagonal tension (shear) failure** caused by principal tensile stresses developed in the web of the beam under transverse (shear) loading.

According to **IS 1343:2012 (Code of Practice for Prestressed Concrete)** and aligned with **IS 456:2000** provisions adopted in KTU syllabus:

The design of transverse reinforcement involves the provision of reinforcement in a beam such that the **ultimate shear force (V)** at any section is resisted by a combination of:
1. **Concrete in the compression zone** ($V_c$)
2. **Prestressing tendons in the flexural tension zone** ($V_p$)
3. **Steel reinforcement in the form of stirrups/bent-up bars** ($V_{us}$)

The fundamental design equation is:

$$V = V_c + V_p + V_{us}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Bridge Girder & The Sandwich Analogy"**
>
> Imagine a heavy **bookshelf** (prestressed concrete beam) supported at its two ends. The shelf carries heavy books (transverse loads) and is also being **squeezed horizontally** by pre-tensioned cables running through it (prestressing force).
>
> When you load the shelf, it tries to bend. The squeezing force (prestress) **pre-compresses** the wood, helping it carry the load. However, near the **supports** (where shear forces are maximum), the shelf experiences a tendency to **tear diagonally** — like a credit card slipping out of your hand under bending.
>
> This diagonal tear is the **diagonal tension failure** (a combination of horizontal and vertical stress components). To prevent this, we glue small **wooden cross-strips** (stirrups) vertically along the depth and spacing the strips closer near the supports (where shear is high) and farther apart in the middle (where shear is low).
>
> This is precisely what transverse reinforcement does — it ties the beam together against **diagonal splitting**, with denser stirrups near supports.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Key values used universally in KTU board examinations:
> - **Grade of Concrete:** M40, M50, M60 (typical for PSC)
> - **Grade of Steel (HYSD bars):** Fe 415, Fe 500
> - **Allowable tensile stress in shear reinforcement:** $\sigma_{sv} = \mathbf{140 \text{ N/mm}^2}$ (for 6 mm dia) or $\mathbf{200 \text{ N/mm}^2}$ (for 8 mm & above) as per IS 1343
> - **Minimum shear reinforcement:** $A_{sv,min}/b \cdot S_v = \mathbf{0.4 / 0.87 f_y}$ (as per IS 456 cl. 26.5.1.6)
> - **Permissible shear stress** $\tau_c$ depends on $p_t \cdot f_{ck}$ ratio (IS 1343 Table 6)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

### Step 1: Identify the Nature of Shear Failure in PSC
In a prestressed concrete beam, the application of transverse (gravity) loads causes a complex **two-dimensional stress state** at every point in the web. The stresses are:
- **Horizontal normal stress ($\sigma_x$):** due to bending + prestress
- **Vertical normal stress ($\sigma_y$):** usually zero (no vertical loads on web)
- **Shear stress ($\tau$):** due to transverse shear force

### Step 2: Apply Principal Stress Theory
The principal stresses at any web fiber are given by **Mohr's Circle** analysis:

$$\sigma_{pt} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_{pc} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

> **Key Point:** If $\sigma_{pt}$ exceeds the tensile strength of concrete (~ $0.33\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for PSC as per IS 1343), diagonal cracks form. Stirrups must be provided to carry the tension across these cracks.

### Step 3: Calculate Design Shear Force
- For **simply supported beams:** Maximum shear occurs at supports
- For **continuous beams:** Check sections at faces of supports and at distances $d$ from supports
- Use **factored loads** as per **IS 1343 Cl. 18.2.1** under **ultimate limit state**

### Step 4: Calculate Shear Stress
$$\tau = \frac{V}{b \cdot d}$$

where:
- $V$ = Factored shear force at the section
- $b$ = breadth of the web
- $d$ = effective depth

### Step 5: Determine Permissible Shear Stress ($\tau_c$)
Refer to **IS 1343 Table 6** (reproduced from IS 456 Table 19), with:

$$p_t = \frac{100 A_s}{b \cdot d} \quad \text{(percentage of longitudinal tension steel)}$$

Note: For PSC beams, the **prestressing steel** $A_p$ in the tension zone is also considered in $p_t$ calculation.

### Step 6: Components of Shear Resistance
The total shear $V$ is resisted by:

| Component | Symbol | Description |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Concrete in compression | $V_c$ | $= \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ |
| Vertical component of prestress | $V_p$ | $= P \sin\alpha$ (for inclined cables) |
| Shear reinforcement | $V_{us}$ | Provided by stirrups/bent-up bars |

### Step 7: Design of Vertical Stirrups
For **vertical two-legged stirrups** (most common in KTU exams):

$$V_{us} = \frac{0.87 f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{S_v}$$

where:
- $A_{sv}$ = total cross-sectional area of stirrup legs ($2 A_\phi$ for 2-legged)
- $S_v$ = spacing of stirrups
- $f_y$ = yield strength of stirrup steel (415 or 500 MPa)

### Step 8: Design of Inclined Stirrups
For stirrups inclined at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal:

$$V_{us} = \frac{0.87 f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{S_v} (\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha)$$

### Step 9: Check Spacing Limits (IS 1343 Cl. 18.6.3)
- $S_v \le 0.75 d$
- $S_v \le 300$ mm
- $S_v \ge 100$ mm (practical minimum)

### Step 10: Check Maximum Shear Stress
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V}{b \cdot d} \le \tau_{c,max}$$

as per **IS 1343 Table 7** (depends on concrete grade).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINT THIS TABLE — HIGH-FREQUENCY IN BOARD EXAMS**

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Code Reference** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Shear Stress | $\tau = V/(bd)$ | N/mm² | IS 1343 Cl. 18.3 |
| 2 | Principal Tensile Stress | $\sigma_{pt} = \sigma_x/2 - \sqrt{(\sigma_x/2)^2 + \tau^2}$ | N/mm² | Mohr's Theory |
| 3 | Permissible Principal Tensile Stress | $\sigma_{pt,allow} = 0.33\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (Class 1) | N/mm² | IS 1343 Cl. 17.4 |
| 4 | Concrete Shear Capacity | $V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d$ | N | IS 1343 Table 6 |
| 5 | Prestress Vertical Component | $V_p = P \sin\alpha$ | N | — |
| 6 | Vertical Stirrups Capacity | $V_{us} = 0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / S_v$ | N | IS 1343 Cl. 18.6 |
| 7 | Inclined Stirrups Capacity | $V_{us} = (0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / S_v)(\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha)$ | N | IS 1343 Cl. 18.6 |
| 8 | Min. Shear Reinforcement | $A_{sv}/(b \cdot S_v) \ge 0.4/0.87f_y$ | — | IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.6 |
| 9 | Max. Spacing of Stirrups | $S_v \le \min(0.75d, 300 \text{ mm})$ | mm | IS 1343 Cl. 18.6.3 |
| 10 | Max. Shear Stress $\tau_{c,max}$ | (Refer Table for M40: 3.5 MPa) | N/mm² | IS 1343 Table 7 |
| 11 | Percentage Steel $p_t$ | $p_t = 100(A_s + A_p)/(bd)$ | % | IS 1343 |
| 12 | Design Shear Force | $V_u = 1.5(DL + LL)$ (working stress / limit state as per case) | N | IS 1343 Cl. 18.2 |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Transverse reinforcement design is **critical in Kerala's bridge infrastructure**:
- **Pre-cast PSC girders** in Kochi Metro and NH-66 flyovers
- **Post-tensioned box girders** in the Kochi-Bengaluru highway
- **Bridge deck slabs** with deep PSC T-beams

Failure to design adequate stirrups led to the **Korba Chimney collapse (2009)** and various bridge shear failures globally. **Strict adherence to IS 1343 stirrup spacing rules** prevents brittle diagonal tension failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL WORKED EXAMPLES

## 3.1 Derivation: Components of Shear Resistance in PSC Beams

**Starting Point — Equilibrium of a Beam Segment:**

Consider a small element of a prestressed concrete beam of width $b$, effective depth $d$, and length $dx$. The forces acting on it are:

- Transverse shear force: $V$
- Bending moment: $M$
- Prestressing force: $P$ at eccentricity $e$ (and inclination $\alpha$)
- Longitudinal tension steel force: $T_s = A_s \sigma_s$
- Prestress steel force: $T_p = A_p \sigma_p$

**Equilibrium of Vertical Forces:**

$$V = V_c + V_{us} + V_p$$

Substituting each:

$$V = \tau_c b d + \frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{S_v} + P \sin\alpha$$

**Solving for Stirrup Spacing $S_v$ (Vertical Stirrups):**

$$\frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{S_v} = V - \tau_c b d - P \sin\alpha$$

$$S_v = \frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{V - \tau_c b d - P \sin\alpha}$$

> **Sign Convention:** $V_p = P \sin\alpha$ is added to resistance when cable slopes **upward toward the support** (cable lifts the shear diagram). It is subtracted when cable slopes downward.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

### **Problem Statement:**
> A **post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam** of rectangular section has the following data:
> - Overall depth: $D = 500$ mm
> - Breadth: $b = 250$ mm
> - Effective cover: 50 mm
> - Effective depth: $d = 450$ mm
> - Prestressing force: $P = 1500$ kN
> - Cable profile: parabolic, with slope at support $\tan\alpha = 0.10$
> - Grade of concrete: **M50** ($f_{ck} = 50$ N/mm²)
> - Grade of stirrup steel: **Fe 415** ($f_y = 415$ N/mm²)
> - Factored shear force at support: $V_u = 450$ kN
> - Effective prestress in concrete at support: $f_{br} = 4.5$ N/mm²
>
> **Design the transverse reinforcement (vertical stirrups) at the support section.**

---

### **Step 1: Calculate Effective Depth**
$$d = D - \text{cover} = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ mm}$$

### **Step 2: Calculate Shear Stress at Support**
$$\tau_v = \frac{V_u}{b \cdot d} = \frac{450 \times 10^3}{250 \times 450}$$

$$\tau_v = \frac{450000}{112500} = 4.00 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

### **Step 3: Check Maximum Permissible Shear Stress**

> Per **IS 1343 Table 7**, for M50 concrete:
> $$\tau_{c,max} = 4.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\tau_v = 4.00 \text{ N/mm}^2 \le \tau_{c,max} = 4.00 \text{ N/mm}^2 \quad \checkmark$$

> Section dimensions are **just adequate**. If $\tau_v > \tau_{c,max}$, section must be redesigned.

### **Step 4: Calculate Vertical Component of Prestress**

$$V_p = P \sin\alpha = 1500 \times 10^3 \times 0.10$$

$$V_p = 150000 \text{ N} = 150 \text{ kN}$$

### **Step 5: Calculate Shear to be Carried by Stirrups**

$$V_{us} = V_u - V_p - V_c$$

We first find $V_c$ (concrete's contribution). Assume design shear stress of concrete $\tau_c$ from Table 6.

For **M50** concrete and assuming $p_t = 1.2\%$ (typical for PSC):
- From IS 1343 Table 6: $\tau_c = 0.92$ N/mm² (interpolated)

$$V_c = \tau_c \cdot b \cdot d = 0.92 \times 250 \times 450$$

$$V_c = 103500 \text{ N} = 103.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$V_{us} = 450 - 150 - 103.5 = 196.5 \text{ kN}$$

### **Step 6: Choose Stirrup Diameter and Calculate $A_{sv}$**

Try **2-legged 10 mm dia stirrups:**
$$A_{sv} = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times 10^2 = 157 \text{ mm}^2$$

### **Step 7: Calculate Spacing $S_v$**

$$S_v = \frac{0.87 \cdot f_y \cdot A_{sv} \cdot d}{V_{us}}$$

$$S_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 157 \times 450}{196500}$$

$$S_v = \frac{25516072.5}{196500} = 129.85 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt: $S_v = 125$ mm** (next lower multiple of 25 mm)

### **Step 8: Check Spacing Limits**

$$S_v \le 0.75 d = 0.75 \times 450 = 337.5 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark$$

$$S_v \le 300 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \text{ (125 mm adopted)}$$

### **Step 9: Check Minimum Shear Reinforcement**

$$S_{v,max} = \frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv}}{0.4 b} = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 157}{0.4 \times 250}$$

$$S_{v,max} = \frac{56717.85}{100} = 567.18 \text{ mm}$$

Since $S_v = 125 \text{ mm} < 567.18 \text{ mm}$ → **Minimum shear reinforcement satisfied** $\checkmark$

### **Step 10: Final Design Summary**

> **Provide 2-legged 10 mm dia vertical stirrups @ 125 mm c/c near the support.**
>
> - First stirrup at 50 mm from face of support
> - Continue at 125 mm c/c for a distance of $d$ to $1.5d$ from support
> - Gradually increase spacing to 200–250 mm c/c in the middle third
> - Provide 2D10 @ 250 mm c/c in midspan region

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Spacing of Inclined Stirrups at Angle $\alpha$

For inclined stirrups (often seen in KTU problems involving bent-up bars):

$$V_{us} = \frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d}{S_v}(\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha)$$

Solving for $S_v$:

$$S_v = \frac{0.87 f_y A_{sv} d (\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha)}{V_{us}}$$

**Numerical Check:** For $\alpha = 45°$, $\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha = 0.707 + 0.707 = 1.414$

This means inclined stirrups at 45° are **41.4% more efficient** than vertical stirrups (which is why bridges use them).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Shear Force Flow & Stirrup Placement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Transverse Load w kN/m] --> B[Beam Element dx]
    B --> C{Bending + Shear}
    C --> D[Longitudinal Stress sigma_x]
    C --> E[Shear Stress tau]
    D --> F[Mohr Circle Analysis]
    E --> F
    F --> G{sigma_pt greater than 0.33 sqrt fck}
    G -->|Yes| H[Diagonal Cracks Form]
    G -->|No| I[Section OK Without Stirrups]
    H --> J[Design Transverse Reinforcement]
    J --> K[Calculate Vc from tau_c b d]
    J --> L[Calculate Vp = P sin alpha]
    J --> M[Calculate Vus = V - Vc - Vp]
    M --> N{Select Stirrup Type}
    N -->|Vertical| O[2 legged 8 or 10 mm dia]
    N -->|Inclined| P[Single or multi legged inclined]
    O --> Q[Compute Spacing Sv]
    P --> Q
    Q --> R{Spacing Checks}
    R --> S{Sv less than 0.75 d}
    R --> T{Sv less than 300 mm}
    R --> U{A_sv by b Sv greater than 0.4 by 0.87 fy}
    S --> V[Final Design Adopted]
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[Provide 2D10 at 125 mm c c near support]
    V --> X[2D10 at 200 mm c c in midspan]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Shear Resistance Component Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Factored Shear V] --> B[Concrete Contribution Vc]
    A --> C[Prestress Vertical Component Vp]
    A --> D[Stirrups Contribution Vus]
    B --> B1[tau_c times b times d]
    C --> C1[P times sin alpha]
    D --> D1[0.87 fy Asv d by Sv]
    B1 --> E[Resist Diagonal Tension]
    C1 --> E
    D1 --> E
    E --> F[Beam Safe Against Shear Failure]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Stirrup Pattern Along a PSC Beam Span

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SUPPORT_LEFT
        L1[Support Reaction]
    end
    subgraph REGION_1_HIGH_SHEAR
        L2[2 legged 10 mm stirrups at 125 mm c c]
        L3[Distance 0 to 0.5 d from support]
    end
    subgraph REGION_2_TRANSITION
        L4[2 legged 10 mm stirrups at 175 mm c c]
        L5[Distance 0.5 d to 1.5 d from support]
    end
    subgraph REGION_3_LOW_SHEAR_MIDSPAN
        L6[2 legged 10 mm stirrups at 250 mm c c]
        L7[Middle third of span]
    end
    subgraph SUPPORT_RIGHT
        L8[Support Reaction]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L4
    L4 --> L6
    L6 --> L4
    L4 --> L2
    L2 --> L8
```

## 4.4 Mermaid: Decision Flow for IS 1343 Shear Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Compute Design Shear V at section] --> B[Compute tau = V by bd]
    B --> C{tau less than tau c max}
    C -->|No| D[REDESIGN SECTION Increase b or d]
    C -->|Yes| E[Compute Vc = tau c b d]
    E --> F[Compute Vp = P sin alpha]
    F --> G[Compute Vus = V minus Vc minus Vp]
    G --> H{Vus less than 0}
    H -->|Yes| I[Minimum stirrups only]
    H -->|No| J[Choose stirrup diameter]
    J --> K[Compute Sv required]
    K --> L{Adopt Sv satisfying 3 checks}
    L --> M[Sv less than 0.75 d]
    L --> N[Sv less than 300 mm]
    L --> O[Min shear reinforcement satisfied]
    M --> P[FINAL DESIGN]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    I --> P
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define principal tensile stress in a prestressed concrete beam. How is it computed?**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **principal tensile stress** ($\sigma_{pt}$) is the maximum tensile stress acting on a plane inclined to the longitudinal axis, where shear stress and normal stress combine to produce a diagonal tension failure plane.

For a beam element with longitudinal normal stress $\sigma_x$ and shear stress $\tau$:

$$\sigma_{pt} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

It governs the **diagonal cracking** in the web of prestressed concrete beams.

---

### **Q2. List the three components of shear resistance in a PSC beam and write the corresponding IS 1343 equation.**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three components are:
1. **Concrete in compression** ($V_c$)
2. **Vertical component of prestress** ($V_p$)
3. **Shear reinforcement** ($V_{us}$)

**IS 1343 design equation:**

$$V = V_c + V_p + V_{us}$$

where $V_{us} = 0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / S_v$ for vertical stirrups.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3(a)** `[7 Marks]` 

A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam has the following properties:
- Section: 300 mm × 700 mm
- Effective depth: $d = 650$ mm
- Prestress force at support: $P = 2000$ kN
- Cable slope at support: $\tan\alpha = 0.08$
- Factored shear force at support: $V_u = 600$ kN
- Grade of concrete: M50
- Grade of stirrups: Fe 415
- $p_t = 1.5\%$

**Calculate the spacing of 2-legged 10 mm dia vertical stirrups at the support.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** $\tau_v = V_u / (bd) = (600 \times 10^3) / (300 \times 650) = 3.08$ N/mm²

**Step 2:** $\tau_{c,max}$ for M50 = **4.0 N/mm²**. So $3.08 < 4.0$ ✓

**Step 3:** $V_p = P \sin\alpha = 2000 \times 0.08 = 160$ kN

**Step 4:** For $p_t = 1.5\%$ and M50, from IS 1343 Table 6: $\tau_c = 0.98$ N/mm²
$$V_c = 0.98 \times 300 \times 650 = 191100 \text{ N} = 191.1 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** $V_{us} = 600 - 160 - 191.1 = 248.9$ kN

**Step 6:** $A_{sv} = 2 \times \pi/4 \times 10^2 = 157$ mm²

$$S_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 157 \times 650}{248900} = \frac{37033801.5}{248900} = 148.8 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $S_v = 145$ mm c/c** (next lower multiple of 5).

**Step 7: Checks:**
- $0.75d = 487.5$ mm → $145 < 487.5$ ✓
- $S_v \le 300$ mm ✓
- $A_{sv}/(bS_v) = 157/(300 \times 145) = 0.00361$
- $0.4/(0.87 \times 415) = 0.00111$ → $0.00361 > 0.00111$ ✓

**Answer: Provide 2-legged 10 mm dia vertical stirrups @ 145 mm c/c near support.**

---

#### **Q3(b)** `[7 Marks]` 

Explain the **IS 1343 provisions for minimum shear reinforcement** in prestressed concrete beams. Why is it essential even when calculated shear demand is low?

#### **Model Solution:**

As per **IS 1343 Cl. 18.6.1** (referencing IS 456 Cl. 26.5.1.6):

$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot S_v} \ge \frac{0.4}{0.87 f_y}$$

For Fe 415 stirrups:
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot S_v} \ge \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times 415} = 0.00111$$

For Fe 500 stirrups:
$$\frac{A_{sv}}{b \cdot S_v} \ge \frac{0.4}{0.87 \times 500} = 0.00092$$

**Why minimum shear reinforcement is essential:**

1. **Sudden failure prevention:** Plain concrete (without stirrups) fails **abruptly** in shear without warning. Minimum stirrups provide **ductility** and visible cracking before collapse.

2. **Torsional resistance:** Even under pure bending, accidental torsion (e.g., unsymmetrical loading on bridge girders) develops. Minimum stirrups resist it.

3. **Confinement of concrete:** Stirrups confine the concrete, increasing its compressive strength and ductility — particularly important at supports where bearing stresses are high.

4. **Resistance to shrinkage and thermal stresses:** Transverse reinforcement restrains shrinkage cracks, especially in mass PSC members.

5. **IS code philosophy:** IS 1343 follows the **limit state design philosophy** where structures should have **redundancy and robustness** even under unforeseen loadings.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### **Q4(a)** `[7 Marks]`

A pretensioned PSC beam of rectangular section 200 mm × 400 mm has effective depth 360 mm. The prestressing force of 1000 kN is applied with a parabolic cable having an eccentricity of 80 mm at midspan and 0 at supports. The cable has a slope of $1/12$ at the support. The beam carries a uniformly distributed load of 30 kN/m (including self-weight) over a simply supported span of 8 m. **Check the adequacy of the section in shear at the support, assuming M40 concrete and Fe 415 stirrups. If inadequate, design the shear reinforcement.**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Reactions $R = 30 \times 8 / 2 = 120$ kN
Factored shear $V_u = 1.5 \times 120 = 180$ kN

**Step 2:** $\tau_v = 180000 / (200 \times 360) = 2.50$ N/mm²

**Step 3:** $\tau_{c,max}$ for M40 = **3.5 N/mm²** → Section OK ✓

**Step 4:** Slope at support $\tan\alpha = 1/12 = 0.0833$
$$V_p = 1000 \times 0.0833 = 83.33 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5:** For $p_t \approx 1.0\%$ and M40, $\tau_c = 0.62$ N/mm² (from IS 1343 Table 6)
$$V_c = 0.62 \times 200 \times 360 = 44640 \text{ N} = 44.64 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6:** $V_{us} = 180 - 83.33 - 44.64 = 52.03$ kN

**Step 7:** Try 2-legged 8 mm stirrups: $A_{sv} = 2 \times 50.27 = 100.5$ mm²
$$S_v = \frac{0.87 \times 415 \times 100.5 \times 360}{52030} = \frac{13062921}{52030} = 251.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Adopt $S_v = 250$ mm c/c** (multiple of 25).

**Step 8: Checks:**
- $0.75 \times 360 = 270$ mm → $250 < 270$ ✓
- $S_v \le 300$ mm ✓
- $A_{sv}/(bS_v) = 100.5/(200 \times 250) = 0.00201 > 0.00111$ ✓

**Answer: Provide 2-legged 8 mm dia vertical stirrups @ 250 mm c/c.**

---

#### **Q4(b)** `[7 Marks]`

**Differentiate between the design of transverse reinforcement using vertical stirrups and inclined stirrups. Under what conditions are inclined stirrups preferred?**

#### **Model Solution:**

| **Aspect** | **Vertical Stirrups** | **Inclined Stirrups** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Orientation | Perpendicular to longitudinal axis | Inclined at angle $\alpha$ (usually 45°) |
| Capacity formula | $V_{us} = 0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / S_v$ | $V_{us} = (0.87 f_y A_{sv} d / S_v)(\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha)$ |
| Efficiency | Lower | ~1.414× more efficient at $\alpha = 45°$ |
| Construction simplicity | Easier; easier tying | Difficult; needs skilled labor |
| Use in deep beams | Common | Preferred in deep transfer girders |
| Cost | Lower | Higher due to fabrication complexity |

**When inclined stirrups are preferred:**
1. **Very deep beams** (transfer girders, deep pile caps) where shear force is extremely high
2. **Pre-tensioned members** with cable drape matching stirrup inclination
3. **Combined shear + torsion** members
4. **Limited web width** situations
5. **Bridges with heavy concentrated loads** at supports
6. **At locations of cable anchorage** where diagonal tension is concentrated

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS**
>
> Students typically **lose 2–4 marks** in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add $V_p = P\sin\alpha$** — many students compute $V_{us}$ ignoring the vertical component of prestress. This is the **#1 mistake** flagged by KTU examiners.
>
> 2. **Not checking $\tau_{c,max}$** — even if the section is adequate, students skip this and lose 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Wrong sign for $V_p$** — if cable slopes *downward* toward the support, $V_p$ must be **subtracted**, not added. Always draw the cable profile!
>
> 4. **Confusing $A_{sv}$** — for 2-legged stirrups, $A_{sv} = 2A_\phi$, not $A_\phi$. For 4-legged, $A_{sv} = 4A_\phi$.
>
> 5. **Not applying IS 1343 Table 6 carefully** — $\tau_c$ depends on BOTH $f_{ck}$ AND $p_t$. Always interpolate.
>
> 6. **Skipping minimum shear reinforcement check** — this is a 1-mark deduction even if your main design is correct.
>
> 7. **Forgetting spacing practical limit** — $S_v \ge 100$ mm for concrete placement; many use 50 mm spacings which are impossible to cast.
>
> 8. **In answer writing — units inconsistency** — keep $V$ in N (not kN) inside formulas, or divide accordingly.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL POINTS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

- ✅ **Master formula:** $V = V_c + V_p + V_{us}$ — the holy trinity of shear design in PSC.
- ✅ **Sequence of design steps:** Calculate $\tau_v$ → Check $\tau_{c,max}$ → Find $V_c$ from Table 6 → Calculate $V_p = P\sin\alpha$ → Solve for $V_{us}$ → Compute $S_v$ → Apply 3 spacing checks.
- ✅ **Three spacing checks (NON-NEGOTIABLE):**
   1. $S_v \le 0.75d$
   2. $S_v \le 300$ mm
   3. $A_{sv}/(bS_v) \ge 0.4/(0.87 f_y)$
- ✅ **Memorize Table 6 (IS 1343) for $\tau_c$** — values for M40: 0.6 (for $p_t = 0.5\%$) to 1.10 (for $p_t = 3.0\%$); for M50: 0.65 to 1.30.
- ✅ **Memorize $\tau_{c,max}$ for M40, M50, M60:** 3.5, 4.0, 4.5 N/mm².
- ✅ **Inclined stirrups formula multiplier:** $(\sin\alpha + \cos\alpha) = 1.414$ at $\alpha = 45°$.
- ✅ **Sign of $V_p$:** + (added) when cable goes UP toward support; − (subtracted) when cable goes DOWN.
- ✅ **Effective depth formula:** $d = D - \text{effective cover}$ (cover = 50 mm for beams, 75 mm for slabs).
- ✅ **Area of stirrups:** 2-legged 8 mm = 100.5 mm²; 2-legged 10 mm = 157 mm²; 2-legged 12 mm = 226 mm².
- ✅ **Always specify stirrups near support with denser spacing** (≤ 125 mm c/c) and reduce density toward midspan.
- ✅ **Punching shear check** is required for PSC slabs — uses $\tau_v = V/(4b_0 d)$ where $b_0$ is perimeter.
- ✅ **Maximum shear capacity of section** is governed by crushing of web — if exceeded, increase $b$ or $d$.
- ✅ **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the cable profile diagram and the stirrup layout in the answer sheet — it carries **2 free marks** for presentation even if the calculation has minor errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Anchorage System and Design of End Block by Latest IS Codes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Different Anchorage System and Design of End Block by Latest IS Codes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Prestressing Anchorage — Formal Definition

An **anchorage system** in prestressed concrete is a mechanical device that permanently transfers the high tensile force developed in the prestressing tendon (wire, strand, or bar) into the concrete member after the tensioning operation is completed. The anchorage must perform three simultaneous duties:
1. Hold the tendon against slippage under sustained prestress.
2. Distribute the concentrated tendon force into the surrounding concrete without exceeding local bearing capacity.
3. Provide adequate fatigue resistance under cyclic live loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IS 1343:2012 (Clause 20.4)** and **IS 6006**, every anchorage used in Indian practice must be type-tested for efficiency ≥ **95%** of the characteristic breaking load of the tendon.

### 1.2 End Block — Formal Definition

The **end block** (also called the **anchorage zone**) is the volume of concrete immediately behind the anchorage, within which the highly concentrated tendon force disperses from the small bearing plate area into the full cross-section of the member. The length of the end block is conventionally taken as **equal to the larger lateral dimension of the member** (i.e., h or b, whichever is greater).

> [!NOTE]
> IS 1343:2012 (Clause 21) defines the end block strictly as the region over which the non-uniform (concentrated) prestress gradient exists before it becomes linearly distributed (Saint-Venant's principle).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Insight)

**Analogy 1 — The Hose Nozzle:** Imagine squeezing a high-pressure garden hose with your thumb. The water jet emerging is narrow and extremely forceful at the thumb, but it spreads and slows down a short distance away. The anchorage is your thumb, the tendon is the high-pressure water, and the end block is the region where the stream "fans out" into the pipe wall.

**Analogy 2 — The Pillow Push:** Press your fist firmly into a soft pillow. Directly under your knuckles the pillow is severely compressed (high local stress), but a few inches away the deformation is uniform. The end block behaves similarly — concrete in the bearing zone experiences a **bursting tensile hoop stress** (the pillow bulges outward at the edges) which must be resisted by reinforcement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The two most critical stresses generated in the end block are:
> 1. **Bursting Tension (σ_bt)** — tensile stress acting perpendicular to the line of the tendon, tending to split the concrete transversely.
> 2. **Spalling Tension (σ_st)** — tensile stress at the free surface behind the anchorage, tending to peel off a corner of concrete.
>
> Both must be resisted by *secondary* (non-prestressed) reinforcement placed orthogonal to the prestressing direction.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Code Standards

| Parameter | Standard Value | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Bearing pressure on concrete | ≤ **0.48 f_ck** | IS 1343:2012, Cl. 20.4 |
| Characteristic cylinder strength | **f_ck** (N/mm²) | IS 456:2000 |
| Bursting reinforcement yield stress limit | **f_s ≤ 0.87 f_y** | IS 1343:2012, Cl. 21.6.3 |
| Minimum clear cover to links | **50 mm** for end block | IS 1343:2012 |
| Transmission length for strands | ≈ **60 d_b** (per IS 1343) | IS 1343:2012 |
| Transmission length for wires | ≈ **100 d_b** (per IS 1343) | IS 1343:2012 |

where **d_b** is the nominal diameter of the tendon.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Anchorage Systems

Anchorage systems in prestressed concrete are classified into **three broad families** based on the mechanism of load transfer:

1. **Bond Anchorage** — Force transfer is achieved purely through the chemical/adhesive bond between the tendon and the surrounding grout (used in *post-tensioned* construction after grouting of the duct). Examples: straight bonded wires, indented strands.
2. **Mechanical Anchorage** — Force transfer is achieved through a positive mechanical gripping action (wedges, buttons, threads, nuts). Used in both pre-tensioning and post-tensioning.
3. **Bearing-Plate Anchorage** — Force transfer is achieved through a large steel plate that bears against the concrete face, spreading the force over a wider area.

> [!NOTE]
> For pre-tensioned members, the *initial grip* is provided by bond alone during stressing; once the concrete hardens and the tendon is de-tensioned, the bond becomes the permanent anchorage.

### 2.2 Important Anchorage Types (Engineer's Reference)

| Anchorage | Developed By | Suitable For | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Freyssinet Cone** | E. Freyssinet (France) | Wires (12 × 5 mm) | Concrete wedge inserts into a steel cone; tapered helical wires |
| **Gifford-Udall** | Gifford & Udall (UK) | Wires (8 wires) | Split cone wedges driven by a power ram |
| **BBRV** (Birsilian, Birkenmeier, Brandestini, Vogt) | Switzerland | Large bars/wires | Cold-formed button heads anchored against a washer |
| **Magnel** | Magnel (Belgium) | Flat metal sandwiched wires (4 × 5 mm) | Sandwich plates of prescribed thickness to suit the load |
| **Dywidag** | Dywidag (Germany) | Dyform strands / Bars | Threaded bars anchored with anchor nuts and bearing plates |
| **Stressteel** | Stressteel Corp (USA) | High-strength bars | Threads cold-rolled onto the bar ends |
| **VSL** (Vorspann System Losinger) | Switzerland | Multi-strand | Wedges grip individual strands inside a multi-hole anchor head |
| **Single/Multi-Strand** | Various | 7-wire strands (12.7 mm / 15.2 mm) | Standard in modern Indian post-tensioning practice |

### 2.3 Why End Blocks Are Required

When a tendon is anchored, the area of the bearing plate (A_a) is typically only **0.5 % to 2 %** of the gross cross-sectional area (A_c). The prestress must therefore "diffuse" from a small area to a large area, generating severe **transverse tensile stresses** at right angles to the tendon. These stresses peak at a distance of approximately **0.4 D to 0.6 D** from the anchor face, where **D** is the largest cross-sectional dimension.

> [!WARNING]
> Without adequate bursting/spalling reinforcement, the end block will fail by **splitting cracks** running parallel to the tendon — a classic catastrophic anchorage failure with no prior warning.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (IS 1343:2012 — Simplified Method)

The following equations are the *board-tested* formulas required for KTU ESE numerical problems. They come directly from **IS 1343:2012, Clause 21.6.2.1**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bearing pressure under plate | $$f_b = \dfrac{P}{A_a}$$ | $P$ = total prestress force; $A_a$ = net bearing plate area |
| 2 | Maximum permissible bearing pressure | $$f_{b,\max} = 0.48 \, f_{ck}$$ | Serviceability limit (IS 1343) |
| 3 | Side of equivalent square prism (single anchorage) | $$2y_0 = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{A_a}} \cdot a$$ | $A$ = gross area; $A_a$ = anchorage plate area; $a$ = side of plate |
| 4 | Simplified bursting zone (square) | $$2y_0 = a$$ | $a$ = larger lateral dimension of the member |
| 5 | Bursting tensile force — single concentric anchorage | $$P_b = 0.32 \, P$$ | (IS 1343, Cl. 21.6.2.1) |
| 6 | Bursting force — closely spaced ($s \le a/4$) | $$P_b = 0.32 \, P \, \left(1 + 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{s}{a}\right)$$ | $s$ = C/C anchorage spacing; $a$ = larger dimension |
| 7 | Bursting force — widely spaced ($s > 4a$) | $$P_b = 0.32 \, P \cdot \left(\dfrac{4a}{s}\right)$$ | Reduction due to dispersion overlap |
| 8 | Location of resultant bursting force (from anchor face) | $$y_p = 0.5 \, y_0$$ | Acts at the centroid of the bursting zone |
| 9 | Spalling tensile force — single anchorage | $$P_s = 0.04 \, P$$ | Acts at the free surface (IS 1343) |
| 10 | Bursting reinforcement area | $$A_s = \dfrac{P_b}{f_s}, \quad f_s = 0.87 \, f_y$$ | $f_y$ = characteristic yield strength of secondary steel |
| 11 | Spalling reinforcement area | $$A_{s,sp} = \dfrac{P_s}{f_s}$$ | Placed as U-bars at right angles to tendon |
| 12 | Transmission length — strand | $$L_t = 60 \, d_b$$ | (IS 1343) |
| 13 | Transmission length — wire | $$L_t = 100 \, d_b$$ | (IS 1343) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All multi-anchorage formulas in the table are valid only for **rectangular sections** and for symmetric anchorages placed near the centroid. Eccentric anchorages require Magnel's or Guyon's method (covered in §3.2).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

End-block design governs the **serviceability and ultimate strength** of every PSC bridge girder, railway sleeper, nuclear containment, and long-span floor beam in India. The Standard Specifications for Road and Rail Bridges (IRC:112, IRS Concrete Bridge Code) and the Ministry of Shipping & Transport (MoRTH) specifications explicitly adopt IS 1343 provisions. A poorly designed end block causes:
- Diagonal splitting cracks during jacking.
- Progressive spalling at corners.
- Loss of prestress due to tendon pull-in.
- Fatigue failure under repetitive vehicle loading.

> [!NOTE]
> The end block is one of the most *frequently under-designed* zones in practical PSC construction, because the biaxial stress state is invisible in routine 1D beam analysis. **IS 1343:2012 (Cl. 21.6.3)** makes the provision of bursting steel **mandatory** in every PSC element.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the IS 1343 Simplified Bursting Force (P_b = 0.32 P)

The IS 1343 coefficient **0.32** is *empirically calibrated* by IS from a large series of 2-D photo-elastic and finite-element studies on rectangular blocks. Below is a *physics-based derivation* using the equivalent prism model (used by the British code BS 8110 and adopted by IS 1343).

**Step 1 — Model the end block as a square prism.**
Consider a square end block of side **2y₀** containing a concentric square anchorage plate of side **a**. The applied force is **P** (tensile in the tendon, compressive on the concrete face).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium in the horizontal (transverse) direction.**
Consider a horizontal section through the prism at depth **y** from the anchor face. Let the burst zone width at depth **y** be **b(y)** and the bursting tensile stress be **σ_bt(y)**. A free-body cut through the prism gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{y_0} \sigma_{bt}(y) \cdot b(y) \, dy &= P_b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume linear stress distribution (Saint-Venant's principle).**
The bursting stress is assumed to be maximum at depth **y_p = 0.5 y₀** and zero at the ends:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bt}(y) &= \sigma_{bt,\max} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y}{y_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the equivalent prism dimension 2y₀.**
For a square anchorage of side **a**, Saint-Venant's theory gives the dispersion of the stress front at 45° (Mohr's circle compatibility). Setting the compressive stress at depth **y₀** equal to the average compressive stress on the gross section (P/A):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{c, \text{avg}} &= \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{P}{(2y_0)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The stress at the front of the equivalent prism equals the bearing-plate stress **f_b = P/A_a = P/a²**. Equating compatibility at depth **y₀**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{P}{(2y_0)^2} &= \dfrac{P}{a^2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{y_0}{y_0}\right) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

A more rigorous result, obtained by integrating the elastic stress bulb (Timoshenko, 1959), gives the side of the equivalent square prism as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 y_0 &= 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{A_a}} \cdot a
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the IS 1343 formula in row 3 of §2.4.

**Step 5 — Calibrate the bursting force coefficient (0.32) from FEM/photo-elastic data.**
Empirical results (which IS 1343 quotes from BS 8110) give:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= k \cdot P, \quad \text{where } k = 0.32 \text{ for } \dfrac{A_a}{A} \le 0.02
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\boxed{P_b = 0.32 \, P}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the IS 1343 simplified result for a *single concentric anchorage in a rectangular section*.

### 3.2 Derivation of Multi-Anchorage Bursting Force (IS 1343, Cl. 21.6.2.1)

When **n** anchorages of size **a** are placed at centre-to-centre spacing **s** within the same end block, the bursting zones **interact**. Two limiting cases govern:

**Case A — Closely spaced anchorages (s ≤ a/4):**
The individual bursting zones fully merge into one large zone. The bursting force *increases* due to superposition. IS 1343 gives:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.32 \, P \, \left(1 + 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{s}{a}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation logic:** When **s → 0** (zero spacing), the formula gives **P_b = 0.32 P** (single anchor case). When **s = a/4**, the formula gives **P_b = 0.32 P × 1.375 = 0.44 P**, reflecting the *amplified* burst.

**Case B — Widely spaced anchorages (s > 4a):**
The bursting zones no longer overlap. The total bursting force is the *sum* of the individual contributions, normalized over the gross length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.32 \, P \cdot \left(\dfrac{4a}{s}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation logic:** As **s → ∞**, **P_b → 0** (each anchor's burst is independent and confined). At **s = 4a**, **P_b = 0.32 P** (the zones just touch, behaving like a single anchor).

**Interpolating between the two limits** (for **a/4 < s ≤ 4a**), IS 1343 recommends linear interpolation or, more rigorously, **Guyon's elastic analysis** (covered in §3.3).

### 3.3 Magnel's Method — Analytical Framework

Magnel (1954) developed a more refined method that considers the **linear distribution of bursting stress**. The bursting zone is defined by a parabolic boundary passing through the edges of the anchorage plate. The bursting force is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= \dfrac{P}{2} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{a}{D}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where **D** is the larger lateral dimension of the end block and **a** is the anchorage plate size. For the limiting case **a → 0**, **P_b = P/2**; for **a = D**, **P_b = 0** (no bursting). IS 1343 simplified formula yields **P_b = 0.32 P**, which is the *least-squares best fit* of Magnel's result for the practical range **a/D = 0.1 to 0.3**.

### 3.4 Guyon's Method — Elastic Compatibility

Guyon's approach treats the end block as a 3-D elastic continuum, where the bursting tensile stress at any point **(x, y)** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{bt}(x, y) &= \dfrac{P}{D} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{1}{1 + (y/y_p)} - \dfrac{x^2}{x_0^2 + x^2} \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

where **x₀** is the half-width of the anchor and **y_p = 0.5 y₀**. Guyon's method is used for **eccentric anchorages** and **irregular cross-sections** where IS 1343 simplified equations are not applicable.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** A post-tensioned PSC beam, 300 mm × 600 mm, is prestressed by a Freyssinet cable carrying a force of **P = 1800 kN** at the centroid. The anchorage plate is **180 mm × 180 mm**. Grade of concrete is **M50**; steel is **Fe 415**. Design the end block using IS 1343:2012 simplified method.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Check bearing pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_b &= \dfrac{P}{A_a} = \dfrac{1800 \times 10^3}{180 \times 180} = 55.56 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Maximum permissible:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{b,\max} &= 0.48 \, f_{ck} = 0.48 \times 50 = 24.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since **f_b > f_{b,max}**, the plate size is *inadequate*. Increase plate to 240 mm × 240 mm.

**Step 2 — Revised plate size.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_b &= \dfrac{1800 \times 10^3}{240 \times 240} = 31.25 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Still exceeds 24 N/mm². Use a **double-stiffened plate** or increase to **280 × 280 mm**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_b &= \dfrac{1800 \times 10^3}{280 \times 280} = 22.96 \text{ N/mm}^2 \; < 24 \text{ N/mm}^2 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Side of equivalent square prism.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 y_0 &= 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{A_a}} \cdot a = 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{300 \times 600}{280 \times 280}} \cdot 280 = 510.6 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

For simplicity, IS 1343 allows the *larger lateral dimension* **D = 600 mm**.

**Step 4 — Bursting tensile force (single anchorage, concentric).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.32 \, P = 0.32 \times 1800 = 576 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Bursting reinforcement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \dfrac{P_b}{0.87 \, f_y} = \dfrac{576 \times 10^3}{0.87 \times 415} = 1595 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide **5-legged 12 mm dia. links** at **75 mm c/c** over the bursting zone depth 510 mm.

Check: 5 × π/4 × 12² = 565 mm² per link. Number of links = 510/75 = 7 (rounded up).

Total area = 5 × 565 × 7 = **19 775 mm²** ≫ 1595 mm² ✓.

**Step 6 — Spalling tensile force and reinforcement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= 0.04 \, P = 0.04 \times 1800 = 72 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{s,sp} &= \dfrac{72 \times 10^3}{0.87 \times 415} = 199.4 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide **3 nos. of 10 mm dia. U-bars** at the free surface: 3 × 78.5 = **235.5 mm²** ✓.

**Step 7 — End-block reinforcement detailing.**

- Closed links 12 mm dia., 5-legged, 75 mm c/c, over length = 600 mm.
- Spalling U-bars 10 mm dia., 3 nos., with 50 mm side cover.
- Cover to reinforcement = 50 mm minimum.

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example directly mirrors the style of **KTU Dec 2023, Question A (Module 4)** — students are advised to memorize the order of the seven design steps above.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Prestressing Anchorage Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Prestressing Anchorage Systems"]
    A0 --> A1["Bond Anchorage"]
    A0 --> A2["Mechanical Anchorage"]
    A0 --> A3["Bearing Plate Anchorage"]
    A1 --> A11["Plain bonded wires"]
    A1 --> A12["Indented or crimped strands"]
    A1 --> A13["Grouted post-tensioned ducts"]
    A2 --> A21["Wedge type"]
    A2 --> A22["Button head type"]
    A2 --> A23["Threaded bar with nut"]
    A2 --> A24["Swaged anchor head"]
    A21 --> A211["Freyssinet Cone"]
    A21 --> A212["Gifford Udall split wedge"]
    A21 --> A213["VSL multi strand wedge"]
    A22 --> A221["BBRV Button Head System"]
    A22 --> A222["Prescon Button Head"]
    A23 --> A231["Dywidag Threaded Bar"]
    A23 --> A232["Stressteel Rolled Thread"]
    A3 --> A31["Single strand plate"]
    A3 --> A32["Multi strand bearing plate"]
    A3 --> A33["Stiffened embedded anchor"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: End-Block Stress Zones (IS 1343 Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0["End Block Stress Analysis"]
    B0 --> B1["Anchor Face"]
    B0 --> B2["Bursting Zone"]
    B0 --> B3["Spalling Zone"]
    B0 --> B4["General Section Far Field"]
    B1 --> B1a["Bearing Plate Region"]
    B1a --> B1b["Bearing Pressure less than 0.48 fck"]
    B2 --> B2a["Tensile Burst Stress perpendicular to tendon"]
    B2 --> B2b["Depth equals 2y zero from anchor face"]
    B2 --> B2c["Centroid at y zero by 2 from anchor"]
    B3 --> B3a["Tensile Spalling Stress at free surface"]
    B3 --> B3b["Acts along the loaded face edges"]
    B3 --> B3c["Tendency to peel off concrete corner"]
    B4 --> B4a["Linear uniaxial prestress distribution"]
    B4 --> B4b["Section behaves as a simple beam"]
    B2 --> B2d["Resisted by closed links or mesh reinforcement"]
    B3 --> B3d["Resisted by U bars orthogonal to tendon"]
    B1 --> B1c["Resisted by concrete in bearing"]
    B1c --> B1d["Allowed limit 0.48 fck per IS 1343"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Design Flow for End Block (IS 1343:2012 Method)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0["End Block Design Flow"]
    C0 --> C1["Step 1: Gather input data P, fck, fy, plate size a, section D"]
    C1 --> C2["Step 2: Check bearing pressure fb less than 0.48 fck"]
    C2 --> C3{"fb less than 0.48 fck ?"}
    C3 -- No --> C4["Step 2a: Increase plate size or stiffen plate"]
    C4 --> C2
    C3 -- Yes --> C5["Step 3: Determine bursting zone side 2y zero"]
    C5 --> C6["Step 4: Compute bursting force Pb using IS 1343"]
    C6 --> C7["Step 5: Compute bursting reinforcement As equals Pb by 0.87 fy"]
    C7 --> C8["Step 6: Compute spalling force Ps equals 0.04 P"]
    C8 --> C9["Step 7: Compute spalling reinforcement Asp"]
    C9 --> C10["Step 8: Detailing of links and U bars in 2D drawing"]
    C10 --> C11["Step 9: Final check on cover minimum 50 mm and lap length"]
    C11 --> C12["Step 10: Prepare bar bending schedule and end block drawing"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Failure Modes in an Unreinforced End Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0["End Block Failure Modes"]
    D0 --> D1["Bursting Failure"]
    D0 --> D2["Spalling Failure"]
    D0 --> D3["Bearing Crushing"]
    D0 --> D4["Edge Spalling"]
    D1 --> D1a["Splitting cracks parallel to tendon"]
    D1 --> D1b["Cause: insufficient closed links or mesh"]
    D2 --> D2a["Surface concrete peels off"]
    D2 --> D2b["Cause: missing U bars at free face"]
    D3 --> D3a["Local crushing under bearing plate"]
    D3 --> D3b["Cause: fb exceeds 0.48 fck"]
    D4 --> D4a["Side spalling of corner concrete"]
    D4 --> D4b["Cause: inadequate edge cover or low fck"]
    D1a --> D1c["Result: sudden anchorage failure"]
    D2a --> D2c["Result: progressive loss of prestress"]
    D3a --> D3c["Result: ductile plate penetration"]
    D4a --> D4c["Result: corner chipping visible externally"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid block diagrams (flowcharts) are *semantic equivalents* of physical engineering sketches. They are the standard academic substitute recommended by KTU for digital notes, where a free-hand sketch of the end block cannot be embedded.
