## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]**
*Define the term "anchorage zone" in prestressed concrete. What is its significance as per IS 1343:2012?*

**Model Answer:**
The anchorage zone (also called the end block) is the volume of concrete immediately behind the anchorage device over which the highly concentrated prestressing force is dispersed into the full cross-section of the member. As per IS 1343:2012 (Clause 21), its length is taken as equal to the larger lateral dimension of the cross-section. Its significance is that within this region the stress distribution is non-linear and biaxial, generating transverse **bursting tension** and **spalling tension** that must be resisted by secondary reinforcement, failing which the member may suffer sudden splitting failure.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*List any three types of mechanical anchorages used in post-tensioned prestressed concrete and state the tendon type for which each is most suitable.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Freyssinet Cone Anchorage** — most suitable for 12 × 5 mm diameter high-tensile wires.
2. **VSL Multi-Strand Anchorage** — most suitable for 7-wire strands (12.7 mm or 15.2 mm) using individual wedges.
3. **Dywidag Threaded Bar Anchorage** — most suitable for high-tensile threaded bars (26 mm to 47 mm dia.) anchored with anchor nuts.
(A third common alternative: **BBRV Button-Head Anchorage** for large bundles of wires or small bars.)

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **IS 1343:2012 simplified method** for computing the bursting tensile force in the end block of a post-tensioned PSC beam. Clearly state the formulas for (i) closely spaced anchorages, (ii) widely spaced anchorages, and (iii) the location of the bursting force from the anchor face.

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The IS 1343:2012 simplified method is an empirical procedure based on extensive photo-elastic and finite-element studies. It assumes a single "equivalent square prism" of side **2y₀** centred on the anchorage and treats the bursting stress distribution as linear.

**Formula (i) — Single concentric anchorage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.32 \, P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula (ii) — Closely spaced anchorages (s ≤ a/4):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.32 \, P \, \left(1 + 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{s}{a}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula (iii) — Widely spaced anchorages (s > 4a):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= 0.32 \, P \cdot \left(\dfrac{4a}{s}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Location of the resultant bursting force** from the anchor face is at **y_p = 0.5 y₀**, i.e., at the centroid of the bursting zone.

**Side of the bursting zone:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2y_0 &= 2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{A}{A_a}} \cdot a \quad \text{(general)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For design simplicity IS 1343 permits using the larger cross-section dimension **D** as **2y₀**.

The bursting reinforcement is then calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s &= \dfrac{P_b}{0.87 \, f_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is provided as closed links uniformly distributed over the depth **2y₀** measured from the anchor face.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A post-tensioned PSC girder, 300 mm wide × 800 mm deep, is prestressed by two cables each carrying **800 kN** at the centroid. The anchorage plates are 200 mm × 200 mm. Grade of concrete is **M40**; steel is **Fe 415**. Design the end block reinforcement as per IS 1343:2012.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total prestress force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 2 \times 800 = 1600 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Bearing pressure check.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_b &= \dfrac{800 \times 10^3}{200 \times 200} = 20.0 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Permissible:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{b,\max} = 0.48 \times 40 = 19.2 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Since 20.0 > 19.2, the plate is slightly inadequate. Increase plate to 210 mm × 210 mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_b = \dfrac{800 \times 10^3}{210 \times 210} = 18.14 \text{ N/mm}^2 \le 19.2 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Spacing of anchorages.**
The two anchorages are placed at the centroid, s = 0 (concentric). Use the single-anchor formula.

**Step 4 — Bursting zone side.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2y_0 = \text{larger dimension} = 800 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Bursting tensile force (per cable).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b = 0.32 \times 800 = 256 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Bursting reinforcement (per cable).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s = \dfrac{256 \times 10^3}{0.87 \times 415} = 708.9 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide **4-legged 10 mm dia. closed links** at 80 mm c/c over 800 mm length.
Area per link = 4 × π/4 × 10² = 314 mm².
Number of links = 800 / 80 = 10.
Total area provided = 314 × 10 = 3140 mm² ≫ 708.9 mm² ✓.

**Step 7 — Spalling reinforcement.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s = 0.04 \times 800 = 32 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{s,sp} = \dfrac{32 \times 10^3}{0.87 \times 415} = 88.6 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide **2 nos. 10 mm U-bars** at each free surface: 2 × 78.5 = 157 mm² ✓.

**Conclusion:** The end block is safe with the proposed 10 mm dia. closed-link reinforcement at 80 mm c/c and 10 mm U-bars at the free surface.

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **classification of anchorage systems** in prestressed concrete with neat sketches (block diagram). Discuss the working of the **Freyssinet cone anchorage** and the **BBRV button-head anchorage**.

**Model Answer:**

**Classification:**

Anchorage systems are classified into three families:

1. **Bond Anchorage:** Force is transferred to the concrete through chemical/mechanical bond between the tendon and the grout. Examples: plain bonded wires, indented strands, grouted post-tensioned ducts.

2. **Mechanical Anchorage:** Force is transferred by a positive mechanical gripping action. Sub-types are:
   - Wedge anchorage (Freyssinet, Gifford-Udall, VSL).
   - Button-head anchorage (BBRV, Prescon).
   - Threaded-bar anchorage (Dywidag, Stressteel).

3. **Bearing-Plate Anchorage:** Force is transferred through a steel plate bearing against the concrete face. Sub-types: single-strand plate, multi-strand plate, stiffened embedded anchor.

**Freyssinet Cone Anchorage (Working):**
A cylindrical steel cone of specified taper houses a bundle of 12 high-tensile wires of 5 mm or 7 mm diameter. A second outer steel sleeve (the anchor body) surrounds the cone. When the wires are stressed, a high-tensile concrete plug (or split wedge) is driven into the cone. The tapered cone then grips the wires by friction and the entire force is transmitted to the concrete through a square bearing plate. The taper angle is critical (≈ 7°) — too steep and the cone slips; too shallow and the wires are not gripped adequately. It is one of the most widely used anchorages for **post-tensioned bridge girders** in Indian practice.

**BBRV Button-Head Anchorage (Working):**
Each wire end is upset-forged into a button (cold-formed head) of ≈ 2 × wire diameter. The wires pass through a perforated steel anchor plate, with their button heads bearing against the plate. A cylindrical steel anchor head is screwed onto the plate, providing a positive mechanical lock. The prestress force in each wire is transmitted to the anchor plate via the button head, and then to the concrete via a large bearing washer. The system is well-suited to **large bars and wire bundles** used in heavy civil engineering, particularly in Swiss and European long-span bridges.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A post-tensioned PSC beam, 400 mm × 1000 mm, is prestressed by a single cable at the centroid carrying **P = 2400 kN**. Anchorage plate is 250 mm × 250 mm. M50 concrete, Fe 500 steel. Compute the bursting and spalling reinforcement as per IS 1343:2012.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bearing pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_b = \dfrac{2400 \times 10^3}{250 \times 250} = 38.4 \text{ N/mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Permissible: 0.48 × 50 = **24 N/mm²**.

Since 38.4 > 24, the plate is grossly inadequate. Adopt a stiffened plate of equivalent effective area 2400 × 10³ / 24 = 100 000 mm², i.e., 320 mm × 320 mm. Use a **double-stiffened plate** of overall 250 mm × 250 mm but with effective bearing area 100 000 mm².

**Step 2 — Bursting zone side.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
2y_0 = \text{larger dimension} = 1000 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bursting tensile force (single anchor).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b = 0.32 \times 2400 = 768 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Bursting reinforcement (f_s = 0.87 f_y = 0.87 × 500 = 435 N/mm²).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_s = \dfrac{768 \times 10^3}{435} = 1765.5 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide **6-legged 12 mm dia. links** at 100 mm c/c.
Area per link = 6 × 113.1 = 678.6 mm².
Number of links = 1000 / 100 = 10.
Total area provided = 678.6 × 10 = 6786 mm² ≫ 1765.5 mm² ✓.

**Step 5 — Spalling tensile force.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s = 0.04 \times 2400 = 96 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{s,sp} = \dfrac{96 \times 10^3}{435} = 220.7 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Provide **3 nos. 12 mm U-bars** at each free surface: 3 × 113.1 = 339.3 mm² ✓.

**Step 6 — Detailing.**
- Bursting links: 12 mm dia., 6-legged, 100 mm c/c, over 1000 mm length behind anchor face.
- Spalling bars: 12 mm U-bars, 3 nos., with 50 mm cover.
- 50 mm cover to all secondary reinforcement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the f_s = 0.87 f_y reduction** in the bursting steel — full marks lost.
> 2. **Confusing the bursting zone dimension** (use larger of D or 2√(A/A_a)·a) — many students pick the *smaller* dimension and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Omitting the spalling check** — the IS 1343 spalling formula (0.04 P) is a *separate* item and must be computed even if the bursting reinforcement "looks enough."
> 4. **Inadequate bearing plate** — students often skip the 0.48 f_ck check. A wrong plate size invalidates the entire design.
> 5. **Forgetting the multi-anchorage case** — if the question specifies "closely spaced" or "widely spaced," the formula changes. The single-anchor formula (0.32 P) is only valid for one anchorage or concentric cluster.
> 6. **Not providing the side cover** — minimum 50 mm clear cover is mandatory per IS 1343:2012 in end blocks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- The **end block** length = larger lateral dimension of the member (D).
- The **transmission length** for strands is **60 d_b**; for wires is **100 d_b** (IS 1343).
- Bearing pressure must not exceed **0.48 f_ck**; always check first.
- Bursting force (single anchor, concentric) = **0.32 P** (IS 1343:2012, Cl. 21.6.2.1).
- Spalling force (single anchor) = **0.04 P**.
- Closely spaced anchorages (**s ≤ a/4**): **P_b = 0.32 P (1 + 1.5 s/a)**.
- Widely spaced anchorages (**s > 4a**): **P_b = 0.32 P (4a/s)**.
- Bursting zone centroid lies at **y_p = 0.5 y₀** from anchor face.
- Bursting reinforcement: **A_s = P_b / (0.87 f_y)**.
- Spalling reinforcement: **A_s,sp = P_s / (0.87 f_y)**.
- Minimum cover in end block = **50 mm**.
- Eccentric anchorages need **Magnel's** or **Guyon's** method.
- Always provide **closed links** (not open stirrups) in the bursting zone.
- **Freyssinet** for wires; **BBRV** for wires/bars; **VSL** for strands; **Dywidag** for threaded bars.
- Failure modes to remember: **Bursting split, spalling peel, edge spalling, bearing crushing**.
- The end block is mandatory in *every* PSC member — IS 1343 makes it a code requirement, not an option.
- For numericals: always write the **seven design steps in order** (data → bearing check → zone → P_b → A_s → P_s → A_s,sp → detailing).
- High-yield point: **P_b = 0.32 P** is a "universal" coefficient; it does *not* depend on f_ck or f_y.
- High-yield point: the bursting zone *side* (2y₀) is governed by the **larger lateral dimension** D in IS 1343 simplified method.
- KTU-favourite phrasing: *"design the end block"*, *"compute the bursting reinforcement"*, *"check the bearing pressure"*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Design and Detailing of Prestressed Deck and Prestressed Beam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Design and Detailing of Prestressed Deck and Prestressed Beam

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Prestressed Concrete Deck Slab

A **prestressed concrete deck** is a thin, horizontally spanning structural slab element in which high-tensile steel tendons (strands or wires) are tensioned either *pre-tensioned* (before casting) or *post-tensioned* (after hardening) to induce a permanent state of pre-compression. This pre-compression counteracts the tensile stresses generated by external loads (dead load, live load, wheel loads, environmental loads), allowing the deck to behave primarily in compression under service conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A prestressed deck is a deck slab in which the concrete is pre-compressed longitudinally and/or transversely by tendons, such that the resultant stress at any fibre under working load remains within permissible limits defined by **IS 1343:2012** and **IRC:112-2011**.

### 1.2 Prestressed Concrete Beam

A **prestressed concrete beam** is a flexural member (typically I, T, U, or box section) in which longitudinal prestressing tendons, draped or straight along the span, generate an internal moment opposite in sign to the external bending moment. The precompressive force **P** acting at an eccentricity **e** produces a counter-moment **P·e**, which resists gravity loads and significantly improves shear capacity, deflection control, and crack resistance.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU Module 4 focus, beams refer to **post-tensioned bridge girders** (simply supported or continuous) and **pre-tensioned standard I/T-beams** used in building floors and bridges.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stack of Books on a Stool"

Imagine a heavy stack of textbooks balanced on a small stool. The stool legs want to *buckle outward* due to the eccentric load. Now imagine someone ties a strong rubber band tightly around the entire stack of books *before* placing them on the stool. The rubber band squeezes the books together (pre-compression). When the load is applied, the books cannot separate, and the stool legs experience less lateral thrust. This is exactly what prestressing does:
- **The books** = concrete (weak in tension)
- **The rubber band** = high-tensile prestressing tendon
- **The stool** = supports / formwork
- **The applied load** = dead + live load

Without the rubber band (ordinary RCC), the books would slip and fall apart. With it, the stack behaves as a single, much stronger unit.

## 1.4 Why Prestress Decks and Beams? — Engineering Motivation

| Performance Parameter | RCC Deck/Beam | Prestressed Deck/Beam |
|---|---|---|
| **Depth-to-span ratio** | 1/10 to 1/15 | 1/18 to 1/28 |
| **Crack behaviour** | Cracks under service load | Crack-free under service load |
| **Material consumption** | Higher (concrete + steel) | Lower (high-strength concrete + strands) |
| **Durability** | Moderate | High (no cracks → no corrosion ingress) |
| **Span capability** | Limited (~15 m) | Long spans (30–250 m) |
| **Deflection** | Significant | Very small (cambers up) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants used in KTU 2024 Module 4:**
> - Density of **prestressed concrete**: **24 kN/m³** (IS 875)
> - Characteristic strength of concrete (deck): **M40 to M60**
> - Characteristic strength of concrete (beam): **M50 to M70**
> - Strand grade: **Fe 1860** (ultimate strength 1860 MPa)
> - Permissible tensile stress in concrete (Class 1): **0** (no tension)
> - Permissible tensile stress in concrete (Class 2): **3 MPa** or as per IS 1343 Table 5

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Conceptual Design Philosophy

The conceptual design of a prestressed deck/beam is a **decision-making phase** that occurs *before* detailed numerical analysis. It establishes the geometry, materials, tendon layout philosophy, and construction methodology. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to justify each design choice based on:

1. **Functional requirements** → span, loading, clearance, aesthetics
2. **Structural efficiency** → depth/span ratio, section type
3. **Constructability** → pre-tensioning vs. post-tensioning, casting yard access
4. **Economy** → material cost, labour cost, time
5. **Durability** → exposure condition, cover, fire resistance

### 2.2 Conceptual Design of Prestressed Deck Slab

**Step 1 — Decide the deck system type:**
- **Solid slab deck** → spans up to 12 m
- **Ribbed/voided slab deck** → spans 10–18 m
- **Cellular deck** → spans 15–25 m (rare in road bridges)

**Step 2 — Decide direction of prestressing:**
- **One-way prestressing** → for slabs spanning parallel to traffic direction
- **Two-way (cross) prestressing** → for wide decks, skew bridges, orthotropic decks

**Step 3 — Select prestressing method:**
- **Pre-tensioning** → factory-produced, used for standard plank/slab units
- **Post-tensioning** → on-site, ducts embedded, used for cast-in-situ decks

**Step 4 — Initial sizing (empirical rules):**
- Depth of solid deck slab: $h = \dfrac{\text{span}}{20}$ to $\dfrac{\text{span}}{30}$
- For a typical 10 m span, $h = 400$ to $500$ mm
- Cover to tendon duct: minimum **75 mm** (IS 1343 Cl. 12.3.1)

**Step 5 — Tendon profile:**
- **Straight tendons** in simply supported slabs (most economical for thin decks)
- **Draped/harped tendons** when eccentricity must vary along span
- **Concentric tendons** at supports (to eliminate negative moment effects at end blocks)

### 2.3 Conceptual Design of Prestressed Beam

**Step 1 — Choose cross-section type:**

| Section Type | Best Span Range | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Rectangular** | up to 10 m | Building beams, short spans |
| **I-section (pre-tensioned)** | 10–30 m | Standard bridge girders, parking decks |
| **T-section (post-tensioned)** | 15–40 m | Building floor beams |
| **U-section / Box girder** | 30–250 m | Long-span bridges, flyovers |

**Step 2 — Establish preliminary dimensions:**

For a simply supported I-beam, the **preliminary depth** is given by the empirical formula:

$$h = \left(\frac{1}{15} \text{ to } \frac{1}{20}\right) \times L$$

where **L** is the effective span. The web thickness $b_w$ is typically $\dfrac{h}{4}$ to $\dfrac{h}{3}$, and the bottom flange width is $\dfrac{L}{50}$ to $\dfrac{L}{30}$.

**Step 3 — Tendon layout philosophy:**
- **Draped profile** (parabolic or polygonal) for simply supported beams → maximum eccentricity at mid-span, zero eccentricity at supports
- **Straight tendons** for pre-tensioned standard I-beams
- **Curved/segmental** for continuous post-tensioned beams

**Step 4 — Magnel diagram concept:**
The Magnel diagram graphically represents the safe zone of prestress force **P** and eccentricity **e** combinations. For a rectangular section of area **A**, moment of resistance **Z**, and design moment **M**, the safe zone is bounded by:

$$P \geq \frac{M}{\left(\dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{e}{Z_t}\right)^{-1} + e}$$

and 

$$P \leq \frac{f_{c,\text{allow}}}{\dfrac{1}{A} - \dfrac{e}{Z_b}}$$

## 2.4 Detailing Rules — The KTU High-Yield Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following detailing rules are **directly testable** in KTU 2024 Module 4 ESE. Memorize them as a checklist.

| Detailing Element | Rule / Value | Code Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum cover to tendon duct (post-tensioned)** | **75 mm** | IS 1343 Cl. 12.3.1 |
| **Minimum cover to pre-tensioned strand** | **40 mm** (slab) / **50 mm** (beam) | IS 1343 Cl. 12.3.1 |
| **Minimum clear spacing between ducts** | **40 mm** or **duct diameter**, whichever is greater | IS 1343 Cl. 12.4 |
| **Minimum clear spacing between strands** | **25 mm** or **1.33 × strand dia** | IS 1343 Cl. 12.4 |
| **Minimum non-prestressed reinforcement (longitudinal)** | **0.2%** of gross cross-section (in each direction) | IS 1343 Cl. 12.5.1 |
| **Minimum shear reinforcement** | $\rho_v = 0.2\% \text{ to } 0.4\%$ | IS 1343 Cl. 12.5.2 |
| **Spacer / bursting reinforcement in anchorage zone** | Required behind anchor; minimum **2%** of prestressing force as bursting steel | IS 1343 Cl. 13.3 |
| **Spalling reinforcement** | Required at ends of beams | IS 1343 Cl. 13.4 |
| **Anchorage zone length** | Approx. equal to **depth of section (h)** | IS 1343 Cl. 13 |
| **Minimum number of strands per duct** | **4 strands** (multi-strand system) | IS 1343 |
| **Max number of strands per duct** | **19 strands** (12T13, 19T13, 27T13 systems) | Freyssinet / VSL systems |
| **Maximum duct size** | **0.4 × web width** | IS 1343 Cl. 12.3.2 |
| **Grout strength (post-tensioned ducts)** | **M30 minimum**, w/c ≤ 0.45 | IS 1343 Cl. 14 |

## 2.5 Anchorage Zone Detailing (End Block)

The anchorage zone is the region behind the anchorage where the concentrated tendon force spreads into the section. It is divided into two sub-zones:

- **Bursting zone**: tensile stresses act transverse to the tendon → requires **bursting reinforcement** (helical spirals or closed stirrups).
- **Spalling zone**: tensile stresses act parallel to the loaded face → requires **spalling reinforcement** (surface mesh).

The bursting force $F_{bst}$ is estimated using the KTU/IIT formula:

$$F_{bst} = 0.32 P_k \left(\frac{d_{duct}}{h_{end}}\right) - 0.003 P_k$$

For practical design, take $F_{bst} \approx 0.04 P_k$ to $0.06 P_k$ per anchorage.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The conceptual design and detailing principles of prestressed decks and beams form the backbone of:
- **Highway bridges** (NHAI, MoRTH projects in India)
- **Metro rail viaducts** (Kochi Metro, Delhi Metro — box girders)
- **Long-span building floors** (airport terminals, convention centres)
- **Nuclear containment structures**
- **Offshore platforms and LNG tanks**

> [!NOTE]
> In Kerala, the **Kochi Metro Rail Limited (KMRL)** viaduct uses **post-tensioned box girders** spanning 25–35 m — directly applying Module 4 concepts.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Magnel Diagram for a Rectangular Beam Section

The Magnel diagram plots the safe zone of (e, P) combinations for a given cross-section under a design moment **M**. The conditions are:

**Condition 1 — Stress at top fibre ≤ allowable tensile stress $f_{ct}$ (Class 2) or 0 (Class 1):**

$$\frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_t} - \frac{M}{Z_t} \geq -f_{ct}$$

(assuming compression positive). Rearranging:

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A} - \frac{e}{Z_t} \right) \leq f_{ct} + \frac{M}{Z_t}$$

$$P \leq \frac{f_{ct} + \dfrac{M}{Z_t}}{\dfrac{1}{A} - \dfrac{e}{Z_t}}$$

**Condition 2 — Stress at bottom fibre ≤ allowable compressive stress $f_{cc}$:**

$$\frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_b} - \frac{M}{Z_b} \leq f_{cc}$$

Rearranging:

$$P \leq \frac{f_{cc} + \dfrac{M}{Z_b}}{\dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{e}{Z_b}}$$

**Condition 3 — Stress at top fibre ≥ 0 (no tension, Class 1):**

$$P \geq \frac{\dfrac{M}{Z_t}}{\dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{e}{Z_t}}$$

These three linear equations in (P, e) form a triangular safe zone on the Magnel diagram.

## 3.2 Derivation: Preliminary Prestress Force for an I-Beam

**Given:** Simply supported I-beam, span $L = 30$ m, design moment $M = 4500$ kN·m (DL + LL), section properties $A = 0.45$ m², $Z_t = Z_b = 0.18$ m³, permissible stresses $f_{ct} = 0$ (Class 1), $f_{cc} = 17$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Required prestress force at mid-span (bottom fibre in compression):**

At mid-span, the bottom fibre is most stressed in tension. To keep $\sigma_{bottom} = 0$:

$$\frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_b} = \frac{M}{Z_b}$$

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z_b} \right) = \frac{M}{Z_b}$$

Assuming maximum permissible eccentricity $e = 0.40$ m (limited by cover and duct size):

$$P = \frac{\dfrac{M}{Z_b}}{\dfrac{1}{A} + \dfrac{e}{Z_b}}$$

$$P = \frac{\dfrac{4500 \times 10^{6}}{0.18 \times 10^{9}}}{\dfrac{1}{0.45 \times 10^{6}} + \dfrac{0.40}{0.18 \times 10^{9}}}$$

Let's compute step by step:

$$\frac{M}{Z_b} = \frac{4500 \times 10^{6}}{0.18 \times 10^{9}} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{0.45 \times 10^{6}} = 2.222 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per mm}^2$$

$$\frac{e}{Z_b} = \frac{0.40 \times 10^{3}}{0.18 \times 10^{9}} = 2.222 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per mm}^2$$

$$\frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z_b} = 4.444 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per mm}^2$$

$$P = \frac{25}{4.444 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.625 \times 10^{6} \text{ N} = 5625 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Number of strands required:**

Using 12.7 mm dia strands of 1860 MPa grade, each strand area $A_s = 98.7$ mm². Allowing 70% jacking stress (effective):

$$P_{\text{per strand}} = 0.7 \times 1860 \times 98.7 = 128{,}489 \text{ N} \approx 128.5 \text{ kN}$$

$$\text{Number of strands} = \frac{5625}{128.5} = 43.8 \approx 44 \text{ strands}$$

**Step 3 — Tendon grouping:**

Use **3 ducts × 15 strands** = 45 strands (1 spare, practical arrangement).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is the **core computational workflow** for KTU Module 4 design questions. Practice computing $A$, $Z_t$, $Z_b$ for I-sections and box girders.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bursting Reinforcement in Anchorage Zone

**Given:** End block of a post-tensioned beam, $P_k = 3000$ kN (jacking force), duct diameter $d_{duct} = 100$ mm, end block depth $h_{end} = 1200$ mm.

**Step 1 — Bursting tensile force:**

$$F_{bst} = 0.32 P_k \cdot \frac{d_{duct}}{h_{end}} - 0.003 P_k$$

$$F_{bst} = 0.32 \times 3000 \times \frac{100}{1200} - 0.003 \times 3000$$

$$F_{bst} = 80 - 9 = 71 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Bursting reinforcement area (Fe 415, allowable $f_s = 210$ MPa):**

$$A_{bst} = \frac{F_{bst}}{f_s} = \frac{71 \times 10^{3}}{210} = 338 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Practical detailing:**

Provide **4 closed stirrups of 10 mm dia** (4 legs × 2 × 78.5 = 628 mm²) — comfortably more than required.

**Step 4 — Spalling reinforcement (surface mesh):**

Provide **10 mm dia bars @ 150 mm c/c** forming a U-shape on the loaded face, length equal to $h_{end}/4 = 300$ mm.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU Exam-Oriented Design Workflow

| Stage | Activity | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Conceptual** | Choose section type, span/depth, prestressing method | Preliminary dimensions |
| **2. Loading** | Calculate DL, LL (IRC Class A / 70R), impact factor | $M$, $V$ envelopes |
| **3. Material selection** | $f_{ck} = 40$–$60$ MPa, $f_p = 1860$ MPa | $f_{ct}$, $f_{cc}$ from IS 1343 |
| **4. Prestress force** | Magnel diagram or $P = M/Z_b$ at mid-span | $P_{\min}$, $P_{\max}$ |
| **5. Tendon selection** | Strand dia, number per duct, duct layout | Tendon profile, eccentricities |
| **6. Stress checks** | Top & bottom fibre at transfer & service | Compliance with IS 1343 |
| **7. Detailing** | Cover, spacing, anchorage zone, bursting steel | Final drawings |
| **8. Drawings** | Tendon profile, reinforcement layout, BBS | Construction documents |
