# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Prestressed Deck. List the four main design considerations.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A **prestressed deck** is a structural slab element in which high-tensile tendons are tensioned to induce pre-compression, counteracting tensile stresses due to external loads.

**Four main design considerations:**
1. **Span and loading type** → dictates solid, ribbed, or cellular form
2. **Direction of prestressing** → one-way or two-way (transverse + longitudinal)
3. **Pre-tensioning vs. post-tensioning** → affects duct detailing and anchorage
4. **Durability requirements** → cover, exposure condition, crack control

### Question 2: Why is minimum non-prestressed reinforcement provided in a prestressed member?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**
Minimum non-prestressed reinforcement (typically **0.2%** of gross sectional area, IS 1343 Cl. 12.5.1) is provided to:
- **Control cracking** due to shrinkage, temperature, and accidental overload
- **Provide ductility** and prevent sudden brittle failure
- **Resist tensile stresses** that may arise at transfer (eccentricities near supports)
- **Distribute localized stresses** and act as binding reinforcement during construction

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B Module 4 follows the standard **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks** sub-part structure. Students must answer ONE of the two questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain, with a neat sketch, the **conceptual design procedure** of a simply supported post-tensioned prestressed concrete bridge deck spanning 25 m. Discuss the choice of cross-section, tendon profile, and cover requirements. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam has a rectangular section 300 mm × 600 mm, simply supported over 8 m. The beam carries a service load (excluding self-weight) of 18 kN/m. The prestress force at mid-span is **P = 1200 kN** at an eccentricity **e = 150 mm**. Concrete grade M40 ($f_{ck} = 40$ MPa). Check the stresses at mid-span at the **bottom and top fibres** under service condition. Take permissible stresses as per IS 1343 (Class 1, no tension). **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Section properties:**

$$A = 300 \times 600 = 180{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 = 0.18 \text{ m}^2$$

$$Z_t = Z_b = \frac{300 \times 600^2}{6} = 18 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^3 = 0.018 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Bending moment at mid-span:**

Self-weight: $w_{sw} = 0.30 \times 0.60 \times 24 = 4.32$ kN/m
Superimposed load: $w_{LL} = 18$ kN/m
Total: $w = 22.32$ kN/m

$$M = \frac{w L^2}{8} = \frac{22.32 \times 8^2}{8} = 178.56 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Step 3 — Stress at bottom fibre (tension side):**

$$\sigma_{bot} = \frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_b} - \frac{M}{Z_b}$$

$$\sigma_{bot} = \frac{1200 \times 10^3}{0.18 \times 10^6} + \frac{1200 \times 10^3 \times 150}{0.018 \times 10^9} - \frac{178.56 \times 10^6}{0.018 \times 10^9}$$

$$\sigma_{bot} = 6.667 + 10.0 - 9.92 = 6.747 \text{ MPa (compression)} \;\; \textbf{SAFE}$$

**Step 4 — Stress at top fibre (compression side):**

$$\sigma_{top} = \frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_t} - \frac{M}{Z_t}$$

$$\sigma_{top} = 6.667 - 10.0 - 9.92 = -13.253 \text{ MPa (tension)} \;\; \textbf{NOT SAFE}$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**

The top fibre shows **13.25 MPa tension**, violating the Class 1 condition ($f_{ct} = 0$). Either:
- Reduce eccentricity at mid-span
- Add prestress in the top fibre (cable in the upper zone)
- Use a different tendon profile (eccentricity reduces near mid-span top fibre consideration)

This is a typical KTU pitfall — students must check **both fibres**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a labelled diagram, explain the **detailing of the anchorage zone** of a post-tensioned prestressed beam. Discuss bursting and spalling reinforcement. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A simply supported pre-tensioned prestressed concrete I-beam has the following data: Span = 18 m, $f_{ck} = 50$ MPa, $f_p = 1860$ MPa, $A = 0.32$ m², $Z_t = 0.085$ m³, $Z_b = 0.115$ m³. The design moment at mid-span (including self-weight) is **M = 2400 kN·m**. Determine the minimum prestress force required using the **Magnel diagram concept** for a Class 1 member (no tension allowed). Assume maximum permissible eccentricity = 0.35 m. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Permissible stresses (IS 1343, Class 1, M50):**

- $f_{ct} = 0$ MPa (no tension)
- $f_{cc} = 0.33 f_{ck} = 16.5$ MPa (compression at transfer may differ; use service here)

**Step 2 — Condition at bottom fibre (no tension):**

$$\frac{P}{A} + \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_b} \geq \frac{M}{Z_b}$$

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A} + \frac{e}{Z_b} \right) \geq \frac{M}{Z_b}$$

$$P \geq \frac{\dfrac{2400 \times 10^6}{0.115 \times 10^9}}{\dfrac{1}{0.32 \times 10^6} + \dfrac{0.35 \times 10^3}{0.115 \times 10^9}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator:**

$$\frac{M}{Z_b} = \frac{2400 \times 10^6}{0.115 \times 10^9} = 20.87 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute denominator:**

$$\frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{0.32 \times 10^6} = 3.125 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per mm}^2$$

$$\frac{e}{Z_b} = \frac{0.35 \times 10^3}{0.115 \times 10^9} = 3.043 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per mm}^2$$

$$\text{Sum} = 6.168 \times 10^{-6} \text{ per mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Minimum P:**

$$P_{\min} = \frac{20.87}{6.168 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.384 \times 10^6 \text{ N} = 3384 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6 — Verify with top fibre compression limit (16.5 MPa):**

At top fibre, $M$ causes compression, while $P \cdot e$ causes tension:

$$\frac{P}{A} - \frac{P \cdot e}{Z_t} \leq f_{cc} + \frac{M}{Z_t}$$

$$\frac{M}{Z_t} = \frac{2400 \times 10^6}{0.085 \times 10^9} = 28.24 \text{ MPa}$$

$$P \left( \frac{1}{A} - \frac{e}{Z_t} \right) \leq 16.5 + 28.24 = 44.74 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\frac{e}{Z_t} = \frac{0.35 \times 10^3}{0.085 \times 10^9} = 4.118 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\frac{1}{A} - \frac{e}{Z_t} = 3.125 \times 10^{-6} - 4.118 \times 10^{-6} = -0.993 \times 10^{-6}$$

Since this is negative, the LHS is negative for positive P, which is automatically ≤ 44.74 MPa. **Top fibre is safe.**

**Step 7 — Final answer:** $P_{\min} = 3384$ kN, providing **0.85 × $f_p$ × $A_s$** prestressing, giving total strand area required = 2400 mm² (use 24 strands of 12.7 mm dia, 98.7 mm² each → 2369 mm² ≈ 24 strands).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Module 4:**
> 1. **Forgetting to check BOTH top and bottom fibres** at mid-span and supports → 3-mark penalty
> 2. **Confusing pre-tensioning cover (40–50 mm) with post-tensioning duct cover (75 mm)** → 1-mark error
> 3. **Using wrong $Z_t$ vs $Z_b$** in asymmetric sections (I-beams) — must distinguish top and bottom section moduli
> 4. **Omitting the bursting reinforcement in anchorage zone** while describing end-block detailing
> 5. **Not justifying the choice of pre-tensioning vs post-tensioning** in conceptual design — examiners expect a reasoned argument
> 6. **Drawing tendon profile at supports with zero eccentricity** without mentioning the resulting **negative moment** consideration
> 7. **Using wrong allowable stress values** — must quote IS 1343 Table 5 explicitly in the answer

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 (KTU 2024):**
> - **Prestressed deck** = thin slab pre-compressed by tendons; used in bridge/building floors
> - **Prestressed beam** = flexural member where $P \cdot e$ counter-moment resists $M$
> - **Empirical depth rule**: $h = L/15$ to $L/20$ for beams, $L/20$ to $L/30$ for decks
> - **Tendon profile**: draped/parabolic (PT) or straight (pre-tensioned standard beams)
> - **Magnel diagram** = safe zone of $(P, e)$ combinations satisfying stress limits
> - **Min cover to duct (PT)** = **75 mm**; **min cover to strand (pretensioned)** = **40–50 mm**
> - **Min non-prestressed steel** = **0.2%** of gross cross-section (longitudinal, each direction)
> - **Anchorage zone** requires **bursting + spalling reinforcement**
> - **Class 1 member** = no tension allowed ($f_{ct} = 0$)
> - **Class 2 member** = limited tension allowed ($f_{ct} \leq 3$ MPa or as per IS 1343)
> - **Strand grade** = **Fe 1860**; effective prestress ≈ **0.8 × $f_p$** after losses
> - **Bursting force** ≈ $0.04 P_k$ to $0.06 P_k$ per anchorage
> - **Min spacing between ducts** = **40 mm** or duct dia, whichever is greater
> - **Grout strength** = M30 minimum (w/c ≤ 0.45)
> - Always verify stresses at **transfer** (initial) AND **service** (final) stages
> - Sketch the **Magnel diagram** for the chosen section during the exam
> - For KTU Part B answers, always include a **neat labelled diagram** of the tendon profile and end-block detailing

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cast in Situ Slab Composite Sections
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cast in Situ Slab Composite Sections

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Cast-in-Situ Slab Composite Section** in Prestressed Concrete (PSC) is a structural cross-section formed by combining a **precast prestressed concrete girder (or beam)** with a **reinforced concrete (or lightly prestressed) slab** cast monolithically *in situ* over the precast member, such that the two components act as a single, integrated load-carrying unit through shear transfer at the interface (achieved by roughening, shear keys, or stirrups projecting from the precast web).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET525 – Module 4):**
> *Composite construction in prestressed concrete refers to structures in which a precast prestressed concrete element is combined with a cast-in-situ concrete component, with the two parts acting together to resist loads after the cast-in-situ concrete has hardened. Differential shrinkage and differential creep between the two components induce additional stresses that must be accounted for in design.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Layered Sandwich Beam"

Imagine a **two-layer sandwich**:
- The **bottom slice of bread** = the precast PSC girder (already strong, already "set" — has its own hardened state and existing prestress).
- The **top layer of cheese/tomato** = the freshly poured cast-in-situ slab (still settling, will shrink as it dries).
- The **mayonnaise bond** = the shear key / roughened interface / projecting stirrups that glues them together forever.

Once dried, when you try to bend this sandwich, both layers bend together — but the **top layer wanted to shrink more than the bottom layer** while curing. This mismatch of "wanting to be smaller" but being held in place by the bond generates **internal stresses** at the interface, even *before* any external load is applied. This is the central engineering challenge of composite PSC design.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Precast Unit** | PSC girder (or beam) manufactured and cured in a controlled casting yard, often pretensioned. |
| **Cast-in-Situ Slab** | RCC slab cast over the precast girder *at site*, after the girder is erected and supported. |
| **Interface** | The horizontal plane where the two concretes meet; designed for full shear transfer. |
| **Differential Shrinkage** | Difference in shrinkage strain between the cast-in-situ slab (shrinks more) and precast girder (already shrunk). |
| **Differential Creep** | Difference in long-term creep deformation between the two concretes of different ages/strengths. |
| **Transformed Section** | Equivalent homogeneous section obtained by multiplying one concrete's width by the modular ratio $m = E_{c2}/E_{c1}$. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?**
> The 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the **additional stresses** generated by differential shrinkage and creep in composite PSC members. A design that ignores these effects will **underestimate tensile stresses at the soffit** of the precast beam and **overestimate stresses in the slab**, leading to unsafe designs in the field.

## 1.4 Classification of Composite PSC Construction

1. **Slab-on-Girder Composite** (most common in bridges & high-rise floor systems)
2. **Precast Stem with Cast-in-Situ Top and Bottom Flanges** (box girders)
3. **Segmental Cantilever Construction** (used in long-span balanced cantilever bridges)
4. **Inverted-T Beams with Cast-in-Situ Deck** (typical of highway bridge superstructures)

> [!TIP]
> In KTU problems, **slab-on-girder (or slab-on-pretensioned beam)** is the most frequently examined configuration.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Loading in a Composite PSC Member

| Stage | Description | What happens |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage I** | Precast girder alone carries its self-weight + initial prestress | Shrinkage of precast already occurred; only its own DL acts. |
| **Stage II** | Cast-in-situ slab is cast on the precast girder (girder is shored or unshored) | Differential shrinkage and creep start developing. Self-weight of wet concrete acts on composite (or non-composite) section depending on shoring. |
| **Stage III** | Composite section is fully effective; live loads + superimposed dead loads (SIDL) applied | Full composite action; entire section resists external loads. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The differential shrinkage and creep occur ONLY after Stage II** — that is, after the cast-in-situ concrete is placed. The precast has already lost most of its shrinkage potential.

## 2.2 Sources of Additional Stresses in Composite Sections

1. **Differential Shrinkage ($\Delta\epsilon_{sh}$)** — Cast-in-situ shrinks more than precast.
2. **Differential Creep ($\Delta\epsilon_{cr}$)** — Cast-in-situ creeps more under sustained loads.
3. **Self-Weight of Slab** acting on the precast girder *before* hardening (if unshored).
4. **Temperature Gradient** between the two layers (often neglected in basic KTU problems).

## 2.3 Strain Compatibility and Force Equilibrium

### 2.3.1 Free Differential Shrinkage Strain
$$\epsilon_{sh,diff} = \epsilon_{sh,2} - \epsilon_{sh,1}$$

Where:
- $\epsilon_{sh,2}$ = shrinkage strain of cast-in-situ concrete (typically $200 \times 10^{-6}$ to $400 \times 10^{-6}$ for KTU standard exposure)
- $\epsilon_{sh,1}$ = remaining shrinkage strain of precast concrete (very small, often taken as 0 after long curing)

### 2.3.2 Restraining Force
If the slab wants to shrink but is held by the bond, an equivalent **compressive force $P_{sh}$** develops in the slab and a balancing **tensile force $P_{sh}$** in the precast girder. The slab is in tension and the precast beam is in compression at the interface.

### 2.3.3 Self-Equilibrating System
The restraining force acts at the **centroid of the cast-in-situ slab** (say, at distance $y_2$ from the composite neutral axis). It produces:
- An **axial force** $P_{sh}$ at the slab centroid.
- A **moment** $M_{sh} = P_{sh} \cdot e_{sh}$, where $e_{sh}$ = eccentricity of slab centroid from the composite NA.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\epsilon_{sh,diff} = \epsilon_{sh,2} - \epsilon_{sh,1}$ | Differential shrinkage strain (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $P_{sh} = \epsilon_{sh,diff} \cdot E_{c2} \cdot A_{c2}$ | Equivalent shrinkage force in cast-in-situ slab (kN) |
| 3 | $M_{sh} = P_{sh} \cdot e_{sh}$ | Self-equilibrating moment due to restrained shrinkage (kN·m) |
| 4 | $m = E_{c2}/E_{c1}$ | Modular ratio between cast-in-situ and precast concretes |
| 5 | $A_t = A_1 + m A_2$ | Area of transformed section (transforming cast-in-situ into equivalent precast) |
| 6 | $y_t = \dfrac{A_1 \bar{y}_1 + m A_2 \bar{y}_2}{A_t}$ | Centroid of transformed section from a reference axis |
| 7 | $I_t = I_1 + A_1 (\bar{y}_1 - y_t)^2 + m [I_2 + A_2 (\bar{y}_2 - y_t)^2]$ | Moment of inertia of transformed section |
| 8 | $\sigma_{c1}^{top} = \dfrac{P_1}{A_1} + \dfrac{P_1 e_1 y_{1,top}}{I_1} + \dfrac{M_{sh} y_{1,top}}{I_t}$ | Stress in precast (top fibre) — composite stage |
| 9 | $\sigma_{c1}^{bot} = \dfrac{P_1}{A_1} - \dfrac{P_1 e_1 y_{1,bot}}{I_1} - \dfrac{M_{sh} y_{1,bot}}{I_t}$ | Stress in precast (bottom fibre) |
| 10 | $\sigma_{c2} = m \left( \dfrac{P_{sh}}{m A_2} - \dfrac{M_{sh} y_2}{I_t} \right) = \dfrac{P_{sh}}{A_2} - \dfrac{m M_{sh} y_2}{I_t}$ | Stress in cast-in-situ slab |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU (and IS 1343):** Compression is positive (+), Tension is negative (−). Eccentricity $e$ below the centroid is positive.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Highway Bridges (PSC Girder + RCC Deck)**: The standard in IRC:112 and IS:1343 composite bridges. Differential shrinkage can reduce prestress by 5–10%.
- **Building Floor Systems**: Pretensioned ribs with in-situ topping — common in parking decks, industrial floors.
- **Railway Sleepers with In-Situ Ballast Walls** (modified composite).
- **Roof Shells and Folded Plates** with cast-in-situ ribs.

> [!TIP]
> **IS 1343:1980 (and revised drafts)** specifies that the differential shrinkage strain should be taken as **$200 \times 10^{-6}$ for normal conditions** and **$300 \times 10^{-6}$ for dry conditions**, unless more accurate data is available. This is the value KTU expects in exam problems unless stated otherwise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stress Due to Differential Shrinkage in a Composite Beam

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a T-beam composite section:
- **Part 1 (precast)**: Rectangular girder of width $b_1$, depth $d_1$, area $A_1$, moment of inertia $I_1$, modulus $E_{c1}$.
- **Part 2 (cast-in-situ)**: Slab of width $b_2$, thickness $t_s$, area $A_2$, modulus $E_{c2}$, located above the precast beam.
- **Modular ratio**: $m = E_{c2}/E_{c1}$.

The free differential shrinkage strain in the slab relative to the precast is $\epsilon_{sh}$.

### 3.1.2 Equilibrium and Compatibility

**Step 1 — Free shrinkage tendency:**
The slab wants to contract by $\epsilon_{sh}$ relative to the precast. If unrestrained, no stress.

**Step 2 — Bond restrains shrinkage:**
Because the bond prevents relative movement, the system is equivalent to applying an **axial force $P_{sh}$** to the slab such that:

$$\text{Strain in slab due to } P_{sh} + \text{Strain in slab due to } M_{sh} + \text{Free shrinkage} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Force equilibrium (axial):**
The horizontal force in the slab equals the horizontal force in the precast (at the interface), and they form a couple:

$$P_{sh} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh}^{restrained} = E_{c1} \cdot A_1 \cdot \epsilon_{sh}^{induced}$$

**Step 4 — Using the transformed section method:**
Transform the slab area by multiplying by $m$ (so the section is treated as a single material $E_{c1}$). The effective shrinkage force in the transformed system:

$$P_{sh}^{eff} = m \cdot E_{c1} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh}$$

**Step 5 — Magnitude of shrinkage force:**

$$P_{sh} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh}$$

**Step 6 — Self-equilibrating moment:**
The force $P_{sh}$ acts at the centroid of the slab, which is at eccentricity $e_{sh}$ from the neutral axis of the transformed composite section:

$$M_{sh} = P_{sh} \cdot e_{sh}$$

### 3.1.3 Final Stress Equations

**Stress in precast girder (at distance $y$ from transformed NA):**

$$\sigma_{c1}(y) = \frac{P_1}{A_1} + \frac{P_1 e_1 y}{I_1} - \frac{M_{sh} \cdot y}{I_t}$$

(Here, $P_1$ and $e_1$ refer to the prestressing force and its eccentricity in the precast girder alone; the third term is from the shrinkage moment on the composite transformed section.)

**Stress in cast-in-situ slab:**

$$\sigma_{c2}(y) = \frac{P_{sh}}{A_2} - \frac{m \cdot M_{sh} \cdot y}{I_t}$$

(Where $y$ is measured from the composite transformed NA. The first term is the direct tensile stress from the restrained shrinkage; the second is the bending contribution.)

## 3.2 Complete Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

### 3.2.1 Given Data
- **Precast girder** (rectangular, simply supported): $b_1 = 200$ mm, $d_1 = 600$ mm.
- **Cast-in-situ slab**: $b_2 = 1000$ mm, $t_s = 150$ mm.
- **Span**: $L = 12$ m.
- **Concrete grades**: $E_{c1} = 32$ kN/mm² (M40 precast), $E_{c2} = 25$ kN/mm² (M25 cast-in-situ).
- **Effective prestress after losses**: $P_1 = 1000$ kN at eccentricity $e_1 = 150$ mm below NA of precast girder.
- **Differential shrinkage strain**: $\epsilon_{sh} = 200 \times 10^{-6}$.
- **Self-weight of slab + SIDL + Live Load at midspan** (all to be applied appropriately).

### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Modular Ratio

$$m = \frac{E_{c2}}{E_{c1}} = \frac{25}{32} = 0.78125$$

### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Areas and Individual Properties

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= b_1 \times d_1 = 200 \times 600 = 120{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_2 &= b_2 \times t_s = 1000 \times 150 = 150{,}000 \text{ mm}^2 \\
I_1 &= \frac{b_1 d_1^3}{12} = \frac{200 \times 600^3}{12} = 3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \\
I_2 &= \frac{b_2 t_s^3}{12} = \frac{1000 \times 150^3}{12} = 2.8125 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Locate Centroid of Transformed Section

Taking the **bottom of the precast girder** as the reference axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{y}_1 &= \frac{d_1}{2} = 300 \text{ mm} \\
\bar{y}_2 &= d_1 + \frac{t_s}{2} = 600 + 75 = 675 \text{ mm} \\
A_t &= A_1 + m A_2 = 120{,}000 + 0.78125 \times 150{,}000 = 120{,}000 + 117{,}187.5 = 237{,}187.5 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t = \frac{A_1 \bar{y}_1 + m A_2 \bar{y}_2}{A_t} = \frac{(120{,}000)(300) + (117{,}187.5)(675)}{237{,}187.5}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_t = \frac{36{,}000{,}000 + 79{,}101{,}562.5}{237{,}187.5} = \frac{115{,}101{,}562.5}{237{,}187.5} \approx 485.31 \text{ mm from bottom}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Moment of Inertia of Transformed Section

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = I_1 + A_1(\bar{y}_1 - y_t)^2 + m[I_2 + A_2(\bar{y}_2 - y_t)^2]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = 3.6 \times 10^9 + 120{,}000 \times (300 - 485.31)^2 + 0.78125 \times [2.8125 \times 10^8 + 150{,}000 \times (675 - 485.31)^2]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = 3.6 \times 10^9 + 120{,}000 \times (34{,}312.96) + 0.78125 \times [2.8125 \times 10^8 + 150{,}000 \times (35{,}879.62)]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = 3.6 \times 10^9 + 4.117 \times 10^9 + 0.78125 \times [2.8125 \times 10^8 + 5.382 \times 10^9]
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = 3.6 \times 10^9 + 4.117 \times 10^9 + 0.78125 \times 5.663 \times 10^9
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_t = 3.6 \times 10^9 + 4.117 \times 10^9 + 4.424 \times 10^9 = 12.141 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Shrinkage Force and Moment

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{sh} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh} = 25 \times 150{,}000 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 750 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

The slab centroid is at $\bar{y}_2 = 675$ mm from the bottom, while the composite NA is at $y_t = 485.31$ mm. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{sh} = \bar{y}_2 - y_t = 675 - 485.31 = 189.69 \text{ mm (above NA)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{sh} = P_{sh} \cdot e_{sh} = 750 \times 0.18969 = 142.27 \text{ kN·m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.7 Step 6 — Stress in Precast Girder Due to Shrinkage

**At bottom fibre of precast** ($y = -485.31$ mm from NA):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{c1,sh}^{bot} = -\frac{M_{sh} \cdot (-485.31)}{I_t} = +\frac{142.27 \times 10^6 \times 485.31}{12.141 \times 10^9} = +5.69 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**At top fibre of precast** ($y = 600 - 485.31 = 114.69$ mm from NA):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{c1,sh}^{top} = -\frac{M_{sh} \cdot 114.69}{I_t} = -\frac{142.27 \times 10^6 \times 114.69}{12.141 \times 10^9} = -1.34 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.8 Step 7 — Stress in Cast-in-Situ Slab

At the centroid of slab ($y = 189.69$ mm above NA):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{c2} = \frac{P_{sh}}{A_2} - \frac{m \cdot M_{sh} \cdot y}{I_t} = \frac{750 \times 10^3}{150{,}000} - \frac{0.78125 \times 142.27 \times 10^6 \times 189.69}{12.141 \times 10^9}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{c2} = 5.0 - \frac{0.78125 \times 142.27 \times 10^6 \times 189.69}{12.141 \times 10^9} = 5.0 - 1.823 = 3.18 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Result from Example:**
> - The shrinkage induces a **compressive stress of +5.69 N/mm² at the bottom of the precast beam** — this is *beneficial* (helps offset prestress losses).
> - It induces a **tensile stress of −1.34 N/mm² at the top of the precast beam** — *potentially harmful*.
> - The slab experiences a net **tensile stress of +3.18 N/mm²** at its centroid — the slab is being pulled apart by its own restrained shrinkage.

> [!WARNING]
> The tensile stress in the slab is **why mesh reinforcement is mandatory** in composite bridge decks. KTU students frequently miss this point: the slab isn't just carrying load; it's *also* fighting its own restrained shrinkage.

## 3.3 Differential Creep Stress (Brief)

For long-term sustained loads, the cast-in-situ slab creeps more than the precast. The differential creep coefficient is:

$$\Delta\phi = \phi_2 - \phi_1$$

Where $\phi_2$ and $\phi_1$ are the creep coefficients of cast-in-situ and precast respectively. The procedure is **analogous to shrinkage**: an equivalent force $P_{cr} = E_{c2} A_2 \Delta\phi \cdot \sigma_{sustained}$ is used.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark problems, usually **only differential shrinkage** is asked. Differential creep is mentioned as a separate sub-case and gets asked as a 3-mark question in Part A.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Analysis Procedure for Composite Sections

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Precast Girder Geometry b1, d1, A1, I1] --> B[Identify Cast in Situ Slab Geometry b2, ts, A2, I2]
    B --> C[Compute Modular Ratio m = Ec2 / Ec1]
    C --> D[Compute Transformed Section Area At = A1 + m A2]
    D --> E[Locate Centroid of Transformed Section yt]
    E --> F[Compute Transformed Moment of Inertia It]
    F --> G[Compute Free Differential Shrinkage Strain epsilon sh]
    G --> H[Compute Shrinkage Force Psh = Ec2 A2 epsilon sh]
    H --> I[Compute Eccentricity esh = ybar2 minus yt]
    I --> J[Compute Shrinkage Moment Msh = Psh esh]
    J --> K[Compute Stresses in Precast Girder at Top and Bottom Fibres]
    J --> L[Compute Stresses in Cast in Situ Slab]
    K --> M[Apply Prestress Stresses and External Bending Stresses Superposition]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Check Stresses against Allowable Limits per IS 1343]
    N --> O[Final Design Verification]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style G fill:#ffe1f5
    style H fill:#ffe1f5
    style I fill:#ffe1f5
    style J fill:#ffe1f5
    style K fill:#e1ffe1
    style L fill:#e1ffe1
    style M fill:#e1ffe1
    style N fill:#ffe1e1
    style O fill:#ffe1e1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Stress Distribution in Composite T-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PrecastGirder[PRECAST PSC GIRDER]
        P1[Top Fibre: Compression due to Prestress and Tension due to Shrinkage]
        P2[Bottom Fibre: Compression due to Prestress and Compression due to Shrinkage]
    end
    subgraph Interface[INTERFACE - Shear Key Zone]
        IF1[Direct Tensile Force from Restrained Shrinkage]
    end
    subgraph CastInSitu[CAST IN SITU SLAB]
        C1[Slab Centroid: Net Tensile Stress from Shrinkage]
        C2[Top of Slab: Combined Bending plus Shrinkage]
    end

    P1 --> Interface
    P2 --> Interface
    Interface --> C1
    Interface --> C2

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff
    style P2 fill:#cce5ff
    style IF1 fill:#ffcccc
    style C1 fill:#ffffcc
    style C2 fill:#ffffcc
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stages of Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Stage 1: Precast Girder Alone - Self Weight plus Initial Prestress]
    S2[Stage 2: Cast in Situ Slab Poured - Differential Shrinkage Starts]
    S3[Stage 3: Composite Action - SIDL plus Live Load]
    S4[Stage 4: Long Term - Differential Creep Effects]

    S1 -->|after 28 days| S2
    S2 -->|after 7 days curing| S3
    S3 -->|after years| S4

    style S1 fill:#d4f1f4
    style S2 fill:#fef3c7
    style S3 fill:#c8e6c9
    style S4 fill:#f8bbd0
```

## 4.4 Schematic Sketch (ASCII Representation of the Section)

```
            ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐  ← Top of slab
            │                                        │
            │     CAST-IN-SITU SLAB  (b2 wide)       │  t_s thick
            │                                        │
            ├════════════════════════════════════════┤  ← Interface (shear key)
            │   █                                   █│
            │   █                                   █│
            │   █   PRECAST PSC GIRDER (b1 wide)    █│  d_1 deep
            │   █                                   █│
            │   █                                   █│
            └────────────────────────────────────────┘  ← Bottom of precast
                ↑ Prestressing tendons located here (eccentric)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define differential shrinkage in the context of composite PSC construction. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> Differential shrinkage in a composite PSC section refers to the difference in shrinkage strains between the **cast-in-situ concrete** (which is freshly placed and undergoes nearly full shrinkage) and the **precast concrete** (which has already undergone most of its shrinkage during the curing period before the slab is cast). Mathematically, $\epsilon_{sh,diff} = \epsilon_{sh,cast} - \epsilon_{sh,precast}$. This restrained shrinkage induces internal stresses — tensile in the slab and compressive in the precast girder at the interface — even before any external loads are applied.

---

### Question 2: List any three sources of additional stresses in composite prestressed concrete members. [3 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The three sources are:

1. **Differential Shrinkage** between the cast-in-situ slab and the precast girder.
2. **Differential Creep** due to different ages and stress histories of the two concretes under sustained load.
3. **Self-Weight of the Wet Slab** acting on the precast girder alone (if unshored) before the slab hardens.

*(A fourth, sometimes listed, is the temperature gradient between the two layers.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Analyse a Composite T-Beam for Differential Shrinkage Stresses [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A pretensioned precast PSC girder of cross-section **200 mm × 600 mm** supports a cast-in-situ slab of cross-section **1000 mm × 150 mm** over a span of **14 m**. The effective prestress is **1100 kN** at an eccentricity of **175 mm** below the centroid of the precast girder. $E_{c1} = 32$ kN/mm² (precast), $E_{c2} = 25$ kN/mm² (slab). Differential shrinkage strain = $200 \times 10^{-6}$.

#### (a) Determine the magnitude of the shrinkage force and the self-equilibrating shrinkage moment. [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Geometric properties:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 = 200 \times 600 = 1.2 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2 \\
A_2 = 1000 \times 150 = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2 \\
I_1 = \frac{200 \times 600^3}{12} = 3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ mm}^4 \\
I_2 = \frac{1000 \times 150^3}{12} = 2.8125 \times 10^8 \text{ mm}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Modular ratio:**

$$
m = \frac{E_{c2}}{E_{c1}} = \frac{25}{32} = 0.78125
$$

**Step 3 — Transformed area and centroid (measured from bottom of precast):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_t = A_1 + m A_2 = 1.2 \times 10^5 + 0.78125 \times 1.5 \times 10^5 = 2.3719 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^2 \\
y_t = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^5)(300) + (0.78125 \times 1.5 \times 10^5)(675)}{2.3719 \times 10^5} \\
y_t = \frac{3.6 \times 10^7 + 7.9102 \times 10^7}{2.3719 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.151 \times 10^8}{2.3719 \times 10^5} \approx 485.3 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Shrinkage force:**

$$
P_{sh} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh} = 25 \times 1.5 \times 10^5 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} = 750 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Eccentricity of slab centroid from composite NA:**

$$
e_{sh} = 675 - 485.3 = 189.7 \text{ mm} = 0.1897 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 6 — Shrinkage moment:**

$$
M_{sh} = P_{sh} \times e_{sh} = 750 \times 0.1897 = 142.3 \text{ kN·m}
$$

#### (b) Compute the resulting stresses at the bottom of the precast girder and at the centroid of the slab. [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Moment of inertia of transformed section (computed similarly to section 3.2.5):**

$$
I_t \approx 1.214 \times 10^{10} \text{ mm}^4
$$

**Step 2 — Stress at bottom of precast** (y = −485.3 mm from NA):

The shrinkage moment puts the precast bottom in **additional compression** because the moment is in the sense that the slab tries to pull itself up (since it is above the NA), so the precast bottom is pushed in.

$$
\sigma_{c1,sh}^{bot} = -\frac{M_{sh} \cdot y}{I_t} = -\frac{142.3 \times 10^6 \times (-485.3)}{1.214 \times 10^{10}} = +5.69 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (compression)}
$$

**Step 3 — Stress at centroid of slab** (y = +189.7 mm from NA):

The slab carries direct tension from restrained shrinkage, plus a bending reduction:

$$
\sigma_{c2} = \frac{P_{sh}}{A_2} - \frac{m \cdot M_{sh} \cdot y}{I_t} = \frac{750 \times 10^3}{1.5 \times 10^5} - \frac{0.78125 \times 142.3 \times 10^6 \times 189.7}{1.214 \times 10^{10}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{c2} = 5.0 - 1.82 = +3.18 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tension)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> 1. **Mixing up which concrete is being transformed.** Most students transform the *cast-in-situ* into equivalent precast (using $m = E_{c2}/E_{c1} < 1$). If you do the reverse, the answers change.
> 2. **Forgetting the modular ratio in the stress equation for the slab.** The bending stress term must be multiplied by $m$ when you de-transform back to the slab material.
> 3. **Taking the eccentricity of slab from the wrong centroid.** It must be from the **composite transformed NA**, not from the precast NA.

---

### Question B: Discuss Differential Shrinkage and Differential Creep Effects in Composite PSC Members [14 Marks] `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Explain the phenomenon of differential shrinkage and derive the expression for the shrinkage force in a composite section. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** In composite construction, the cast-in-situ concrete is placed at a later age than the precast concrete. The cast-in-situ concrete, being freshly placed, undergoes most of its drying shrinkage after hardening. The precast concrete, having already cured, exhibits very little additional shrinkage. The difference in these shrinkage strains, when restrained by the bonded interface, induces internal stresses.

**Derivation:**

The free shrinkage strain in the cast-in-situ slab relative to the precast girder is:

$$
\epsilon_{sh} = \epsilon_{sh,2} - \epsilon_{sh,1}
$$

For the slab to remain compatible with the precast (no slip at interface), the slab must be stretched back by a strain $\epsilon_{sh}$, requiring a tensile force:

$$
P_{sh} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sh}
$$

This force acts at the centroid of the slab and is balanced by an equal and opposite compressive force in the precast girder at the interface. Together they form a self-equilibrating couple of moment:

$$
M_{sh} = P_{sh} \cdot e_{sh}
$$

where $e_{sh}$ is the vertical distance between the slab centroid and the neutral axis of the transformed composite section.

#### (b) Describe differential creep, its effect on the precast and cast-in-situ parts, and the additional stresses induced. [7 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Differential Creep:** Under sustained loads (e.g., self-weight + SIDL), the cast-in-situ concrete creeps more than the precast because:
- It is younger and less mature.
- It typically has a lower characteristic strength.
- The applied sustained stress is higher (slab carries more stress in composite action).

The differential creep strain is:

$$
\Delta\epsilon_{cr} = \phi_2 \cdot \epsilon_{sustained,2} - \phi_1 \cdot \epsilon_{sustained,1}
$$

Where $\phi_2$ and $\phi_1$ are the creep coefficients of the cast-in-situ and precast concretes respectively.

**Effects:**
- The slab **shortens more** in the long term than the precast girder.
- This additional shortening is restrained by the bond, leading to **further tensile stress in the slab** and **compressive stress in the precast beam**.
- In the precast beam, the additional compression occurs at the **soffit level**, partially offsetting prestress losses.
- In the slab, the cumulative tensile stress from shrinkage + creep can cause **transverse cracking** if not properly reinforced.

**Magnitude:** The equivalent creep force is:

$$
P_{cr} = E_{c2} \cdot A_2 \cdot \phi_2 \cdot \sigma_{sustained,2} - E_{c1} \cdot A_1 \cdot \phi_1 \cdot \sigma_{sustained,1}
$$

The total additional moment from time-dependent effects:

$$
M_{td} = M_{sh} + M_{cr} = P_{sh} e_{sh} + P_{cr} e_{cr}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> 1. **Differential creep ≠ total creep.** Always subtract the precast's creep from the cast-in-situ's creep.
> 2. **Do NOT add differential shrinkage and creep stresses without checking sign convention.** They may reinforce or oppose each other depending on the location.
> 3. **Forgetting that creep is a function of the sustained stress level** at the time of loading. A 7-mark question that does not mention "sustained stress" is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cast-in-Situ Slab Composite Sections (Module 4)**

- ☐ A composite section = precast PSC girder + cast-in-situ slab acting monolithically.
- ☐ Three critical stages: Precast alone → Casting slab → Composite under live load.
- ☐ **Differential shrinkage** is the **primary additional stress source** in composite sections.
- ☐ The **differential shrinkage strain** is **always positive** because the cast-in-situ shrinks more than the precast: $\epsilon_{sh} = \epsilon_{sh,2} - \epsilon_{sh,1} > 0$.
- ☐ The shrinkage force acts at the **centroid of the slab**, NOT at the interface, because the slab's mass is concentrated there.
- ☐ The **transformation is done ONCE**: transform the cast-in-situ area by $m = E_{c2}/E_{c1}$ so the whole section is treated as precast material.
- ☐ Centroid of transformed composite section: $y_t = (A_1 \bar{y}_1 + m A_2 \bar{y}_2) / (A_1 + m A_2)$.
- ☐ $P_{sh} = E_{c2} A_2 \epsilon_{sh}$ (this uses the **actual** $E_{c2}$ — do not divide by $m$).
- ☐ Stress in precast: $\sigma_{c1} = P_1/A_1 \pm P_1 e_1 y/I_1 \mp M_{sh} y/I_t$.
- ☐ Stress in slab: $\sigma_{c2} = P_{sh}/A_2 \mp (m M_{sh} y)/I_t$ — note the **$m$ factor** in the bending term.
- ☐ Sign convention (IS 1343): **Compression positive, tension negative.** Eccentricity **below NA = positive**.
- ☐ Differential creep adds more **tensile stress to the slab** and **compression to the precast soffit** over time.
- ☐ Always include **mesh reinforcement** in the cast-in-situ slab to resist shrinkage + creep tension.
- ☐ KTU expected shrinkage strain: **$200 \times 10^{-6}$** for normal, **$300 \times 10^{-6}$** for dry exposure.
- ☐ Allowable stresses per IS 1343 at service: $\sigma_c \leq 0.33 f_{ck}$ (no tension at soffit for Class 1, limited tension for Class 2/3).

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line memory aid:**
> *"The slab pulls itself together (tension), the precast beam resists and gets compressed at the bottom, and the two together form a moment couple that must be added to the prestress analysis."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET525 - DESIGN OF PRESTRESSED CONCRETE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Analysis" in Prestressed Concrete Design?

In the context of Prestressed Concrete (PSC) structures, **Analysis** refers to the systematic evaluation of internal stresses, strains, deformations, and ultimate strength of a prestressed member under various loading conditions. Unlike a plain reinforced concrete member, a PSC member has an *internally induced* stress system (prestress) that must be superimposed with stresses from external loads to determine the *net* state of stress at every critical fiber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (IS 1343 : 2012 aligned):**
> *Analysis of a PSC member is the process of determining the stresses, strains, and resultant forces at a cross-section, at any given loading stage, by superposing the effect of the prestressing force (and its associated eccentricity, profile, and losses) with the effect of the applied external loads (dead load, live load, etc.).*

In KTU Module 4, this analysis is performed at **two distinct limit states**:

| Limit State | Purpose | Governing Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Serviceability Limit State (SLS)** | Check stresses, deflections, cracking | At transfer & at service |
| **Ultimate Limit State (ULS)** | Check flexural/shear strength | At factored design load |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Squeezed Book Stack"

Imagine you are carrying a stack of heavy books with both hands, palms up. Your hands push the books from below to support the load. Now replace your hands with **steel tendons pulled tight**, and replace the books with a **concrete beam** under a downward load.

- The tendons are stretched and anchored against the concrete (your hands pulling the books toward your body).
- This pre-compresses the bottom fiber of the beam.
- When external loads are applied, the bottom tends to be in **tension** and the top in **compression**.
- The *prestress* cancels out part (or all) of the tensile stress, delaying or even preventing cracking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:**
> The job of "analysis" is essentially asking: *"At every cross-section, what is the net stress at the top fiber, the bottom fiber, and at the level of the tendon, after the prestress force and external moments are combined?"*

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Parameters

The following standard values are routinely used in KTU numerical problems:

- **Concrete grade:** M25, M30, M40, M50 (characteristic compressive strength $f_{ck}$ in N/mm²)
- **Prestressing steel grade:** Fe415, Fe500 (relaxation-prone) or low-relaxation strands
- **Modular ratio (short-term):** $m = E_s / E_c$
- **Effective modular ratio (long-term):** $m_{eff}$ (accounts for creep and shrinkage)
- **Partial safety factor for steel at ULS (IS 1343):** $\gamma_s = 1.15$
- **Partial safety factor for concrete at ULS (IS 1343):** $\gamma_c = 1.5$
- **Design bond stress for plain wires (IS 1343 Cl. 7.4.2):** $\tau_{bd}$ values
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stages of Analysis (Operational Logic Steps)

A PSC member is analyzed at **three distinct stages** during its life. Each stage has a different value of prestress force and a different external loading condition.

### Stage 1: At Transfer
- Prestress force is at its **maximum** (initial prestress $P_i$)
- Only **self-weight (dead load)** of the member acts
- Concrete strength is lower (typically 0.8$f_{ck}$ at transfer for post-tensioned; lower for pre-tensioned)
- Tendon profile is parabolic or straight with eccentricity $e$

### Stage 2: At Service (Working Load)
- Prestress force is the **effective** force $P_e$ (after all losses)
- Full dead load + live load act
- Concrete has reached its full 28-day strength $f_{ck}$
- This is the **SLS check stage**

### Stage 3: At Ultimate
- Prestress force is also $P_e$ (or sometimes $\approx 0.8 P_e$ per IS 1343)
- Factored loads act: $1.5(DL + LL)$
- This is the **ULS check stage**
- Concrete may be in plastic state, steel may have yielded

## 2.2 Core "Why" Behind the Analysis

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Question KTU Examiners Love:**
> *Why do we analyze a PSC section at both transfer and service?*
> **Answer:** Because the stress distribution *inverts* between the two stages. At transfer, the beam may be sagging upward (camber) — top fiber can crack. At service, the beam sags downward — bottom fiber can crack. Both extremes must be checked.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial Prestress Force | $P_i = A_p \cdot f_{pi}$ | N | $f_{pi}$ = initial stress in tendon |
| 2 | Effective Prestress Force | $P_e = P_i - \Delta P$ | N | $\Delta P$ = total losses |
| 3 | Stress at a fiber (SLS) | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_e}{A} \pm \dfrac{P_e \cdot e}{I} \cdot y \pm \dfrac{M}{I} \cdot y$ | N/mm² | Superposition principle |
| 4 | Stress at Top Fiber (SLS) | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P_e}{A} - \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y_t}{I} + \dfrac{M}{Z_t}$ | N/mm² | Sign convention: compression $(-)$ |
| 5 | Stress at Bottom Fiber (SLS) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_e}{A} + \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y_b}{I} - \dfrac{M}{Z_b}$ | N/mm² | $Z = I/y$ |
| 6 | Equivalent Pressure (Load Balancing) | $w_{eq} = \dfrac{8 \cdot P \cdot e}{L^2}$ | N/m | For parabolic tendon |
| 7 | Cracking Moment | $M_{cr} = \dfrac{P_e \cdot I}{A \cdot y_b} + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$ | N·mm | $f_{ct}$ = modulus of rupture |
| 8 | Ultimate Moment (Rectangular, tension failure) | $M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p \cdot (d - 0.42 x_u)$ | N·mm | Per IS 1343 Cl. 8.1 |
| 9 | Depth of NA at ULS | $x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}$ | mm | For rectangular section |
| 10 | Design Shear Strength (IS 1343) | $\tau_c = \dfrac{0.85 \cdot \sqrt{0.8 f_{ck}}}{6\beta} \cdot \left(\sqrt{1 + 5\beta} - 1\right)$ | N/mm² | $\beta = 0.8 f_{ck} / (6.89 p \cdot f_p)$ |
| 11 | Centroidal Stresses (Composite) | $\sigma = \dfrac{P_e}{A_c} + \dfrac{M_s \cdot y}{I_c} \pm \dfrac{M_{LL} \cdot y}{I_t}$ | N/mm² | $I_t$ = transformed section |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention (Strictly follow in KTU papers):**
> - **Compression** = **Negative (−)**
> - **Tension** = **Positive (+)**
> - Eccentricity $e$ is positive when tendon lies **below** the centroid (for simply supported beam under gravity loads, this is the typical case).
> - External moment $M$ causing tension at the bottom = **positive sagging moment**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The analytical framework above is the *backbone* of:
- Design of PSC **bridge girders** (I-girders, box girders) — Kerala PWD & NHAI adopt IS 1343 directly.
- Design of **long-span floor beams** in commercial buildings.
- **Railway sleepers** (prestressed concrete sleepers used by Indian Railways).
- **Nuclear containment structures** and **offshore platforms** where crack control is critical.
- Design of **PSC poles** for electrical transmission (KSEB standard designs).

## 2.5 Load Balancing Concept — The "Magical" Analytic Tool

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Load Balancing" Method (Lin/Burns):**
> For a *parabolic* tendon, the prestress force $P$ acting with eccentricity $e(x)$ produces an upward transverse load $w(x) = P \cdot \frac{d^2 e}{dx^2}$. For a parabola $e(x) = 4e_o \cdot x(L-x)/L^2$, this gives a **uniform upward load** $w_{eq} = 8 P e_o / L^2$. The beam then experiences only the *net* of $w_{eq}$ and the applied load. If $w_{eq}$ exactly balances the applied load, the beam is in **pure compression** along its length — *no bending, no deflection*. This is the essence of the **load balancing method** of analysis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: General Stress Equation at a Fiber

### 3.1.1 Setup and Free Body

Consider a simply supported PSC beam of cross-section area $A$, moment of inertia $I$ about the centroidal axis, with a tendon at eccentricity $e$ below the centroid. The effective prestress force is $P_e$.

A fiber at distance $y$ from the centroid is to be analyzed.

### 3.1.2 Stress Contribution from Prestress Force

The prestress force $P_e$, applied at eccentricity $e$, is statically equivalent to:
- An **axial compressive force** $P_e$ at the centroid, and
- A **bending moment** $M_p = P_e \cdot e$ about the centroid.

The stress at a fiber distance $y$ from the centroid due to prestress alone is:

$$\sigma_p(y) = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} - \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$$

The first term is **uniform compression** (negative). The second term is **linearly varying**: for $y$ measured downward (positive), and $e$ also positive (downward), the term $\dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$ produces:
- **Compression** at the top fiber ($y$ is negative → stress becomes more negative)
- **Tension reduction** at the bottom fiber ($y$ is positive → the compression is partially offset, but the $-\dfrac{P_e}{A}$ term is what we subtract carefully)

In the standard KTU sign convention (compression negative), the net effect of prestress is to put the **bottom fiber in compression** (since eccentricity is downward), which is the desired effect.

### 3.1.3 Stress Contribution from External Loads

The external moment $M$ (sagging, positive) causes bending stress:

$$\sigma_M(y) = +\dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

This produces:
- **Tension** at the bottom fiber ($y > 0$)
- **Compression** at the top fiber ($y < 0$)

### 3.1.4 Superposition — The Final Result

The total stress at any fiber distance $y$ is the sum:

$$\sigma(y) = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} \mp \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y}{I} \pm \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign rule for the $e$ term:** When the tendon is *below* the centroid, eccentricity $e$ is positive and the term $\dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$ has the same sign as $y$. To get **compression** at the bottom (where $y$ is positive), we **subtract** this term. Hence the negative sign in front of $\dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y}{I}$.

For the two extreme fibers (top: $y = -y_t$, bottom: $y = +y_b$):

$$\sigma_{top} = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} + \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y_t}{I} - \dfrac{M \cdot y_t}{I}$$

$$\sigma_{bottom} = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} - \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y_b}{I} + \dfrac{M \cdot y_b}{I}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Cracking Moment $M_{cr}$

### 3.2.1 Definition

The **cracking moment** is the external moment at which the maximum tensile stress at the bottom fiber just reaches the **modulus of rupture** $f_{ct}$ of concrete (per IS 1343, $f_{ct} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ for normal concrete, in N/mm²).

### 3.2.2 Condition

At cracking:

$$\sigma_{bottom} = f_{ct} \quad \text{(tensile, positive)}$$

Substituting:

$$f_{ct} = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} - \dfrac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b} + \dfrac{M_{cr}}{Z_b}$$

Wait — the bottom fiber is in compression due to prestress (negative stress). The external moment $M$ produces *tensile* stress at the bottom. So as $M$ increases, the compressive prestress is *neutralized* and eventually reversed. At the cracking moment, the *net* stress is $f_{ct}$ (tensile).

Mathematically (with the convention that tensile = positive):

$$+\dfrac{M_{cr}}{Z_b} - \dfrac{P_e}{A} - \dfrac{P_e \cdot e}{Z_b} = f_{ct}$$

Solving for $M_{cr}$:

$$M_{cr} = P_e \cdot \left( \dfrac{Z_b}{A} + e \right) + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$

In a more compact form using $Z_b = I / y_b$:

$$M_{cr} = \dfrac{P_e \cdot I}{A \cdot y_b} + P_e \cdot e + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$

Or, recognizing that the **eccentric prestress** itself has an equivalent moment arm of $\left( \dfrac{r^2}{y_b} + e \right)$ (where $r^2 = I/A$ is the square of the radius of gyration):

$$M_{cr} = P_e \left( e + \dfrac{r^2}{y_b} \right) + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation appears almost every year in KTU Module 4 derivations. **Memorize the form** with $\left( e + r^2 / y_b \right)$ — the examiners love testing this.

## 3.3 Derivation: Ultimate Moment of Resistance (IS 1343, Rectangular Section)

### 3.3.1 Assumptions (IS 1343 Cl. 8.1)

1. Plane sections remain plane.
2. Maximum compressive strain in concrete at the extreme fiber = **0.0035** (post-IS 1343:1980).
3. Tensile strength of concrete is **ignored** at ULS.
4. Effective stress in the prestressing steel at ULS: $f_p = f_{pu} - \Delta f_p$ (where $\Delta f_p$ accounts for losses and is usually taken as per the stress-strain curve of the steel type).
5. Partial safety factors: $\gamma_m = 1.15$ for steel.

### 3.3.2 Equilibrium

For a rectangular section of width $b$ and effective depth $d$, the area of prestressing steel is $A_p$. At ULS:

$$T = C$$

$$f_p \cdot A_p = 0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_u$$

where $x_u$ is the depth of the neutral axis, and the stress block has a uniform value of $0.36 f_{ck}$ over a depth $0.9 x_u$ (or for the IS 1343 stress block, area = $0.36 f_{ck} \cdot b \cdot x_u$).

Therefore:

$$x_u = \dfrac{f_p \cdot A_p}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}$$

But applying the safety factor (per IS 1343: $f_{sd} = f_p / 1.15$ for non-brittle steel):

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}$$

### 3.3.3 Moment Capacity

The lever arm between the centroid of compression and the centroid of tension is approximately $(d - 0.42 x_u)$. Hence:

$$M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p \cdot \left( d - 0.42 x_u \right)$$

### 3.3.4 Classification of Section at ULS (IS 1343)

- **Under-reinforced (tension-controlled):** $x_u / d < 0.53$ (for Fe500) — ductile failure, preferred.
- **Balanced:** $x_u / d = 0.53$ (for Fe500) — simultaneous crushing & yielding.
- **Over-reinforced (compression-controlled):** $x_u / d > 0.53$ — brittle, to be avoided.

## 3.4 Derivation: Load Balancing — Equivalent UDL from Parabolic Tendon

### 3.4.1 Tendon Profile

A parabolic tendon profile is given by:

$$e(x) = 4 e_o \cdot \dfrac{x (L - x)}{L^2}$$

where $e_o$ is the maximum eccentricity at midspan, and $L$ is the span.

### 3.4.2 Equivalent Transverse Load

For a prestressing force $P$ applied along a curved profile, the transverse load per unit length is:

$$w(x) = P \cdot \dfrac{d^2 e(x)}{dx^2}$$

Compute the second derivative:

$$\dfrac{d e}{dx} = \dfrac{4 e_o}{L^2} (L - 2x)$$

$$\dfrac{d^2 e}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{8 e_o}{L^2}$$

Therefore:

$$w_{eq} = -\dfrac{8 P e_o}{L^2}$$

The negative sign indicates the equivalent load acts **upward** (opposite to gravity). Magnitude:

$$w_{eq} = \dfrac{8 P e_o}{L^2} \quad \text{(upward)}$$

### 3.4.3 Net Load on Beam

If the applied load is a downward UDL $w_{applied}$, the net load is:

$$w_{net} = w_{applied} - w_{eq}$$

If $w_{net} = 0$, the beam is in **pure axial compression** — the most elegant design condition.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Tip:** When asked *"Design a PSC beam using the load balancing method"*, you will balance *part* of the load (typically the dead load + a fraction of the live load) and let the remaining load produce a small, acceptable bending moment.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Analysis Methods

| Method | Used For | Complexity | Best For KTU Numericals? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Equivalent Pressure (Load Balancing)** | Finding net moment/shear under service load | Low | Yes, Part A & conceptual B |
| **Superposition (P/M Stress Sum)** | Finding stress at transfer & service | Medium | **Yes, most KTU problems** |
| **Strain Compatibility** | Cracking moment, ultimate moment with mixed reinforcement | High | Yes, advanced 14-mark problems |
| **Magnel Diagram** | Finding allowable tendon zone at service | High | Rare in KTU, but asked in theory |
| **Pressure Line (Thrust Line)** | Continuous beams, indeterminate PSC | Very High | Theory questions only |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Analysis Workflow for PSC Members

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete analytical workflow a KTU student must follow when analyzing a prestressed concrete member:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Section Geometry, Materials, Tendon Profile, Loads] --> B[Compute Section Properties: A, I, Zt, Zb, yt, yb]
    B --> C[Compute Initial Prestress Pi and Effective Prestress Pe]
    C --> D[Stage 1 Analysis: At Transfer]
    D --> D1[Apply Pi plus Self-weight Moment M0]
    D1 --> D2[Compute stress at top, bottom, and tendon level]
    D2 --> D3{Check IS 1343 Transfer Stresses}
    D3 -->|OK| E[Stage 2 Analysis: At Service]
    D3 -->|Fail| X1[Revise tendon profile or Pi]
    X1 --> D
    E --> E1[Apply Pe plus Full Service Moment M = M0 + ML]
    E1 --> E2[Compute stress at top and bottom fibers]
    E2 --> E3{Check IS 1343 Service Stresses}
    E3 -->|OK| F[Stage 3 Analysis: At Ultimate]
    E3 -->|Fail| X2[Increase Pe or Add Non-prestressed Steel]
    X2 --> E
    F --> F1[Apply Factored Moment Mu = 1.5 M]
    F1 --> F2[Compute xu from Equilibrium]
    F2 --> F3[Compute Mu from Lever Arm]
    F3 --> F4{Is Mu greater than Factored External Moment?}
    F4 -->|Yes| G[Design Safe]
    F4 -->|No| X3[Increase Ap or revise section]
    X3 --> F
```

## 4.2 Stress Distribution Schematic: At Transfer vs. At Service

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the qualitative stress distribution at the two critical stages of analysis:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[At Transfer: Pi acts, only self-weight]
        A1[Top Fiber: Slight Tension or Zero] --> A2[Centroid: Pure Compression = -Pi/A]
        A2 --> A3[Bottom Fiber: Maximum Compression]
    end
    subgraph B[At Service: Pe acts, full load]
        B1[Top Fiber: Maximum Compression] --> B2[Centroid: Pure Compression = -Pe/A]
        B2 --> B3[Bottom Fiber: Minimum Compression or Slight Tension]
    end
    A -->|Losses occur, load increases| B
```

## 4.3 Load Balancing Block Schematic

The following block diagram represents how the parabolic tendon creates an equivalent upward UDL that counteracts the applied downward UDL:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Applied Downward UDL: w_app] --> N1[Net Resultant]
    L2[Parabolic Tendon with Force P] --> L3[Equivalent Upward UDL: w_eq = 8Pe_o / L squared]
    L3 --> N1
    N1{Balance Condition w_eq equals w_app}
    N1 -->|Yes| O1[Pure Axial Compression along Beam]
    N1 -->|No| O2[Residual Uniform Load equals w_app minus w_eq]
    O2 --> O3[Bending plus Compression: Analyze with Superposition]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip:** The block diagram is a functional mapping. In an actual KTU drawing, you would draw a parabolic curve and a horizontal line representing the equivalent load, but the *conceptual flow* is captured here for revision.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the term "cracking moment" of a prestressed concrete member. Write the expression for the cracking moment of a PSC beam.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **cracking moment** $M_{cr}$ of a prestressed concrete member is the external bending moment at which the maximum tensile stress at the extreme tension fiber just reaches the **modulus of rupture** $f_{ct}$ of concrete, causing the first visible crack.
>
> Per IS 1343, $f_{ct} = 0.7\sqrt{f_{ck}}$ (N/mm²).
>
> For a PSC beam with effective prestress $P_e$, eccentricity $e$, radius of gyration $r = \sqrt{I/A}$, and section modulus at the bottom fiber $Z_b$:
>
> $$M_{cr} = P_e \left( e + \dfrac{r^2}{y_b} \right) + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$
>
> Equivalently:
>
> $$M_{cr} = P_e \cdot \left( \dfrac{Z_b}{A} + e \right) + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**State the load balancing concept for the analysis of prestressed concrete beams.**

**Model Answer:**

> The **load balancing method** (Lin and Burns) is an analytical technique in which a curved prestressing tendon is replaced by an equivalent transverse load acting on the concrete beam. For a parabolic tendon of sag $e_o$ carrying an effective prestress $P_e$ in a simply supported beam of span $L$, the equivalent uniform upward load is:
>
> $$w_{eq} = \dfrac{8 P_e \cdot e_o}{L^2}$$
>
> If the applied downward UDL $w$ equals $w_{eq}$, the beam experiences **no bending** and is in pure axial compression. If they are unequal, the net uniform load $(w - w_{eq})$ produces residual bending, which can be analyzed by simple statics. This method greatly simplifies the analysis of indeterminate and continuous PSC structures.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`

A post-tensioned prestressed concrete beam of rectangular cross-section 250 mm × 500 mm (deep) has a simply supported span of 10 m. A parabolic tendon with maximum sag of 80 mm at midspan carries an effective prestress force of 1100 kN. The beam supports a self-weight of 4 kN/m (already included in dead load) and a live load of 6 kN/m.

**Compute the following:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] The stresses at the top and bottom fibers of the critical section (midspan) at the **service stage**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The **cracking moment** $M_{cr}$ and the **ultimate moment of resistance** $M_u$, using $f_{ck} = 40$ N/mm², $f_p = 1500$ N/mm² (stress in steel at ULS), and $A_p = 750$ mm².

### Model Solution

#### Given Data
- Section: $b = 250$ mm, $D = 500$ mm
- Span: $L = 10$ m
- $P_e = 1100$ kN $= 1100 \times 10^3$ N
- Maximum eccentricity: $e_o = 80$ mm (at midspan)
- Dead load (self-weight) UDL: $w_{DL} = 4$ kN/m
- Live load UDL: $w_{LL} = 6$ kN/m
- $f_{ck} = 40$ N/mm²
- $f_p = 1500$ N/mm² at ULS
- $A_p = 750$ mm²

#### Section Properties (Rectangular, b = 250, D = 500)
- Area: $A = b \cdot D = 250 \times 500 = 125{,}000$ mm²
- Moment of Inertia: $I = \dfrac{b D^3}{12} = \dfrac{250 \times 500^3}{12} = 2.604 \times 10^9$ mm⁴
- Section modulus (top and bottom are equal for rectangle): $Z = \dfrac{I}{D/2} = \dfrac{2.604 \times 10^9}{250} = 1.0417 \times 10^7$ mm³
- $y_t = y_b = 250$ mm

---

#### **Part (a) — Stresses at Service**

**Step 1: Compute the service moment at midspan.**

Total service UDL (DL + LL): $w = 4 + 6 = 10$ kN/m.

Midspan moment for a simply supported beam with UDL:

$$M = \dfrac{w L^2}{8} = \dfrac{10 \times 10^2}{8} = 125 \text{ kN·m} = 125 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute stress at the top fiber (compression dominant).**

Using the general formula (compression negative):

$$\sigma_{top} = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} + \dfrac{P_e \cdot e_o \cdot y_t}{I} - \dfrac{M \cdot y_t}{I}$$

Compute each term:

$$\dfrac{P_e}{A} = \dfrac{1100 \times 10^3}{125{,}000} = 8.8 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\dfrac{P_e \cdot e_o \cdot y_t}{I} = \dfrac{1100 \times 10^3 \times 80 \times 250}{2.604 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^{10}}{2.604 \times 10^9} = 8.448 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\dfrac{M \cdot y_t}{I} = \dfrac{125 \times 10^6 \times 250}{2.604 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{3.125 \times 10^{10}}{2.604 \times 10^9} = 12.002 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Substitute:

$$\sigma_{top} = -8.8 + 8.448 - 12.002 = -12.354 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

(Compression, **12.35 N/mm²**)

**Step 3: Compute stress at the bottom fiber (prestress dominant).**

$$\sigma_{bottom} = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} - \dfrac{P_e \cdot e_o \cdot y_b}{I} + \dfrac{M \cdot y_b}{I}$$

$$\sigma_{bottom} = -8.8 - 8.448 + 12.002 = -5.246 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

(Compression, **5.25 N/mm²**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Both fibers are in compression. The beam is **uncracked** and performing as designed. The top fiber has higher compression because both the external moment and the moment due to eccentric prestress add up at the top.

---

#### **Part (b) — Cracking Moment and Ultimate Moment**

**Step 1: Modulus of rupture per IS 1343.**

$$f_{ct} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}} = 0.7 \sqrt{40} = 0.7 \times 6.3246 = 4.427 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Cracking Moment.**

Compute $r^2 = I / A$:

$$r^2 = \dfrac{2.604 \times 10^9}{125{,}000} = 20{,}833.33 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$y_b = 250 \text{ mm}$$

$$M_{cr} = P_e \left( e_o + \dfrac{r^2}{y_b} \right) + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$

$$\dfrac{r^2}{y_b} = \dfrac{20{,}833.33}{250} = 83.33 \text{ mm}$$

$$M_{cr} = 1100 \times 10^3 \times \left( 80 + 83.33 \right) + 4.427 \times 1.0417 \times 10^7$$

$$M_{cr} = 1100 \times 10^3 \times 163.33 + 46.12 \times 10^6$$

$$M_{cr} = 179.66 \times 10^6 + 46.12 \times 10^6 = 225.78 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_{cr} \approx 225.78 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 3: Ultimate Moment of Resistance.**

Compute the depth of the neutral axis:

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p}{0.36 \cdot f_{ck} \cdot b}$$

$$x_u = \dfrac{0.87 \times 1500 \times 750}{0.36 \times 40 \times 250} = \dfrac{978{,}750}{3600} = 271.875 \text{ mm}$$

Check the section classification (for Fe500, $x_u/d \le 0.53$; assume $f_p$ per IS 1343 corresponds to a ductile steel). Assuming effective depth $d \approx 450$ mm (with cover and duct):

$$\dfrac{x_u}{d} = \dfrac{271.875}{450} = 0.604$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** If $x_u/d > 0.53$, the section is *over-reinforced* and the formula for $M_u$ given above is **not strictly valid** — a more rigorous strain-compatibility analysis (or compression-controlled design) is required. For a 14-mark question, you should state this check explicitly and conclude accordingly. In practice, designers limit $A_p$ or add non-prestressed reinforcement to keep $x_u/d \le 0.53$.

Compute the lever arm:

$$z = d - 0.42 x_u = 450 - 0.42 \times 271.875 = 450 - 114.19 = 335.81 \text{ mm}$$

Ultimate moment:

$$M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p \cdot z = 978{,}750 \times 335.81 = 3.286 \times 10^8 \text{ N·mm}$$

$$\boxed{M_u \approx 328.6 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 4: Compare with factored applied moment.**

Factored service moment: $M_{u,\text{applied}} = 1.5 \times 125 = 187.5$ kN·m.

Since $M_u = 328.6$ kN·m $> M_{u,\text{applied}} = 187.5$ kN·m, **the section is SAFE at ULS** with a factor of safety of $\approx 1.75$.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]`

A simply supported PSC beam of span 12 m and rectangular cross-section 300 mm × 600 mm carries a parabolic tendon with a maximum sag of 100 mm at midspan. The effective prestress in the tendon is 1500 kN. The beam supports a uniformly distributed load of 18 kN/m (including self-weight).

**(a)** [7 Marks] Using the **load balancing method**, find the residual UDL acting on the beam and the net bending moment at midspan.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the stresses at the **top** and **bottom** fibers of the midspan section and verify if the section satisfies IS 1343 serviceability limits (no tension, compression $\le 0.45 f_{ck}$). Take $f_{ck} = 35$ N/mm².

### Model Solution Outline

**Given:**
- $b = 300$ mm, $D = 600$ mm, $L = 12$ m
- $P_e = 1500 \times 10^3$ N
- $e_o = 100$ mm
- $w_{app} = 18$ kN/m
- $f_{ck} = 35$ N/mm²

**Section Properties:**
- $A = 300 \times 600 = 180{,}000$ mm²
- $I = \dfrac{300 \times 600^3}{12} = 5.4 \times 10^9$ mm⁴
- $Z = \dfrac{I}{300} = 1.8 \times 10^7$ mm³
- $y_t = y_b = 300$ mm

**Part (a) — Load Balancing:**

$$w_{eq} = \dfrac{8 P_e e_o}{L^2} = \dfrac{8 \times 1500 \times 10^3 \times 100}{(12 \times 10^3)^2} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9}{1.44 \times 10^8} = 8.333 \text{ N/mm} = 8.333 \text{ kN/m}$$

Residual UDL (downward, positive):

$$w_{net} = w_{app} - w_{eq} = 18 - 8.333 = 9.667 \text{ kN/m}$$

Net midspan moment:

$$M_{net} = \dfrac{w_{net} L^2}{8} = \dfrac{9.667 \times 12^2}{8} = 174.0 \text{ kN·m}$$

**Part (b) — Stresses at Midspan:**

Total service moment (alternative way: $M = w_{app} L^2/8 = 18 \times 144/8 = 324$ kN·m, which should equal $M_{net} + P_e e_o$):

Check: $M_{net} + P_e e_o = 174.0 \times 10^6 + 1500 \times 10^3 \times 100 = 174 \times 10^6 + 150 \times 10^6 = 324 \times 10^6$ N·mm ✓

$$\dfrac{P_e}{A} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3}{180{,}000} = 8.333 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\dfrac{P_e e_o y_t}{I} = \dfrac{1500 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 300}{5.4 \times 10^9} = 8.333 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\dfrac{M y_t}{I} = \dfrac{324 \times 10^6 \times 300}{5.4 \times 10^9} = 18.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{top} = -8.333 + 8.333 - 18.0 = -18.0 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_{bottom} = -8.333 - 8.333 + 18.0 = +1.334 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Mistakes:** The most common error is mixing compression positive and compression negative. *Always* state your sign convention at the start.
> 2. **Tension vs. Compression Confusion:** A *positive* stress here means tension. IS 1343 (Cl. 8.1) generally **disallows tension** at service for Class 1 (Uncracked) structures. If $\sigma_{bottom} = +1.334$ N/mm² is not allowed, the section fails — increase $P_e$, increase $e_o$, or enlarge the section.
> 3. **Allowable Compression Check:** $0.45 f_{ck} = 0.45 \times 35 = 15.75$ N/mm². Our top fiber stress is 18.0 N/mm², which **exceeds** the limit. The section also fails the compression check.
> 4. **Common Loss of Marks:** Forgetting to use $\gamma_m$ at ULS, using the *initial* prestress $P_i$ instead of *effective* $P_e$ at service, and confusing $Z$ with $I$ are the top three mistakes cited by KTU examiners.

The section is **NOT adequate** at service stage. Solutions: increase $P_e$ to $1700$ kN, or increase eccentricity, or use a higher grade of concrete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for the night before the KTU exam):**
>
> - **Three Stages of Analysis:** Transfer (with $P_i$, self-weight only), Service (with $P_e$, full load), Ultimate (with factored loads).
> - **Sign Convention:** Compression $(-)$, Tension $(+)$; $e > 0$ for tendon below centroid.
> - **General Stress Formula (MUST memorize):**
>
> $$\sigma(y) = -\dfrac{P_e}{A} \mp \dfrac{P_e \cdot e \cdot y}{I} \pm \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$$
>
> - **Cracking Moment Formula (MUST memorize):**
>
> $$M_{cr} = P_e \left( e + \dfrac{r^2}{y_b} \right) + f_{ct} \cdot Z_b$$
>
> - **Modulus of Rupture (IS 1343):** $f_{ct} = 0.7 \sqrt{f_{ck}}$.
> - **Ultimate Moment (Rectangular, IS 1343):**
>
> $$M_u = 0.87 \cdot f_p \cdot A_p \cdot (d - 0.42 x_u), \quad x_u = \dfrac{0.87 f_p A_p}{0.36 f_{ck} b}$$
>
> - **Section Classification:** Tension-controlled if $x_u/d \le 0.53$ (for Fe500), else compression-controlled.
> - **Load Balancing Formula (Parabolic Tendon):** $w_{eq} = 8 P e_o / L^2$ (upward).
> - **Service Stress Limits (IS 1343 Cl. 8.1):**
>   - Class 1 (no tension): $0 \le \sigma_c \le 0.45 f_{ck}$
>   - Class 2 (no cracking): $-f_{ct} \le \sigma_t \le 0.45 f_{ck}$
>   - Class 3 (cracked): $0 \le \sigma_c \le 0.45 f_{ck}$
> - **IS 1343 Safety Factors:** $\gamma_c = 1.5$, $\gamma_s = 1.15$.
> - **Kerala PSC Application:** Kerala PWD bridges (AASHTO Type I/II girders) and NHAI overpasses are designed per IRC:18 and IS:1343, both of which use the formulas above.
> - **Common Exam Traps:** Initial vs. effective prestress, $M_0$ (self-weight) vs. $M$ (full service), $Z_t$ vs. $Z_b$ for unsymmetric sections, missing the partial safety factor in $M_u$.
> - **Numerical Tip:** Always compute $P_e/A$ and $P_e e / Z$ *separately* before combining — this prevents arithmetic errors in KTU 14-mark problems.
